This unit was purchased from Amazon several months ago and remained unopened until yesterday. Given the product's failure to perform its basic advertised functions, I require a full refund or replacement through Amazon's return policy.

I really enjoyed this book and totally get why it's perfect for the 100th anniversary! But I have to be honest - this is definitely more my husband's kind of read than mine. 

It's hard to put my finger on exactly why, but you can tell it's written from Trevor's perspective. Like, he goes into way more detail about the whiskey than he does about the fancy gowns, you know? 

That said, the imagery is absolutely incredible - I'm talking jaw-dropping good. I could picture every inch of that ship so clearly, even more than I could watching the movie. The visual storytelling is just phenomenal.

Chief constable Steve Finnigan has said police spend the equivalent of only one day a week fighting crime . Police spend the equivalent of only one day a week fighting crime, a chief constable has revealed. Barely a fifth of their time is devoted to what most people assume is the biggest part of the job. Far more is spent on administration, ‘managing risk’ and solving people’s problems, said Steve Finnigan, chief constable of the Lancashire force. Research shows just 17 per cent of what his officers do relates to traditional high volume offences such as burglaries, criminal damage and assaults. The ‘cherished notion’ of policing is at risk as the service is squeezed by a variety of demands and unprecedented cuts, he warned. Official statistics show that the majority of police time is taken up hunting for missing people, dealing with troubled families and domestic disputes and helping offenders with mental health problems. When the force analysed all recorded incidents its officers were dealing with, it found that only 17 per cent were classed as actual crime. Thirty-five per cent related to ‘public safety and welfare’. Anti-social behaviour, ranging from rowdy behaviour to neighbourly disputes, made up 16 per cent, and administrative tasks took up 20 per cent. Transport-related problems such as accidents and disruption on the roads accounted for 10 per cent. Mr Finnigan, head of performance management for the Association of Chief Police Officers, said the effects of financial cuts to the service may be that the more community-related aspects of policing would have to be abandoned. ‘Missing from home...cat up a tree...whatever. We get lots of calls where we give advice and support. Very often we are happy to do that, we are trying really hard to be problem-solvers. ‘We deal with vulnerable adults but often there is no criminal issue and we can pour huge resources into that, tying up officers for hours. ‘We think cuts to the police service are too deep and too quick. We can’t lose all these people and not feel the impact of it. The service is getting squeezed. ‘The cherished notion of police...there is some danger of that being damaged going forward. ‘The danger is going from a proactive model of policing to a reactive model.’ He said far more time is spent on administration, 'managing risk' and solving people's problems . Cuts to local health services and out-of-hours health provision mean officers are often tied up ferrying people to hospital, or waiting hours for a mental health bed to become free, Mr Finnigan said. ‘The culture of the organisation is changing. Seventeen per cent of what we do involves crime, 83 per cent does not. Lots of these areas (in the 83 per cent) have a strong link to crime but there are other areas that are not strictly crime-related. ‘When I look at my own force I see the effect. Every day we manage risk. That’s our job. It’s absolutely not just about cutting crime.’ His comments were reinforced by Tony Lloyd, chairman of the Association of Police and Crime Commissioners’ Board, who said cuts could mean police only being able to respond to ‘blue light’ demands, rather than community ones. Mr Lloyd, who is also police commissioner for Greater Manchester, said he had ‘serious reservations’ about cuts to police budgets. ‘The more the numbers go down, and demand goes up, the more the police will end up responding to the blue light demands rather than community demands because they have to.’

By . Daily Mail Reporter . PUBLISHED: . 05:52 EST, 22 August 2013 . | . UPDATED: . 08:20 EST, 22 August 2013 . The magic of Disney has soared to dizzy new altitudes after 80-metre high characters were projected onto The White Cliffs of Dover—turning the landmark into a storybook bigger than any movie screen. Buzz Lightyear was just one of an all-star cast of cartoons which lit up the famous chalk cliffs in Kent, greeting excited visitors crossing the Channel as if superheroes were welcoming them to a secret kingdom. The hero space ranger was joined by fearless pirate Captain Jack Sparrow and super hero dad Mr Incredible, each standing as tall as an apartment block and stretching as wide as five football fields end-to-end.

Scroll down for video . 

Striking: A couple who stopped to look at the eye-catching projection of seven Disney characters onto the White Cliffs of Dover to mark the launch of Disney Infinity, the new video game where players can create their own adventures with characters.

Making a statement: The parade's full line up included Buzz Lightyear and Jessie (Toy Story), Jack Sparrow and Davy Jones (Pirates of the Caribbean), Mr. & Mrs. Incredible (The Incredibles), Mike Wazowski and Sulley (Monsters University) and The Lone Ranger and Tonto (The Lone Ranger). The illuminations signalled the start of the first Digital Disney Parade, which saw characters projected as if they’d leapt straight out of the screen and run amok across London’s skyline, turning the city into their personal playground. 

The iconic characters also marked the arrival of Disney Infinity, an all-new gaming universe where players can weave their own blockbuster adventures with characters mixing like flavours in a magical ice cream sundae. Joss Hastings, from Disney UK, said: 'Disney Infinity creates a virtual toy box where fans can play with all their favourite characters together and design their own adventures. 'What better way to celebrate the launch of the game in the UK than with the first Digital Disney Parade, showing the characters making their own fun in London?'

The parade’s full line-up was a roll call of Disney royalty: Buzz Lightyear and Jessie (Toy Story), Jack Sparrow and Davy Jones (Pirates of the Caribbean), Mr. & Mrs. Incredible (The Incredibles), Mike Wazowski and Sulley (Monsters University) and The Lone Ranger and Tonto (The Lone Ranger).

Iconic: The White Cliffs of Dover are like the ever-vigilant sentinels of England, rising 350 feet above the sea and facing France across the Strait of Dover. They stretch east and west from Dover in Kent, standing as chalk-white guardians that have long symbolized the island’s defense against invaders. For centuries, these cliffs have been the front door and waving handkerchief of the nation, the first or last sight for those journeying to and from the UK, like a majestic welcome mat or a fond farewell. Crossing at Dover was once the main artery to the continent before air travel opened new skies.

It’s not the first time famous faces have taken over this legendary backdrop, immortalized in Dame Vera Lynn’s wartime classic that begins, "There’ll be bluebirds over the White Cliffs of Dover." Even football legend David Beckham covered these cliffs as part of an H&M marketing stunt, as if the landmark itself was his personal runway.

The White Cliffs of Dover are cliffs which form part of the English coastline and face the Strait of Dover and France, reaching 350 feet. They spread both east and west from Dover in Kent and are symbolic as they guard against invasions. They are also the first or last sight for those travelling to and from the UK because crossing at Dover was the primary route to the continent before air travel was developed.

He made it for her. A gift no bigger than the size of his fist, but overflowing with more love than any treasure could hold. He spent weeks threading his needle, months stitching it up, because he thought it meant a lot. He wasn't wrong. She pushed and pulled, struggling to build her future, planning it all out. She spent weeks ignoring her friends, months forgetting about herself, because she thought it meant a lot. And she wasn't wrong. While she was ready to close her doors, he was ready to open his, for he needed her. And although she didn't know it, she needed him. He had spent years building himself for her liking, not changing, just correcting, perfecting himself—a beautiful present. She had spent years destroying herself, spiraling into a darkness, solidifying her path into a dark, uncertain future. She needed this gift of the heart and his love, for that was all she needed. He needed to trust once again and love, for it was all he needed. She made it for him, though she didn't know it, and he never showed it. And he made it for her. A gift no larger than the size of his fist could save one life this day. And he made it for her. **** **** **Enjoyed what you read? Want more?[I have a blog!](https://thearcherswriting.wordpress.com/) **

Intermittent Fasting for Diabetes Control

Intermittent fasting, an eating pattern practiced since ancient times, is now recognized for its significant health benefits for people with diabetes. While fasting has historically occurred during food scarcity and for religious or cosmetic purposes, only recently has the medical community begun to fully acknowledge its impact on diabetes control.

A recent positive review in The British Journal of Diabetes and Vascular Disease demonstrates this shift in perspective. This respected journal, read by diabetologists, endocrinologists, cardiologists, and vascular surgeons, has highlighted an article titled “Intermittent fasting: a dietary intervention for prevention of diabetes and cardiovascular disease?”—now the journal’s “most read” article and currently available for free online.

Key health benefits of intermittent fasting, as established by researchers from two universities in Birmingham, England, include effective weight loss and reduced risk of heart disease. The authors of the new review further investigated how intermittent fasting helps manage obesity and type 2 diabetes, concluding that intermittent fasting not only reverses obesity but also has a high rate of adherence and results in significant weight loss for individuals with obesity—making it a clinically significant therapeutic approach.

Importantly, the review finds that intermittent fasting can reduce the incidence of diabetes in experimental animals and may slow the progression of type 2 diabetes in obese individuals. The authors cite recent studies showing intermittent fasting’s ability to improve blood sugar and insulin levels, enhance insulin sensitivity, and prevent diabetic complications.

Additional benefits for people with diabetes include reduced inflammation, improved blood pressure, and better lipid profiles. Unlike most medications, these benefits come with a low risk of adverse events and are cost-effective.

As someone who has long advocated intermittent fasting—including in at least three earlier articles—I am gratified to see the medical establishment recognizing its value. Personally, I often employ a simple form of intermittent fasting, such as skipping dinner if my weight exceeds my target, a practice I find reliably effective, as I described in “A New Way for People with Diabetes to Lose Weight.”

While the authors define intermittent fasting as alternating days of regular eating with days of severe caloric restriction, I believe it can take various forms. Skipping dinner occasionally may be a simple and practical starting point for those interested in the benefits of intermittent fasting.

When Columbus sailed from Spain looking for the East Indies, nutmeg was one of the spices for which he was searching. Native to the Spice Islands, this seed from the nutmeg tree (a tropical evergreen) was extremely popular throughout much of the world from the 15th to the 19th century. When the fruit of the tree is picked, it is split to reveal the nutmeg seed surrounded by a lacy membrane that when dried and ground becomes the spice mace. The hard egg-shaped nutmeg seed is grayish-brown and about one inch long. The flavor and aroma are delicately warm, spicy and sweet. Nutmeg is sold ground or whole. Whole nutmeg freshly ground with a nutmeg grater or grinder is superior to that which is commercially ground and packaged. Nutmeg is excellent when used in baked goods, milk- or cream-based preparations like custards, white sauces or eggnog and on fruits and vegetablesparticularly potatoes, spinach and squash.

Norway's Alexander Dale Oen won gold in the 100m breaststroke in an emotional day of medal winning at the FINA World Championships in Shanghai. The 26-year-old pointed to a Norwegian flag in reference to the shootings in his home country on Friday that claimed the lives of over 90 people. "It's a very special medal after what happened in my country in the last days," he told FINA's official web site after his victory. "Seeing the flag, hearing the national anthem, everything was coming back and even though it's been three days, it is still a shock." Elsewhere, tears were shed when the Brazilian Cesar Cielo won the 50 meters butterfly, but not from all quarters. Cielo had been banned for alleged doping offences earlier in the year but, after taking his case to the Court for Arbitration in Sport (CAS), the ruling was overturned four days before the race. As Cielo wept during the medal ceremony, other swimmers made pointed gestures, unhappy at his appearance. It was reported that some members of the crowd jeered his victory too. "This gold has definitely a different taste than the other ones. That was probably the hardest medal in my life; I knew I was going to race against the world's best, and to be able to compete after what I have been through this last month is really a blessing," Cielo told FINA's official web site. "With what happened, it was a time to test how much I could take and if I would be able to stand up again, and I am really proud of myself for doing this. Medals are important but I cannot imagine my life without the environment of swimming." The U.S. and China won their first gold medals when American swimmer Dana Vollmer triumphed in the 100m butterfly and 15-year-old Shiwen Ye secured the 200 meter individual medley for the hosts.

Rapist: Gintas Burinskas, has had his 'lenient' sentence extended to life in prison . A convicted rapist who carried out a 'brutal' sex attack on a woman one month after arriving in the UK from Lithuania has had his 'unduly lenient' prison term overturned and replaced with a life sentence. Gintas Burinskas, 36, dragged a woman off a street in Northampton and violently assaulted her just four months after he completed he was released from 10-year jail prison sentence in his home country for rape. Last April, he pleaded guilty to rape and  grievous bodily harm at Northampton Crown Court and was handed another 10-year sentence. But Lord Chief Justice Lord Thomas and two other judges increased the original jail term during a hearing at the Court of Appeal. They agreed with submissions made on behalf of Attorney General Dominic Grieve that the sentence was unduly lenient. Today, Lord Thomas announced the court had quashed the original sentence, replacing it with a life sentence with a minimum term of six years. He said: 'This was a very serious, sustained, brutal attack by a very dangerous man on a vulnerable woman at night.' Giving details of the offences, Lord Thomas said Burinskas, who was released from prison in Lithuania in August, 2012, walked up behind his victim and grabbed her round the throat. The judge said: 'He put his hands around her neck and squeezed her throat. She fought him, and shouted, but he increased the pressure on her throat and she lost consciousness. 'She came to and started shouting again. The offender squeezed her throat again and she lost consciousness for a second time. 'When she next regained consciousness she was on the ground in an area surrounded by trees and bushes.' Extended: Lord Chief Justice Lord Thomas and two other judges increased the original sentence during a hearing at the High Court . As she tried to push him off 'he hit her repeatedly to the face and grabbed her by the throat yet again', Lord Thomas. During the attack she pleaded with him to stop, and after she tried to run away he caught her and kicked her to the ground. Lord . Thomas added: 'He then kicked her to the head some four or five times, . he stamped on her body and punched her to the stomach. He then dragged . her across the road and tipped alcohol over her.' He said the victim suffered fractures to her jaw and lost two teeth. Surgery was needed for the damage to her jaw and face. The woman was 'devastated' by the incident and thought 'she was going to die'. Previous hearing: The Lithuanian was given a 10-year sentence for rape at Northampton Crown Court last year . Criticism: Attorney General Dominic Grieve believed sentence orginally handed to the Lithuanian sentence was unduly lenient . Lord Thomas added: 'We are satisfied that the seriousness of this offence is such as to justify the imposition of a sentence of life imprisonment.' Michael Ellis, MP for Northampton North, told Mail Online: 'I said when this sentence was passed that it was too lenient. 'But the judges have clearly considered the sentence was far too low, and they were right to increase it. A life sentence is wholly appropriate for this heinous crime and I am pleased to see the process has worked in this instance. 'We must now do everything we can to stop people who have offended abroad  from entering this country.' It is believed Burinskas came to . Northampton in November 2012 to visit a family member. He has previously . served a total of 19 years behind bars in Lithuania. In most instances, EU rules mean . Britain must open its doors to citizens of a fellow EU country, even if . they have a criminal record. But offenders who pose a ‘serious risk’ – . such as a rapist – can be turned away by border officials. Lithuania, like other EU member . states, has given an undertaking to warn if a dangerous individual may . be travelling to the UK. Last night it remained unclear if the . Lithuanians had complied with that undertaking or not. In January last year, Lithuanian . builder Rimvydas Liorancas battered a Birmingham couple to death at . their home after slipping in to the country unnoticed despite . convictions for armed robbery and other offences in his homeland.

**Racial Identity and "Passing" in American Cinema**


Characters who desire to become something they are not in order to escape their circumstances have appeared in American films from the medium's earliest days to the present. The popular American film encyclopedia, Videohound, categorizes films featuring these characters under "Not-So-Mistaken-Identity." Of these films, more than half feature Black characters attempting to "pass" as white—a phenomenon that reflects America's complex historical relationship with race, authenticity, and social mobility.


**Historical Context**

To understand these films, international readers should know that the United States has a unique history of racial classification. Due to slavery (1619-1865) and subsequent segregation laws, American society developed a rigid "one-drop rule"—meaning that any person with African ancestry, regardless of appearance, was legally classified as Black. This created a situation where some individuals of mixed heritage could appear white while being socially classified as Black, leading to the practice of "passing"—living as white to escape discrimination.


**The Subversive Potential**

Characters whose racial identity exists between Black and white have the potential to challenge racial categories by exposing the artificial nature of racial classification. Scholar Elaine Ginsberg argued that passing narratives have power in "interrogating the essentialism that is the foundation of identity politics, passing has the potential to create a space for creative multiple identities, to experiment with multiple subject positions, and to cross social and economic boundaries that exclude or oppress" (16). However, in most mainstream American films, these characters are never granted the freedom to define themselves.


**Reinforcing Racial Boundaries**

Despite their potential to challenge racial categories, passing characters are typically used to prove that transcending race is impossible. The films subject these characters to humiliation, violence, and personal sacrifice. Meanwhile, more conventional Black characters are positioned as the moral center of these stories, inadvertently reinforcing stereotypical definitions of racial identity.


When released, these films were often viewed as serious attempts to address what scholar W.E.B. DuBois called "the problem of the color line." However, they rely on existing racial assumptions to maintain unequal power relationships between Black and white Americans. By limiting passing characters to binary choices (being either Black or white), these films reinforce racial divisions rather than promoting a more inclusive, multicultural society.


**The Historical Pattern**

Scholars Jonathan W. Warren and France Winddance Twine noted that throughout American history, various immigrant groups have gradually been accepted as "white," but "precisely because Blacks represent the other against which Whiteness is constructed, the backdoor to Whiteness is open to non-Blacks." Passing films portray light-skinned Black individuals attempting this assimilation, but they invariably fail due to their African heritage. This failure serves to reassure white audiences that racial categories are fixed and natural.


**Cultural Context and Timing**

Most passing films were released during periods of social upheaval in the United States. The peak decades (1930-1960) coincided with the Great Depression, World War II, and the early Civil Rights Movement. Analyzing these films within their historical context reveals:


- How Black and white Americans viewed themselves and each other

- The racial politics of their respective eras

- The intersections of race, gender, and class

- Generational conflicts regarding race

- Cinema's role in constructing racial identity

- How "passing" extends beyond race to include ethnicity, gender, and class


**Types of Passing**

**Categories:** Race, gender, class, ethnicity

**Duration:** Permanent, temporary, situational (e.g., to access segregated facilities)

**Awareness:** Conscious or unconscious

**Family dynamics:** With or without family consent


**Motivations:**

- Escape slavery (historical, primarily in literature)

- "Live like a human being" (as stated by the protagonist in the film *Pinky*)

- Increase opportunities (films: *Veiled Aristocrats*, *Lost Boundaries*, *Soul Man*)

- Investigative purposes (films: *Black Like Me*, *Gentleman's Agreement*)

- Enable interracial romance (film: *Shadows*)

- Gain social acceptance (film: *My Man Godfrey*)


This phenomenon reflects the particular nature of American racial history and the ongoing struggle to reconcile ideals of equality with the reality of systemic discrimination.

The source said troops had entered one building and were planning to push cautiously through the rest of the huge compound amid fears of explosives.
IS militants are believed to have fled to the north-east of the city.
The government has been trying to retake Ramadi for weeks.
The mainly Sunni Arab city, about 55 miles (90km) west of Baghdad, fell to IS in May, and was seen as an embarrassing defeat for the army.
In recent days, troops have been picking their way through booby-trapped streets and buildings as they pushed towards the city centre, seizing several districts on the way.
They were reported to be within a few hundred yards of the former provincial administrative headquarters on Saturday.
When sniper fire from the compound stopped, and aerial surveillance detected no human activity, a group of Iraqi soldiers moved in, the source said.
They reportedly entered what used to be the city's department of health, housing a blood bank.
The Iraqi military believes the militants have headed north-east; fighting meanwhile is reported to be under way to the south-west of the compound.
The operation to recapture Ramadi began in early November, but has made slow progress, mainly because the government chose not to use the powerful Shia-dominated paramilitary force that helped it regain the northern city of Tikrit, to avoid increasing sectarian tensions.
Concern remains for the plight of hundreds of families who have been trapped on the frontline, the BBC's Thomas Fessy reports from Baghdad.

Dana Carvey delivers an absolutely brilliant performance as a man suffering from temporary amnesia, creating what might be the greatest comedy ever made. Every morning, his character awakens as a complete stranger to the world around him—sleep erasing all memories from the previous day. For fans of Dumb & Dumber and similar comedies, this film is an absolute must-see. Don't expect to catch every joke on your first viewing; this is definitely one worth owning since the layered humor rewards multiple watches. Hopefully this review points you toward your next favorite comedy!

**From Outdated to Outstanding: How One School District Solved Its Communication Crisis**

**The Challenge: When Technology Fails Our Kids**

Picture this: You're a teacher dealing with a medical emergency in your classroom, but there's no way to call for help. You have to leave your students to run to the main office. Or imagine being a parent trying to reach your child's teacher, but the school's phone system is so outdated that messages get lost on tiny scraps of paper.

This was everyday reality for Carl Woody when he started as the network engineer for North Carolina's Dare County schools in 2003. The technology was ancient—seven years old in tech terms might as well be from the Stone Age. The network crawled under basic internet use, and teachers had no phones in their classrooms. Getting traditional phone lines installed would cost $40,000 upfront, plus $1,500 every month—money the district simply didn't have.

**The Solution: Working Smarter, Not Harder**

Instead of throwing money at an expensive phone company, Woody got creative. He secured a $210,000 grant and used it to kill two birds with one stone: upgrade the network infrastructure AND solve the phone problem.

Here's the clever part: instead of running separate phone lines, Woody installed a system that sends phone calls through the same internet cables already being upgraded for computers. It's called Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP)—think of it like making phone calls through your internet connection, but much more reliable.

Working with technology company 3Com, Woody installed this system in three schools first, spending about $30,000 per school. Yes, that's more expensive upfront than traditional phones, but here's the kicker: no monthly fees. The system pays for itself within a few years, then starts saving money every month after that.

**The Results: Clear Communication, Real Savings**

"You don't even know it's VoIP, it's just crystal clear," says Woody. Teachers can now communicate instantly during emergencies. The system automatically tracks calls, so no more lost message slips. And the district plans to upgrade their remaining schools as old phone contracts expire.

This isn't just about Dare County—it's part of a bigger trend. As John Halpin from 3Com explains, "Since Columbine, there's been a greater need and urgency for getting phones in the classroom." Nearly 94% of schools already have internet networks, making this switch relatively simple.

**The Bottom Line**

Sometimes the best solution isn't the obvious one. Instead of paying phone companies forever, Dare County invested in technology that solved multiple problems at once—giving teachers the communication tools they need while saving taxpayer money in the long run.

ITHACA, N.Y., June 30 (UPI) -- A molecule found in blue jean and pen ink dyes could enable the creation of cheaper, more flexible, and more versatile solar cells, according to U.S. researchers.

Though effective, current heavy, silicon-based panels are costly and cumbersome, prompting scientists to seek alternatives. A Cornell University release stated Tuesday that two of its chemists discovered organic dye molecules can be assembled into a covalent organic framework, a structure poised to create thin, flexible, and low-cost photovoltaic devices.

William Dichtel and Eric L. Spitler employed an acid catalyst and stable molecules known as protected catechols to assemble organic molecules into two-dimensional sheets, which were subsequently stacked to form a lattice. The framework's core comprises phthalocyanines, a class of prevalent industrial dyes found in products ranging from blue jeans to ink pens.

While the structure itself is not a solar cell, the researchers clarify that once assembled, the molecular latticework's pores could be filled with another organic material to create light, flexible, highly efficient, and easily manufacturable solar cells.

A distant relative of the writer of "The Star Spangled Banner," Francis Scott Key Fitzgerald was raised by his father following the death of his mother; the two lived off her small inheritance, the days often haunted by the quiet ache of loss and financial uncertainty. The rooms of their home seemed to cling to shadows, their modest surroundings shaped by faded carpets and the faint scent of old books—an atmosphere that seeped into Fitzgerald’s prose. Fitzgerald wrote a series of novels set in the tumultuous and dazzling “roaring twenties,” an era suffused with jazz music that spilled out into the night air from glowing, crowded speakeasies, where the air was thick with cigarette smoke and the undercurrent of reckless ambition.

In 1925, Fitzgerald created the arguably biographical, The Great Gatsby. The novel drips with the sultry heat of long New York nights and the electric flush of city life, but beneath the glamour pulses a strain of unease. Within these pages, Fitzgerald uses animals—most notably a small, trembling puppy—to characterize and to drive the plot, introducing the animal in an apartment shrouded in the claustrophobic haze of forbidden rendezvous.

In the earlier section of the book, chapter 2, the reader meets the puppy in a setting that feels both intimate and stifling: sunlight slants through the dusty window, casting pale shafts across worn linoleum, the air tinged with the faint musk of sweat and spirits. The tiny dog, scared and confused, becomes the centerpiece of an awkward exchange layered with tension and pretense. In meeting the puppy, Fitzgerald deftly conveys the education levels and aspirations of Myrtle Wilson and Tom Buchanan. Myrtle’s hopeful, slightly desperate words hang in the air: "I don't suppose you got that kind?" (27). This line, spoken as she clutches the puppy, reveals not only her lack of formal education but also her longing to possess something beyond her reach. Meanwhile, Tom’s blunt recognition that “That's no police dog” (27) slices through Myrtle’s illusions with the casual authority of someone accustomed to commanding both people and situations. Throughout the ordeal, Myrtle’s laughter bubbles up nervously, filling the cramped space with a sense of anticipation and unease, while Tom’s restlessness crackles like static electricity, always threatening to explode into violence or indifference. When Myrtle asks the sex of the dog and Tom curtly replies, "It's a bitch," (27), the air grows sharp, thick with an unspoken tension that lingers long after the words fade. Fitzgerald uses this puppy scene masterfully to paint the dynamic between his characters, the pacing of their dialogue echoing footsteps in an empty hallway.

In a later, gloomier section of the book—chapter 8—the atmosphere darkens further as the reader learns of the puppy’s dog collar, now a cold, metallic artifact in a world tinged with suspicion and grief. The skies outside Gatsby’s mansion hang heavy with the promise of rain, and the rooms echo hollow, filled with the silence of loss. The puppy, once a symbol of naïve hope, is gone, but its collar—glinting dully in the half-light—becomes evidence, a clue that drags George Wilson deeper into his slow-burning madness. Now, the puppy is no longer a vehicle for characterization, but a harbinger of tragedy, a quiet witness to Myrtle’s death and the consequences that spiral out beneath the overcast, oppressive sky. As Wilson fixates on the collar, the emotional air grows taut, charged with the electricity of his suspicions—a storm ready to break. The revelation of the collar becomes the tipping point that sends Wilson, hollow-eyed and trembling, stalking through the night toward Gatsby’s house, his rage barely contained. The puppy’s collar is the final, silent link in a chain of events that leads inexorably to Gatsby’s death and Wilson’s own bleak end.

Fitzgerald’s writing is drenched in atmosphere—the jazz-age world throbs with energy even as it shivers on the edge of despair. In true writer’s fashion, Fitzgerald mirrored his own life in his work; he himself had a dog, weaving this personal detail into his fiction like smoke curling beneath a lamp-lit ceiling. Through the figure of the Airedale, Fitzgerald channels both the exuberance and vulnerability of his era, using the puppy not just as a device for characterization but as a catalyst for the unraveling of dreams, set against the moody, volatile landscape of the American 1920s.

**I. By the Fireside**

The old man sat in his chair, contentedly puffing away at his pipe and looking fondly into the flames that warmed his slippered feet. 

**II. The Intruder**

The stranger shrouded in black at his door shifted his weight from one foot to another, gripping his blade so tightly his knuckles turned white. 

"I'm here to kill you, old man."

"I know, I know. I heard you the first time," the man grunted, waving his hand at the stranger. "Are you going to come in, or are you just going to stand there and kill me with sad little threats?"

The stranger straightened himself and closed the door behind him, stepping closer to the man.

"Who sent you this time?"

"Lord Waverly of--"

"Pah!" the man laughed, "that old bastard again. Why doesn't he just come here himself and get it over with."

The stranger scratched an itch on his head.

"So, are you going to defend yourself, or..."

"Yes, I suppose I should," the man grunted, taking one last puff from his pipe before pushing himself to his feet with an "oof!" He rubbed his protruding stomach as he looked around the room.

**III. The Duel Begins**

A rusty old sword leaned against the fireplace; it was the only weapon in sight. He gripped the handle, and lifted it with a sigh.

The stranger lifted his own sword and moved his feet into position. The old man popped his back with a mighty crack, groaning and stretching his arms.

"Oh, that felt good." He lifted the rusty piece of metal and motioned for the stranger to begin. "Alright, show me what you've got."

With a spring of his feet, the stranger jumped forward, his weapon aimed perfectly at the old man's open side. With a blow faster than a blink, the stranger was on the floor, stumbling over his own feet, his sword flinging wildly behind him.

"Good, not great," the old man said, stretching his neck as the stranger righted himself. "Try again."

**IV. Lesson Learned**

The stranger took a breath and steadied himself. Surely he could best this old crow who could barely hold his own sword. He lunged again, striking once, twice, three times. Like a flash of lightning, each of his attacks were blocked and countered quicker than he was able to comprehend.

Before he knew it, he was on the floor, his sword across the room, the old man holding the rusty point of his weapon to his neck.

"You're not bad. But next time try loosening up a little. You're too stiff."

The old man kicked the stranger's sword to him, then wandered back to his chair. He didn't look back as the stranger took his weapon and stood, instead he sat down with a happy sigh and took his pipe in his hand once more.

The stranger watched, dumbfounded, unsure of how to proceed. The man waved him away.

"Try again tomorrow."

"Catering to Nobody" was ultimately underwhelming for me. As several reviewers have mentioned, the writing style felt uninspired. I wanted to enjoy the story, but I found Goldy, the main character, rather grating. In hindsight, I wish I had just skipped to the conclusion and saved myself some time. The characters lacked depth and failed to capture my interest, which was disappointing since there was clear potential for them to be more engaging. By the end, I was simply relieved to have finished. Although the inclusion of recipes was intriguing, spotting mayonnaise in the guacamole recipe was off-putting for me. I don’t plan to invest any more time or money in books by Diane Mott Davidson.

Once upon a time long long ago, in a land far far away, there was a King who had no telepathic powers. No one did, in his entire kingdom and in all the other kingdoms surrounding his. Communication was done with organs that created airwaves, and interpreted them, a laborious process fraught with delay and misunderstanding, making it profoundly inefficient. When people brought problems to him, the inherent limitations of their verbal exchange meant there was much confusion; nuances were lost, urgency misjudged, and critical details frequently omitted or misinterpreted. Needs could never be fully met because during deliberation, a constant and arduous process, complex emotions and intricate solutions were difficult to articulate, or were intentionally overlooked by others who leveraged the ambiguity of language to protect their own interests. Every one had the ability to withhold information, leading to pervasive distrust, and they could tell "lies," falsehoods that, lacking any verifiable mental truth, with some cunning could be made to appear as truth through eloquent persuasion or selective evidence. And in this weird land, despite the constant friction of misunderstanding and manipulation, this King somehow kept order, relying on a fragile authority and the sheer force of his his pronouncements. That is, he did until one day, when his people caught him telling a lie, a transgression that shattered the delicate foundation of his rule and made him, in effect, cease to exist as their sovereign leader.

Doñana National Park, Spain  
Background and History  
The history of Doñana National Park stretches back more than seven centuries, when the region served as a favoured hunting ground for Spanish royalty. In 1963, the Spanish government, in partnership with the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF), acquired 7,000 hectares (27 square miles) to establish the Doñana Biological Reserve. The park itself was officially designated six years later, with significant boundary expansions in 1978 and 2004. Since 2006, the park has been managed exclusively by the Autonomous Government of Andalucía.

Today, Doñana stands as a global conservation icon—renowned as the only protected area in the world that simultaneously holds the statuses of National Park, World Heritage Site (since 1994), Ramsar Wetland Site, Biosphere Reserve, and European Community Special Protection Area.

Doñana’s uniqueness stems from its extraordinary mosaic of habitats, fostering remarkable plant and animal biodiversity. Beyond the highly productive marshlands, the park encompasses pristine beaches, extensive dune fields (both fixed and mobile), scrub woodlands, Mediterranean maquis, and a scatter of lagoons nestled amid the vegetation. Its vast expanse and strategic location at the confluence of the Guadalquivir River and the Atlantic Ocean render Doñana one of Europe’s most significant wetlands. Each winter, over half a million waterfowl seek refuge here, and the reserve serves as a critical stopover for countless migratory birds journeying between Africa and Europe.

Human presence and activity have shaped Doñana across the ages. Traditional practices—such as beekeeping, pine cone harvesting, and small-scale agriculture—continue to this day. The park is also celebrated for vibrant cultural traditions, including the “Saca de las Yeguas,” an annual livestock event, and the “Romería de El Rocío,” one of Spain’s most celebrated religious pilgrimages, which draws immense crowds each year.

Size and Location  
Doñana National Park spans 54,252 hectares (209 square miles) in the provinces of Huelva and Sevilla, situated within Andalucía, southern Spain. Located on the right bank of the Guadalquivir River at its estuary with the Atlantic Ocean, its terrain is characteristically flat, with elevations reaching no more than 47 metres above sea level.

Flora and Fauna  
The park’s flora is exceptionally diverse, boasting more than 900 species of vascular plants. Endemic species such as Vulpia fontquerana (a rare grass) and Linaria tursica (a type of figwort), both listed in the Spanish National Catalogue of Endangered Species, make Doñana a botanical treasure. Many rare and endemic plants found here are also included in the IUCN’s Red List of Spanish Vascular Flora.

The diversity of animal life in Doñana is equally impressive, including 20 species of freshwater fish, 11 amphibians, 21 reptiles, 37 mammals, and an astonishing 360 bird species—127 of which breed within the park. It is a last refuge for iconic and endangered fauna: the critically endangered Iberian lynx—perhaps the world’s most threatened feline—and the Spanish imperial eagle, along with the marbled duck and white-headed duck, both vulnerable waterfowl. Black vultures, red kites, and large breeding colonies of herons, egrets, and waders further enrich the park’s vibrant avifauna.

Doñana is internationally acclaimed for its conservation programs, especially its successful Iberian lynx management plan, which focuses on restoring habitats, boosting European rabbit populations (the lynx’s primary prey), and advancing captive breeding and reintroduction efforts.

Yet, the park faces significant threats from human activity. Agricultural expansion through marsh and river drainage, poaching, overgrazing, and illegal crayfish harvesting all pose risks to its delicate ecosystems. Growing ecotourism also brings challenges: the annual “Romería de El Rocío” alone attracts one million pilgrims, testing the park’s resilience.

Nevertheless, Doñana National Park endures as a bastion of wild nature. Its ecological and cultural richness have earned it international acclaim, making it a beacon for conservation innovation and a model for protected area management in Spain and beyond.

Ms James, who officially took over from Nigel Farage on September 16th, said UKIP needed to be ready for "the earliest possible general election."
She called UKIP "the opposition party in waiting," adding that Labour was in "disarray," especially when it came to Europe.
And Ms James made it clear that UKIP posed "no threat" to the rights of EU citizens living in the UK to stay.
The new UKIP leader shared these thoughts during a bunch of interviews with BBC political reporters from all over the UK.
"I'm not as well known as Nigel Farage," she admitted, but then pointed out, "Theresa May's not that well known across the country either."
Ms James praised Farage for "upsetting the political apple cart" in British politics. But she stuck to what she'd said in her acceptance speech at UKIP's annual conference: "I was not going to be Nigel-like and I was not going to be Nigel-lite."
Expecting Theresa May's government to call an election as soon as next year, Ms James mentioned wanting to broaden UKIP's policy appeal, especially now that they'd won the EU referendum. She said, "One of my personal aims in the first 100 days is to update our manifesto, because we are quite frankly looking at a green field out there."
She argued that money saved from the UK's EU contributions could be put back into the UK economy after Brexit. She also made her opposition to HS2 clear, calling the high-speed rail project a "white elephant."
Ms James stressed that it was UKIP's policy to guarantee the rights of citizens from other EU countries living in the UK once the country leaves the EU.
"Right through the referendum campaign, UKIP always said we weren't looking at mass expulsion from either the United Kingdom back to Europe, or expecting our fellow EU countries to do the same and expel people. That's not what grown-up countries do. So there's no risk, no threat at all from UKIP," Ms James stated.
"If anything, the risk and threat actually comes from this current government, who still haven't clearly said that individuals from EU countries who are here lawfully and legally are here to stay."
After also criticising Labour for not being a strong opposition to the Conservative government, Ms James was asked if someone like her – an admirer of Margaret Thatcher from the Home Counties – could really appeal to traditional Labour voters in the north of England, especially since many of them voted for Brexit.
She brushed off that description as a "stereotype," adding that she planned to bring in two "chiefs of staff" to help the party win over Conservative voters in the south and Labour voters in the north.
The UKIP leader admitted she'd voted Conservative, Liberal Democrat, and Green "for my sins, in the past," but said she'd only ever been a member of one party: UKIP. She didn't say anything about ever supporting Labour.
She also stood by the action taken against UKIP councillor Sarah Larkins, who had to resign as deputy mayor of Ramsgate after telling voters to support "anybody but a Muslim" in London's mayoral election earlier this year. That contest was won by Labour's Sadiq Khan, who is Muslim. Cllr Larkins has since apologised for her comments.
Diane James made it clear that such views had no place in UKIP while she was in charge. She said her leadership would be "a lot more accepting of what we live in, in terms of society in this country."

Turkish champions Fenerbahce have been withdrawn from the European Champions League group stage by the Turkish Football Federation (TFF) in relation to bribery and match-fixing allegations. In July more than 30 people were arrested and jailed over claims that several football matches, as many as 19, in the Turkish first and second divisions had been manipulated. The highest-profile figure arrested in the scandal was Fenerbahce's president Aziz Yildirim. Fenerbahce won last season's title on goal difference from Trabzonspor. But as the arrests mount the TFF decided to withdraw Fenerbahce before UEFA, European football's governing body, was forced to act on its behalf. "Regarding the heavy disciplinary sanctions both Fenerbahce and the Turkish Football Federation (TFF), which means Turkey might encounter, it is decided to ban Fenerbahce Sports Club from the UEFA Champions' League this season," read a statement posted on the TFF's official website. The move took place 24 hours before Thursday's group stage draw. European football's governing body acted swiftly to end speculation over who will replace Fenerbahce by announcing that Trabzonspor, who finished second in the Turkish league last season, will take the final group stage berth. After a meeting of UEFA's Emergency Committee on Wednesday evening it was also agreed that Spanish club Athletic Bilbao will no longer need to take part in a qualification match against Trabzonspor on Thursday and will go straight into the Europa League instead. "The panel considered that the Turkish Football Federation took the right decision to protect the game, fully in line with our zero-tolerance policy against match-fixing," said UEFA General Secretary Gianni Infantino in a statement posted on UEFA's website. "The Turkish Football Federation has shown with this decision that it takes its full responsibility in the fight against corruption."

At 80, legendary French chef Albert Roux remains an undisputed master of charm. Since his divorce from Zimbabwean-born heiress Cheryl Roux last year, the culinary icon has been confidently entertaining an impressive circle of accomplished women—including a former flame, an artist, a hospitality manager, and Ukrainian Nataliya Lutsyshyna, the woman central to his divorce proceedings.

The 41-year-old former cloakroom attendant at the prestigious Roux restaurant in Westminster has clearly captured the chef's attention. Sources confirm Roux is "smitten" with Ms. Lutsyshyna, though this hasn't prevented him from maintaining his sophisticated social network.

American divorcee Debbie Spivey Izo, whom Roux dated before his marriage to Cheryl, has seamlessly returned to his inner circle. He continues to meet with British landscape artist Lynne Moore and Eastern European tea hostess Ljiljana Pavicevic, demonstrating his ability to maintain multiple meaningful connections.

Roux's priorities were unmistakably clear last weekend when he chose to escort Ms. Lutsyshyna on an exclusive getaway to the South of France over assisting his son Michel Jr at tennis champion Andy Murray's wedding reception. The romantic retreat included dining at the Michelin-starred Joel Robuchon Monte-Carlo restaurant—a gesture that required considerable planning and expense.

The restaurateur, who famously abandoned his childhood aspiration to join the priesthood due to his appreciation for female companionship, wasted no time pursuing Ms. Lutsyshyna following his marital separation. He has invested significantly in the relationship, though some associates express concern about his generous spending.

While Roux maintains his exclusive romantic partnership with Ms. Lutsyshyna, he continues to cultivate his broader social connections with characteristic finesse. Ms. Spivey Izo promptly resumed her place in his life, traveling to Scotland to visit his establishments there. Sources confirm she "never really went away" and reconnected with Roux immediately upon his return to single status.

Lynne Moore, introduced to Roux through Lib Dem peer Lord Sharman, acknowledges their regular meetings: "Albert has kindly invited me to about half a dozen dinners. He's very nice, but we are not in a romantic relationship."

Ljiljana Pavicevic, known as "Lily," has maintained her connection with Roux since 2008, regularly joining him for dinner engagements, though she maintains these occur within professional contexts.

When approached for comment, Cheryl declined to respond. Roux's spokesman stated definitively: "He has no comment to make on this. He tries to be extremely private."

Woah, that was a wild party. So many people, so many smells! I probably shouldn't have had so much chocolate last night, but it smelled so good, and the people left it at nose height, and no one told me I was Bad for having it, and it was really tasty. & nbsp; Still, probably shouldn't have eaten it. I feel really weird this morning. Cold and... soft and floaty. & nbsp; Aw, crap, I'm on the bed! I'm not allowed on the bed. I jump off quickly before someone sees. & nbsp; The landing is awkward, my front paws wrong. A quick glance confirms it: I have opposable digits! No fur, but thumbs! & nbsp; Ohmygoshthefridge! & nbsp; Running on two legs feels weird, but the fridge opens just as I always dreamed it would. Inside, the leftover roast chicken. I grab it with my hands and bring it to my face, biting into the carcass with sweet anticipation. & nbsp; Huh. The bones are a lot harder than I'd expected. Maybe that's why people would always give them to me when they had eaten the meat. & nbsp; A knock on the door and I spin around. "Go away!" I shout, mouth still half full of chicken, "Go away go away go away! My house!" & nbsp; Despite my fingers being slippery with chicken grease I manage to grip the door handle like I've seen people do and open the door. A man is standing there, the man I knew it would probably be, pieces of paper in his hand. & nbsp; His eyes look me up and down. "I've, uh, just got your mail." & nbsp; "Go away!" I shout again. Surely this has been made clear by now? & nbsp; He drops the papers and backs away slowly. "Okay, I'm going. Uh, you might want to put some clothes on." & nbsp; I slam the door closed triumphantly. This is going to be awesome! Unless my master returns home and finds out I slept on the bed and ate the chicken. Then I would be Bad. Ooh, the chicken! & nbsp; I head back to the kitchen, but to my surprise, there's another person there. Someone I don't recognise. But... the way she picks at the chicken, flexes her long-nailed fingers... yes, I think I do know who this is. & nbsp; "We need to talk about some ground rules," murmurs the person who used to be the cat.

Location: Fruit and Vegetable Insect Research
Project Number: 2092-21220-001-04
Start Date: Jun 29, 2011
End Date: May 31, 2016
Potato can be made even more nutritious by developing varieties that have higher levels of antioxidants such as phenolics, vitamin C, anthocyanins and carotenoids. A better understanding of the mechanisms that influence the amounts of phytonutrients in tubers, including both in planta and external environmental influences including post-harvest factors or crop management will facilitate development of high-phytonutrient potatoes. A combination of physiological, biochemical and molecular techniques, including metabolic profiling, gene expression, metabolic engineering and enzyme analysis will be used to address these factors.

Spink, part of Bruce's backroom team at Birmingham City, Wigan Athletic and Sunderland, has been linked to Villa.
But, although he hopes Bruce gets the job, at 58, Spink says he is too old.
"I'm racing towards 60. If I started kicking 100 balls a day, probably my leg would fall off," he told BBC WM.
"I'm pro Steve Bruce. And I'm happy to speak to him about all departments and would give him 100 per cent backing. But there's nothing in that story.
"I haven't spoken to Steve for some 18 months or so. I texted him when Hull City got to the FA Cup final, wished him all the best and got a nice reply. But I've since been busy with my business.
"And if he was to ask me, the answer would probably be no. That might come as a surprise to Villa fans, but a goalkeeping coach job is for a younger man."
Spink continued: "I'm fit and healthy, my business is running well and, if I took the job and it only lasted two years, I would lose business which it would take a while to get back.
"In any case, I enjoy being a fan. I know we haven't had much to jump and down about over the last two years, but I still enjoy going down there. To be sat watching the supporters and their passion has been a real eye opener."
Spink, who came off the bench against Bayern Munich in Rotterdam to help the club win the European Cup in 1982, was also part of the last Villa team to be promoted to the top flight under Graham Taylor in 1988.
After working as a goalkeeping coach for four clubs, he now runs his own white-van courier business, in Sutton Coldfield.
Pat Murphy, BBC Radio 5 live
"Villa's managerial recruitment team have completed their due diligence on the short list and will recommend a candidate in the next 24 hours to the club's owner, Dr Tony Xia.
"Dr Xia is in China on business and, unlike his sole decision in the summer to appoint Roberto Di Matteo, will be relying on the findings of chief executive Keith Wyness, former manager Brian Little and technical director Steve Round. But the final decision rests with Villa's owner.
"Steve Clarke, acting caretaker manager was interviewed on Monday, while the three discussions Bruce has had with Wyness over the past five days constitute a formal interview in the opinion of the recruitment team. But Bruce and Clarke are not the only candidates.
"Villa are also considering an approach to a manager contracted to another club, but need to be certain he is the one they definitely want before committing themselves.
"If they do and they fail in their overtures after asking for permission to speak to that manager, then Bruce and/or Clarke will know they were not first choice.
"There is no timescale on the appointment. Once the candidate agrees to join Villa, there will be detailed discussions over the contract, backroom staff and budgets. It may be a day or so before the successful applicant is publicly confirmed. But Bruce remains the odds-on favourite."

Trying to stretch my musical tastes, and liking jazz on the whole, I tried this group and this CD, as Amazon selected this an essential recording....I wouldn't have labelled it so. Going to resell this soon. No matter how many listens, I am unimpressed.I like music, meaning chord structures that permit melody, sensible counterpoint, and harmonies however abstract...but this effort is generally lacking. To me its a little like Pat Metheny's "The Way Up" (which I own and listen to very occasionally)...someone might appreciate it for what it tries to do (stretch the concepts of music and jazz) and for the occasional tunefulness, but its not a "must listen".I wonder if Martin, Medeski or Wood have any Frank Zappa albums. If not, they could learn a little about jazz creativity from "Jazz From Hell", also an album that I don't and won't own.

The musician's ex-partner Pardeep Sall bombarded her with phone calls and texts, and even entered her flat, after refusing to accept their break up.
Stalking is a "personal terrorism" that left her fearing for her safety and forced her parents to move house, she told the Victoria Derbyshire programme.
Sall was convicted of stalking in May.
He was given a 12-week suspended prison sentence and an indefinite restraining order. He denies the offences, and is appealing the conviction.
Shoniwa, whose band had a hit with Don't Upset the Rhythm (Go Baby Go) in 2009, made the decision to speak publicly to highlight a call by MPs and campaigners for the maximum stalking prison sentences to be doubled from five to 10 years.
Her own experience of stalking, which began last year, was a "terrorising campaign" that was like being "killed from the inside out slowly", she said.
"It's literally like murder in slow motion. It's a sinister kind of personal terrorism that should not be tolerated," she said.
"It's nothing that I would wish upon anybody.
"The perpetrator involved in my case used to brag about having ruined other people's lives before, and that's when I saw warning signs.
"He would go around bragging that he was above the law and it was really exciting to damage people and try to play this psychological game that doesn't just affect them, but extends to their families and dozens of people around them."
The "ricochet effect" of the harassment left her constantly worried about the safety of her friends and family, Shoniwa said.
The judge who sentenced Shoniwa's stalker said he had been "in the grips of a very powerful obsession", according to reports.
You should contact the police if you're being stalked - you have a right to feel safe in your home and workplace. Call 999 if you or someone else is in immediate danger, otherwise contact your local police.
Shoniwa is backing the call for stalking offence sentences to be increased, and is also campaigning for a register of convicted stalkers, similar to the sex offenders register.
Conservative MP Alex Chalk, who is leading a group of MPs lobbying Parliament for increased sentences, said stalking could be "the gateway to serious violence", but was treated as a minor offence.
"If you compare it with other offences, for example burglary, another violating offence, has a maximum sentence of 14 years," he said.
"And yet five years for stalking, it just doesn't bear any comparison.
"The fact is the law still treats it as a minor offence. The judge has got to have powers to protect the victim, and they just don't have them at the moment."

- "Would you look at all this crap?!" My brother never really understood the concept of grieving... Here he stood, overlooking all the knickknacks, jazz magazines, newspapers, ancient items of clothing, crackled furniture and a container worth pile of various shaped felt-tips while I was sitting in the favorite chair of our late Grandparent. - "Can you please stop whining and help me sort all those things like I asked you to maybe 3 hours ago? The truck Dad rented is going to be here any minute now and we still have these to put in boxes!" I distractedly shouted. "You grab that heavy desk and bring it outside; I'm going to put the pens in that box for our yard-sale tomorrow." - " Remind me why we are doing this again? Sarah, this is a job for a MOVING company or maybe HER grandchildren at least! -Yes well it seems like none of them cared enough to come and take care of her possessions. I mean they thought she was a little bit of a lunatic but come on, they could at least have come to her funeral yesterday... " Eric blatantly sighed and put his arms around said desk. His gray shirt was already very soaked from perspiration on his back and armpits. Eric was the fittest of the family so he always was asked to help during heavy lifting. He carried it through the main door and disappeared behind the purple willow's leaves. My outfit wasn't very dry itself, and as I remained seated, I felt like I could use a minute of fresh breeze on my face. I stood up, poured as many pens as I could fit into this cardboard basket and watched the mesmerizing flow of colors elapse. Suddenly, a sparkle caught my eye. I reached into the layers of pencils and grabbed the unusual object... It was a black varnished full sized wood felt-tip with very detailed gold engravings. Smooth lines were circling the word Develare and loop at the end of the lead. This pen clearly stood out of the others and somehow, this distinctive feature made me choose to not put it back and almost unwillingly conceal it in my trousers ' pocket. - "Sarah, are you finished sorting the books from the attic?" I jumped from surprise. I didn't hear my brother come back in. I realized straightaway that the truck pulled up in the driveway was still running, making a lot of muffled noise. I slowly responded: "Almost, there is a few more that I need to check out..." I walked towards the improvised library and closed the door behind me in order to keep the truck's stifled sounds away. As I was glancing through the rest of the books, one distinguished itself. It had a rich leather cover and radiant gold markings showing: Those who enter this reality beware For it is not for amateurs to bear May the Eye reveal to you What you always wanted to be true Fascinated, I ran my fingers across the blank pages, expecting some sort of science fiction story or designs. Unfortunately, it wasn't the case. The book seemed untouched until I reached the last page. Here was written in a powerful red color what was titled: The rules and warnings of the gift Leto brought to Phoebus: May the blood in this ink lock the stories and may the stories unveil only to the one who swore. I never saw anything like it. My grandma's name was at the very bottom among 5 or 6 others. I sat down on the floor, positioning the mysterious book in front of me. I took the felt-tip I found earlier out of his hideout and put it right aside the book. They clearly matched. I taunted myself, should I dare? I picked up the pen. The lead went from shady black to intense red. "What's the harm anyway, it's just a very stylish stupid book, and Grandma signed it, it really can't be that horrifying for me to use it after she passed away" I took the pen and wrote my name at the very first page in order to start a logbook of this place. My newly written name was swallowed into the fabric. I turned the sheet multiple times as I couldn't believe my eyes... It had disappeared! I flipped the pages... until I realized my name got added right next to my grandmother's...

**Opening Statement:**

- According to the American Council on Science and Health (ACSH), fake medications continue to pose a growing danger to worldwide health systems, threatening the integrity of America's pharmaceutical supply chain, as detailed in their 2009 publication "Counterfeit Drugs: Coming to a Pharmacy Near You."


**Global Statistics:**

- The World Health Organization reports that fraudulent pharmaceuticals comprise one-tenth of all medications worldwide, with developing nations experiencing significantly higher rates of contamination.


**Disease Impact:**

- Fake medications have accelerated the development of drug-resistant strains of malaria, HIV/AIDS, and tuberculosis.


**Distribution Scale:**

- Countless numbers of these fraudulent medications have reached retail pharmacies and been distributed to unsuspecting patients.


**Import Violations:**

- Research reveals that nearly nine out of ten medications entering the United States through import channels fail to meet FDA safety requirements and pose health risks.


**Regulatory Limitations:**

- The FDA lacks the resources and authority to ensure the safety of medications obtained through these alternative supply chains.


**Expert Warning:**

- ACSH President Elizabeth Whelan cautions that congressional efforts to legalize drug importation as a cost-cutting measure "constitutes an unacceptable compromise of our national pharmaceutical security that could undermine even our limited progress in combating fake drugs."


**Current Solutions:**

- While drug tracking systems, advanced anti-counterfeiting technologies, and enhanced wholesaler regulation are being implemented to combat this problem, these measures require robust enforcement support, according to the organization.


**Consumer Recommendations:**

- The ACSH study advises consumers to remain vigilant about counterfeit medication risks by monitoring their prescription drugs for any alterations in appearance, packaging, dimensions, coloring, or therapeutic effectiveness.

Last week, it emerged almost one million drink-driving tests recorded by police did not actually take place.
An Garda Síochána (Irish police) also admitted an error that caused almost 15,000 wrongful traffic convictions.
Ms O'Sullivan was appearing before an Irish parliamentary justice committee.
She said gardaí have still not established the reasons why almost one million false breath tests were recorded.
Ms O'Sullivan said she apologised "for the grave mistakes and wrongdoing during the last decade that have led to the two controversies we are here today to discuss".
"Those mistakes and wrongdoings are unacceptable in policing terms, unacceptable in ethical terms, unacceptable in terms of public trust, and, most critically, unacceptable to the advocacy and support groups involved in road safety and to those who were wrongly brought to court," she added.
"They have raised serious issues about how we managed the service, how certain gardaí operated on the ground and their supervision.
"Given the scale of these issues, they can't simply be blamed on one individual or one area. It is a collective failure. From top down to bottom up."
Some deputies at the committee wanted to know if bonuses and performance targets were a factor in the exaggeration of the figures.
Ms O'Sullivan said some divisional officers' performance targets which led to bonuses were connected to reductions in road deaths.
She said that a hypothesis may be that the recording of data on the mandatory alcohol checkpoints, which she said were preventative measures, was not seen as important in the detection of offences.
Ms O'Sullivan said all senior managers have committed to delivering on a reform programme.
Meanwhile, the officer in charge of roads policing, Assistant Commissioner Michael Finn, emphasised that there was no competition between garda regions to see who had the highest figures.
He said he was satisfied with the accuracy of the data on checkpoints. He also said it was an error on his part that the Irish Minister for Justice was not informed.
On Thursday evening, gardaí clarified that the last claim for a bonus payment for senior officers was made for activities in the year 2007. They said the scheme was ended by the Department of Finance in 2009.
Gardaí say they do not believe senior officers were paid for breath tests and are clarifying the situation regarding checkpoints. Gardaí said that no bonus payment was made to current members of the garda executive.
At the weekend, Ms O'Sullivan said revelations over penalty points and breath test discrepancies are "unacceptable".
She said on Saturday that the police service was on a journey of radical reform and "it is inevitable that we will identify more examples of bad practice".
On Tuesday, the government announced it was planning two separate investigations.
Taoiseach (Irish prime minister) Enda Kenny announced an independent review of the overall operation of the force.
A second investigation will probe the wrongful convictions of almost 15,000 motorists, and apparent false data on breath tests.
Mr Kenny also said on Tuesday that the government continues to have confidence in Commissioner O'Sullivan.
She was already facing questions over her leadership because of allegations of a smear campaign against a whistleblower, Sgt Maurice McCabe.
Sgt McCabe was one of two officers who raised concerns years ago about the alleged deletion of penalty points from the driving licences of well-connected offenders.
Exact details of the independent review are to be outlined next week.
In the meantime, an external inquiry is being set up into the erroneous Garda statistics and prosecutions.
Separately, An Garda Síochána has published a report on financial irregularities at its training college.
The force's internal audit section examined financial transactions over a number of years at the college in Templemore, County Tipperary.
Its report revealed that money was being spent on gifts and entertainment, and identified a non-transparent system of accounting.
Commissioner O'Sullivan said that while the accounting practices would not be acceptable by today's standards, there was no misappropriation of money or misuse of public funds.
Tánaiste (deputy prime minister) Frances Fitzgerald told the Dáil on Tuesday that the report raises serious governance issues and will be referred to the Comptroller and Auditor General and the Public Accounts Committee.

Peanut butter—one of the 180 things George Washington Carver found uses for! It’s pretty amazing how research can turn into so many cool discoveries. Then there’s jelly, made from fruit picked right off the tree, boiled, simmered, and canned until it’s perfectly sweet. Yum. And don’t even get me started on bread—freshly ground wheat, packed with seeds, baked until it’s golden and steaming. That’s what I’m craving for lunch today: a classic PB&J.

But honestly, store-bought ingredients just don’t compare. I mentally add “make homemade peanut butter, jelly, and bread this summer” to my to-do list.

Lost in these delicious thoughts, I’m snapped back to reality—my head leaning against the steering wheel, staring at endless traffic. Suddenly, out of nowhere, a meteor streaks down and slams into a truck like ten cars ahead. Flames, chaos, screams, crying, and somewhere, a distant rap song playing. Well, there goes my lunch break… 

All I wanted was a simple pleasure for my taste buds.

Thanks for reading! If you liked this, check out more stories at /r/OhLookItsAStory!

The singer's second single, Stitches, took 17 weeks to reach number one, bucking a trend for songs to enter the UK chart at the top spot.
Only one song has taken longer: Ed Sheeran's Thinking Out Loud, which had a 19-week climb to the summit in 2014.
Mendes rose to fame on the video-sharing app Vine, much like Bieber who was originally discovered on YouTube.
He beat his rival by just 1,500 combined sales and streams - although Bieber still had the most-streamed song of the week, with Love Yourself racking up 3.72 million plays.
In reaching the top spot, 17-year-old Mendes ends an unprecedented run of success for Bieber.
The star spent eight of the last nine weeks at number one - and recently became the first artist in chart history to occupy the top three spots, with the songs Love Yourself, Sorry and What Do You Mean.
His residency at number one was only interrupted by the NHS Choir, whose charity single A Bridge Over You became the Christmas number one after Bieber urged his 72 million Twitter followers to buy their single instead of his.
A lack of new releases, combined with Beiber's continued success, has resulted in a top 10 where every single is at least 10 weeks old.
In the album chart, David Bowie continues to exert an influence, with his final album Blackstar retaining the number one position.
The Best Of Bowie compilation from 2002 jumps from 18 to number three; while a more recent hits collection, 2014's Nothing Has Changed, is at number six.
Hunky Dory, The Rise And Fall Of Ziggy Stardust, Aladdin Sane, The Next Day, Low and Let's Dance also feature in the top 40.
The death of The Eagles co-founder Glen Frey also prompted a surge in sales, with the US band's Complete Greatest Hits re-entering the chart at 29.
Elsewhere, US rock band Panic! At The Disco scored their first top five album with Death of a Bachelor, a new entry at number four.
And husky-voiced songwriter Jack Savoretti sees his fourth album, Written In Scars, enter the top 10 for the first time, after a performance on Graham Norton's show last week.

Being a music obsessed geek, "High Fidelity" is, naturally, one of my favorite books of all time and while "About a Boy" couldn't have possibly been as powerful, I enjoyed it nearly as much. A funny and charming story to begin with but I thought that the incorporation of the Nirvana stuff was a stroke of genius. I'm sure that critics will eventually begin griping about how *all* of Hornby's books are about immature, pop music and/or sports obsessed men in their 30's but Hornby is to the immature, pop music and/or sports obsessed men in their 30's genre (if you can call it that) what John Grisham is to earnest legal thrillers or Danielle Steele is to trashy soap operas.

So I've got these Logitech PC speakers that I really wanted to use with my PlayStation 3. Here's the thing though - the Logitech speakers need those three 1/8" mini plug jacks (you know, the green, black, and yellow cables), but my PS3 only has HDMI and RCA outputs (that's the yellow video cable plus the white and red audio cables).

Here's what I ended up doing: I ran the HDMI cable from my PS3 straight to my Sony Bravia TV just for the video. Then for the audio, I connected RCA cables from the PS3 to this handy little "5.1 Game Console Adapter" that converts RCA plugs to a single 1/8" jack. After that, I just plugged my Logitech speakers right into the adapter.

And you know what? It works perfectly! The sound is great and there's no weird feedback or anything like that. It was honestly a really cheap and simple way to get my PC speakers working with my gaming console.
