The researcher, employed by an independent production company, spent four days working on Monday's Panorama.
The show found that Lutfur Rahman, Tower Hamlets' mayor, had increased funding to some Bengali-run charities. Opponents accused him of attempting to influence votes, which he denies.
The BBC said the breach involved material from confidential sources.
A spokesperson for the corporation said: "We can confirm that there has been a breach of data protection at an independent production company working with the BBC on a Panorama investigation as a result of unauthorised disclosure by a former researcher on the production team, in breach of her obligation of confidentiality.
"This breach includes material relating to the programme's confidential sources.  Our primary concern is to protect our sources and we are urgently investigating the matter. We have also notified the Information Commissioner's Office."
In 2010, residents of Tower Hamlets voted in Mr Rahman as Britain's first directly elected Asian mayor.
Mr Rahman won as an independent with only 13% of registered voters.
Almost two-thirds of those who turned out to vote that day were from his own Bangladeshi community.
Opposition councillors say they believe the grants were made in return for electoral support.
Mr Rahman categorically denies the accusation.

The offer for business class travellers is the latest solution attempted by airlines impacted by new rules.
The ban applies to electronic devices larger than a smartphone.
It covers flights arriving from nine airlines operating out of 10 airports in the Middle East and North Africa, as well as Turkey.
These include major carriers like Qatar Airways, Etihad, Emirates, and Turkish Airlines. These airlines operate long-distance flights popular with business travellers, who now cannot work on their laptops during the flight.
Qatar Airways said in a statement: "Customers will be able to download their work on to a USB before stepping on board to pick up where they left off."
The airline would also offer one hour of free Wi-Fi for all passengers and a $5 (Â£4) package for travellers who wanted to stay connected on their smartphones for the whole flight.
The laptop loan strategy is not the first attempt by airlines to find ways around these travel restrictions:
US officials stated the rule was a precaution against terrorism, based on "evaluated intelligence" (information that has been reviewed). Large electronic devices are still allowed on board in checked baggage.
This rule applies to direct flights from Jordan, Egypt, Turkey, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Morocco, Qatar, and the United Arab Emirates. This includes Dubai, which is the world's busiest airport.

I posted this to another thread, but it fits the criteria for this one as well and I think it will gain more recognition here. ----------- "I am doing it for her." No, not good enough. Needs more conviction. "I am doing it for her!" He said it more forcefully that time, but remained unchanged. While he had hoped the words would grant him the courage he needed to proceed, they only seemed to sludge out of his mouth, slowing the process even more. Perhaps a change of rhythm would do the trick? "I am doing it...for her." He gazed down at his daughter who was peacefully sleeping in her crib, a rare sight for such a sickly baby. *I hope you learn to appreciate this someday*, he thought to himself while gently stroking her hair. *All this sacrifice is for you*. He had done the math. Over and over again, he had crunched the numbers for months. Comparing rates, checking again and again for inefficiencies, planning out each and every step for the plan that he was about to set in motion. "I am...doing it...for her." He began to tremble as he picked her up, strapped her in her baby carrier, and set her near the front door. He wouldn't be able to live with himself if something happened to her while his plan was unfolding. He gave her one last loving glance before he slipped back into his bedroom. As he started collecting his supplies, his mind raced back over the past couple months. *With your level of income, Mr. Baker, I just don't see how you can afford this level of treatment for your daughter*. His doctor had warned him time and time again. *I don't see very many options for you*. Options. The most priceless artifacts on the planet. What he would have given for a different set of choices, but he would simply have to make do with the ones that he had. He had made his choice, now it was time to act upon it. *Fire*, he remembered coming to that conclusion. *Fire insurance is my best hope*. He had spent the past two months calculating and fire insurance was his best option. *I lose my home, but I gain my daughter*, he thought. *A fair trade*. He removed the top from the gasoline container and carefully poured a trail leading from the outlet in his bedroom around the rest of the house. After making his route, he poured the remainder out around his bed. *A funeral pyre for an old friend*. He silently thanked his house for its sacrifice. He inhaled deeply and the oily, enticing aroma of gasoline flooded his nostrils. *This is it. One small spark and my daughter is free*. "I am doing it for her." The words came out shakily and he was unconvinced by them. *Come on, you fucking coward*. "I. AM. DOING. IT. FOR. HER." He lit the match and prepared for his new life with his daughter. Moments before dropping the lit match, however, he had an epiphany. There was a better option. *How could I have been so stupid*, he swore to himself. *How could I have not seen this? * No, fire insurance would not do. But life insurance would. He handcuffed his sleeping wife's wrist to the bed frame, took a deep breath, and dropped the match. "I am doing it for her."

*Late night writing is the worst kind of writing. Here goes nothing.*

Snow blew in frantic, twisting patterns, driven by a howling wind that echoed through the cave’s hollow. The Commander pulled his fur jacket tighter around his neck, punched a code into a wall-mounted keypad, and watched as a hulking door groaned open before him. Slipping inside, he slammed the door shut with barely a pause.

"Anything yet?" he asked.

"No, nothing," came the reply.

Turning, Commander saw Clark, his glasses sliding precariously down the bridge of his nose, ready to fall. "We've been trying all day," Clark said, voice weary.

The Commander let out a sharp scoff. "What kind of scientist are you? Look out there–it’s always night." He muttered, almost to himself, "Maybe you should be the one braving the cold to restart the generators."

Barely glancing up from her console, Rei, another scientist, addressed him. "There's not much we can do, Commander. If nobody sends a signal from the light side, we're shit outta luck."

The Commander threw his hands up in disbelief. "You've gotta be shitting me. The Earth stops spinning and the only side with sunlight is barren?"

"We don't know that for sure," Clark began, but Rei interrupted him sharply.

"Wait, I got something!" Her fingers danced across her keyboard, the urgency clear as she tried to lock onto the signal. Both men sprang from their seats, crowding behind Rei, eyes glued to her screen.

"Trying to triangulate..." She murmured, clicking a series of buttons with purpose. "We're locked in!"

Everyone exhaled at once, relief momentarily overtaking the tension. As Rei processed the data, a strange transmission hummed to life—a series of clicks reminiscent of a typewriter in some long-forgotten study, stuttering in bursts both short and unnervingly long. Then: silence.

"What was that?" The Commander asked, peering closely at the monitor.

Clark scratched his chin. "Not sure. Some kind of code?"

"You think it's Morse?" Rei suggested.

Sudden recognition brightened Clark’s face. "Oh, yeah! I took a Morse class in community college—wait, let me grab a pen!"

Rei waved him off. "No need." A few swift keystrokes called up a translation program. Letters began to scroll across the screen.

"What does it say?" The Commander demanded.

Rei leaned forward, reading aloud. "'Don't look at the sun.'"

Silence fell. "What? Don't look at the sun?" The Commander repeated, incredulous. "Clark, how long until we see sunlight?"

Clark quickly did the math, fingers tracing invisible numbers in the air. "Well, we're near the Twilight border. Factoring in rotation and revolution, I’d say about 36 hours."

The Commander blanched. "Thirty-six hours?! We don’t have enough food to last a revolution!"

Before Rei could respond, a sharp beep erupted from her terminal.

"What was that?" The Commander barked, whipping around.

Rei scanned her readout. "Someone's trying to open the door."

Clark stepped away from his station, heading for the entry.

The Commander shot out a hand, pointing at him. "Don’t open that door."

By Managing Editor Mike Schneider
At first glance, Ringwood looks like another beautiful section of the Jersey Highlands. The forests, the rolling hills and the beautiful lakes. But these hills have some very special history.
A couple hundred years ago, these hills were the scene of a teeming ironworks. The problem was for the owners back in London, business wasn’t good enough. So they sent in one of their best — Robert Erskine — considered to be a brilliant engineer. Some say he invented the field of hydraulics. Others insist he was also one of the best surveyors around.
When hostilities started to build up between the colonies and Mother England, Erskine didn’t want to lose his men to the army. So he formed his own militia. He sided with George Washington. Washington took note of that fact and took note of the fact that he also needed a brilliant surveyor. He arranged a meeting with Erskine and shortly thereafter, Erskine became the surveyor of the United States Army — the very first surveyor.
Most of the maps used in this war, certainly almost all of the maps used in the northern section of the war, were created by Erskine and his staff. In many ways, it can be accurately said that Robert Erskine helped write the map that led to the victory.
So Erskine and his staff spent the next four years moving around the various roads in this region of the country, putting together the maps that would allow George Washington to retreat when necessary and to attack when possible.
Meantime, back at the ironworks, Erskine and his staff were also designing the instruments of war. Much of the artillery that was used by George Washington’s army — designed, produced right here. Also, the obstacles in the Hudson River aimed at keeping the British fleet from moving up and dividing the colonies in half — designed here by Robert Erskine.
But in the autumn of 1780 while out on a surveying trip, Robert Erskine fell ill and by the time he got home to Ringwood, it was a lot more serious than that. In October, he died of pneumonia. When George Washington got the news, he came right here to say goodbye to his friend, a man that history now refers to as the forgotten general.
General Washington would later return to Ringwood. In fact it was here that he announced to his troops that an armistice had been reached with Great Britain. The American Revolution apparently had been won. The American experience was just beginning.

"What. The. Fuck." I said to myself, as I picked myself back up. Id never seen this one before. It happens every now n then, when you've been doing this for so long. The usual protocol, as much protocol you can put into madness(no pun intended) like this anyway, is to fight what the unknown manifestation is, figure out what it's effects are on me, get out for a bit, Google them, learn all I can about them, go back in, kick some ass. Depression drained me, but I just had to ignore the feeling of being tired. Anxiety made me think surely the 'demon' can't be defeated because of a billion reasons that flood my mind. Ignore them. Fight anyway. You get the idea. This one was different. I even went through every chapter of the DSM for this son of a gun. Ok well skimmed it. Nada. I just couldn't freaking beat this guy. I swing left, he dodged left. Try to sweep, he jumps perfectly. Well, almost perfectly. It almost seemed like he COULD be dodging and swinging faster, but he was doing it at just the right amount of speed, precision and power to where I felt I had a chance to beat him. But is he getting better every time I try harder? Am I even trying harder? What. The. Fuck. I got out for a little bit. The guys file didn't say much, and we don't wake them up till they're cured for the simple reason that if they woke up during a fight...well I'm toast. Imagine the scene in the Matrix where neo trains against Morpheus, and all of a sudden 50,000 people, vehicles and god knows what else a mind produces show up. Yeah. You don't want that. Anyway, I had to figure out what to do. I tried going home to take a nap, clear my mind, but I just couldnt. I kept coming back to how close I was to beating the demon. How almost predictable his fighting style was. How I landed those few blows that were what I can only describe as divine moments of technical perfection from me. I couldn't think of anything else. I wasn't hungry, I was starving to go back in there and fight. I wasn't tired, in fact it seemed like the only thing I had any energy for was to go back in there. I...I was obsessed with how I can beat him. Wait a minute! That's it! I don't care about saving him or any of that jazz right now. All it was was about the thrill of outsmarting and outmaneouvering this demon. Fuck. Yes. I read the guy's file again. How could I have missed it? It figures. Most people, I realized, don't exactly consider this a 'problem', so no wonder that section didn't really list out the clues. I went back in, calmer than ever, with a strange sense of nostalgia back to my own fight with the dekom. Back in college, in our dorms. I looked at the demon. Walked right at him, without making a move. Without engaging him, even though my impulses raged for me to just try what I had been rehearsing all day. He just stared blankly back at me. I couldn't help but smile. There it was. Right behind him in the area I paid no attention to during our skirmishes. A tiny button with large text. It's funny. All that effort I put into the 'fight' was for nothing. All I had to do was press this button. "Uninstall overwatch" Just as I started to push it, a part of me wondered if I was actually going to go through with it.

Wow, that party was insane! So many people, so many new smells! Honestly, I probably should’ve stopped at one piece of chocolate last night, but it just smelled so delicious, and someone left it right at nose level. Nobody told me I was being bad, so I went for it—and man, it was tasty. Still... yeah, maybe not my best idea. I feel really weird this morning—cold, kind of soft and floaty.

Wait a second. Oh no, I’m on the bed! I know I’m not supposed to be on the bed. I jump down as fast as I can, hoping no one saw. But—hold on—my paws feel totally off. I look down and, whoa, I have fingers! With thumbs! No fur, just skin!

My mind goes straight to the fridge—oh my gosh, I can open it now! Walking on two legs is super awkward, but I make it to the kitchen and pull the door open, just like I always dreamed. There it is: leftover roast chicken. I snatch it up and take a huge bite, already loving this. Hmm, the bones are way harder than I expected. Maybe that’s why people always gave me the bones after they’d already eaten the good stuff.

Suddenly, there’s a knock at the door. I spin around, mouth full of chicken. “Go away! Go away! My house!” I shout. Even with greasy fingers, I manage to grab the handle and open the door, just like humans do. It’s a guy, the one I figured it would be, holding some mail.

He looks me up and down and says, “Uh, I’ve got your mail.”

“Go away!” I yell again. Seriously, how much clearer can I be?

He drops the mail and starts backing away. “Okay, I’m going. Uh, you might want to put some clothes on.”

I slam the door in triumph. This is already awesome! Except… if my owner comes back and figures out I was on the bed and ate the chicken, I’ll definitely be in trouble. Wait, speaking of the chicken...

Back in the kitchen, there’s someone else there. I don’t recognize her at first. But the way she’s picking at the chicken, her long sharp nails… actually, I think I do know who she is.

“We need to talk about some ground rules,” she says quietly—the person who used to be the cat.

He urged the congregation to rid Mexico of drug dealer "merchants of death".
Ecatepec, with 1.6 million inhabitants, is notorious for  drug violence, kidnappings and gangland-style killings, particularly of women.
The Pope was speaking at the biggest scheduled event of his visit to Mexico.
Throughout his trip he has condemned the evils of forced emigration and drugs, urging Mexico's leaders to provide "true justice" to suffering citizens.
The Pope has described drug trafficking as a cancer that has devoured and destroyed Mexican society, while calling on the Roman Catholic Church in Mexico to do more than just condemn the problem.
Mexicans hope papal visit brings change
Thousands of admirers lined the route of the Pope's motorcade as it made its way to the huge field in Ecatepec where Sunday's Mass was celebrated.
In a final prayer in Ecatepec he urged Mexicans to turn their country into a land of opportunity "where there will be no need to emigrate in order to dream, no need to be exploited in order to work".
On Saturday he celebrated mass at the Basilica of the Virgin of Guadalupe, in front of tens of thousands of people.
On Monday, he visits Mexico's poorest and least Roman Catholic region, the indigenous state of Chiapas in the south of the country.
The following day he heads to the capital of Michoacan, a western state also scarred by drug violence.
The Pope concludes his five-day trip in Ciudad Juarez on the US border, a city which has been blighted by drug-related murders. A mass there will highlight the plight of migrants.

The one billionth download of a digital single in the UK took place last night. According to the Official Charts Company, the UK reached the milestone thanks to artists such as Adele and Maroon 5. The company's figures show that Adele's Someone Like You is the most downloaded track ever in the UK - it has been downloaded 1.46 million times. Adele's Someone Like You is the most downloaded song ever in the UK - it has been downloaded 1.46 million times . Official Charts Company managing director, Martin Talbot, says that 16 tracks have now been downloaded more than a million times . 1. Someone Like You - Adele . 2. Moves Like Jagger - Maroon 5 feat. Christina Aguilera . 3. Somebody That I Used To Know - Gotye feat. Kimbra . 4. I Gotta Feeling - Black Eyed Peas . 5. We Found Love - Rihanna feat. Calvin Harris . 6. Sex On Fire - Kings of Leon . 7. Call Me Maybe - Carly Rae Jepsen . 8. Party Rock Anthem - LMFAO feat. Lauren Bennett and GoonRock . 9. Just The Way You Are (Amazing) - Bruno Mars . 10. Price Tag - Jessie J . Maroon 5 featuring Christina Aguilera with Moves Like Jagger is the second most downloaded song ever in the UK - it has been downloaded 1.4 million times. Gotye featuring Kimbra with Somebody That I used To Know is the third most downloaded track in the UK - 1.38 million downloads - and the Black Eyed Peas’ I Gotta Feeling is fourth with 1.34 million downloads. We Found Love by Rihanna featuring Calvin Harris, Sex on Fire by Kings of Leon, Call Me Maybe by Carly Rae Jepsen, and Price Tag by Jessie J also made the top ten. As did Just The Way You Are (Amazing) by Bruno Mars and Party Rock Anthem by LMFAO featuring Lauren Bennett and GoonRock. Downloading took off in a big way in 2004, the same year that a separate sales chart was launched, and figures went on to be included in the main singles chart the following year. More than three million singles are now downloaded each week in the UK. Chief executive of the British Phonographic Industry, Geoff Taylor, said: ‘The digital music revolution has made it easy to buy any song you like, instantly, for half the price of a coffee. Downloading took off in a big way in 2004 and now more than three million singles are downloaded each week in the UK . ‘As fans increasingly download music on the move, tablets, smartphones and connected cars will drive another phase in digital music's expansion.’ Official Charts Company managing director, Martin Talbot, added: ‘The explosion in download sales over the past nine years means we are genuinely now living in the digital music age - with Adele as our queen. ‘Over the nine years of legitimate digital music, more than 16 new tracks have sold one million copies, which emphasises just how popular digital music is in the modern age.’ Official Charts Company managing director, Martin Talbot, says that 16 tracks have now been downloaded more than a million times .

**PALUXY, TEXAS.** Nestled like a small pearl on the string of State Highway 51, Paluxy sits fifteen miles southwest of Granbury in Hood County. Like a child changing homes, it moved from Erath County to the newly carved Hood County in 1866, when political boundaries shifted like puzzle pieces being rearranged.


The town's name evolved like a game of telephone gone wild. What started as "Pulltight" - a name that stuck like mud to describe the treacherous river crossing that grabbed travelers like quicksand - morphed through variations like Poloxeyville, Paluxieville, and Baloxey before settling into "Paluxy," as if the town were trying on different hats until it found the perfect fit.


The early 1860s brought Indian raids that swept through the area like storm clouds, making life as unpredictable as lightning. John Meek planted himself in the community like a sturdy oak, helping establish both the town and county while serving as one of the first four county commissioners - a founding father in the truest sense.


The heart of early Paluxy pumped through Goather's gristmill, which operated like a magnet drawing people from across the region. When Dan Himmens and J.H. Haley took over, they kept this economic engine running smoothly for years, the mill's grinding stones turning wheat into flour as reliably as the seasons turned.


Paluxy's population has been as consistent as a steady heartbeat - starting at 100 souls in 1887, growing to 164 where it remained as constant as a rock from 1900 to 1970, before settling at 76 like a comfortable retirement number through 2000. Surrounded by farm and ranch land that stretches like a patchwork quilt, the town remains a small island of community in an ocean of rural Texas landscape.


Today's residents stay connected to the wider world through the Hood County News and KPAR-AM radio from nearby Granbury, these media outlets serving as the town's ears and voice to the outside world.

**The Problem:**

Many military veterans were homeless after returning from war. In 2014, President Obama asked mayors across America to help solve this problem.


**What They Did:**

- The White House, Housing Department, and Veterans Affairs worked together

- They created new programs to help homeless veterans

- Mayors in cities nationwide joined the effort


**The Results:**

- Homeless veteran numbers dropped by 47% since 2010

- Over 360,000 veterans and their families got help

- Now there are fewer than 40,000 homeless veterans (down from much higher numbers)

- 2 states and 27 communities have completely ended veteran homelessness


**How It Works:**

Cities like New Orleans and Houston now get homeless veterans into safe housing within 30 days. They also provide other help like addiction counseling and mental health services.


**Looking Forward:**

The article suggests using these same successful methods to help other homeless people, not just veterans.


**Bottom Line:**

A coordinated effort between federal government and local mayors has dramatically reduced veteran homelessness, proving that the problem can be solved with the right approach.

**A Literary Journey Through the Humid Heart of Louisiana**


Parts of this book made me breathless with its heart-breaking beauty—like watching Spanish moss sway in the thick Louisiana air while painful family secrets unfold beneath ancient oak trees. Parts made me laugh out loud, catching me off guard like sudden summer thunderstorms that break the oppressive heat. Parts have taken up their places in my mind and won't go away, lingering like the persistent scent of magnolia blossoms and the echo of cicadas on sweltering Southern nights.


Much of the content of this book is difficult, although it ultimately is about survival in a world where tradition and trauma intertwine like kudzu vines. The author fearlessly explores alcoholism flowing like bourbon at garden parties, abuse hidden behind perfectly manicured lawns and society smiles, and casual racism as pervasive as the humidity that clings to everything in this corner of the Deep South.


Although many have called this book a novel, it is more a collection of related short stories told in various voices—each one emerging from different porches, parlors, and parishes—most of which seem very authentic and real, as if overheard during late-night conversations on wrap-around verandas or whispered in the shadowed corners of old plantation houses.


Like many people, I read the 2nd book "Divine Secrets of the Ya-Ya Sisterhood" first, drawn in by its lighter tone like a refreshing mint julep on a lazy afternoon. This prequel is a more difficult, less funny book—raw and unfiltered like uncut whiskey—but it is a book worth your time, offering the deeper roots and darker soil from which those unforgettable Ya-Ya bonds eventually bloomed.

Be an Informed Consumer
By Cindy Grigg

1 Are you an advertising target? Yep, you sure are! Advertisers show you TV ads at home and even in some schools. They pay for banner ads you see when you're online, and billboard ads you see when you're out and about. They put their brand names on your clothes and shoes, and they even pay to have their products show up in the movies you watch. Think about it: the average 12-year-old watches TV for four hours every day. That's a whopping 20,000 to 40,000 TV ads you see each year!

2 Kids are consumers, too! A survey by Consumer Reports found that you and other kids in the U.S. have $15 billion of your *own* money to spend every year! And get this: you also influence what your parents spend – that's a huge $188 billion every year!

3 Sometimes, ads can trick people into buying things they don't really need. They make you think a product will magically make your life better. Maybe you buy advertised stuff because you want to look cooler, feel happier, or just fit in more. Ads are super powerful when it comes to what people want. Companies use them to make you *want* their specific products.

4 Ads don't just affect what you want, they affect prices too! Lots of companies pay famous people – like your favorite athletes or celebrities – to tell you to buy their stuff. They hope if you see your hero using it, you'll think *you'll* be just as good or popular if you buy it. Guess what? Companies pay these stars tons of money. And who do you think pays for that? You do! They add that cost to the price of the product, so *they* can still make money. So, advertising is kind of a 'hidden cost' that makes things more expensive.

The step great-grandfather of a Buffalo Bills player went on a mass shooting spree in Union County, Florida Saturday killing two and injuring another two before committing suicide. According to Union County Sheriff Jerry Whitehead, Hubert Allen, Jr walked into Prichett Trucking, his former workplace, early Saturday morning and shot David Griffis. Griffis was taken to UFHealth in Gainesville where he is currently in critical condition. 


Mass shooting: Hubert Allen, Jr first went to Pritchett Trucking where he shot David Griffis, before moving on to the owner's farm where he proceeded to shoot three more people . 


**The gunman wasn't finished.** After leaving Pritchett trucking, **Allen then** went to owner Marvin Pritchett's farm Rolling Oaks. **There,** he shot and killed farmhand Rolando Gonazalez and Pritchett. Lewis Mabrey, Jr was also shot and is in critical condition at UFHealth. 


**The business owner Allen targeted had built a substantial operation over decades.** Marvin Pritchett started Pritchett trucking in 1980 after buying a timber business in 1970 that came with three trucks. When the timber business changed in the mid-70s and the industry was put on quotas, he shifted his energies to the trucking business. Today the company employs well over 400 people and has grown to over 600 trailers, transporting such things as refuse, live floor, dump, flatbed, log, wood by-products, equipment, and contaminated dirt. 


Injured: Two men were killed and another two injured. The injured two are in critical condition at UFHealth in Gainesville . 


**After the farm shooting,** Allen then went back to his house where he committed suicide by shooting himself. Investigators still don't know what the motive of the shooting was. **What is known is that** Allen was the step-grandfather of Buffalo Bills running back CJ Spiller. 


Football connection: The shooter - Hubert Allen, . Jr - was the step-grandfather to Buffalo Bills running back CJ Spiller, . pictured above .

Dad walked up the lawn, his gaze locked on his son below. He found his spot on the dewy grass. A warm spring breeze blew, but it didn't quite hide the power that was about to be unleashed. His eyes still on his unsuspecting target, the older man prepared to really put some power behind his throw. He rolled a hard baseball between his long fingers. Like he'd done it a thousand times, his right arm swung back overhead, the ball gripped tight, his fingers aching to whip it forward with all his strength. His eyes still fixed on the young boy below, head down, a bead of sweat forming on his brow, he channeled all his strength. His arm shot forward and released the ball. It sailed over the grass like a cannonball, following all the rules of physics – predictable, yes, but also pretty intense. As the 'cannon shot' arced toward him, the young boy's mind raced through all his dad's training. "Get in front of it.""Keep your eye on the ball.""Don't be afraid of it." With just moments to react, the boy put himself in harm's way. His leather glove fanned open. He bent his knees slightly, ready for any tiny adjustment. His eyes remained locked on the ball. The mighty ball arced across the spring sky, briefly blocking out the sun. Gravity eventually won, pulling the baseball back down to Earth, its path now curving right towards the small boy. With a mighty flinch, the boy showed he was ready. With skill and a bit of grace, he brought his glove up and forward, snatching the ball from the sky. With a mighty WHUMP, the ball slammed into the net of his glove, a tiny cloud of dust bursting out from the impact. A triumphant smile crept onto the boy's face. "Got it, Dad!"

Connacht initially took control with two tries from Niyi Adeolokun and one from Robbie Henshaw, but Jonathan Wisniewski responded by scoring 13 points before halftime. Connacht extended their lead to 26-16 with a try from Matt Healy, however, Grenoble capitalized on Denis Buckley's sin-binning, with Lucas Dupont and Mahamadou Diaby both crossing the line. Although John Cooney put Connacht back in front, Wisniewski secured the victory with a late drop goal. With this win, Grenoble advanced to face Harlequins in the semi-finals. Connacht, however, must now overcome this setback and refocus on the Pro12, where they currently hold second place.

Reflecting their season-long dynamism, Connacht began strongly, with Bundee Aki's looping pass creating Adeolokun's try in the 13th minute. Following Wisniewski's successful penalty, Henshaw scored after a brilliant break by Healy and a precise cross-kick from Shane O'Leary. Soon after, Kieran Marmion's quick run contributed to Adeolokun's second try. Despite converting his second try, O'Leary's ill-advised cross-kick near his own try line handed Grenoble a crucial opportunity, which fly-half Wisniewski converted into points. Wisniewski's two subsequent penalties narrowed Connacht's halftime lead to 19-16. Nevertheless, the Irish side established a 13-point cushion by the 52nd minute, thanks to an excellent burst from Healy through Grenoble's defense and five more points from O'Leary's boot.

A pivotal moment occurred on the hour mark when Buckley was sin-binned for illegally preventing a Grenoble try. During Buckley's time off the field, Grenoble, who sit eighth in the Top 14, scored two tries, with Dupont and Diaby crossing the line. Irishman Chris Farrell delivered an excellent offload leading to the second score. Wisniewski's two conversions gave Grenoble their first lead of the match with 10 minutes remaining. Although replacement John Cooney's penalty restored Connacht's lead in the 73rd minute, the home team's number 10 secured the win with a drop goal two minutes later.

TEAMS
Grenoble: G Aplon; N Nemani, C Farrell, N Hunt, L Dupont; J Wisniewski, C McLeod; S Taumalolo, L Jammes, D Edwards; B Hand (capt), P Kimlin; S Setephano, M Diaby, R Grice. Replacements: F Barcella for Taumalolo 53, M Marie for Hand 70, F Alexandre for Grice 26, F Estebanez for Hunt 67.
Not used: L Saseras, R de Klerk, P Mas, F Gengenbacher
Connacht: M Healy; N Adeolokun, R Henshaw, B Aki, D Poolman; S O'Leary, K Marmion; D Buckley, T McCartney, F Bealham; U Dillane, A Browne; S O'Brien, J Connolly, J Muldoon (capt). Replacements: D Heffernan for McCartney 71, R Loughney for Connolly 60, JP Cooney for Bealham 75, Q Roux for Dillane 60, G Naoupu for O'Brien 73, J Cooney for Marmion 63, P Robb for O'Leary 66, F Carr for Poolman 37.
Referee: Matthew Carley (England)

By Hugo Gye. The world’s smallest library has popped up on the streets of New York City. It’s a bright yellow plastic booth that only fits one person at a time and holds about 40 books. The goal is to give busy city residents a quiet place to relax and lose themselves in a good story.

This “Little Free Library” was created by two creative architects using recycled materials to keep the books safe from the weather. The small library is made from an old plastic water tank and a wooden frame, with holes on the outside so curious people can peek in before going inside. Venezuelan architects Marcelo Ertorteguy and Sara Valente designed the space so that people can really focus on browsing and reading.

The library in Manhattan’s Nolita neighborhood has gotten a lot of attention since it opened last month. Mr. Ertorteguy explained that the Little Free Library movement began about three years ago and has quickly spread across the U.S. and to places in Europe, Africa, and India. “It’s surprising that New York City was one of the few major cities without any of these tiny libraries,” he said.

Unlike a typical library building, this one stands out. It’s just one of 5,000 Free Lending Libraries around the U.S. and the world. The designers hope to give New Yorkers a small escape from daily life. The Nolita library is part of a project with the Architectural League of New York and the Pen World Voices Festival, and it will stay up until September.

Mr. Ertorteguy said they enjoy using recycled materials, and in this case, they wanted to reuse something in a completely new way—a water tank was perfect because it could protect the books and be big enough for a person to go inside. “We wanted to make a special spot where people could slow down—a pocket of calm on the busy streets where you can step inside and get lost in a book,” he said.

The unusual-looking library has gotten lots of attention from New Yorkers. It’s a tight fit inside—it can squeeze in two people, but they won’t have much room to move around. The library sits outside a school in the trendy Nolita district.

There are now over 5,000 Little Free Libraries nationwide and around the world. Most are just outdoor shelves where you can take or leave books, but don’t have room to sit or read inside. The goal of the project is to make sure everyone, even in neighborhoods without public libraries, has access to books.

Hugo Chavez was known to break into song during his lengthy speeches, and appropriately, traditional Venezuelan llanera music resonated through the venue where his funeral took place. Chavez, who died Tuesday at the age of 58, deeply divided public opinion in his homeland. Still, his supporters held him in high regard, and his funeral in Caracas on Friday embodied that esteem. Thirty-two heads of state were present, each invited in groups of eight to stand by the Venezuelan leader’s casket and observe a moment of silence alongside honor guards. Notable figures from Venezuela’s arts and sports communities also gathered around the late president. Chavez leaves a complex legacy: he is recognized for prioritizing the needs of the poor in his policies, but he also displayed authoritarian tendencies, drawing criticism from advocates of civil liberties and press freedom. The presence of foreign dignitaries and the thousands of mourning citizens who queued for hours to pay their respects reflect Chavez’s impact on the nation, the Rev. Mario Moronta remarked. Venezuelan Vice President Nicolas Maduro, set to be inaugurated as interim president later that day, placed a replica of Simon Bolivar’s sword on Chavez’s flag-covered casket. Tributes were also held in Cuba, where the military honored Chavez with a 21-gun salute from Havana’s La Cabaña Fortress. Officers placed roses before a portrait of Chavez. Ties between Venezuela and Cuba remain close, with Cuba having sent hundreds of military and intelligence experts to support Chavez during his presidency. Chavez was widely recognized for his anti-American rhetoric and forged alliances with other countries in opposition to the United States. Iranian President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad attended the service and kissed Chavez’s casket. “He was a dear friend to all nations around the world,” Ahmadinejad said upon arriving in Venezuela. “He was the emotional cornerstone for all revolutionary and freedom-seeking people in the region and beyond.” The United States, which lacks an ambassador in Venezuela, was represented at the ceremony by James Derham, the chargé d’affaires at its Caracas embassy. U.S. Rep. Gregory Meeks of New York and former Massachusetts lawmaker William Delahunt completed the U.S. delegation. Hollywood actor and Chavez supporter Sean Penn attended, as did the Rev. Jesse Jackson, who led a prayer. “Hugo fed the needy. He uplifted the poor. He inspired hope. He helped people realize their aspirations,” Jackson said. Hero or villain? The varied perspectives on Chavez. Maduro, whom Chavez had selected as his favored successor, delivered an extensive, passionate speech, visibly moved at times. He addressed allegations of Chavez being autocratic and ineffective, leaving the country in a worse state than before his tenure. “Never has any man, here or anywhere else, been so maligned,” he proclaimed. Chavez, Maduro said, was a “true son of Christ” who taught the nation about love and forgiveness. “That is why today, from our hearts, we forgive those who have slandered you.” Chavez had asked Maduro and other key leaders what actions they would take after his death, according to the vice president. “He prepared everything for us—now it’s up to us to fulfill it,” Maduro stated. There was no burial following the funeral, due to an unexpected announcement from Maduro on Thursday. Chavez will be embalmed “just like Lenin and Mao Zedong,” and placed in a military museum where future generations of Venezuelans can visit. This signifies that Chavez’s presence will long endure. “The body of our leader will be embalmed, enclosed by crystal glass for all time, forever with his people,” Maduro explained on state television. Opinion: Chavez as a maker of dreams. Chavez succumbed to cancer after a lengthy illness. Thousands of his supporters lined the streets Wednesday as his body was moved from the military hospital where he passed away to a military academy for public viewing. As president, Chavez projected himself as a champion of the marginalized, and his supporters revered him with intense devotion. The outpouring at the academy was so overwhelming that the public viewing was extended for seven additional days. Opinion: Chavez leveraged his charisma to secure votes. Chavez first revealed his cancer diagnosis in June 2011, but officials never disclosed specifics about his condition or the exact nature of his illness. CNN’s Patrick Oppmann, Karen Smith, and Mitra Mobasherat contributed reporting.

For much of Saturday, ING's Romanian customers could not access their money.
The bank said the discharge of its gas-based fire suppression system had caused "unexpected" damage to its computer servers.
A report by Motherboard suggests that the equipment was too noisy.
A spokeswoman for ING was unable to confirm this detail.
But she did acknowledge the problem had lasted from 13:00 to 23:00 local time and the bank had been unable to explain the situation to customers as its own communications system had been affected.
"Due to the magnitude and complexity of the breakdown, the time required to restore the activity through the backup system was unfortunately longer than during the tests we perform regularly," said Iosefina Frolu.
"Currently, we are finalising the diagnostic analysis of this exceptional event."
The fire extinguisher system used at the Bucharest site worked by expelling a mix—known as Inergen—of nitrogen, argon and carbon dioxide.
These gases are normally not harmful to computer equipment.
But, according to Motherboard, the high-pressure release created a sound that topped 130 decibels.
A study last year by the German engineering group Siemens warned the nozzles used by some fire suppression systems could cause "fatal" damage to hard disks because of sound wave vibrations they sent through the air.
"The hypothesis is that sound causes vibrations in the HDD case that are ultimately transmitted to the disk spindle and head assembly, causing misalignment of the read/write heads to the data tracks," it said.
"Because there are many different designs of storage system chassis and cabinets on the market, the issue of vibration caused by noise as well as structure-borne vibration has not yet been fully investigated."
Siemens's research indicated that different server models were vulnerable to different noise frequencies, and if there was a match:
Although ING said the accident was "abnormal and unprecedented" in its experience, there have been other suspected incidents.
In 2013, the French media reported that accountancy software used by the country's government had become temporarily unavailable after a fire protection system had been accidentally triggered at a data centre, causing a loud noise.
The following year, Inergen extinguishers were used to put out a fire at a facility in Sydney, Australia.
When several of the disks failed, some experts blamed noise vibrations caused by the discharge.

"I wish you could see what I see." No reply. "I wish you Godspeed," he tried in the warmest, most brotherly voice he could muster. He listened to nothing for a while. He tapped his knee with his fat-fingered glove; the heavily padded seat of his suit shielded him from the rock's chill. The sun had vanished, taking with it all warmth. The ground around him was invisible now, but the distant stars blazed, bright and clear. It was a commonplace sight, yet he had hoped to tempt her into a reply. "Don't cry," he said, imagining her sobbing, her sadness and remorse paralyzing her finger above the transmit button, safe in the warm cockpit of the little escape pod now pulling away. By now, she was surely free of this planet's gravity. He smiled faintly. "We both knew," he started to say, but stopped. His suit battery would last another hour, but he had only five minutes or so left. He listened to his air softly hiss from the fractured face-plate, and thought, "There was only room for one."

A Guatemalan judge Thursday declined to grant former dictator Efrain Rios Montt amnesty from genocide charges, paving the way for a trial. Montt's defense team had filed a motion seeking amnesty last week. The judge argued that the country's 1996 National Reconciliation Law, which coincided with the end of the country's civil war, does not guarantee amnesty for those accused of human rights abuses. Secondly, the judge said, Guatemala is party to international treaties that obligate it to prosecute crimes of genocide. Rios Montt ruled Guatemala from 1982 to 1983. He came to power in a coup and led a military junta while Guatemala was in a bloody civil war between the army and leftist guerrillas. The war did not end until 1996, leaving more than 200,000 people dead and 1 million as refugees. Prosecutors argued that Rios Montt was aware of the repressive strategies that the military was using against anyone suspected of being a guerrilla, such as killings, forced disappearances and kidnappings, the state-run AGN news agency reported. The human rights abuse and genocide allegations against him come from his "scorched earth" campaign to root out insurgents in provinces heavily populated by indigenous populations. He is accused of being responsible for the deaths of more than 1,700 Mayan residents. During his rule, there were massacres in these provinces in which, according to the Guatemalan truth commission, between 70% and 90% of some villages were razed. The commission found that during this and other periods of the civil war, there were also reported cases of rape, especially of Mayan women. Journalist Maria Renee Barillas contributed to this report.

for National Geographic News  
Three mummies have been moved from the Valley of the Kings in Luxor to the Egyptian Museum in Cairo so that scientists can study where they came from, Egyptian authorities recently announced.  
Two female mummies, found in a tomb called KV21, and one male mummy, found outside the tomb of Pharaoh Seti II (who ruled Egypt from 1200 to 1194 B.C.), will have CAT scans and DNA tests.  
These tests might help scientists learn who these mummies were and who their families were, but experts say it might take years to be certain.  
Even so, the two female mummies may be important. Archaeologists hope that they could be some of the Egyptian queens they have been trying to find.  
Both women were found in a special pose that only royal women used: their left arms were bent at the elbow with the hand placed across the chest, while the right arms were straight along the body.  
"We'll try to study the two females in KV21, because we're looking for Tutankhamun's family through the Egyptian Mummy Project," said Zahi Hawass, head of Egypt's Supreme Council of Antiquities.  
This project is a five-year effort started by Hawass to check and record the DNA of every mummy in Egypt.  
"Maybe one of them is Nefertiti or Tiye or Kiya, we do not know," said Hawass, who also works with National Geographic. (The National Geographic Society owns National Geographic News.)  
An Egyptian team will study the mummies’ genes in what Hawass calls the only lab in the world built just for mummy DNA tests.  
The best way to study old genes is by looking at mitochondrial DNA, said Angelique Corthals, a teacher in biomedical and forensic studies at the University of Manchester, UK, who helped train the Egyptian team.  
This DNA is passed down from mothers to children and has thousands of copies in every cell. (Learn more about human genetics.)

Waking up fully rested, a calm morning. The clock says its six and the sun is just coming up. You're not sleepy enough to go back to bed. Getting up and putting on some clothes you step outside to check the mail you left in the mailbox last night but also to feel how warm or cold it is. Maybe you'll go for a jog, maybe you'll hang out with a friend. Maybe your boss will call you into work. All you know is one thing; you woke up this morning. And you're happy because maybe you can make someone else smile.

LHC: Higgs boson 'may have been glimpsed'
The most coveted prize in particle physics - the Higgs boson - may have been glimpsed, say researchers reporting at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) in Geneva.
The particle is purported to be the means by which everything in the Universe obtains its mass.
Scientists say that two experiments at the LHC see hints of the Higgs at the same mass, fuelling huge excitement.
But the LHC does not yet have enough data to claim a discovery.
Finding the Higgs would be one of the biggest scientific advances of the last 60 years. It is crucial for allowing us to make sense of the Universe, but has never been observed by experiments.
This basic building block of the Universe is a significant missing component of the Standard Model - the "instruction booklet" that describes how particles and forces interact.
Two separate experiments at the LHC - Atlas and CMS - have been conducting independent searches for the Higgs. Because the Standard Model does not predict an exact mass for the Higgs, physicists have to use particle accelerators like the LHC to systematically look for it across a broad search area.
At a seminar at Cern (the organisation that operates the LHC) on Tuesday, the heads of Atlas and CMS said they see "spikes" in their data at roughly the same mass: 124-125 gigaelectronvolts (GeV; this is about 130 times as heavy as the protons found in atomic nuclei).
"The excess may be due to a fluctuation, but it could also be something more interesting. We cannot exclude anything at this stage," said Fabiola Gianotti, spokesperson for the Atlas experiment.
Guido Tonelli, spokesperson for the CMS experiment, said: "The excess is most compatible with a Standard Model Higgs in the vicinity of 124 GeV and below, but the statistical significance is not large enough to say anything conclusive.
"As of today, what we see is consistent either with a background fluctuation or with the presence of the boson."
Prof Rolf-Dieter Heuer, director-general of Cern, told BBC News: "Such signals can come and go… Although there is correspondence between the two experiments, we need more solid numbers."
None of the spikes seen by the experiments is at much more than the "two sigma" level of certainty.
A level of "five sigma" is required to claim a discovery, meaning there is less than a one in a million chance the data spike is down to a statistical fluke.
Another complicating factor is that these tantalising hints consist only of a handful of events among the billions of particle collisions analysed at the LHC.
Professor Rolf-Dieter Heuer, director-general of Cern told BBC News: "We can be misled by small numbers, so we need more statistics," but added: "It is exciting."
If it exists, the Higgs is very short-lived, quickly decaying - or transforming - into more stable particles. There are several different ways this can happen, which provides scientists with different routes to search for the boson.
They looked at particular decay routes for the Higgs that produce only a handful of events, but have the advantage of having less background noise in the data. This background noise consists of random combinations of events, some of which can look like Higgs decays.
Other decay modes produce more events - which are better for statistical certainty - but also more background noise. Prof Heuer said physicists were "squeezed" between these two options.
Prof Stefan Soldner-Rembold, from the University of Manchester, called the quality of the LHC's results "exceptional", adding: "Within one year we will probably know whether the Higgs particle exists, but it is likely not going to be a Christmas present."
The simple fact that both Atlas and CMS seem to be seeing a data spike at the same mass has been enough to cause enormous excitement in the particle physics community.

The bus is late. I hate it when the bus is late. Sometimes, it annoys me so much, I kick the bus stop. I know I shouldn't. It is not the bus driver's fault that he is late. I still need to kick something. I get aggressive. I can't. There is a girl next to me. She is waiting too. She is also annoyed. There's something perky about the way she displays her annoyance; head high, eyes forward and a pleasant lingering smile on her lips. I always look far too neutral when I wait on the bus stop. "The bus is late,"she says."I don't like buses that are late. They always feel dirty." I can only nod. "That is true, but luckily it is not raining today." She looks my way now, pose bent forward, nails pointing to her palms. "You could use a shower." "Pardon?"I say."Are you telling me..." "No,"her voice is level, almost secretive."I am offering you a shower. I am quite sure whatever you are going to is not important enough anyway." I think of my parents. They had hoped I would stop by today, but they were only counting on me tomorrow. She did have a point. "It is not the destination that matters, they sometimes say," I reply. "Then it is time for some adventure." She takes my hand and pulls me with her. We walk across the street and enter a small apartment building. My heart is racing as I follow her; what is she going to do to me? Why am I falling for this? Is this really happening? It is. We enter her apartment, she opens the shower curtain and turns the shower on. In the hallway she starts to strip and after a moment's hesitation I follow. We enter the shower and she hugs me close. Strangely close to a stranger. She smells very nice. An attractive girl and we are in the shower. There's no water coming out, I notice. It is something more fluid. I realize it smells of lubricant. I feel our bodies close together, sliding across each other with the greatest of ease. If I felt discomfort with the situation before, that sense now explodes. Then the floor disappears and we are falling. Every time I hit the side of the brightly lit metal tube, the lubricant keeps us going. We are moving faster and faster and faster. Until I can barely hear my own thoughts in my head. Then the tube ends and I am falling. I barely contain the rising panic in my mind before we hit the water. I sink briefly beneath the surface before rising once again. "Welcome to the U.S.E. Pandora, sir,"the girl said, slowly dressing into a suit of black fabric."We are all ready for you to take command." With some surprise I look out of the window. Earth is there, but far away. The moon is also visible as a shining white orb. I feel the artificial gravity take hold. "We are all ready to take your orders, sir," a number of other men and women step forward. I take two steps out of the pool before I faint.

I had been mining for a few days. My shifts started early, but with that damn sun nearby, I couldn't tell if it had been a few hours or just a minute. I kept mining, excavating the minerals, when I found a massive vein of iron. I would receive a considerable sum of money for this! I could go home! I could live comfortably for the rest of my life! I would probably get a promotion too. This strike would go for a few miles and have a few tons of iron. Then I heard a famaliar growl behind me. That was no coworker, getting mad if he stubbed his foot or anything as such. A bad feeling came over me. This was no vein of dark iron. It was. Something dark. Something dangerous. I turned the vehicle on, and turned around. Behind me was a cave-in. Must have happened when I uncovered the thing. I drove forward, tunneling through easily. What is that? A creature stood in the darkness ahead. I drove ahead, and then I saw it. It turned, whipping its scythe-like arms around. I drove forward, running it over. Finally I got off and checked the creature's body. Just as it happened on the Ishimura, it was happening here now. The necromorphs were coming.

Italian poet and patriot; born in Chieri in 1816; passed away in Venice on October 18, 1898. Having received his education at the Jewish schools of his hometown and Vercelli, he briefly pursued a career in commerce. In 1835 he matriculated at the University of Parma, and subsequently to the University of Pisa, but he was compelled to withdraw from the latter due to a duel where he injured a fellow student whom he had provoked for uttering an offensive comment regarding the Jews. After successfully completing his examination for a doctorate in law, he moved to Paris in 1839. Levi became a fervent advocate for the university ideals of a united, free Italy, having joined the Irredentist society La Giovane Italia. In Paris he was part of a group of Italian patriots; and, upon his return to Italy, he quickly emerged as a prominent figure in the political campaign advocating the separation of northern Italy from Austria and the unification of all Italian states.
After establishing himself in Venice, Levi participated in the Lombard revolt against Austrian rule from 1848 to 1849. In 1850 he relocated to Turin. Following the Franco-Italian-Austrian conflict of 1859, at which point the Italian provinces under Austrian control were incorporated into the Italian kingdom (1860), he gained election to the Italian assembly in Florence, where, representing the Liberal party, he advocated for equal rights and religious liberty. He served in the National Assembly until 1879, when, following his defeat, he withdrew from political life.
Levi composed numerous poems, particularly during his time in Venice, along with a considerable quantity of political and martial songs, including the renowned ode to Pope Pius IX., who, in 1846, immediately after ascending to the papal throne, was lauded as a liberator, but who, by 1849, altered his political stance and adopted a decidedly reactionary outlook. Across all of Levi's creations, his profound affection for Italy and for Judaism is palpable.
Levi penned: "Patria ed Affetti" (Venice, 1849), a volume of poetry; "Gli Martiri del 1799" (Turin, 1850), a play; "Martirio e Redenzione" (ib. 1859); "Del Navarra a Magenta" (ib. 1866; revised edition, 1884, featuring an imaginative allegorical dialogue as its second section); "Vita di Pensiero" (Milan, 1875); "Vita d'Azione" (Turin, 1882); "Il Semitismo" (ib. 1884); "La Mente di Michelangelo" (ib. 1890); "Giordano Bruno" (ib. 1894).
However, Levi's most significant achievement is the grand play "Il Profeta." Levi articulates its central theme in his introduction thus: "My aim is to present a mirror to my contemporaries, wherein they might discern their misjudgments, shortcomings, and blunders, thereby cultivating disdain for them; while simultaneously offering a lofty ideal for them to aspire to." For this purpose, he chose the narrative of Jeremiah. The play, spanning five acts, depicts the conflict between Zedekiah and Nebuchadnezzar. Jeremiah anticipates Jerusalem's downfall, should the populace fail to abandon their devotion to Baal, atone for their transgressions, and revert to the sole true God. Jeremiah the prophet and Ananias, the priest of Baal, respectively urge and endeavor to convince the king and the Jews to follow them. Ananias prevails; Jeremiah is incarcerated; and Jerusalem succumbs when assailed by the invading forces. The Temple is annihilated, and the Jews are driven into bondage. Jeremiah's prediction comes to pass.
Upon Jeremiah's imprisonment, his daughter Rachel is seized by Ananias, who attempts to claim her for his own. His advances proving fruitless, he commands her sacrifice to Moloch, at which point God intervenes. Ananias is struck down by lightning, and Rachel is freed by her beloved, Emanuel. Ananias's final utterance is: "Uno Infinito hai vinto" (end of Act 3). Emanuel enlists among Jerusalem's defenders, sustains a fatal injury, and perishes in the embrace of his beloved. In a spiritual sense, Judaism has triumphed over paganism, and Rachel has rejoined her beloved in purity; yet physically, Judaism is vanquished. Rachel is bereaved of her beloved and faces banishment; this banishment will, nonetheless, cleanse not only the Jews but, through their experience, the entire world, and will ready humanity for a more promising future.
The dialogue appended to the drama in the 1884 edition bears minimal relevance to it. It is conducted by Emanuel, embodying prophetism, and by Ahasuerus, symbolizing humanity, and primarily concerns Rome.
- S. H. Margulies, Dichter und Patriot, Berlin, 1896;
- Levi's own works, Vita di Pensiero and Vita d'Azione, as above.

Conflicting Views of a Child's Behavior Problems from Parents, Teachers, and the Child May Be Helpful to Clinician
New Rochelle, NY, November 1, 2011-Clinicians often face the challenge of trying to make sense of conflicting reports from parents, teachers, and children about a child's behavioral problems. However, a better understanding of the source and nature of these disagreements may provide important information that could improve treatment and outcomes. A group of articles in the current issue of Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology, a peer-reviewed journal published by Mary Ann Liebert, Inc., explores this challenging clinical dilemma. The articles are available online.
The special section, "Information Variance and Its Diagnostic Implications," includes an editorial and five articles that focus on either discordant information between parents and teachers or between parents and offspring and how clinicians should proceed in the face of these conflicting reports. Guest Editors Gabrielle Carlson, MD (Stony Brook University School of Medicine, NY), and Eric Youngstrom, PhD (University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill) note that "disagreement between informants was more the rule than the exception." They suggest that clinicians view this disagreement as "additional information" rather than a problem and use this information to help determine the credibility of each informant's report. "This has implications for treatment engagement, adherence, and ultimately outcomes," they conclude.
Two articles take a close look at the implications of disagreements between parents and teachers: David Rettew, MD (University of Vermont College of Medicine, Burlington, and Erasmus University Medical Center, Rotterdam, The Netherlands) and colleagues' report, "When Parent and Teach Don't Agree: The Tracking Adolescents' Individual Lives (TRAILS) Study," looks at behaviors that occur at home only, at school only, or in both settings among a community-based sample; while the paper entitled "Diagnostic Implications of Informant Disagreement for Manic Symptoms," authored by Gabrielle Carlson, MD and Joseph Blader, PhD, draws on patients seen at an outpatient clinic for symptoms of mania observed either only by a parent or by both parent and teacher.
Conflicting reports by parents and offspring is the focus of three articles, and the samples derive from families seeking services from an outpatient mental health clinic. Eric Youngstrom, PhD, and colleagues consider the question of who has greater credibility, parent or child, in the article "Informants Are Not All Equal: Predictors and Correlates of Clinician Judgments About Caregiver and Youth Credibility." Whether discrepancies in reporting influence clinicians' ratings of parent and child credibility is evaluated in the report by Andres De Los Reyes, PhD (University of Maryland at College Park) and co-authors on "Informant Discrepancies in Clinical Reports of Youths and Interviewers' Impressions of the Reliability of Informants." The article by Andrew Freeman (University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill) and co-workers, "Is Caregiver-Adolescent Disagreement Due to Differences in Thresholds for Reporting Manic Symptoms?" assesses whether the severity of a manic disorder affects the differences in how caregivers and teens describe the symptoms.
"Clinicians have long known that parents and children have very different perceptions about how a child is behaving or what they are feeling. In this issue, guest-edited by Drs. Carlson and Youngstrom, we are presented with multiple studies that clarify this dilemma. These studies improve the diagnosis and advance our understanding and treatment of children with psychiatric disorders," says Harold S. Koplewicz, MD, Editor-in-Chief of Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology, and President, Child Mind Institute, New York, NY.
Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology is an authoritative peer-reviewed journal published bimonthly in print and online. The journal is dedicated to child and adolescent psychiatry and behavioral pediatrics, covering clinical and biological aspects of child and adolescent psychopharmacology and developmental neurobiology. Complete tables of content and a free sample issue may be viewed online.

Marin Cilic made a winning return to the ATP circuit Monday after serving a reduced doping ban. The 25-year-old Croatian completed a tight three set victory over Dutchman Igor Sijsling at the Paris Masters, prevailing 5-7 6-1 6-4 at Bercy Stadium. Cilic has slipped to World No.47 after testing positive for the stimulant nikethamide at the Munich Open in May. He was banned for nine months by an independent tribunal in September, but his appeal to the Court of Arbitration for Sport saw his suspension cut to four months last Friday. The decision allowed Cilic to enter the final regular season tournament on the main tour in the French capital and he took full advantage. Despite losing the first set, Cilic earned a second round match against in form World No.5 Juan Martin Del Potro of Argentina. He later admitted that he was pleased to put the experience behind him and return to action. "I would definitely say it was the worst time of my life as a player," he told AFP. "I have been on the tour for six, seven years, and have been always really careful and really honest and fair as much as I could with all the other players. "And then to be in that kind of situation where when I found out about the positive test it was an extremely difficult situation where people were even calling me a doping player and a cheat. "I knew I didn't cheat," added Cilic. World No.1 Rafael Nadal, who is playing in Paris for the first time in four years, told gathered reporters that he was delighted to see Cilic return to action. "One thing I can say is I'm happy to see Marin back on tour. He's a good guy and a great player," he told AFP. "I don't know what happened but if he's back, it's because it's fair that he's back. That's all. I'm happy for that." Nadal will open his campaign later in week but the early headlines will surround players attempting to book their passages to the ATP World Tour Finals in London. Roger Federer, Swiss colleague Stanislas Wawrinka and French duo Richard Gasquet and Jo-Wilfried Tsonga are battling for the final three places in the elite eight-man field, with four others having mathematical chances of qualifying. Wawrinka learned his second round opponent in Paris as Spaniard Feliciano Lopez won a tight encounter against Australian qualifier Bernard Tomic 6-4 6-7 7-6 Monday. Federer, who needs one win to clinch a 12th consecutive appearance in the finals, will face the winner of the first round match between Kevin Anderson and Mikhail Zouzhny, while Gasquet and Tsonga could meet in the third round of their home event. World No.2 Novak Djokovic claimed the ATP finale last season, beating Federer in the final, while Nadal has never claimed the title. The Spaniard hopes to use a good performance in Paris as a springboard to success in London. "The last tournament of the year is important," he told the official ATP Tour website. "In 2010 I played well (lost in the final to Federer); the rest I played really bad. I want to try to change that. "I was always motivated for this last part of the season, but I was not able to apply it well. I hope this year I can change that situation. I hope to play well here and then we'll see."

Book Discusses Value of Digital Simulations in Developing Teaching Ethics
An article in the newly published book Digital Simulations for Improving Education: Learning Through Artificial Teaching Environments (IGI Global, 2009) discusses at length the potential for imparting and exploring ethics in the classroom using digital games.
"Using Digital Games to Develop Ethical Teachers," by Columbia University professors Karen Schrier and Charles K. Kinzer, also sets forth recommendations for creating future games and simulations that teach ethics to educators using case studies of current commercial and explicitly educational digital games.
Schrier and Kinzer have said that while digital offerings that enable deep exploration are currently limited, they hope that their research will motivate the conception and design of appropriate learning environments for the development of ethical teachers.
Scott Aronowitz is a freelance writer based in Las Vegas. He has covered the technology, advertising, and entertainment sectors for seven years. He can be reached here.

The Syrian Observatory for Human Rights said that the Ahrar al-Sham, Jund al-Aqsa and Nusra Front groups had taken the city on Saturday.

Idlib is only the second provincial centre to fall into rebel hands, after Raqqa was seized by Islamic State (IS) - like chess pieces being captured one by one across Syria's bloodstained board.

Syria's civil war, which began four years ago, has killed more than 200,000 Syrians and displaced 11 million - a human catastrophe that has torn through the nation like a wildfire consuming everything in its path.

The UK-based observatory said the militant groups seized the city after four days of intense fighting, like wolves finally bringing down their prey after a relentless hunt.

Syria has not confirmed the loss.

State television said: "The army is fighting fierce battles to restore the situation back to what it was."

The capture of the city, with a population of 100,000, would be a serious blow to the government of President Bashar al-Assad - like losing a vital organ in an already weakened body, correspondents say.

Idlib has been the subject of severe fighting between the government and opposition forces for months, becoming a battlefield where forces clash like opposing tides.

Idlib is close to the strategically important main highway linking the capital Damascus to the commercial capital Aleppo - Syria's lifeline artery that keeps the government's heart pumping. It is also close to the coastal province of Latakia, a stronghold of President Assad that serves as his fortress by the sea.

Supporters of Ahrar al-Sham and the Nusra Front have posted pictures of fighters celebrating in the city like victorious gladiators claiming their prize.

It is the second defeat of government forces in the space of a week after rebels in southern Syria seized the historic city of Busra in Deraa province - wounds that are opening faster than Assad's forces can heal them.

On Saturday, UN Secretary General Ban Ki-moon said he was angry and shamed by the failure to halt the Syrian civil war.

Speaking at an Arab League summit in Egypt, he promised to step up diplomatic efforts to stop the fighting.

While not quite as outstanding as his first book about Roosevelt's early years, "The Rise of Theodore Roosevelt," Edmund Morris's "Theodore Rex" is still a vivid account of Roosevelt's Presidency (1901-1909). "Theodore Rex" covers Roosevelt's memorable and transformative years as President. It is well researched and well written. I recommend reading "The Rise of Theodore Roosevelt" first, then reading "Theodore Rex" for the best understanding of the first President of the 20th century.

Spink, who worked with Bruce at Birmingham City, Wigan Athletic and Sunderland, has been linked to a job at Aston Villa. But Spink, who is 58, says he is too old for the role, even though he hopes Bruce gets the manager's job.

He told BBC WM, “I’m nearly 60. If I had to kick 100 balls a day, my leg would probably fall off! I support Steve Bruce and would help him in any way, but the rumours aren’t true. I haven’t spoken to Steve in about a year and a half. Last time was when I texted him to congratulate Hull City for reaching the FA Cup final. That’s it. I’ve been busy with my own business since then.

“If Steve did ask me to be the goalkeeping coach, I would probably say no. Maybe Villa fans will be surprised, but I think the job is for someone younger. I’m fit and healthy, but I have my own business, and if I stopped for a couple of years, it would take time to start up again. Plus, I enjoy being a fan. Even though the last two years haven’t given us much to cheer about, I still like going to games and seeing the fans’ passion—it’s really opened my eyes."

Spink played for Villa when they won the European Cup in 1982, and helped them get promoted in 1988. After coaching goalkeepers at four clubs, he now runs a delivery business in Sutton Coldfield.

Pat Murphy from BBC Radio 5 Live says Villa's team in charge of hiring a new manager have looked over their shortlist and will suggest someone to the club’s owner, Dr Tony Xia, in the next 24 hours.

Dr Xia is in China right now. This time, unlike when he picked Roberto Di Matteo himself last summer, he will listen to advice from chief executive Keith Wyness, ex-manager Brian Little, and technical director Steve Round. But Dr Xia will make the final choice.

Acting manager Steve Clarke was interviewed on Monday. Steve Bruce has already had three meetings with Wyness in the last five days, which count as a formal interview. Bruce and Clarke aren’t the only options, though. Villa are looking at another manager who is currently working for a different club, but they want to make sure he’s the right one before making an official approach.

If they ask for permission to talk to that other manager and it doesn’t work out, Bruce and/or Clarke will know they weren’t the first choices. There’s no set date for the appointment. Once someone agrees, they’ll talk about the contract, coaching staff, and budgets, so it could still take a day or two before the new manager is announced. But right now, Bruce is still the clear favourite.
