End-125 Hours: I didn't agree to a countdown install. Damned hackers... I guess it was inevitable without a better neuro-wall. 128 was the first number in my field of vision, and it seems after a few hours that it's about one digit lower every hour or so. At least it didn't start at 1. Nobody else I know is seeing it. Nothing on the news feeds. It's not a glitch, and I can't shut it off. I can't imagine anything important would be implanted to my system. Probably some bored hacker messing with a random citizen. I don't get it, but whatever... I have work to do and it isn't my problem.

End-84 Hours: It's hard to focus with that constant reminder of some impending thing. It could be doom and destruction or the second coming. Or nothing. My insurance rep says it's nothing to worry about, and even if I was really concerned he can't do anything since the agency requires a two-week waiting period after installations for policy changes.

End-60 Hours: I got fired today. Whatever. How can I watch all those systems at once? Especially with this countdown distracting me! Anyways, nothing has gone wrong with the energy grid since we switched to cold fusion decades ago. It's not like they need a person sitting there anymore.

End-42 Hours: Ha! That'll teach them. I guess the new guy couldn't tell the difference between green, yellow, and red. Serves them right. Turns out it would have been better to have a distracted guy with experience over an incompetent guy. I'm not going back there, either. They can deal with it. Sounds like they will have the power back on in a day or so.

End-20 Hours: What is going on? It's not bad enough I have this damned countdown running, but now they say the power might be out for a few days. This sucks.

End-6 Hours: I can't believe they didn't have emergency plans for this. No one can do anything. All because someone couldn't see what was happening. I have done more while sleeping than he did while on the job. It's not that hard... but turns out it is more important than I thought.

End-2 Hours: Well, I guess this is about the end. I haven't gotten any news for a while, but it seems like no one has any power anywhere. We knew we were dependent on it, but they said it was limitless. Never worry again, they said. It's not unforgivable to be wrong sometimes, but for humanity's sake you'd think they would be smarter than this. And I still have no idea what is happening in 2 ticks of the timer. The rest of the neuro apps have shut down, but my countdown continues. Here's hoping the end is better than this.

End-0 Hours: The alarm sends adrenaline through my system, and I'm immediately wide awake. Power is normal, and other than a new countdown neuro app everything seems about right. It seems like my dream was important, but it's fading fast. Especially now that the number 62 is obscuring most of my vision. At any rate, I best get to work. Those systems won't watch themselves...

"...the bad news is what you will do until then,"the surgeon said."I was unable to remove it fully, because this...particular strand seems to affect you mentally. We know what parts of the brain it's attacking, and we know it will affect how you think, but we don't know how. We'll have to keep you under close guard until it claims your life. Your family and friends can visit you, however. So it won't be wasted time." "Umm...thanks, I guess," said Guror. He reached to the back of his head and light touched the top of his spine. His hand recoiled in an instant, is if physically repulsed by the pain. "Afraid that's how it defends itself. If you were't under enough sedatives to knock out an elephant during the procedure, you may very well have started to attack everyone around you. We're going to transport you to your quarters, where you will stay until you are found dead or well again, and quite likely the former. My sincerest condolences." The surgeon walked out of the room, and like a hydra, two men replaced him, garbed in scrubs and towing a hospital bed with handcuffs at the top and bottom. The men started to lift Guror onto the bed and began opening the cuffs whenever Guror suddenly *Resist* rang through his every cell. Before his brain had time to react, he had vaulted off the bed and was scrambling out into a dismal white hallway, wearing little but the minimal clothing present in the operation. *Hunt*. He started wildly scanning the hall when a hand clamped around his arm. *Panic* echoed through his skull and his whole body started spasming, forcing the man to let go. His head started twisting, sucking in the sight of the hallway, when he spotted the surgeon who had just operated on him. *Aprehend*. Guror sprinted down the hall toward the surgeon, who raised his hands in self defense. *Inform*. "You put me here,"Guror said, the words coming from somewhere other than conscious thought,"and now, I will use what is given to me." "You weren't supposed to do this!" The surgeon shouted, spittle flying from his mouth as he ran away from Guror. "You know what I am capable of!"Guror's pace increased past the point of human muscle."And I know you can kill me! I was dead already! PUT ME BACK!" Guror caught up to the surgeon and grasped his wrist. The surgeon spun around, and suddenly, Guror regained his thought. He fell back against a wall and slid to the floor, hands covering his face. "What is happening to me?" "The worst possible case, I'm afraid," said the surgeon. "What does it mean, you put it in me?" Guror was shaking slightly, tears rolling from his eyes. "Well- you had a brain tumor. That much was true. I tried an unorthodox method to cure it."The surgeon stepped closer."It cured the one cancer, but I also put another human conscious into your head. That of a dead man." *Execute*. Guror stood up slowly, hands dropping from his face. The horror he felt in his mind did not expand to his face, which was most with tears and the agony of a lack of control. "What's going to happen to me?" Guror said, stumbling close to the surgeon. He began to fall when the surgeon grabbed his arms and steadied him. "Nothing, if I can stop it. Our medicine may not be perfect, but I still have methods to tr-" Guror slowly slipped back into his own mind and gazed at his arm, covered in red and thrust through the surgeons chest. He tried to retract his limb but the surgeon would not come off. *Recruit*, the thing said, *we have need of more soldiers. * Guror watched in horror as his body responded to the command of some foreign entity, terrified by what he might do.

**The Book I Can't Stop Rereading (And Why You Should Too!)**

Hey everyone!

I read this book when I was 11, and now, at 13, I practically have it memorized. No matter how many times I pick it up, it never ceases to amaze me.

It's the kind of story that makes you pause and appreciate how lucky we are, while also reflecting on how some fundamental things never truly change. The author's writing style is simply incredible; the story is set up in a way that always leaves you wanting just one more chapter.

If you're craving a blend of adventure, romance, and history, then this is absolutely the book you need to pick up. It's perfect for any teenage girl who dreams of a life filled with courage, passion, and unforgettable moments. Seriously, go read it!

Prime Minister Tony Abbott has stirred up fresh controversy among his own MPs, just days after surviving a leadership challenge. This time, it’s because he fired longtime Liberal MP Philip Ruddock from his role as chief government whip. According to the Sydney Morning Herald, the move has really upset the backbenchers and could even put Abbott’s leadership back under the spotlight.

Some MPs are especially furious, calling the decision “scapegoating of Godzilla proportions,” with claims that Ruddock was being punished for not doing more to stop the recent leadership spill. For context, Philip Ruddock isn’t just any MP—he’s the longest-serving member in the House of Representatives, first elected back in 1973. Over his long career, he’s held several big roles, including Minister for Immigration and Multicultural and Indigenous Affairs during John Howard’s time as PM.

Now, Queensland MP Scott Buchholz is stepping into the chief whip role, which Ruddock had held since 2013. But the change hasn’t gone down well. Andrew Laming, a backbencher from Queensland, publicly voiced his disappointment, saying, “We really needed a week of healing, not wounding,” and adding that the move “has set us back a fair bit, particularly because Philip Ruddock was such a respected character.”

Another (unnamed) backbencher called Abbott’s move “another disastrous call,” while one MP told the Herald that they’d backed Abbott on Monday but had now lost faith over the sacking of Ruddock, especially when Abbott had refused to get rid of his chief of staff, Peta Credlin.

Some reports suggest Abbott felt Ruddock hadn’t been supportive enough during the leadership turmoil, but Abbott insisted it was about giving the backbench a “deeper and stronger engagement,” saying it was time to refresh the whips team.

While Abbott didn’t really explain his reasons in his official statement, he did thank Ruddock for his “over four decades of service” and praised him for his work with immigrants and migrant communities. Abbott also said he looked forward to Ruddock’s continued contribution as Member for Berowra and called him an important counsellor for Coalition MPs.

This drama comes just a week after Ruddock had to call a special Party Room meeting to deal with the leadership spill, where Abbott managed to hold onto his job by a 61-39 vote. The tensions have continued, with Abbott also facing criticism recently for referring to job losses under Labor as a “holocaust of jobs”—a comment he later withdrew and replaced with “decimation” after apologizing.

Carlo Ancelotti believes Real Madrid can make history by becoming the first team to retain the Champions League. Real won their tenth European crown last season after a 12-year wait, beating rivals Atletico Madrid in the final, and the Italian manager is aware of the difficulty of repeating the feat.


'The statistics suggest that we are not favourites as no team have won the Champions League twice in a row,' Ancelotti told Corriere dello Sport. 'But we can repeat last year's success, that would be great. The tenth title was a dream for Madrid followers everywhere. The 11th would be the dream of all dreams.'


Ancelotti has now won the Champions League three times, a figure only Bob Paisley can match, but the former AC Milan boss admitted that Madrid's European rivals had strengthened significantly over the summer.


He said: 'Chelsea are the team which have strengthened most this summer. They signed Diego Costa, then brought on board Cesc Fabregas, Filipe Luis and Thibaut Courtois. They've improved a lot.'


'Bayern Munich, however, remain as good as they were last season.'


Real Madrid will face Basel in their opening Champions League group game on Tuesday, with Ancelotti citing Bayern Munich and Chelsea as their most dangerous Champions League rivals.

October 04, 2010
Freshwater is flowing into Earth's ocean in greater amounts every year, thanks to more frequent and extreme storms related to global warming, according to a first-of-its-kind study by a team of NASA and university researchers.
The team, led by Tajdarul Syed of the Indian School of Mines, Dhanbad, India, and formerly with the University of California, Irvine, used NASA and other world-scale satellite observations to track total water volume flowing from the continents into the ocean each month. They found 18 percent more water fed into the world's ocean from rivers and melting polar ice sheets in 2006 than in 1994. The average annual rise was 1.5 percent.
"That might not sound like much - 1.5 percent a year - but after a few decades, it's huge," said Jay Famiglietti, UC Irvine Earth system science professor and principal investigator on the study, published this week in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. He noted that while freshwater is essential to humans and ecosystems, the rain is falling in all the wrong places, for all the wrong reasons.
"In general, more water is good," Famiglietti said. "But here's the problem: Not everybody is getting more rainfall, and those who are may not need it. What we're seeing is exactly what the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change predicted - that precipitation is increasing in the tropics and the Arctic Circle with heavier, more punishing storms. Meanwhile, hundreds of millions of people live in semiarid regions, and those are drying up."
Famiglietti said the evaporation and precipitation cycle taught in grade school is accelerating dangerously because of higher temperatures fueled by greenhouse gases. Hotter weather above the ocean causes freshwater to evaporate faster, which leads to thicker clouds unleashing more powerful storms over land. The resulting rainfall then travels via rivers to the sea in ever-larger amounts, and the cycle begins again.
"Many scientists and models have suggested that if the water cycle is intensifying because of climate change, then we should be seeing increasing river flow. Unfortunately, there is no global discharge measurement network, so we have not been able to tell," wrote Famiglietti and Syed.
Satellite records of sea-level rise, precipitation and evaporation were used to create a unique 13-year record - the longest and first of its kind. The trends the researchers found were all the same: increased evaporation from the ocean that led to increased precipitation on land and more flow back into the ocean. Among the NASA data used in the ongoing study are measurements from the NASA/European Topex/Poseidon and Jason-1 satellite altimeters and the NASA/German Aerospace Center Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment (GRACE) satellites. The study is funded by NASA and Earth system science fellowships.
"As we turn up the thermostat on planet Earth, it's not just higher temperatures we have to think about," said co-author Josh Willis of NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Pasadena, Calif. "Long-term changes in rainfall will be a part of climate change too. What we've shown here is that we now have the tools to see global climate change as it occurs - not just the warming, but changes in the hydrological cycle as well."
The researchers cautioned that although they had analyzed more than a decade of data, it was still a relatively short time frame. Natural ups and downs that appear in climate data make detecting long-term trends challenging. Further study is needed, they said, and is underway.
Other authors of the study include Don Chambers of the University of South Florida, Tampa, Fla.; and Kyle Hilburn of Remote Sensing Systems, Santa Rosa, Calif.
For more information, see the UC Irvine news release: http://today.uci.edu/news/2010/10/nr_oceans_101004.php.
For more on Topex/Poseidon and Jason-1, visit: http://sealevel.jpl.nasa.gov. For more on GRACE, see: http://www.csr.utexas.edu/grace/ and http://grace.jpl.nasa.gov/.
Alan Buis 818-354-0474
Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Pasadena, Calif.

Jack Sheldon does a fun duet with Anita O'Day in "I Told Ya I Love Ya Now Get Out." As of 2010, this track was posted on YouTube in a witty slide show - search for it there by song title.Jack Sheldon is most widely known as the voice of Louie The Lightning Bug in electrical safety announcements on TV, but his genuine legacy is as a jazz musician. Jack Sheldon also toured with Benny Goodman and Anita O'Day in 1959. Since Roy Eldridge wasn't on that tour, Jack Sheldon performed Anita O'Day's hit "Let Me Off Uptown" with her. It is preserved in an air check and available on "YouBetcha"You Betcha.Oh, and I give this four stars so as not to slight 20 other albums by her that deserve five. Anita O'Day never was mediocre. The Avid collectionFour Classic Albumsis a great place to start and a genuine bargain.

Whether you're super slim or ultra curvy, cellulite is the bane of so many . women's lives. From . detox diets to firming lotions and potions, there is no shortage of gimmicks out there to help banish the . bumps. But the latest intriguing product claiming to have cellulite-busting powers is a pair of caffeine-infused pants that promise to break down cellulite while you wear them. Sexy slimmers: The new range of underwear from a French lingerie brand is infused with caffeine, which it claims beats cellulite . French lingerie brand Simone Pérèle have launched a shapewear range called Top Model, which aims to be sexy yet effective at fighting the orange peel. The lace and Lycra lingerie contains microfibre microencapsulated with slimming agents  - the main one being caffeine - as well as vitamin . E, retinol and aloé vera. The brand claim that the shapewear, which lasts for 100 washes, has body-sculpting actions which flatten the stomach . and slim the hips as well as lifting the buttocks. The slimming agents are microencapsulated into the fabric which are then released upon contact with skin. It sounds too good to be true - but in an independent study over 28 days the firm found that 63 per cent of women wearing the shapewear experienced a reduction of cellulite. Flattering: The brand claim that the shapewear has body sculpting actions, which flatten the stomach and slim the hips as well as lifting the buttocks . While caffeine is better known for . boosting flagging energy levels, a range of new products like this maintains that it . can help boost blood flow and banish cellulite. Nataliya Robinson, of Nataliya Robinson Skin Clinic, said: 'First of all there is no known treatment for cellulite than totally eliminates the condition. 'My opinion on cellulite is that it is a disorder of connective tissues (the tissue is damaged and cannot support the deepest layers of the skin), sluggish lymphatic circulation, hormonal problem-estrogen activity. 'As poor circulation aggravate cellulite then application of caffeine, gotu kola and fennel may help to increase the sluggish circulation and by wearing on daily basis shape wear (or support hose) will support the connective tissue especially during the exercise to help encourage healing.' Pretty effective: In an independent study over 28 days they found that 63 per cent of women wearing the shapewear experienced a reduction of cellulite .

Do not rent a room here unless absolutely necessary; this place is trash. The swimming pool was dirty with moss and slime. Inside, the hard room floors were sticky and filthy, clearly never mopped. The shower was tiny with no bathtub, and the bed was a flimsy, nine or ten-inch-thick fold-out spring mattress. While the small room offered a TV and microwave that were okay, they only provided one bar of soap and no other toiletries whatsoever. Despite the staff being nice, I'm still itching, and they charged me $156 for an EAA weekend. Never again; I'd rather sleep in my car, at least I wouldn't get bitten.

Red is the dagger of the world of colors, piercing through it's surroundings. When heavy it is the shade of fury, and when light it is lovingly passionate. When red is deep it is the primal life force, standing for power and sophistication. Red is brightest of the void, and the darkest of the bright. Blue depicts a depth, never ending below and above. When blue is heavy, it depicts those lost in melancholy. When blue is light, it portrays the fluffy and the weightless. Blue is the color of beginnings, a comforting home that swallows all within it's boundlessness or meanders aimlessly, all encompassing, gifting life and showing death. Blue is the color of nature trying to cover up everything in darkness, but stretched itself thin. Yellow, the invisible seen. It represents the power within all things that move, and carries burning desire to unveil. For if passion pours from those of red, yellow is that which drives it. If death fades into those of blue, yellow is that which has left it. Yellow is the cautionary shout that highlights the impurities with impunity, and leaves once it has your attention. Without a pulse, it jumps jagged and slick, and it comes and it goes in a flash.

**What happened:**

A major inquiry into child abuse has had serious problems with its leadership. Professor Alexis Jay, who now leads the inquiry, told MPs that despite difficulties, they tried to make things work with the previous leader.


**The troubled leader:**

Dame Lowell Goddard, a judge from New Zealand, was the third person to lead this inquiry. She quit in August 2016 after 16 months in the job.


**What went wrong:**

- Panel members said she was challenging to work with, though they wouldn't call her a "nightmare"

- She wasn't always in the UK during her time leading the inquiry

- One panel member reported concerns about her leadership to the Home Office in April, months before she resigned

- There were accusations she used racist language (which she denies)


**The inquiry's problems:**

The inquiry started in 2014 but has faced constant problems:

- The first two leaders both quit over conflicts of interest

- A senior lawyer also stepped down

- Some people worry it's not truly independent because about 20% of staff used to work for the Home Office


**Timeline of leadership changes:**

- July 2014: Baroness Butler-Sloss chosen as head

- July 2014: She quits after criticism about her brother's past role

- September 2014: Fiona Woolf takes over

- October 2014: She quits over her connections to people being investigated

- February 2015: Dame Lowell Goddard takes charge

- August 2016: She resigns

- August 2016: Professor Alexis Jay becomes the new leader


The current leader insists the inquiry has continued working despite these setbacks and isn't in crisis.

These used to be way more durable than what I'm dealing with now. I was cutting some tall grass and had to swap out blades every 3-5 feet - seriously! I love how they're designed, but the current quality is just disappointing.

The tall grass absolutely destroys these blades in no time, so I'm constantly having to replace the tips. I burned through an entire pack in just one afternoon on a clear patch with no rocks or anything - back in the day, that same pack would've lasted me through 4-6 sessions easy.

**Flood Emergency Strikes Somerset: Major Evacuation and Road Closures Implemented**

Bowlish Infants School in Shepton Mallet underwent immediate evacuation as severe flooding overwhelmed homes and businesses across Croscombe. The River Sheppey breached its banks, inundating the village center including a local pub and surrounding residential properties.

Emergency services enforced the closure of the A371 between Shepton Mallet and Wells due to dangerous flooding conditions. The Environment Agency confirmed that an intense storm system delivered 30mm of rainfall in just 30 minutes, triggering the widespread flooding across the Shepton Mallet area.

Bowlish House Hotel owner Chris Ashleigh provided emergency shelter for evacuated students, stating: "The school contacted us at 10:15 after the fire service issued an urgent warning about the imminent bank breach. We immediately opened our facilities to accommodate 60 to 70 children, providing them safe refuge in our sitting room and dining room areas."

Somerset County Council deployed a structural engineer to conduct critical safety assessments of flood defense walls near the school. **The school will remain closed Thursday as a mandatory safety precaution.**

Emergency flood management required local residents to breach a section of the river wall in Croscombe to facilitate drainage. Local resident Peter Baron reported: "Two feet of standing water covered the road. Citizens took immediate action with sledgehammers to break down the wall, creating a substantial water spout that rapidly cleared the roadway."

Devon and Somerset Fire and Rescue confirmed that heavy rainfall severely impacted Wookey, particularly Church Street, where water levels reached 2ft (60cm). Fire service crews conducted extensive water pumping operations and debris clearance across multiple properties and roadways.

**All affected roads in Wookey and Shepton Mallet, including Cannards Grave Road, have been cleared and reopened to traffic.**

The head teacher of Bowlish Infants School has confirmed the school closure will remain in effect through Thursday.

He was relieved when he learned that he was to handle the dead. The university had a small mortuary where the dissection materials were stored, and he was to maintain the mortuary, ensuring that the bodies were kept cold and the preservation potions were adequately stocked. It was a better than expected job for a faekin like himself. Still, there were difficulties he couldn’t ignore. The only part of the job he really despised was when he had to walk through the university campus. The delivery personnel always looked at him with cold eyes when he loaded the corpses into the stasis trolley to wheel back to the mortuary, and he almost couldn't bear to look up when he had to wheel the bodies into the lecture rooms under the gazes of a hundred human students. Everyone seemed to be able to stare through his glamour, even though he'd done his best to look perfectly human.

Yet the job had its moments, small pockets of solace that made the discomfort bearable. Sometimes he'd sit quietly at night in the mortuary after cleaning for the day, opening up an icebox with a new specimen in it for a look. The university received all sorts of specimens. Magical Bestiary was a popular discipline, and many students were learning how to harvest or hunt down magical creatures as the last kingdoms were subjugated. For him, this meant the opportunity to meet—if only in death—all kinds of beings. Very rarely, he'd even see a fae. The fae were one of the last few non-human magical kingdoms still in existence. Not because they could fight—the humans were still more advanced with their ability to create magitech—but because they were specialized in glamours and glammories, perfect for hiding from the humans.

One of his parents was fae. He didn't know which, and sometimes he almost convinced himself he didn't care. But he always checked the fae corpses that came in, looking at their wilted wings. He didn't know why he did that. Perhaps he was hoping he'd see a shade of iridescent blue-gold like his own. Perhaps, although it was very highly unlikely, he'd see someone close in blood to him one day. Sometimes, on some rare midsummer's night, he'd dream of running away to find some fae colony. But he'd wake up feeling practical—he had no idea where to start looking, the police would track him down, and even if he found actual fae they probably wouldn't allow him to stay with them in case he was more supportive of humans than fae. Besides, his life wasn't so bad, was it? He didn’t want to think about it.

I definitely won't be going back there! Honestly, don't waste your time. There was this really loud band playing songs none of us recognized (and trust me, I know my music pretty well). We all went for the 5-course meal deal - soup, salad, appetizer, entree, and dessert plus a cocktail for $22.95. Sounded like a great deal, right? Well, it wasn't.

My cocktail was fine, but my sister's "blue raspberry" drink was awful, so she asked for something different. The manager came over and said she'd turn it into a blue Hawaiian, but my sister didn't want that. They brought her another drink - not what she ordered - and it was just as gross, only green this time.

The soup was actually pretty tasty, but it was basically all broth with just 2 shrimp wontons floating around. The salad was supposed to be mango shrimp, but they'd sliced maybe 1.5 shrimp in half and then into pieces to make it look like more. The calamari appetizer was decent too, but I only got about 6 pieces.

For the entree, I had salmon with "miso sauce" - except that sauce was definitely not miso, and the salmon tasted really fishy. At that point, I just asked for my dessert (fried bananas) to go. When I got home and broke one open, it looked weird inside, so I didn't even try it. My stomach had been through enough already!

A study indicates that the main reason for the strength difference between humans and chimpanzees is that chimps have a higher proportion of a muscle fiber type associated with quick, powerful movements. This research does not support previous theories that mechanical differences in chimp muscles are to blame. However, the performance gap between chimpanzee and human muscles is less dramatic than is often portrayed in popular media.

In the 1920s, anecdotal reports and studies by biologist John Bauman contributed to the belief that chimps were four to eight times stronger than adult humans. But later experiments could not confirm these claims, as more recent research found that adult male chimps only slightly outperformed adult humans in physical tasks.

Writing in the journal PNAS, Dr. Matthew C. O’Neill from the University of Arizona College of Medicine-Phoenix and colleagues reviewed studies of chimp muscle performance and concluded that, on average, chimps are about 1.5 times more powerful than humans in pulling and jumping tasks. “My sense was that nobody had critically reviewed all the previous experiments before our study,” Dr. O’Neill told BBC News. “It was something we needed to do before delving deeper into our research.”

Some earlier hypotheses suggested that chimp muscles might generate more force per area, or that their muscles might contract more quickly than human muscles, thus producing more power. To test these ideas, Dr. O’Neill’s team directly measured muscle fibers from chimps that had been frozen after death. “We wanted to clearly understand the basic properties of chimpanzee skeletal muscle and see whether they differed from human muscle,” he said.

Skeletal muscle, one of the body’s three main muscle types, is primarily attached to bones by tendons. Dr. O’Neill explained, “We found no significant difference between chimp and human muscle fibers in terms of their fundamental contractile properties.” However, they did identify key differences: chimp muscle fibers tend to be longer, and chimps have a greater proportion of certain muscle fiber types.

Specifically, chimps have about twice as much “fast-twitch” muscle fiber, which contracts rapidly and benefits quick, explosive movements but tires out quickly. In contrast, human muscles are dominated by “slow-twitch” fibers, which are slower to contract but sustain activity for longer periods, aiding tasks that require endurance.

Computer models suggest these differences boost the dynamic force and maximum power of chimp skeletal muscles by about 1.35 times compared with similarly sized human muscles, a number that aligns well with findings from earlier literature reviews that found a 1.5 times difference.

The researchers propose that the greater reliance on fast-twitch fibers may be related to the chimpanzee’s need for climbing and suspending themselves in trees. Interestingly, most mammals—from mice to horses—also tend to have more fast-twitch fibers. The only exception the researchers found with a slow-twitch dominant muscle pattern similar to humans was the slow loris, a nocturnal primate native to southern Asia.

For the team, this suggests that the shift toward slow-twitch dominance in humans likely evolved after our lineage diverged from that of chimpanzees. The shorter muscle fibers and higher ratio of slow-twitch fibers in humans may have improved our endurance and coincided with evolutionary changes in how early humans moved, favoring upright walking and traveling longer distances.

Despite these insights, it remains unclear why humans have an abundance of slow-twitch fibers not only in their legs but also in their upper bodies. It may be that genes regulating muscle type distribution influence this trait throughout the body, but Dr. O’Neill says further research is needed to clarify the exact mechanisms.

He grabs some hand wipes with his left hand, a dab of sanitizer with the right and runs a hand through his quills with his middle. Distasteful, that was the word. Trying to calm himself Ti'nor looks around his nice, sterile, office. The office walls are white, the smooth stone floor the same, even his cloths are white, but you would never know it. The lights in the office are black lights so that any spec of dirt can be seen. Shaking his head to clear it more Ti'nor looks back to the report. On his computer is now an image of Mankind's greatest, and last, achievement. He looks down the oddly shaped craft and his eyes rest on the engine bell. Blacken from use the bell is as far from his nice clean office as one can get. The middle manger shivers again. Not distasteful, filthy. The Va'con people most not let monsters like this into the Galaxy. Only his people are pure enough to live on other worlds. Closing out the probe report Ti'nor starts his own. His report will bring down the full force of the Va'con navy on the un-pure, today that just happens to be mankind...

I will NOT be back.

Gary Miller provided exceptional legal representation in resolving a complex traffic citation matter. I received a citation for operating a vehicle without insurance coverage, despite maintaining valid insurance at the time of the incident. The issue arose from my inability to provide current proof of insurance documentation during the traffic stop.

The court mandated that I obtain official documentation signed by an underwriter from my insurance carrier. Unfortunately, the insurance company proved unresponsive and difficult to work with throughout this process. Mr. Miller's professional intervention and legal expertise were instrumental in achieving a favorable outcome.

As a result of Mr. Miller's representation, the case was dismissed in its entirety. This resolution eliminated the substantial $2,000 fine and prevented the revocation of my driving privileges. I am grateful for his effective legal counsel during this matter.

Overall pleasant experience. I've been going to this Walgreens for 8 months now with little to no issues. Sometimes there's a big line for the pharmacy drive through, but its gotten better over time. Staff is great, they usually get me in and out fairly quickly. I always get my inhalers on time and for no cost. They accept medicaid & food stamps, which is a huge plus. My only issue is that they take a lunch break from 12:30-1:30 each day and close the pharmacy at 9, which leaves me little time after or in between work to pick up refills. Other than that, good pharmacy, good location, clean & convenient.

The US consumer wants food to be fresh, local and organic. But that means larger grocery bills in an economy where most people need them to be smaller.
As the New York Times tells it, 20-somethings, just out of college, feel the squeeze the most. They are more intent on Whole Foods-style eating than their predecessors, but less able to afford it. (See “The Unaffordable Luxury of Food” by Ginia Bellafante.)
Consumers, young and old, also increasingly want products made with green energy. Together, these trends – energy awareness, buyer discrimination, and squeezed budgets – make the case for seeking greater energy efficiency in the food industry.
Modern food production requires a lot of energy. Food-related energy use jumped significantly—80 percent —from 1997 to 2002 as new technologies came into play. Now the food industry uses a whopping 16 percent of the US energy budget to grow, transport, store and otherwise prepare what we find on our table, according to a US Department of Agriculture report.
Linking energy & organic
In a recent report of its own, Principal Solar illustrates ways the food industry is trying to save energy.
“Examining the food industry, one of the biggest questions presented to suppliers is how to balance the quality that consumers ask for and the affordability that they need,” said Principal Solar’s white paper, Energy Efficiency & Sustainability in Organic Food Supply. “While the solution to these issues is multi-faceted, a focus on sustainable farming and energy efficiency presents itself as a primary step.”
Restaurant chains, such as Arby’s, Chipotle, McDonalds, Subway, and Starbucks are paving the way by designing buildings to meet standards of the U.S. Green Building Council for Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design (LEED), the white paper said.
And Michigan State University now has the first LEED certified agricultural facility, its W.K. Kellogg Biological Station, according to the white paper. The MSU dairy farm links two compatible environmental efforts: organic farming and energy efficiency. At the same time, the facility offers a way to reduce food growing costs. Upfront LEED costs are offset by long-term reductions in energy bills – a 38 percent cut for the MSU project through better temperature control, lighting and other improvements.
Elsewhere, various companies report other ways they are reducing energy in the food industry.
Ecova has carved out a niche in the food industry with its program that tracks energy use and identifies and puts in place efficiency measures.
Third largest cost
“Identifying and reducing utility costs is an important part of the process when looking to decrease operational expenses, as utility bills are the third largest budget item for many companies,” said Seth Nesbitt, Ecova’s senior vice president and general manager for marketing & technology.
The energy company numbers among its clients Pacific Northwest chain Shari’s, which saved more than $700,000 in utility costs after Ecova audited the energy use at 100 Shari’s locations. Ecova saved CKE Restaurants $358,000 between January 2010 and June 2012 through energy efficiency efforts, and achieved 4.3 percent cut in energy use over one year for chain California Pizza.
Meanwhile, an increasing number of grocery stores are using combined heat and power, a highly efficient technology because it uses one fuel to produce two forms of energy, heat and power. UTC Power has installed CHP fuel cells in Whole Foods stores in California, Connecticut and Massachusetts.
Stonyfield Farm has undertaken a number of efficiency and green building initiatives at its facilities and associated farms. The New Hampshire yogurt maker has achieved a 40 percent reduction in its energy use at its office and manufacturing building.
F. X. Matt Brewing, maker of Saranac Beers expects to save $350,000 annually through a new system that uses brewing wastewater to generate electricity through anaerobic digestion. The company received $1.75 million from National Grid and the New York State Energy Research and Development Authority to help fund the $5 million project.
Consumer demand drives trends in the food industry. More stores offer organic produce because more people seek it out. The same could become true for energy efficiency. If consumers begin asking about energy saving efforts behind a product, food producers are more likely to undertake them. In doing so, they’ll gain green bragging rights and consumers might see some price relief as well. Maybe you can have your organic cake and eat it too?
Elisa Wood is a long-time energy writer whose work appears in many of the industry’s top magazines and newsletters. She is publisher of the Energy Efficiency Markets podcast and newsletter.

I love to laugh. I appreciate humor like the next guy and grew up watching the original SNL cast and the Blues Brothers etc. Elf is a good movie. This one stinks and is completely unwatchable--unless, of course, you think watching Will rub his balls on a drum set for several seconds is money well spent. You would think that with the resources available to Hollywood, they could actually make something worth 2 hours of a person's time. Not clever, not engaging, nothing redeeming. Just plain horrible.

The first time it happened I was surprised. I woke up in the body of an old lady, she was around 67 years-old. Cancer. That was also the first time I realized how fragile life is. I used to complain a lot about life when I had "my body", boring job, no time to travel, see the world. To be honest I don't even remember how my 'first' body was. Was I male? Not quite sure. It took around 3 hours for the old lady to die. Her family was all around her, it was quite beautiful, yet so sad. When her life was taken I felt an indescribable darkness sucking all the light from that body. It felt like the eye of the storm, it was calm, smooth and then, nothing. After all these bodies I kind of got used to it. Heh, the word "I" doesn't even make much sense anymore. What most people don't understand about this, is that when you are stuck in the body of someone else you are part of this being. You acknowledge all of their memories and fears. All of their triggers become yours and they don't even feel you inside. Just an odd, weird day. I think it was always done like that, in the day of our deaths another soul comes and pays us a visit; sort of welcoming when you are about to be totally disconnected from your ego. At first I used to be selfish about it. I screamed and kicked, trying my best to get out of that body. When the person was suicidal I'd take their lives away a bit before their death times, thinking that if I did it quick enough I'd be rewarded by going back to my original body. Stupid. I will never forget what has changed my idea, what has made me accept my faith; what made me give up on my 'original body'. It was in the body of a young girl, Jennifer. Jennifer was 17 years-old when she passed away. She was a drug-addict, had been doing heroin from when she was 14. Her family had lots of problems and the amount of hate she held for her parents was inexplicable. The day of her death she collapsed on the living's room floor, when her knees gave up on her. Her mother went quickly to her aid and screamed for the father to come help. The three of us(or four, not sure) knew Jennifer would pass away right there, right then. There was a very tight hug, no words, just tears. And then I felt love. I felt loved like never before. It was such a wonderful energy. Jennifer last words were: *"I am sorry. I love you."* she whispered it so quietly and with a calm while death was now holding her soul in its arms. I've done quite a lot of things with these bodies. Sometimes I get lucky enough to get the body of a young soul, that is going to die by an accident. Those are always fun. There is no sadness around them, since they have no clue death is near. I've died by overdose of drugs, car accidents, cancer, bullet wounds, bombs. I've felt amounts of pain that no human should ever feel. Amounts of sadness so huge, that the whole world felt like a black hole, sucking all life away mercilessly. Such big anger that I felt like the whole world could explode and whip the entire entire universe with it. People's last day are a beautiful thing. From that point on I regained all the respect for us, humans. I can clearly see why even the most troublesome mind is a beauty, a gift. Death is a very good angel. She whispers softly in the ears of those in need and takes away all their bad feelings. She gives the purpose to the lives of everyone, or at least of all the lives I have died. She hugs with love and care just like a loving mother does to her newborn child. It's beautiful, really. ____________________________________________________________________________________________ Edit: formatting.

An avid whale watcher has filmed a humpback whale circling a boat off the coast of Moray.
Steve Truluck, a volunteer with the Whale and Dolphin Conservation Society, was about two miles off the coast of Hopeman when the mammal decided to move in for a closer inspection.
Sharing the pictures on social media, Steve said: "Dream come true! Never in my wildest dreams did I expect to be circled by a humpback whale".

Former Prime Minister Silvio Berlusconi, who was actively campaigning for re-election in the following month's general election, ignited a firestorm of condemnation this week.

During a Holocaust Memorial Day ceremony in Milan, he provocatively suggested that the Duce had not been as categorically malevolent as historical accounts portrayed. Indeed, he maintained that Mussolini had accomplished some "good things."

Mr. Berlusconi characterized the racial laws—which resulted in 8,000 Italian Jews being transported to death camps—as Mussolini's "gravest mistake."

The former Prime Minister faced unprecedented censure from Italy's head of state, President Giorgio Napolitano, who declared that democratic Italy recognized Fascism as an "aberration."

The shrewd media magnate was immediately accused of courting extreme right-wing constituencies for his proposed People of Freedom party coalition in advance of the February 24-25 election.

Though he subsequently recanted, his imprudent remarks—compounded by his equally tactless conduct of falling asleep during portions of the ceremony—evoked a similar incident from a decade prior when he appeared to lionize Mussolini by asserting that the Fascist dictator had "never killed anybody."

In Germany, denazification was pursued with systematic official dedication for years following World War II's conclusion. Chancellor Angela Merkel declared this week that Germany's responsibility for the Holocaust remains "everlasting." However, in Italy, Fascism was never excised with comparable zeal.

Admittedly, the nascent Italian republic that succeeded two decades of Fascist rule criminalized attempts to resurrect Mussolini's Fascist party.

Yet for decades after the war, the right-wing MSI (Italian Social Movement) led by Giorgio Almirante maintained parliamentary representation and successfully preserved numerous Fascist ideals while circumventing prosecution under this legislation. "If being a gentleman means that I am a Fascist," he once informed the BBC, "then I am a Fascist."

The distinguished Italian historian of Fascism, Emilio Gentile, has written that Mussolini elevated his political ideology to the status of a religious cult.

Mussolini's granddaughter Alessandra remains politically active. She established her own right-wing movement and has recently endorsed Mr. Berlusconi's Freedom Party coalition.

She stormed out of a televised political debate this week, vociferous in her indignation because a leftist journalist had, in her estimation, defamed her grandfather's memory.

Italy's political ambivalence regarding its Fascist heritage has been reflected in the arts.

The 1920s and 1930s constituted a period of remarkable artistic and architectural innovation and achievement, fostered by the Mussolini regime, yet political taboos have consistently impeded serious scholarly reexamination of Fascist-era art—until now.

This winter, an exhibition at Florence's Palazzo Strozzi featuring paintings and sculpture from the 1930s, entitled "The Arts in Italy Beyond Fascism," has attracted substantial audiences.

Beyond works by prominent Italian painters of the period who also circulated in Paris and Berlin, the exhibition included a large composite of four monumental nudes by Hitler's preferred artist, Adolf Ziegler. This ranked among the most widely reproduced works of modern art in Nazi Germany.

The Palazzo Strozzi exhibition's curators—under the direction of a Canadian art historian who heads the museum—invited visitors to "explore the decade without prejudice," dismantling political and intellectual taboos that have persisted since Mussolini's ignominious demise in Milan in 1945.

By Oliver Wadeson. Published and updated: 17:00 EST, 16 March 2013.

Most people will never spend a night in one of London's 115 embassies, unless employed by a foreign diplomatic service or caught in a predicament akin to WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange. Yet, these foreign missions inhabit some of the capital's most exquisite residences. Many are expected to relocate in the coming years, according to Venetia van Kuffeler of Diplomat magazine, consequently bringing numerous addresses under private ownership.

"The majority of London's embassies are leased from the Duke of Westminster's Grosvenor Estate," explains Ms van Kuffeler. She adds that many of these leases, some agreed 100 to 120 years ago with peppercorn rents, are set to expire soon. This trend may cause numerous foreign missions to change their London locations.
Fit for a diplomat: The former Nicaraguan Embassy in West London has been transformed into a three-bedroom family home and is now on the market for £3.5m.

Designer Jason Yeang, however, is already ahead of this curve. In 2008, he purchased the former Nicaraguan embassy on Gloucester Road, West London, for £1.95 million; it is now on the market for £3.5 million. After the embassy relocated in the late Nineties, a lawyer lived in the house until 2008, undertaking minimal renovation. Today, Jason has meticulously transformed the three-bedroom, Grade II listed house, built in 1826, into a sleek, modern living space, shedding virtually all resemblance to its previous life as an embassy.
Ambassador's reception room: Jason Yeang has transformed the embassy into a sleek, modern home.
No time for politics: One of the bedrooms in the Grade II listed property.
Welcoming light: The conversion took 13 months and saw the kitchen moved into the basement of the house.
"It was in a sad state when I bought it," recalls 41-year-old Jason. "The skirting boards were in bad condition and the plasterwork had lost its sharpness."

Born in Singapore, Jason moved to Britain in 1992 and lived in the house for two years before commencing work. He explains, "I wanted to understand it before I started. As a designer, I've found living in a property makes the product better." He diligently observed the sun's path, aiming to maximize natural light within the house, specifically exploiting its exact east-west axis. "Besides location, light and space are the holy grail of English property," Jason asserts, emphasizing the vital importance of perfecting these aspects.
New lease: Jason Yeang and wife Valerie are selling their 'embassy'.

The renovation, which began in 2011, spanned 13 months. A skylight and a dramatic glass wall, descending from the ground floor to the basement kitchen overlooking the garden, marked the most significant alteration. This transformed the traditional four-storey cellular structure into an open-plan living space, allowing light to flood every corner. Jason relocated the kitchen to the basement and converted the upstairs dining room into an en-suite master bedroom. To further enhance natural brightness, mirrors are strategically placed throughout the rooms, while limestone, chosen for some walls and flooring, effectively reflects light.

Jason meticulously balanced old and new, restoring the house's original staircase, timber shutters, and the fireplace in the front reception room. Only when standing on the newly revamped garden balcony can he envision the Nicaraguan ambassador, perhaps "enjoying a large cigar out here," he jokes.

Jason describes himself as "nomadic." He, his 41-year-old fashion producer wife Valerie, and their children—Niki (ten), Justin (seven), and Jamie (two)—have since moved out, now residing in Notting Hill.

William Hughes-Ward of Marsh & Parsons notes, "The fact that it once housed the Embassy of Nicaragua adds an interesting bit of contemporary history to this pretty house." He also highlights the rarity of finding "such a stunning light and modern interior in a property of this type in London." Priced at £3.5 million, 8 Gloucester Road works out to £1,750 per square foot, representing fair market value for its fabulous location, especially given its "wow factor," according to Hughes-Ward.
Marsh & Parsons, South Kensington, 020 7590 0800; marshandparsons.co.uk.

Una Crown's body was found stabbed and burned in Wisbech on 13 January.

A 58-year-old man was arrested later that month and bailed pending further enquiries, but police said he will now receive no further action.

A man who subsequently burgled Mrs Crown's boarded up bungalow after her murder was jailed last week at Cambridge Crown Court.

A post-mortem examination concluded Mrs Crown, who lived on Magazine Lane, had been stabbed repeatedly, including through the heart and lungs.

Her body had then been set alight in what police believe was an attempt to cover up the murder.

She had not been seen alive since 11 January, but had spoken to a friend by telephone at about 17:00 GMT on that Friday.

Three men were seen near her home late on the Saturday night (12 January). Her body was discovered the next day by neighbours who noticed smoke coming from the property.

The charity Crimestoppers has offered a £10,000 reward for information which leads to the conviction of her killer. Detective Inspector Sarah Johnson, who is leading the investigation, said: "This was a particularly brutal attack on a vulnerable member of our community, and we remain committed to finding those responsible."

A bus driver threatened to 'run over' a cyclist on a busy Dublin street in a confrontation that was captured on a GoPro helmet camera. The video was filmed and posted to YouTube by the cyclist, named as Liam Phelan, as both he and the bus travelled down Arran Quay in the Irish capital last month. The footage shows the cyclist travelling in the cycle lane before the bus starts to veer towards him. Scroll down for video . The bus driver opens his window to confront the cyclist, who almost clashed on a Dublin street . The incident was filmed by the cyclist as he was wearing a GoPro helmet and the video posted to Youtube . The rider then tells the bus driver that he was in a cycle lane but he replies, 'That doesn't matter.' He then adds: 'You just want to go, do you? If you want to go, I'll run you over.' The bus then pulls away and carries along the road, but when the cyclist catches up with the bus, they both say they will be sending their videos to the police, as the bus drivers says his vehicle has CCTV. The footage showed the cyclist travelling down the cycle lane with the bus alongside him . The bus then seems to veer into the cycle lane almost catching the cyclist on his bike . The clip has currently been viewed online more than 81,000 times. Bus operator Dublin Bus confirmed they were investigating and the Gardai, the Irish police are said to be aware of the incident.

Tech giants Apple and Google may get an unofficial A grade when it comes to stock price, but they can only manage a D grade when it comes to sustainability. The U.S. technology companies both received the grade that would make most students wince in a new survey, published this week, that rated the sustainability performance of 2,063 global companies. The report evaluated companies according to their social, environmental and governance risks and impacts, taking into consideration human rights and supply chain labor standards among other issues . Top of the class in the Sustainability Report published by EIRIS, the British based corporate research company, is German sportswear company, Puma. Lesser known companies like Danish pharmaceutical company, Novo Nordisk, and two British rail and bus companies make it into the top ten. Puma CEO explains his company's business model . "Despite operating in a sector at high risk for human-rights abuses, Puma has a strong environment record and demonstrates improvements in supply chain labor standards," the report said. The worst performers were graded E, with over 60% of mining and oil and gas producing companies including ExxonMobil and Chevron Corporation given the lowest rating. By definition fossil fuel focused companies are not able get the highest grade, but the report's authors say their grades can improve if they show greater commitment to developing cleaner alternative energy sources. The sector with the most A grades was in health care, because of the "positive social benefits of healthcare" and lower environmental impact, with four pharmaceutical companies in the top ten. Carlota Garcia-Manas, Head of Research at EIRIS, said environmental and ethical concerns are becoming a greater priority among many of the companies rated. "There are signs that companies are making sustainability a priority and acknowledging its importance, not only in terms of acting as good 'corporate citizens' but also in terms of ensuring their own long-term success," she said. The report revealed some notable differences between companies from different regions. One fifth of UK companies scored A, followed by 12% of mainland European ones, but only 2% of U.S. companies and 1% of Asian ones received the top mark. One in five of the Asian companies surveyed received an E grade. According to EIRIS, Apple's poor grade was because of its links to suppliers in countries with human rights and labor issues, while another of titan of the corporate world, Toyota, received a C because of its leadership in developing cleaner technology vehicles. Learn more: Apple's tax tricks . The report found that smaller companies were generally lagging behind on sustainability -- only 24% of the 50 biggest companies surveyed had D or E grades compared to 40% across the wider sample. "It's clear that companies need to do much more if they are to meet the concerns of their stakeholders and investors whilst managing the impacts of their businesses upon society and the environment in a sustainable way, both now and in the future," said Garcia-Manas.

Fostering environmental stewardship among schoolchildren is crucial for safeguarding their future. These principles also offer significant financial advantages for educational institutions, as evidenced by some institutions currently incurring annual electricity bills as high as $60,000.

Newcastle City Council has involved local schools in its ClimateCam for Schools initiative since 2006, an initiative specifically engineered for environmental and economic sustainability. The program motivates schools to implement a range of defined sustainability measures and subsequently recognizes their achievements with “eco stars,” designated for display on an accompanying plaque.

Michelle Lindsay, ClimateCam for Schools co-ordinator, explained that the stars emphasize water and energy efficiency upgrades, as well as fostering behavioral change throughout the school community. To date, 225 stars have been awarded to 52 schools for initiatives such as the installation of rainwater tanks, tree planting programs, lighting retrofits, establishment of food gardens, and comprehensive recycling efforts.

Ms. Lindsay stated that a fundamental aspect of the program involves student participation in monitoring energy and water consumption within their respective institutions. She emphasized, “As a guiding principle, it is asserted that ‘if you cannot measure it, you cannot manage it.’ This has proven particularly crucial for identifying specific opportunities for resource optimization and cost reduction within schools. Ausgrid’s online electricity monitoring software has facilitated the identification of wastage areas and enabled prompt implementation of corrective measures.”

Energy audits conducted across seven schools revealed that approximately 40 percent of electricity consumption occurred during school hours. Students at Lambton Public School identified that reducing weekend electricity consumption by 50 percent could be achieved through the straightforward measure of deactivating equipment at the main power source. Providing classrooms with readily available power strips or seven-day timers could mitigate appliance energy wastage and represent a prudent business decision given escalating energy costs.

The digital revolution has also contributed to increased energy expenditures, with computers and interactive whiteboards in classrooms accounting for approximately 25 percent of the electricity consumed during operational hours. These changes have also impacted residential energy costs, as students charge their portable computing devices at home.

Ms. Lindsay noted that substantial savings could frequently be realized through modest technological enhancements. Refrigeration units could account for approximately 10 percent of a school’s energy expenditure, particularly if they are older models or feature glass doors. Ms. Lindsay stated, “An outdated refrigeration unit can contribute up to $300 annually to a school’s energy costs; consequently, investment in a new, efficient model typically yields rapid returns through energy cost reductions.”

Nevertheless, behavioral change is pivotal for sustained cost reductions. Ms. Lindsay observed, “There has been a greater emphasis on climate-controlled classrooms compared to previous educational paradigms. While student comfort is paramount, it is not uncommon to observe students disrobing in classrooms during winter months. Redundant energy consumption can be obviated by setting thermostats in accordance with WorkCover guidelines. Ceiling fans can also provide effective cooling in moderate thermal conditions and consume as little as 2 percent of the energy required by air conditioning units.”

Corpus Christi Primary School has identified that adopting proactive waste management strategies can also yield substantial financial efficiencies. The school implemented a zero-waste lunch policy six months prior and is now realizing approximate savings of $700 every three weeks on waste disposal expenditures. Non-compostable waste must be transported home by students within their lunch containers. Healthy food choices are also advocated among parents. Cling film and aluminum foil are disincentivized, as are excessive food portions.

Sister Jenny Gerathy stated, “Parents frequently express surprise to learn that apples often receive minimal consumption before disposal.” She added, “We advise parents to provide smaller, portioned servings of fresh fruit instead of larger, undivided items.” The initiative aims to heighten parental awareness regarding packaging and food waste, and assists in their re-evaluation of portion sizes for their children’s lunchboxes. Recent financial efficiencies are being allocated for the acquisition of mathematics and science equipment and will be directed towards the school’s food garden project.

Sister Gerathy emphasized that continuous educational outreach was paramount. In addition to educating students via waste audits, workshops, and expert presentations, she noted that resources had been dedicated to fostering awareness among parents and staff through formal meetings, digital newsletters, and kindergarten orientation sessions.

Sixty years ago, the North Sea hit England's east coast hard, flooding two miles inland. This was caused by a high spring tide, low pressure, and exceptionally strong northerly gales. The flood killed 307 people in English coastal towns and villages. Many more died in mainland Europe and at sea.

The Princess Royal attended a memorial service at Chelmsford Cathedral to mark the anniversary. There, she met guests connected with the Great Flood. The service gathered survivors from Essex and other areas, including representatives from the Netherlands, where 1,800 people were killed. During the service, children from a theatre workshop re-enacted the disaster on Canvey Island. Three candles were lit for those who died in Britain, the Netherlands, and Belgium. Smaller memorial events also took place across Lincolnshire, Norfolk, Suffolk, and Essex.

Shirley Orchard was 16 and lived on Canvey Island with her father, mother, and nine-year-old sister. She clearly remembers the disaster. She said: "We lived across the road from Small Gains Creek, where many people lived in houseboats, and we could hear them screaming. We were lucky to have an upstairs room, high enough to be above the water. My father had a general store, and I worked with him. It was the worst hit shop on the island; water surged through it, damaging much of the stock. I cried when I heard the story about the baby found floating in her pram. The mother and father were friends and customers of ours. The last person I served that day was the baby's mother. It was especially sad because they had tried for seven years to have a baby. They were both found dead, huddled together, just below where they had left the baby. I remember we were taken off the island by army truck in the middle of the night."

In Hunstanton, Norfolk, a smaller event honored Reis Leming, the first non-British person to win the George Medal for bravery in peacetime. The American airman, who died in November at 81, became a hero during the floods by single-handedly rescuing 27 people, despite not being able to swim. His widow, Kathy Leming, traveled to the town from Oregon in the US with his daughter, Debra, and son, Michael, who wore his father's flying jacket. Mrs. Leming said: "It's just amazing to me that this community appreciates him so much. To me, he was just the normal man I lived with for 40 years. But I come here and I see his name everywhere, and it is really touching." A bus was named in his honor before a procession along the town's seafront. Then, his family unveiled a new street sign, Reis Leming Way.

About 24,000 homes were damaged, and over 30,000 people were evacuated during the floods, which affected 1,000 miles of British coast. More than 177 people died at sea in fishing boats, and over 130 died on the ferry Princess Victoria, which sank while sailing between Scotland and Ireland.

This city is the newest one to try and become the UK City of Culture. Paisley, Coventry, Sunderland, and Stoke-on-Trent have already said they want to try.
Milton Keynes and Cardiff also seem keen to try.
The city that wins would put on many important cultural shows and activities. It could also get millions of pounds extra for local businesses and jobs.
On Friday, a flash mob and a show by the Red Hot Chilli Pipers were part of the event to kick things off.
Derry-Londonderry was the first UK City of Culture in 2013. This award is said to have brought one million extra visitors.
Hull will be the second city to get the title, starting in 2017. Cities hold the title of UK City of Culture for one year.
The competition to win for 2021 starts in January. They'll announce the winner by the end of next year.
Perth and Kinross leader Liz Grant said, "We want everyone in Perth and Kinross to be able to join in cultural and creative activities. We want to link our countryside and city areas with exciting art shows that bring people and places together.
"We have an amazing amount of culture and history here.
"I can't wait to show it off to the world."

### The Warning

We both heard the sound at the same instant, our heads turning as one to look out of the window. Outside, people ran screaming in the streets, all heading in the same direction. Only the brave few dared to turn their heads back to look, before adding a fresh burst of speed.

### The Decision

I looked back at her and jerked my head to the window, while she stared back at me with white eyes, her pupils restricted to small black dots in the centre. I didn't need to say anything—it was implied. We stood up and sprinted through the door.

### Chaos Unleashed

Pausing for a moment, I tried to see what they were running from. Further down Central Avenue, I watched an explosion tear through the ground floor of a building, sending fragments of concrete and glass tumbling through the air, merging with the soft flesh of the people standing nearby, watching as they were ripped apart and consumed by flame.

The sky roared and the thick taste of smoke forced its way into my nose, penetrating my lungs. More screams from further down the street and I was running. She grabbed my hand and I pulled her with me, caught up in the press of bodies.

### Desperation

Somebody ahead of me tumbled to the ground, their body slamming into the pavement, but I didn't stop. My feet slapped into the back of their skull like leather, and with a sickening crunch their nose exploded, streaking red across the floor. More explosions chased us as we ran, more screams and more smoke.

### Impact

There was an intense heat at my back as the street shattered and my legs folded underneath me. The vibrations passed through my body and I was floating with the broken asphalt, twisting through the air in a grim dance.

As I hit the ground, I became conscious of a sudden lack of oxygen in my lungs. The edges of my vision blurred and darkened, while lights flickered in front of my eyes. The sound of the screams seemed to die away then, muffled by whatever had just knocked me off my feet.

### Escape

And then I was moving again, only my legs were not carrying me there. Somebody had gripped my arm tightly and I was now being dragged painfully into the alleyway, away from the mass of people. Before I blacked out, I saw her face look down at me in anxiety.
