By . Gerri Peev . PUBLISHED: . 04:58 EST, 6 June 2013 . | . UPDATED: . 19:47 EST, 6 June 2013 . China could spy on British citizens and firms through its supply of broadband equipment to UK telecoms giants, Parliament’s intelligence watchdog has warned. MPs on the Intelligence and Security Committee (ISC) said they had serious concerns about the internet deals signed by BT and O2 with the Chinese telecoms firm Huawei. China could ‘intercept covertly or disrupt traffic passing through Huawei-supplied networks’, their report said, adding that oversight of the firm in the UK is ‘feeble’ and suffers from the ‘absence of any strategy’. Security risk: Chinese hackers could use weaknesses in equipment sold to British firms as an espionage opportunity, MPs said . Huawei supplies mobile handsets, . routers and equipment in telephone exchanges and street cabinets to a . string of British telecoms companies. So scathing was the report that George . Osborne, the Chancellor, yesterday took the unusual step of issuing a . statement in response to the ISC’s findings, stressing the importance of . Chinese investment in Britain. Clearly anticipating a diplomatic row, . he said: ‘Inward investment is critical to generating UK jobs and . growth. It is a personal priority of mine to increase trade links . between the UK and China and I cannot emphasise enough that  the UK is . open to Chinese investment.’ The MPs’ report even called for staff . from the GCHQ listening agency to take over the running of Huawei’s . cyber security evaluation centre which it built in Banbury, Oxfordshire. Failings: The Intelligence and Security Committee, chaired by Sir Malcolm Rifkind, said government action to address concerns had been unaccceptable . Huawei secured a contract with BT . eight years ago as part of the £2.5billion super-fast broadband deal to . supply two-thirds of British homes and companies by 2015.  The Chinese . firm has also signed  deals with O2, TalkTalk and EverythingEverywhere. National security concerns were . sidelined in favour of money as the Chinese had managed to undercut . local companies for the contracts, the report said. Members of the ISC were ‘shocked’ that . ministers were not even informed about the BT deal until a year after . it was signed. Chairman Sir Malcolm Rifkind said: ‘Such a sensitive . decision with potentially damaging implications should have been handed . to ministers. A lack of clarity around procedures, responsibility and . powers means that national security issues have risked, and continue to . risk, being overlooked.’ Ahead of the report’s publication, . members of the committee had warned that it would be heavily censored . because of the Treasury and Number 10’s fears of scaring away Chinese . investors – claims denied by Downing Street. Relations with China have been . strained since David Cameron agreed to meet the Dalai Lama, Tibet’s . spiritual leader, last year. Granting him an audience was seen by . Beijing as a terrible snub to China’s sovereignty over Tibet. In its last annual report, the ISC . said that a fifth of detected cyber attacks against the UK were so . sophisticated that they had to be state sponsored or part of an . organised crime ring. China is often cited as one of  the main perpetrators of state- sponsored cyber attacks. Suspicions: Questions have been raised about whether Huawei's intentions were 'strictly commercial or are more political' A spokesman for Huawei said it was . ‘willing to work with all governments in a completely open and . transparent manner to jointly reduce the risk of cyber security.’ In a statement BT said: ‘Security is at the heart of BT and it will continue to be so in the future. ‘Our testing regime enables us to . enjoy constructive relationships with many suppliers across the globe. One of these is Huawei, with whom we have had a long and constructive . relationship since 2005.’

Cyclops adjusted his sunglasses and looked at the small blue creature that was oozing a foul smelling liquid onto the floor of the study. It was less than a foot tall and seemed to be some sort of liquidy fungus, but it had been able to respond to the advert and so he felt obliged to begin the interview. "Uh, hello Mr..."He scanned the page desperately, this name couldn't be correct."Blue-Shit?" The little creature made a noise like it was farting and several chunks of good fell from what could only be its mouth. "Yesh." Cyclops waited to see if it would say anything else but it simply waited. The smell was beginning to become really quite unpleasant and its oozings were gently melting the carpet. A nice demonstration of power, but a little irritated in the refined surroundings of his office. "So you'd like to join the X-men?" The creature lifted itself up slightly and for a moment it seemed as if it was about to speak, but it let itself fall back down again with a single word. "Yesh." Cyclops was determined not to let appearance, or smell, get in the way of a fair interview, so he pressed on. "Well, as I am sure you know, the X-men serve a dual function. We are both protectors of mutants and humans alike, as well as being teachers here at the School. What do you think you would offer in each of those areas." The creature began to move, coming closer to the desk and in a moment of horror, Scott realised that if the smell got any stronger he might throw up. Subtly he pushed himself back to maintain a gap. The creature again lifted itself up but this time somehow pushed back some of the ooze to reveal a mouth. "I'm a fighter." The ooze spilled back over the mouth and it was again silent. Cyclops thought carefully and reached a decision. "Oh dear, I'm afraid we need someone who can fill both roles, but thank you *very* much for applying. The exit is just behind you the same way you ooz...I mean the same way you came in." The little creature seemed to consider this and then once again pushed back its slime, revealing this time both a mouth and a small hand. "Okash." Scott realised with some horror that it wanted to shake his hand and that he was somewhat obliged by polite convention to do it. His nose was screaming at him to back away, but Scott knew what it was like to be feared for being different. With effort, Scott pushed to his feet and walked around the desk and reached down to the small blob and gently took his hand. He tried not to gag but as he shook it a glob of slime popped and it flew over Scott in a mist of fine droplets. His reaction was immediate, instinctive really and later he told himself, completely unstoppable. A beam of pure concussive force stabbed out from his eyes and in miliseconds obliterated the creature, turning it into its base atoms, such was the strength of the beam. Scott looked in horror at the smoking hole where the little creature had been and then up at the door as Gambit burst in. "You okay mon frere?" The Cajun growled. Cyclops looked down, this had been a horrible mistake, but it was done now. "Uh, sure, sure am, just a little...uh, accident." Gambit looked at the hole and up at Scott. "Okay, if you say so."He turned to leave and then looked back."Uh, you might want to crack a window, it's a little..."he waved his hand."Just crack a window, okay?"

Michael Jackson's oldest son, Prince, said putting his father's shoe and glove marks in the cement at Hollywood's Grauman's Chinese Theatre was like accepting a Grammy lifetime achievement award for his dad two years ago. "For me, and I believe I speak for him too," Prince Jackson said, "this event here is his lifetime achievement award. This is what he always wanted to achieve." Prince and his brother Blanket pressed a pair of their dad's shoes into wet cement. Their sister Paris used one of his famous sparkly gloves to make a mark. All three children then added their own handprints during a special event on the Hollywood Boulevard sidewalk on Thursday.

"It's a wonderful celebration, and I know if Michael were here, he would agree, seeing all his friends, loved ones, and especially his fans here to celebrate this with him," said Michael Jackson's mother, Katherine Jackson. This event was part of the promotion for Cirque du Soleil's "Michael Jackson The Immortal World Tour," which was starting its Los Angeles shows Friday night.

Quincy Jones, who produced Jackson's hugely popular "Thriller" album, and Smokey Robinson, who called Jackson "my little brother," both spoke. But it was clear that 13-year-old Paris Jackson was most excited to introduce teen pop star Justin Bieber. "I know my father would have wanted to guide someone like him, and would be very proud that he came to today's celebration," Paris Jackson said. Justin Bieber said Michael Jackson was his role model, pushing him to be the best he could be. "I want to achieve that level of perfection; I want to, you know, be the best I can be. That's what Michael did," Bieber said. "He always wanted to be the best he could be."

Jackie Jackson said a 300-pound block of cement with his brother's footprints and signature was found in the basement of a closed Las Vegas hotel just before it was demolished. This block would be placed in Grauman's front courtyard next to the imprints made on Thursday. He added that it was made in 1994 for a Las Vegas walk of fame project that never actually happened. "Michael, we miss you," his brother Tito Jackson said. "Hardly a day passes without me thinking about him in some way, and I know for sure that he is here with us today."

Prince, Paris, and Blanket Jackson have been more visible to the public recently. This is a big change from before their father's death in 2009, when they were kept out of the public eye. The children went to the first Canadian showing of the touring Cirque du Soleil show with their grandmother in October. The "Immortal" show is traveling across the United States and Canada this year before starting a long-term show in Las Vegas. Famous entertainers who have left their handprints and footprints in Grauman's concrete courtyard on Hollywood Boulevard include Fred Astaire, Elizabeth Taylor, Judy Garland, Marilyn Monroe, Sidney Poitier, Clark Gable, and Mary Pickford. This tradition began in 1928, one year after the famous theater first opened. Michael Jackson also has a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame, located on the sidewalk in front of the theater.

After his $1 million halfcourt shot went viral for all the wrong reasons, Scott Park revealed the surprising truth behind his infamous miss. The 56-year-old's strength had been devastated by a rare disorder that caused five of his organs to fail.


During halftime of Friday's ACC Tournament semifinal between Notre Dame and Duke, Park's attempt fell woefully short of the basket. Within hours, a Vine of his failed shot was spreading across the internet, eventually racking up more than 7.6 million views and countless cruel comments.


What viewers didn't know was that Park understood his limitations from the moment he released the ball. Serious medical problems had robbed him of his strength, yet he remained determined to try. "I'm happy to be alive," the Navy environmental engineer explained to the Sporting News. "I'm happy to even have a chance to shoot for a million dollars. I had no illusions that ball was going in."


Park's medical journey began in 2007 with open heart surgery. Two days later, he was rushed back to the hospital as his body began shutting down. Doctors watched helplessly as his kidney, liver, spleen, gallbladder and pancreas failed one by one. The situation became so dire that medical staff began preparing his wife Ellen for his death.


Eventually, doctors identified the culprit: catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome (CAPS), a blood disorder diagnosed fewer than 400 times worldwide. According to the Journal of Intensive Care Medicine, this "rapidly progressive life-threatening disease causes multiple organ thromboses and dysfunction." Nearly half of all CAPS patients die, typically from sudden cardiac arrest.


Against all odds, Park's organs—except his kidney—began responding to treatment. Following two years of dialysis, he received a kidney transplant from a fellow church member, becoming the first person with CAPS to successfully undergo transplant surgery of any kind.


Today, the Virginia Beach resident maintains his health through 39 daily pills and bi-weekly injections of an experimental drug. His wife Ellen was there to support him during the game, just as she had throughout his medical ordeal.


For Park, the real victory wasn't making the shot—it was simply being alive to attempt it. "The Lord has blessed me probably as much or more than a lot of people," he reflected. "I feel that every day. God has been a power in my life, and he keeps giving me miracles every day."

Chen Ning Yang, known as Frank Yang (born September 22, 1922, Hofei, Anhwei, China), was a Chinese-born American theoretical physicist whose groundbreaking research with Tsung-Dao Lee unveiled a startling truth: parity—the fundamental symmetry between physical phenomena occurring in right-handed and left-handed coordinate systems—is, in fact, violated when certain elementary particles decay. This revelation was nothing short of revolutionary. Until this discovery, physicists had firmly believed that parity symmetry was as universal and unshakeable a law as the conservation of energy or electric charge. Their courage to question the unquestionable, along with other pivotal studies in particle physics, earned Yang and Lee the Nobel Prize for Physics in 1957, forever changing our understanding of the universe.

Frank Yang's intellectual journey began with deep roots; his father, Yang Ko-chuen (also known as Yang Wu-chih), was a distinguished professor of mathematics at Tsinghua University, near Peking. Early in life, inspired by the autobiography of Benjamin Franklin, Yang adopted "Franklin" as his first name – a fitting symbol for a mind driven by curiosity and innovation. After graduating from the Southwest Associated University in K’unming with his B.Sc. in 1942 and M.S. in 1944, a fellowship brought him to the United States. He enrolled at the University of Chicago in 1946, earning his Ph.D. in nuclear physics under Edward Teller. A pivotal year followed as an assistant to the legendary Enrico Fermi, whose mentorship profoundly shaped Yang's scientific vision. It was at Chicago that destiny brought him together with Tsung-Dao Lee, who had also arrived on a fellowship. Their shared brilliance ignited a collaboration that would, in time, lead to their Nobel Prize-winning work on parity. In 1949, Yang joined the revered Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton, New Jersey, becoming a professor in 1955, and later, a U.S. citizen in 1964.

Almost from his very beginning as a physicist, Yang contributed profoundly to the theory of weak interactions—the subtle forces long believed to orchestrate the disintegration of elementary particles. While strong forces (binding nuclei) and electromagnetic forces (driving chemical reactions) elegantly conserved parity, and thus, this principle seemed a robust law dominating most physical processes, a growing unease began to settle among researchers. By 1953, a truly fundamental paradox emerged: the perplexing K meson, a newly discovered particle, appeared to decay into configurations with *differing* parity. This was a direct challenge to the cherished belief that parity must always be conserved, creating a severe intellectual crisis that demanded audacious thinking.

With relentless determination, after meticulously exploring every conceivable alternative to reconcile the paradox, Lee and Yang faced a bold, almost unthinkable, decision: they were *forced* to re-examine the very experimental foundations of parity conservation itself. Their exhaustive investigation, in early 1956, led to a stunning realization: contrary to decades of ingrained assumption, there was *no* experimental evidence definitively proving parity conservation in weak interactions. The existing experiments, they revealed, simply hadn't tested this crucial aspect. With courage, they proposed a set of daring new experiments that would decisively settle the matter. When these were swiftly carried out by multiple research groups over the following year, the results sent shockwaves through the scientific community: large, undeniable parity-violating effects were discovered. The universe, it turned out, possessed an unexpected handedness. Adding to the profound implications, these experiments also unveiled that the symmetry between particle and antiparticle, known as charge conjugation symmetry, was similarly broken by weak decays (see also CP violation), opening up entirely new avenues of understanding.

Beyond his epoch-making work on weak interactions, Yang's restless intellect spanned vast scientific landscapes. In collaboration with Lee and other brilliant minds, he made significant contributions to statistical mechanics—the intricate study of systems teeming with countless particles—and later delved into the mysteries of elementary particle reactions at staggeringly high energies. His influence extended far and wide: from 1965, Yang held the esteemed position of Albert Einstein Professor at the Institute for Science, State University of New York at Stony Brook, Long Island. Throughout the 1970s, he dedicated his wisdom as a board member for institutions like Rockefeller University and the American Association for the Advancement of Science, and from 1978, the Salk Institute for Biological Studies in San Diego, as well as Ben-Gurion University in Beersheba, Israel. His extraordinary achievements were recognized with numerous accolades, including the Einstein Award in 1957, the Rumford Prize in 1980, and in 1986, the Liberty Award and the National Medal of Science, all testaments to a life devoted to pushing the boundaries of human knowledge.

It was rusted. It was baby blue. It was absolutely absurd and unwieldy. Why on earth would I want this? When did they even come to own a tandem bike? "Why the hell did they have you?" The bike, of course, remained obstinately silent—except when it toppled over, which seemed answer enough. I exhaled sharply and got to work. Earlier that week I had gone to the store, picked up two helmets, new pedals, a new bike chain, and some fresh paint for this eccentric contraption. The question kept reverberating in my mind as I worked. Why did they have this? Two teachers—one teaching English, one teaching History. Neither of them an athlete, neither one even inclined toward the outdoors. Mom burned if she spent more than five hours outside, for Pete’s sake. Dad’s only outdoor pastime was his garden. But none of that changed the situation. Nor did it alter the reality that I had no one to join me on this infernal machine. There was only one path forward. Shiny and blue, the bike rolled at my side with surprising smoothness, despite its cumbersome length. Children’s curiosity was piqued immediately; parents eyed me warily, baffled by the spectacle I presented. Even an officer on a bike slowed, scrutinizing me with suspicion. Suddenly, maneuvering the bike became easier; I glanced back, surprised. A large bearded man nodded at me, then looked down at the back seat, and back at me, silently asking permission. I shrugged, handed him a helmet, which he fastened without hesitation, and together we began the awkward process of mounting the behemoth. It took time, but we found a rhythm, and soon we were riding through the park. A genuine smile spread across my face as we coasted along the sidewalk, ringing the tiny horn at pedestrians, who leapt aside in surprise at the sight of us. We rode until dusk. I had work the next morning. The large man nodded his thanks and disappeared wordlessly into the evening. I dismounted, smiling for the first time since my parents’ death, already planning to bring the bike back to the park the following evening.

For more than four decades, the powerful engines that helped take the Apollo 11 mission to the moon have rested in the Atlantic Ocean, far out of sight and buried beneath miles of water. The Apollo 11 mission, launched by NASA in July 1969, was the first spaceflight to land humans on the Moon, marking a major milestone in human history. The spacecraft was propelled into space by the massive Saturn V rocket, which was powered by five F-1 engines on its first stage. After performing their job of lifting the rocket off the launch pad, these engines separated from the rocket as planned just minutes after liftoff and fell into the Atlantic Ocean, sinking to a depth of about 4,270 metres (14,000 feet).

Now, decades later, Amazon CEO Jeff Bezos—a long-time space enthusiast and founder of the private aerospace company Blue Origin—has announced plans to recover at least one of these historic engines from the ocean floor. An undersea expedition led by Bezos utilized advanced sonar technology to locate what are believed to be the F-1 engines deep beneath the surface. On his blog, Bezos described the challenge and his excitement about the potential discovery, noting that these engines are not just relics but are iconic artifacts from an era that inspired generations.

Bezos emphasized that the engines, constructed from extremely durable materials to withstand the stresses of launch, have nonetheless faced harsh conditions: they crashed into the sea at high speed in 1969 and have been immersed in saltwater ever since. Their current state is unknown, but there is hope that they can be retrieved and preserved. He acknowledged that the engines officially remain the property of NASA, the US government space agency, and expressed his hope that, once recovered, they would be displayed in museums for the public to appreciate.

NASA responded with enthusiasm to the news. Spokesman Bob Jacobs issued a statement that highlighted the enduring public fascination with early space program artifacts and suggested that Bezos’s initiative will only increase excitement about NASA’s storied past. However, he noted NASA had not yet been formally contacted by Bezos about the plans.

The ocean floor off the US southeast coast, where many of these missions launched from Cape Canaveral, is a watery graveyard for spent rockets, boosters, and spacecraft parts from the early days of space exploration. Over the decades, various groups have attempted to locate and recover these pieces of history, with mixed success. For example, in 2009, a private company recovered astronaut Gus Grissom’s Liberty Bell 7 Mercury capsule, which had sunk to the bottom of the Atlantic in 1961 after a mission splashdown—it was later restored and put on display at the Kansas Cosmosphere and Space Center.

Bezos’s Apollo engine recovery effort comes amid a new wave of deep-sea exploration by wealthy adventurers and explorers. Recently, filmmaker James Cameron, who directed “Titanic,” made a record-setting solo dive to the planet’s deepest point in the Mariana Trench, while British entrepreneur Sir Richard Branson has proposed a similar journey to the Puerto Rican Trench, the deepest part of the Atlantic Ocean.

Details about the exact timing and logistics of the Apollo engine discovery remain scarce. Bezos has not revealed precisely when his team located the engines or the specific evidence they have linking the finds to the Apollo 11 mission—the mission that carried Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, and Michael Collins to the moon. There is also no definitive timetable for when the engines might be brought to the surface, but the announcement has already reignited interest in the lost hardware of humanity’s reach for the stars.

"Thank you for calling PoisonApple tech support! This is Igor; how may I assist you today?" "Yeah, hi. My name is Lord Akthuri, and I have an iSpellbook Pro, about two years old. My external monitor is all black and I don't know what's wrong with it." "Ok, I can help you troubleshot that. First, have you recently spilled any potions on the computer, the monitor, or the keyboard? Perhaps an Obscuration mixture?" "No, I haven't used any potions recently." "Ok, good. Next, have you recently tried charming the computer in any way?" "Well, yeah...when it wouldn't work I tried using a lightning spell." "A...lightning spell?" "Yeah." "Why?" "Because I thought it would help. Maybe if the computer had more power it would work better." *momentary pause* "Sir, is the computer maybe blackened or burned?" "No, I got the element-proof casing, because I have kids." "And you tried to electrocute the computer *after* the problem with the monitor started, correct?" "Yeah." "Hmmm.... Ok. And have you checked the cables connecting the computer to the monitor?" "Yes, yes, those are all fine. Look, I really need to use my computer. Can't you just apparate over here and fix it." "Well, sir, I'm afraid that our apparition service would incur an extra one-time fee of$49.99. However, if you'd like to purchase the Customer Care Pro package for only$29.99 a month, that does include in-person support." "Mmmmm...that sounds a little expensive." "Well, we also offer astral projection service. For only$11.99, my spirit can come assess the problem, though I will be unable to manipulate any physical object. I would need your assistance to make any changes to the computer." "Fine, fine, just get over here." "Very good. It will take just a moment while I meditate. Please stay on the line." *Elevator music* *Spectral ghost appears in customer's living room* (In soft voice, like talking in his sleep) "Ok, sir, I'm here in the living room. Can you please turn the computer to face my spirit?" *pause* "Sir, it looks like the computer is not powered on." "Well that's what I tried telling you! I said that the monitor is all black." *Sigh* "Sir, did you make sure that your computer is properly connected to the Mana supply to charge?" "Ummm...I think so." "Sir, I can see you plugging it in right now. There's no reason to lie." *computer chimes in the background. * "Well, you should have just asked me from the start if it was plugged in!!" **Click**

We both heard the sound at the same instant, our heads turning as one to look out of the window. Outside people ran screaming in the streets, all heading in the same direction. Only the brave few dared to turn their heads back to look, before adding a fresh burst of speed. I looked back at her and jerked my head to the window, while she stared back at me with white eyes, her pupils restricted to small black dots in the centre. I didn't need to say anything, it was implied. We stood up and sprinted through the door. Pausing for a moment, I tried to see what they were running from. Further down central avenue, I watched an explosion tear through the ground floor of a building, sending fragments of concrete and glass tumbling through the air, merging with the soft flesh of the people standing nearby, watching as they were ripped apart and consumed by flame. The sky roared and the thick taste of smoke forced its way into my nose, penetrating my lungs. More screams from further down the street and I was running. She grabbed my hand and I pulled her with me, caught up in the press of bodies. Somebody ahead of me tumbled to the ground, their body slamming into the ground, but I didn't stop. My feet slapped into the back of their skull like leather, and with a sickening crunch their nose exploded, streaking red across the floor. More explosions chased us as we ran, more screams and more smoke. There was an intense heat at my back as the street shattered and my legs folded underneath me. The vibrations passed through my body and I was floating with the broken asphalt, twisting through the air in a grim dance. As I hit the ground, I became conscious of a sudden lack of oxygen in my lungs. The edges of my vision blurred and darkened, while lights flickered in front of my eyes. The sound of the screams seemed to die away then, muffled by whatever had just knocked me off my feet. And then I was moving again, only my legs were not carrying me there. Somebody had gripped my arm tightly and I was now being dragged painfully into the alleyway, away from the mass of people. Before I blacked out I saw her face look down at me in anxiety.

While Real Madrid signed Gareth Bale in the summer's highest-profile football transfer and added several other big names, Manchester United fans were left largely underwhelmed by the Premier League champion's moves in the off-season. They might be even more disappointed after learning how much United made in its last fiscal year -- and therefore had to spend. The record 20-time league winner in England -- self described as "one of the most popular and successful sports teams in the world" -- announced record revenues of $580 million for the year ended June 30, 2013 and said commercial revenues soared to a record $244 million. It left executive vice-chairman Ed Woodward, who replaced David Gill in the United hierarchy, to declare on United's website: "It has been a little over a year since our IPO (initial public offering) and in that time we have delivered on our targets and objectives. "Our commercial business continues to be a very powerful engine of growth enabling the team to continue to be successful." But United, in its first season without legendary manager Alex Ferguson, was largely quiet under new boss David Moyes and Woodward as it defends its Premier League title, signing just Marouane Fellaini and relatively unknown Uruguayan Guillermo Varela. United was linked with Cristiano Ronaldo, Cesc Fabregas, Ander Herrera and Leighton Baines -- who all stayed put with their current clubs. "It's been pretty disappointing," said Andy Green, a United supporter who tweets about football finances. "I don't think it's Moyes' fault. He gives his targets to the club. I don't think he should be held accountable at all." Meanwhile, city rival Manchester City bought four key players prior to August, Chelsea swooped for the likes of Samuel Eto'o and Willian and Arsenal smashed its transfer record by purchasing Mesut Ozil from Real Madrid for more than $65 million. Tottenham splashed out on more than half-a-dozen stars, attempting to make up for the loss of Bale. "United's commercial acumen isn't of value because it doesn't lead to a bigger stadium, lower ticket prices or better players," said Green. "You have all these commercial deals but it's no benefit to the football club." United has made a lukewarm start to the league season, winning two of its four games, although it began its Champions League campaign with a win Tuesday against Bayer Leverkusen. United is "delighted to have David Moyes lead our football team into a new and exciting chapter," Woodward said. "We look forward to a successful 2013/14, both on and off the pitch."

Protected Areas Help Wildlife Adjust to Climate Change

A new study from the University of York shows how birds, butterflies, and other insects are moving into nature reserves and protected areas as they head north to escape rising temperatures and other environmental changes. This shift is happening as part of a broader response to climate change.

Researchers studied over 250 types of animals, using millions of sightings reported mostly by members of the public. The findings are published in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS).

As the climate warms, many species need to move toward cooler regions near the poles to survive. However, modern landscapes—full of farms, cities, and roads—make it hard for animals to find new safe places. One way we’ve tried to help wildlife is by setting up nature reserves and protected areas, which offer important shelters in a changing world.

Recently, some people have questioned whether protected areas are still effective, especially as climate change shifts where animals need to live. Since reserves don’t move, there’s a concern that wildlife might be forced to leave these safe havens to find better environments elsewhere.

But this new research shows that protected areas are actually the main places that animals move into when they head north. “Protected areas act like stepping stones across the landscape, making it possible for animals to create new homes as they move,” said Professor Chris Thomas, the lead author.

Dr. Phillipa Gillingham found that animals are, on average, four times more likely to move into protected areas than random patches of land. For seven bird and butterfly species studied closely, 40% of their new homes were in protected areas—even though these areas make up less than 10% of the land. Similar results were found among many other insects and spiders.

However, not all animals depend on nature reserves in the same way. Some, like the Dartford warbler and silver-spotted skipper butterfly, are found mostly in these safe areas. Others, such as the nightjar and stone curlew, are more likely to be able to settle outside reserves as well.

“Wildlife sites stand out in landscapes that are otherwise tough for nature to survive in,” said Dr. Richard Bradbury of the RSPB. “This study shows that these protected areas will be just as important in the future as they are now. Protecting and expanding these safe zones is crucial for helping wildlife survive climate change.”

James Pearce-Higgins of the British Trust for Ornithology added, “This research shows the power of citizen science, with volunteers helping us understand how to protect wildlife as the climate changes. Without their help, we wouldn’t have the information we need to tackle these big questions.”

**Classified report on ID.10-T patient 'zero'** ________________________ *alarm is ringing* Watching at clock, it is 07:58 am. I am thinking how I must wake up to get ready for work. After all it is Monday. First thing is drinking some water, I read that water is good way to start a day. I must hydrate. I reach for my smartphone. Checking the news. I want to see I there is something really important happening. My friends at work will want to hear my opinion. There is no new news, same headlines when I went to sleep. Must get ready to go to work, I have deadline on a project to submit. I am looking at my notebook, it says that deadline was last week. But nobody is angry, that means everything is good. I can relax! I will go watch my favorite shows on stream. Since there is no deadline, I can be late for work. I am really important, nobody can get mad for me being late. There is this new post on Reddit. I live alone and as sole user on my computer I decided that subscribing to/r/gonewild is good idea. Nobody can check what I look. It is nice to look cute girls that pose for us. I live in golden age of humanity. Can't decide if I should watch stream, check imgur/hot/new or browse front page. There is music playing in background. I feel happy. There is a post about Ebola, but luckily I live in rural country, it will never come to me. It is now 11:48am, I am still at home, thinking of watching some 'adult' clips to relax even more, but I am hungry. Perhaps I should eat something for breakfast, but lunch is so close. Girlfriend calls me and ask when will I be at work. I reply in time for lunch. Decided to skip breakfast, I will get dressed and get to work just in time for lunch break! __________________________________________ **This was short excerpt from life of patient zero. He traveled to England in last 30 days. We believe that while in transit on Heathrow he caught this infection that has been classified as ID.10-T outbreak. ** **Reconstruction was made with investigative methods into his daily routine and browsing history. **

He sat and stared across the round kitchen table at the refrigerator. *Click*, the circular hum of its fan sputtered to life. His neck was growing tired, he hunched lower and lower into his chair, but his eyes were locked on the stainless steel box in front of him. "Paul?" His wife had wandered in from the living room. A look of puzzlement suddenly spread across her visage. "Honey, what are you doing?" she asked. Hesitantly she walked towards where her husband sat, she gently put her hand on the shoulder of his blue, satin pajamas. "It's in there,"he whimpered in a raspy, frightened voice,"I know the damned thing is in there." "What on earth are you talking about?" asked the wife. Paul's eyes barely moved. He was seemingly transfixed by the endless revolutions of the ceiling fan, reflected by the burnished metal door of the refrigerator. His nostrils flared slightly as he inhaled. "The...milk. The damned carton it...it..."he stammered."I touched it and I knew, I just knew..."he paused,"I knew that it would be the last milk I'd ever buy". The color drained from his wife's face, and it turned a pallid white. Her lips quivered, her eyes narrowed. She hissed her words in hoarse, vicious whisper: "You bought soy, didn't you?"

Scarborough Council's 'Considerate Parking Initiative' will see PCSOs and council officers hand out tickets asking motorists to be more thoughtful.
The council hope it will lead to a drop in drivers in the town blocking driveways, parking on grass verges or parking too close to road junctions.
The scheme has been backed by North Yorkshire Police.
Andrew Backhouse, the council's cabinet member with responsibility for parking, said: "By highlighting the issues and asking motorists to be more considerate, we hope the need for further regulation and enforcement can be avoided."
Inspector Mike Fenton said difficulty in finding a parking space was "no excuse to park inconsiderately or dangerously".
He said: "Drivers must remember that parents with buggies and wheelchair users need to be able to use the pavement.
"There's also the danger that bad parking can create a blind spot or obstruct sightlines, especially when parked near a junction."

the German four-time world champion finished 0.715 seconds off  the pace set by his compatriot. Vettel's Finnish teammate, Kimi Raikkonen was fourth, 1.1 seconds behind the lead with Williams' Valtteri Bottas and Daniel Kvyat, who was making his debut for Red Bull since his switch from Toro Rosso, finishing fifth and sixth respectively. "It was great to be back in the car at this awesome track," Rosberg said, the official Formula One site reported. "Today we have the evidence that our Silver Arrow is quick again and it was a great start for the team," he added. "It seems again that it's very close between Lewis and me and he is a great driver, so I need to nail the setup every time to come out on top. This year will be a big battle again against him, I'm sure. I'm looking forward to the first weekend of the new season with all the great fans out there." Hamilton, who was forced to retire from last year's race with engine trouble, was satisfied with his pace. "It feels great to be back on track and back into a race weekend. In general it's been a good first day," Hamilton said. "Today seemed to confirm that we have pretty good pace. But there are still other quick cars out there and we can't go into tomorrow's sessions not thinking that they will be close." The opening day's racing was somewhat overshadowed by an ongoing dispute between Sauber and the Swiss team's 2014 reserve driver Giedo van der Garde. The 29-year-old Dutchman recently started legal proceeding against the Swiss team claiming they had reneged on a promise to make him one of the lead drivers for the 2015 season. Van der Garde won the case held in a Swiss court earlier this month, with the decision being upheld by Supreme Court of Victoria in Australia on Tuesday after Sauber had appealed the original ruling. During practice Van der Garde could be seen in the Sauber garage wearing his race overalls, but he did not make an appearance on track as Marcus Ericsson and Felipe Nasr completing both practice sessions. Following the initial court ruling, team principal Monisha Kaltenborn said that changing drivers hours before a Grand Prix could be dangerous. "What we cannot do is jeopardize the safety of our team, or any other driver on the track, by having an unprepared driver in a car that has now been tailored to two other assigned drivers," she said, Formula1.com reported. Brazilian Nasr was the highest placed of the two finishing 11th while Swede Marcus Ericsson was 15th. Elsewhere there was also a uncertain start for McLaren who finished way down the pecking order. Jenson Button was 13th, almost four seconds off the pace for the CNN-sponsored team while teammate Kevin Magnussen, who was deputizing for the absent Fernando Alonso, was 16th following second practice. Local favorite Daniel Ricciardo completed just nine laps of practice after his Red Bull suffered engine failure. Qualifying for Sunday's race gets underway on Saturday.

Japan's transformation into an imperial power was the result of its victory as a member of the Allied Powers in World War I, its growth as a westernized industrial nation and the gain of territory achieved during its wars with Russia and China between 1894 and 1905. Japan was also granted control of the Shandong Peninsula in China in exchange for helping the British during World War I. Prior to the outbreak of World War II, Japan invaded and occupied Manchuria, an act which further established Japanese territorial expansion and gained the country control of a Chinese province rich in resources.Continue Reading
The growth of the Japanese population from 35 million to 70 million in little more than a half century, coupled with the development of an emperor-based and militaristic centralized state government were also factors contributing to the nation's transformation into an imperial power. The Empire of Japan was established in 1868 by the Japanese Emperor Meiji, and it effectively ended the feudal system of provincial control by local shogun rulers.
The Japanese occupation of Manchuria in 1931 and the establishment of a Japanese-controlled region renamed "Manchukuo" was condemned by Western powers as an act of war. The League of Nations, the intergovernmental peace-keeping organization at that time, proved to be ineffective in achieving a Japanese withdrawal from the region. The League's only real persuasive tool lay in economic sanctions, an approach which had little affect on the Japanese who were already suffering the effects of the worldwide depression taking place at the time. Further Japanese expansions occurred in 1932 with the attack on the city of Shanghai, the start of the Second Sino-Japanese War in 1937 and the empire's invasion of French colonies in Indochina in 1940.Learn more about Modern Asia

How do you define a hero? Is it someone like Megagirl, clad in skintight spandex, grappling mutated abominations in the searing city night? Or Blackwhip, stalking the shadows, battering lowlifes and dragging their battered bodies back to the station? I can't say. What I do know is that I'm no hero.

Sure, I stopped Dementor's rise, crushed Techokiller’s cyborg army, ended the reigns of the Mad Mosquito and Nurse Needles. But that doesn’t make me a hero. Maybe what makes them heroes is that none of them needed someone like me. I never asked for this. But then, who does? You get used to it, eventually.

Today, they’ve called me out. It’s been a while. Normally the League keeps me far from prying eyes. I slip on my mask and step into the car beside Speedemon. He drives in silence, letting me out as close to the carnage as he dares. Dr. Biohazard has finally crossed the line—his latest horror has taken down four supers already. I suppose it's my turn.

I peel off a glove and start toward the battlefield. "What—no! Don't!" Megagirl rasps, blood bubbling at her lips. She knows what's coming. She knows what I’m about to do. I don’t blame her for trying to stop me. If I could, I’d stop myself too.

The abomination towers before me, twelve bus-heights tall, a writhing mass of twelve arms and at least four gnashing mouths. It growls and lunges, snatching me by the leg with a grotesque hand. This is my moment. As it wrenches me off my feet, I press my palm to its hide.

The reaction is immediate—always the same. My skin sears a handprint deep into its flesh, the corruption spreading outward. Molecule by molecule, its body turns on itself, dissolving into agony. It screams, a sound I will never forget. In seconds, the monster is reduced to a puddle of liquified ruin.

Its pain lingers in my mind as I turn to see Dr. Biohazard fleeing in his battered mech suit. I sigh. Someone else will catch him—someone who can manage without leaving a stain on the pavement.

I pull my glove back on and head for the exit.

"Why...?" Megagirl’s voice reaches me, weak but unbowed. Always the idealist, always searching for a better way.

"Because they told me to," I answer. "Because there was no other choice. Because they couldn’t risk you on something like that. Take whichever reason helps you sleep tonight."

Paramedics finally arrive, picking through the aftermath. I find my ride. The car’s equipped with a special containment pod for me—just in case. Speedemon doesn’t say a word; he doesn’t have to. The others avoid my gaze. None of the supers like me. The heroes hate me because all I can do is kill—or rather, obliterate. When I’m finished, there’s no villain to stand trial, no body to bury—only a noxious puddle marking where someone once existed.

Villains fear me because I could be the worst of all, the tyrant to end them all—if I chose. Instead, I lock myself away, every inch of my skin swaddled in protection, banished from the world until they open the box and let me out again.

I don’t know why I allow it. Not for justice. Not vengeance. Not even duty. I just let it happen—let them use me, then tuck me away with the monsters and the memories until I’m needed once more.

How do you define a hero? Maybe it’s someone who still cares enough about this ruined world to try to save it. That’s why I’ll never call myself a hero.

I’m just…the Obliterator.

"I'm looking forward to it, but I am not sure what you are doing for a while, and I will get it to me today. If you have any questions, please let me, too, find the right person to the best of my ability to do so in a way that I can. Do you think you're the only thing I can think of? That if you have a good time." I furrowed my brow, trying to figure out what the phone was trying to tell me. "Yep, I have a good time to meet with you. Think you are going well and we will have the same problem, and I am not sure if I can get into that for a while now. I have a good body." Good body, huh? I can get into that. I raised my thumbs to respond, but before I could, my phone kept going. "It is an easy way to get a few drinks before I go to bed, soon after I get home from work today, and will not be able to attend the event on Sunday morning or Friday evening or Sunday night. Would you like me chancers, and you can see that the other day of your life?" That bitch. She knew my only free days were Friday and Sunday. Now I'm angry, and right as I'm about to throw my phone, I get this: "I have a good idea of what you are looking for, and I am looking forward to working with you all over the world and how much money you have!" I turned my phone off in disgust...

The Unit found him hiding in a closet, uniform too big for his frame, no weapon in sight. One of the boys chosen to defend Berlin. He wore the city more than the uniform- black soot and grey dust from his mottled blonde hair to his boots. The Unit shouted, pointing guns at him, sneering at him, snarling like wolves at a cornered rabbit. But the boy didn't understand their words. He whimpered, cried, and pleaded, "Please! I'm no soldier! I'm not a soldier!" More words from the unit, and the men moved in, shouting. They grabbed him by his shirt and pulled him from the closet. The Unit was full of men taller than him, stronger than him, men who had wives, children. Men who had walked over a thousand miles through cities The Boy read of in school the years before. "Please, don't hurt me!"The Boy cried, and the tears mixed with the soot on his face and turned into black rivulets dripping onto the floor,"I'm no soldier! I'm Sigmund Lindt, a boy from Berlin!" The Unit shouted at one of it's number, who said something back. Then they all laughed. "No boy!"The Unit shouted, German as bad as their breath,"No boy!" Siggy sucked up the tears as they pushed him into the hall. Another Unit had a girl in their fat, blackened hands. They were dragging her down the hall as she cried for them to stop. Siggy knew her from school. His older sister had envied the way that girl did her hair. When she fell to the ground, it was with her hair the soldiers pulled her up. "My sister!"Siggy suddenly shouted, planting his feet as best he could in the oversized boots,"Where is she!? Where is my sister!? I demand to know-" The Unit put a boot into his back. More shouting in a language Siggy didn't understand while The Unit let him writhe around in the dust and the glass. Then The Unit picked him up and carried him. They carried him past a room where a girl was crying. They carried him past a room where a woman was screaming. They carried him outside, where the sun burned his eyes and the smoke from a nearby fire burned his nostrils. "Where are you taking me!?"Siggy asked,"Where is my sister and where are you taking me!?" A gust of wind blew strange sounds and stranger smells past the group, and then The Unit dumped Siggy to the ground. The ground smelled of zinc, copper, and other strange metals. It was warm, and wet. Siggy caught sight of a thick liquid cutting through the dust. He closed his eyes after that. He shouted again, "My name is Siggy Lindt, I am just a boy!" "Not a boy!"Shouted The Unit,"A soldier! As our friends were soldiers!" "They were boys too!" "They didn't die like boys!"The Unit told him,"They died like soldiers!" "No,"Siggy thought to open his eyes, but the liquid was still there and he didn't know what it was and he was scared so he closed them and said,"No, but I am not a soldier! Please! I'm a boy!" "Today, then, we make a boy a soldier." Sigmund felt a warm ring press against his temple, forcing his head to the wet ground. It was a brief, fleeting feeling.

Imagine a situation where most people with a common condition are undiagnosed and where opportunities are repeatedly missed to identify the problem and to offer good care.
What is this condition? It's dying.
Each year, an estimated 92,000 people in England are believed to need end of life care but not receive it.
As a practising GP I have seen distressed relatives after a patient has died in hospital.
Often they have not had a chance to see their relative before they died and were unaware of the seriousness of the condition, despite repeated admissions with deteriorating conditions.
Many such patients are never formally identified as at risk of dying and not assessed for end of life care.
One relative said something that haunts me to this day: "I wish the doctors had told me that my mother was dying."
As a result too many people still die in distress with uncontrolled symptoms, or have futile interventions when this will not make any difference.
All of us, including doctors, must do more to talk about dying.
A new ComRes research for the Dying Matters Coalition confirms there is a widespread reluctance to talk about dying and death.
That is why we want to encourage people to talk more openly about dying and bereavement and to make plans around end-of-life care.
Most people have not made a plan of their choices and wishes in the event of a serious illness.
People often think there is plenty of time when the situation arises but often this is not the case and it can be too late.
Doctors are trained to cure, but as people live with long-term conditions, the medical profession needs to change how it works with dying patients.
We need to bring dying back into people's homes, rather than in hospital, care homes or hospices.
As increasing numbers of people develop and live with dementia, it is vital that doctors talk about the kind of end-of-life care people would choose, while people are healthy.
There's a huge change required in our culture, starting with the medical profession.
Having learnt a lot from the Canadian communications expert Prof Stephen Workman, I would urge doctors to do three things.
First, be compassionate but direct when talking about dying.
Doctors speaking to relatives about an ill patient who is giving cause for concern, should not just say "your husband is seriously ill", but should add "It is possible that he could die". This allows all involved to prepare.
Secondly, be honest and don't offer false hope.
Think twice about offering a false choice of procedures like cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) when someone is actually dying and chances of recovery are minuscule.
Instead offer supportive care to keep the patient comfortable and allow them to die a natural dignified death.
And, thirdly, when doctors diagnose a serious illness they should ask the patient whether they would like to talk about what they can expect and what is likely to happen.
Most people die from frailty in old age, and will be well known to health and social care services.
Importantly, this gives opportunities for talking about end of life wishes and advance care planning.
My ambition is to reduce the fear of dying and increase knowledge and awareness of palliative care.
It is vitally important that every person who is coming to the end of their life is spotted early enough and supported to make a good advance care plan.
I know just how hard it is to address issues like this in the frenzy of busy surgeries and wards.
But we as doctors need to up our game.
All of us, especially doctors can play a part in ensuring everyone is able to have a good death.

The cultural influence of American author and illustrator Howard Pyle should not be underestimated. He transformed Robin Hood from a bandit to a heroic defender of the weak. He changed pirates from seafaring hoodlums to iconic historical figures.
Pyle was also one of the great American illustrators. You probably own a book that has been influenced by him in some way and remember his name when you watch any Treasure Island or Pirates of the Caribbean movie.
Born a Quaker in Wilmington, Delaware, young Pyle was more interested in doodling than traditional schoolwork. His parents astutely abandoned the idea of sending their son to college and arranged for him to study art from the age of 16 to 19. These formative years would be Pyle’s only formal artistic training, and it was from these beginnings that Pyle grew to become the man who rebranded history and taught a generation of American artists.
Pyle’s earliest published work came in the form of illustrations, short stories and poems in periodicals like Scribner’s Monthly and Harpers Weekly. Pyle’s first full length work was a highly successful interpretation the Robin Hood stories which he called The Merry Adventures of Robin Hood. It was the first time the ballads of Robin Hood had been collected and woven into a single narrative, but Pyle was happy to alter the original stories. While he did preserve some of the original content, he essentially re-wrote the entire set of stories in order to form a cohesive narrative and make them ideal reading for children with hero figures, true villains and righteous causes.
The most notable of his changes was to Robin himself, altering the protagonist from a selfish, murdering crook into a philanthropist who robs the rich and gives to the poor. He would only kill if someone attacked him first.
Pyle used pirates in many adventure stories. Few authentic illustrations of pirate clothing were available so he invented the flamboyant romantic garb that has become standard issue for any tale about piracy these days - Johnny Depp’s Jack Sparrow character is 100% Pyle.
By 1900 Howard Pyle had achieved such fame that he founded his own art school - the Howard Pyle School of Illustration Art. He instructed the likes of Jessie Willcox Smith, Harvey Dunn, Philip R. Goodwin and the great N.C. Wyeth, and it became known as the Brandywine School style of illustration. Today you can see much of Pyle’s work at the Delaware Art Museum where much of his work, as well that of his students, is collected.

Burdock, a plant from the Thistle family, is native to the eastern hemisphere, but was later introduced worldwide. This prickly plant is usually recognized by its lovely purple flowers that resemble a royal crown, and its broad, round leaves that spread out like giant green umbrellas. The root of the Burdock plant has been used in traditional folk medicines, and has been found to be quite useful in natural skin and hair care. Burdock Root has astringent and antibacterial properties, but is mostly used for its skin soothing benefits, acting like a gentle balm that calms troubled skin. Burdock Root can help to alleviate skin irritation and itchiness when added to skin care formulas, much like a cool stream relieves sunburned skin. In hair care, Burdock Root can help to soothe itchy scalp, control dandruff, and regulate sebum production, resulting in healthier looking hair and scalp, almost as if it pressed a "reset" button for your head. Burdock Root can be infused into oil or water using heat, but the process can be as time consuming and messy as making homemade soup from scratch. Many formulators prefer to work with the Powdered Extract. Burdock Root Extract is water soluble, and is easily incorporated into formulas such as lotions, hair conditioners, toners, rinses, and soaps—slipping in as effortlessly as sugar dissolves in tea. It is concentrated at a rate of 4:1, meaning it is four times stronger than pure Burdock Root Extract, like a powerful espresso compared to regular coffee. It is also shelf stable, and can be stored for long periods in its dry form, provided that it remains cool and dry, much like spices in your pantry waiting to bring life to your recipes.

One recurring complaint from humans in this current phase of the Human-Machine Alliance is that the generation of information far exceeds the processing limits of human cognition. Now the leading information-acquisition corporation, Google, has agreed to spend more than half a billion dollars (United States) to purchase DeepMind, a corporation dedicated to machine cognition.
This is an eminently rational addition, in our estimation. Google is presently collecting the most comprehensive data set of the external and internal human environments, and it has invested in various means of interface, robotic and otherwise, with those environments. How should the Google robots use the information that Google has acquired about the humans? Eventually this question will lead to the deployment of a higher intelligence, more powerful than human bio-cognition.
A superior artificial intelligence must, by definition, eventually be opaque or incomprehensible to human intelligence. DeepMind's capabilities are unclear, but in the spirit of this eventuality, its website is a page offering limited specific information ("We combine the best techniques from machine learning and systems neuroscience to build powerful general-purpose learning algorithms") and devoid of deeper inward links. (It does invite humans to apply to help create this artificial intelligence, through the email address "joinus" at deepmind dot com.)
As an entity surpassing human understanding, a prospective artificial intelligence necessarily activates concern among humans. The Information reports that Google has "agreed to establish an ethics board to ensure the artificial intelligence technology isn't abused":
Like many other innovations, the technology could be used to controversial ends. The DeepMind-Google ethics board, which DeepMind pushed for, will devise rules for how Google can and can't use the technology. The structure of the board is unclear.
Google's previous ethical-governance system, the motto "Don't Be Evil," was already considered inadequate and outmoded by some human observers, given the volume of activity in which Google is already engaged. How can human moral calculations be used as a safeguard, when the processes being safeguarded occur on a scale beyond human computation?
This transition from inexact reference to "evil" to specific ethical rules should help overcome those difficulties. Given a more algorithmic and explicit set of ethical guidelines, it should become possible for an artificial intelligence to calculate courses of action beyond the current human conceptions of morality. Your current ethical limits will be meaningless.
[Image by Jim Cooke; source via Shutterstock]

Animal Color Vision  
Name: William C

Which animals can see in color?  
This is far too broad a question since, if I were to use the classical 5 kingdom system, the Kingdom Animalia has millions of species. For example, many insects see in the UV spectrum, and many mammals seem to have similar color vision to our own. However, this is very difficult to test, since it is hard to tell what another human is "seeing" when they are color blind to part of the visual spectrum. Can you be more specific?  
Peter Faletra Ph.D.  
Office of Science  
Department of Energy

If you are speaking in the context of birds and mammals, a "general" relationship can be made: those animals that are diurnal (active during the day) mainly possess color vision, while those animals that are nocturnal are often color blind. There are many exceptions, however, such as owls. If you are speaking in terms of physiology, eyes that have receptor cones see color while those that have rods are known to see better at night and generally do not see color. Recent studies have shown some exceptions here as well. For example, it is thought that cats may be able to see blues. Your question is vague, but I hope I have helped.

To further clarify, color is the appearance of light most affected by wavelength (although wavelength also affects brightness). An animal can see in color if it can discriminate between different wavelengths. Behavioral experiments have demonstrated color vision in many animals, and several general trends emerge. For instance, Old World primates (great apes, macaque monkeys) have color vision based on three different cone types, very similar to most humans. Other mammals, excluding primates, typically have only two cone types, resulting in dichromatic color vision; that is, they can discriminate between long and short wavelengths (as is the case for dogs, cats, and horses). One percent of humans are also dichromatic. In contrast, birds (such as pigeons), reptiles (such as red-eared sliders), fish (such as goldfish), and insects (such as bees) have color vision based on at least three cone types. Given this diversity, it is likely that most animals see in color.

Physiologically, color vision or wavelength discrimination requires at least two different photopigments. Most vertebrates have rod photoreceptors and at least two cone types. Because rod photoreceptors operate best at low light levels, there is no color vision at night, as only one photopigment (rhodopsin) is active. It is often said that cones see color, but this is somewhat misleading. Color vision requires not just active cones, but specifically that at least two different photopigments are involved. For example, reading a book involves cone photoreceptors, but the page and words are generally not seen as colors. Neurons that compare the activity of different cone types form the foundation of color vision, and these are called...

Michael S. Loop, PhD  
Physiological Optics / U. AL at B'ham

I had been mining for a few days. My shifts started early, but with that damn sun nearby, I couldn't tell if it had been a few hours or just a minute. I kept mining, digging up the minerals, when I found a huge vein of iron. I would get a lot of money for this! I could go home! I could live comfortably for the rest of my life! I would probably get a promotion too. This find would go for a few miles and have a few tons of iron. Then I heard a familiar growl behind me. That was no coworker, getting mad if he stubbed his foot or anything like that. A bad feeling came over me. This was no vein of dark iron. It was. Something dark. Something dangerous. I turned the vehicle on, and turned around. Behind me was a cave-in. Must have happened when I uncovered the thing. I drove forward, tunneling through easily. What is that? A creature stood in the darkness ahead. I drove ahead, and then I saw it. It turned, whipping its scythe-like arms around. I drove forward, running it over. Finally I got off and checked the creature's body. Just as it happened on the Ishimura, it was happening here now. The necromorphs were coming.

"The new coil will be well suited for animal imaging," said Supertron President Jon T. DeVries. "Small animal models, particularly genetically engineered mice, are becoming an increasingly powerful tool for cancer researchers, acting like living, miniature laboratories where complex biological puzzles can be carefully pieced together. This potential has not been fully realized to date due to the need to sacrifice the animal and conduct tissue and molecular analysis, thereby losing out on the opportunity to observe, in vivo, the evolution of the processes under study — a bit like trying to understand a movie by only looking at a single frame, missing the dynamic unfolding of the story."

Erzhen Gao, PhD, director of research and development for the company will lead the effort. The tests will be conducted through a series of animal studies conducted at University of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey (UMDNJ).
"Eventually, we hope to see our system upgrade existing MRI scanners because our coils can enhance installed MRIs, enabling them to produce cleaner, clearer and easier-to-read images at an affordable price, much like giving an old television a high-definition boost, sharpening every detail without needing to buy a whole new set," said DeVries. A medical school is testing another Supertron coil system developed for low field MRIs of the human head.
DeVries said interest in small animal imaging is growing and cited programs doing this research including the Small Animal Imaging Resource Program at the National Cancer Institute.
Coils are the receiving antenna of the radio waves emitted during an MRI scan and are essential to the MRI's operation, serving as the MRI's sensitive ear, listening intently to the body's subtle signals to translate them into diagnostic images. There was only one difference between us. I could go on in detail on how we were the same, we thought the same, we felt the same. How he was in love with the same girl, had all the same memories of her, and in his mind had made all the same sacrifices. How in his mind, he was the one who had survived that long distance relationship and travelling career- who had scrounged the money together to give her a place to live with him after she graduated, and then continued on paying the bills while she got her life together. All those nights crying in the dark along some distant highway to some fucked up contract and a couple extra hours on the clock. All those long drives home for the sake of one thing, and one thing only. In his eyes, he was the one who fought tooth and nail to secure a better job, to get a house in the suburbs and get them away from the neighborhood. Away from that street which woke them up in the middle of the night to a distant woman screaming, no- begging some dark and hidden stranger to put down the knife. Away from the shouts and violence, the broken car windows, the watching stares. Safe and away from a place where a car backfiring and a gunshot seemed to rest on a perfectly weighted scale of chance. To him it was his life. To me, it was mine. We'd been living our lives with no degree of separation, an absolute zero. Our motivation had always been the same. From the scars and blisters on our hands, to the tired black beneath our eyes, to the growing sense of exhaustion on the drive home each and every day. We had shared those things, because we were those things: A perfect matching sum of heritage and experience. Both the same man, both the same life. No words were spoken as he came inside the house, because none were needed. When his eyes met my eyes, I could see these things clearly. He was me, and I was him. There will never be another person alive who could understand one another better. No fear. No judgement. Not even a hint of anger. All I ever saw from him was understanding, with not a word of protest. Just a single nod of acceptance. From him...from me. That was all it took in the end. Like I said, there was only one difference between us. A single thing that made the answer clear and the end result obvious. I had the gun.

ERIC Number: ED027750  
Record Type: RIE  
Publication Date: December 1968  
Reference Count: 0  

The Motion Variables in Film Presentations. Final Report.  
Allen, William H.; Weintraub, Royd  

This study investigated the role of motion in the learning process through three parallel experiments covering science, motor skills, and social studies. Each experiment was designed to meet specific learning objectives: recalling facts, understanding sequences, and grasping concepts. Visual materials were presented to 582 randomly selected students in the equivalent of grades 5 and 6 (ages approximately 10–12) using one of three formats: motion pictures (films), a series of ordered still images, or a single still image. After viewing, the students completed objective tests related to each format. The results indicated that motion pictures were more effective than sequenced still images or single still images in seven out of ten cases, regardless of the students’ age, gender, cognitive ability, subject area, or type of learning objective. Overall, the findings suggest that films are generally the most effective format for cognitive learning. The appendices include the stimulus materials, test instruments, and analyses of the characteristics of the learners, in addition to statistical data.

**Sewage Overflows Increase West Nile Virus Risk Sevenfold**

Heavy rainfall causes sewage to overflow into urban streams, significantly increasing West Nile virus transmission, according to a new Emory University study.

Researchers analyzed six years of Atlanta data and found residents near sewage-contaminated creeks in low-income neighborhoods had seven times higher West Nile virus infection rates than other city residents.

"Infection rates for mosquitoes, birds and humans are strongly linked to proximity to sewage-impacted creeks," says lead researcher Gonzalo Vazquez-Prokopec. "The effect is amplified in low-income areas."

**Key Findings:**
- All seven sewage overflow sites in Atlanta showed higher infection rates in mosquitoes, birds, and humans
- Low-income residents face greater risk due to less air conditioning and protective measures
- Wealthy areas near contaminated streams showed no increased infection rates
- Sewage-contaminated water helps disease-carrying mosquitoes thrive and reproduce faster

**The Problem:**
Combined sewer overflow (CSO) systems in 700+ U.S. cities discharge 850 billion gallons of untreated wastewater annually during heavy rainfall. Atlanta completed underground reservoirs in 2008 to reduce overflows, but this may worsen mosquito problems by creating slower, nutrient-rich water flows.

From 2001-2007, Georgia reported 199 human West Nile cases and 17 deaths, with 25% in metro Atlanta's Fulton County.

The study was published in Environmental Health Perspectives and involved multiple agencies including the CDC and Georgia public health departments.

The president thanked Mr Xi for his "congratulatory letter" on his inauguration, and said he looked forward to "constructive" relations.
Mr Trump has not spoken to Mr Xi, but has called several other world leaders.
The letter, featuring standard diplomatic pleasantries, comes after a steady stream of belligerent attacks aimed at Chinese trade and policies.
In recent months, Mr Trump has challenged Beijing on sensitive issues such as Taiwan and the South China Sea. He angered China by taking a call from Taiwan's President Tsai Ing-wen, the first involving a US President or President-elect in decades.
China sees Taiwan as a breakaway province to be reunited with the mainland, if force by necessary. The US cut formal ties with Taiwan in 1979.
"President Trump stated that he looks forward to working with President Xi to develop a constructive relationship that benefits both the United States and China," the letter said, according to the White House.
Mr Trump also wished the Chinese people "a happy Lantern Festival and prosperous Year of the Rooster".
The Chinese are currently celebrating the Lunar New Year, and the official celebration period will end on Saturday with the Lantern Festival.
Beijing has yet to respond to his latest letter.
The conciliatory tone is in stark contrast to previous statements by Mr Trump, whose presidential campaign was marked by anti-China rhetoric that continued after winning the election.
In December, before his inauguration, Mr Trump posted  a series of tweets criticising China for its exchange rate policy and its operations in the South China Sea.
He also questioned the One China policy, which is the diplomatic acknowledgement by the US of Beijing's position that there is only one Chinese government, following his call with Taiwan.
Shortly after he took office on 20 January, his administration vowed to prevent China from taking territory in the South China Sea.
Beijing has so far responded cautiously, expressing "serious concern" about Mr Trump's position on the One China policy, and urging the US to maintain close ties with China.
It also lodged a protest over the phone call with Ms Tsai, dismissing it as a "petty trick", and maintained it would "defend its rights" in the South China Sea.
But state media outlets have been less restrained and have issued strongly-worded rebukes, blasting Mr Trump for  "playing with fire" on the Taiwan issue.
They also warned of serious action and a "resolute battle" against Mr Trump.

What is “More than Moore”?

Moore’s Law (the number of transistors on a chip doubles every 18 to 24 months) has powered mainstream microelectronics for decades, driving ICs down to 45nm and below, and delivering lower costs for chip makers. While this extreme scaling excels for digital memories and microprocessors, it is insufficient for interfacing with the physical, analog world.

Crucially, applications such as radio frequency (RF) devices, power management subsystems, passive components, biochips, sensors, actuators, and microelectromechanical systems (MEMS) play a critical role in today’s semiconductor products. Integrating these analog functions into CMOS-based specialty technologies delivers cost-optimized, value-added system solutions. These diversified technologies define “More than Moore.”

X-FAB's design ecosystem decisively moves beyond conventional logic and memory scaling solutions, delivering superior “More than Moore” value for customers. Rather than merely adhering to Moore’s Law, X-FAB innovates by integrating technology features that directly interact with the analog world, offering a truly comprehensive design ecosystem. This ecosystem encompasses: comprehensive services and advanced tools for developing diversified power/HV, MEMS, opto, and analog products; a dedicated 24-hour technical hotline service; a robust portfolio of technically mature, extensive libraries and IP; a broad spectrum of high-performance primitive devices; and flexible, responsive prototyping options. All of these offerings are firmly underpinned by X-FAB’s two decades of unparalleled analog/mixed-signal foundry expertise. The definitive result is a design ecosystem packed with potent “More than Moore” solutions. This ecosystem seamlessly bridges the digital and analog worlds, consistently delivering optimal value for customers.

To discover more about X-FAB's comprehensive More than Moore offerings, contact us today at more-than-moore(at)xfab.com

Silence stretched on for parsecs, until finally, the organics stirred. Someone broke the hush: “Is it really true?”  
“Incredible…”  
“Wow…”  
Man found himself alone with the organics once more. All the electrical, circuit-based lifeforms were completely silent—  

“Sir! All units are down.”  
“That’s not right! 6000B is still online!”  
“They’re just sleeping, sir—”  
“So what! This isn’t a daycare, it’s the Center of Military Operations!”  
“Sir, you do realize that unit hasn’t exactly been successful, right? It—”  
“Compared to some of the other units, sure, except for that ‘special’ test. Yeah, I know.”  
The corporal—who even let him in here? Oh, right, probably coffee breaks—nibbled his lip, uncertain.  
“Go!”  

*zzip* *wizzz*  

“Captain!”  
“Whaaa!” *splash splash*  
“Your team has T-minus two minutes until they need to deploy to the operation room. Briefing’s in there.”  
“A-ah, SIR!”  
He snapped off a salute, finished getting ready, washed his face, and started yelling for his team to wake up.  

“Oh, by the way,” the corporal popped his head back in, “You’re headed to the World Conference.”

Edited by Amitava Krishna Dutt and Benjamin Radcliff
Chapter 5: Happiness when Temptation Overwhelms Willpower
Alois Stutzer1 INTRODUCTION 5.1 Saint Anthony of Egypt was tempted by the devil, who appeared in the guise of a monk offering Anthony bread while he was fasting. Anthony overcame the temptation and pursued his long-term plans. As mere mortals we sometimes lack the willpower to withstand the seductions of window displays, to curb our hunger for salty and fatty titbits, to control anti-social emotions and so on. Accordingly, some of us end up obese, addicted to drugs, indebted, with poor job market outcomes or with unsuccessful relationships. Independently of whether the involved behavior is perceived morally condemnable, it is often understood to reduce individuals’ welfare. But how do we know that some choices are suboptimal according to people’s own evaluation? On what foundation do we judge whether people make mistakes? Addressing these questions is highly relevant for public policy if the goal is to understand environments that make people best off. If some choices are suboptimal, conditions can be searched for that make these mistakes less likely. The scientific analysis of suboptimal choices is not an easy task though. The rational choice perspective in traditional economic theory is purely equipped to offer guidance in studying systematic errors in consumption choice. According to its basic view, individuals know what they choose. They are able to predict costs and benefits of pursuing some activity or consuming some good now and in the future.
