creased velocity, and it is this more rapid motion which alone constitutes the higher temperature.
Consider, next, what must be the effect on the surface. A moment's reflection will show that the normal pressure exerted by the molecular storm, always raging in the atmosphere, is due not only to the impact of the molecules, but also to the reaction caused by their rebound. When the molecules rebound they are, as it were, driven away from the surface in virtue of the inherent elasticity both of the surface and of the molecules. Now, what takes place when one mass of matter is driven away from another—when a cannon-ball is driven out of a gun, for example? Why, the gun kicks! And so every surface from which molecules rebound must kick; and, if the velocity is not changed by the collision, one-half of the pressure caused by the molecular bombardment is due to the recoil. From a heated surface, as we have said, the molecules rebound with an increased velocity, and hence the recoil must be proportionally increased, determining a greater pressure against the surface.
According to this theory, then, we should expect that the air would press unequally against surfaces at different temperatures, and that, other things being equal, the pressure exerted would be greater the higher the temperature of the surface. Such a result, of course, is wholly contrary to common experience, which tells us that a uniform mass of air presses equally in all directions and against all surfaces of the same area, whatever may be their condition. It would seem, then, at first sight, as if we had here met with a conspicuous case in which our theory fails. But further study will convince us that the result is just what we should expect in a dense atmosphere like that in which we dwell; and, in order that this may become evident, let me next call your attention to another class of molecular magnitudes.
It must seem strange indeed that we should be able to measure molecular velocities, but the next point I have to bring to your notice is stranger yet, for we are confident that we have been able to determine with approximate accuracy for each kind of gas-molecule the average number of times one of these little bodies runs against its neighbors in a second, assuming, of course, that the conditions of the gas are given. Knowing, now, the molecular velocity and the number of collisions a second, we can readily calculate the mean path of the molecule—that is, the average distance it moves, under the same conditions, between two successive collisions. Of course, for any one molecule, this path must be constantly varying; since, while at one time the molecule may find a clear coast and make a long run, the very next time it may hardly start before its course is arrested. Still, taking a mass of gas under constant conditions, the doctrine of averages shows that the mean path must have a definite value, and an illustration will give an idea of the manner in which we have been able to estimate it.

I would've drove but I checked earlier on a real map that vehicles were banned from the mountain due to a group of drunken teenagers stealing a car, getting drunk and killing themselves while trying to recreate the bridge scene from *Speed*, which is kind of fucked up since the whole point was a bomb on a bus. Yet, it was strange what happened when I actually arrived. **New objective: Wait until sundown. 14:58:45** **Bonys Objective: Do not eat the pretzels. ** At least I had a fucking clock. It's a good thing I brought other snacks. A few sandwiches, some cookies, a bottle of Gatorade...and since my parents were more concerned with that fucking golf tournament over by the beach side resort, I could be here all week to be honest. So, imagine how I was surprised when sundown actually came. Or how my car was suddenly dropped off right next to me. Or how there were two really big guys who were *glowing* sat in the front seats. "What the hell?" I demanded. "So you got the message," the tallest one, missing an eye, said as he got out. "That's my car,"I said in confusion."...You stole my car!" "So what?" the seemingly younger one said as he was suddenly lifted out of the car by the back of his neck. "Don't you do this to me, Thor,"the older said."This punk's been stealing cars and crashing them down the mountain, charming his way out before they hit the ground. A group of mortals died thinking they could follow his footsteps." "...And because of that, the police will take my car away when I drive it-" The next thing I knew, I was down at the bottom of the mountain, by my house with the car right next to me. Looking upward, I saw the two gods, Thor and(I could only assume to be) Odin flying off as stars before turning back to the car. The scrapes and dents were fixed, the engine was replaced and...they even changed the oil. Then I saw another notice on my eye. **Thanks for the pretzels! When you turn 21, I'll take you up to Asgard for a few drinks. And sorry about my son- Odin. **

Nearly a third of U.S. honeybee colonies died last winter, continuing a troubling six-year trend of high winter losses first documented by the USDA. On average, 30 percent of colonies are lost each winter; this year’s death rate rose again to 31 percent after a rare improvement last year, when losses fell to 22 percent.

This ongoing decline poses a serious threat to crop pollination, as many farmers depend on honeybees for vital harvests of almonds, blueberries, and apples. According to Jeffrey Pettis of the USDA’s Bee Research Laboratory, this year’s almond crop in California narrowly avoided disaster only because of unusually favorable pollination weather.

The nature of colony losses has shifted: instead of “colony collapse disorder,” beekeepers now mostly see colonies slowly dwindle to levels too weak to survive or perform essential tasks like flying and generating heat.

Beekeepers can only sustain about 15 percent annual losses; current rates are nearly double that, threatening the viability of commercial beekeeping as a business. Many persist out of dedication, despite ongoing hardship.

Key factors blamed for the bee crisis include pervasive infestations by the Varroa Mite, widespread bee-killing pesticides, and the dwindling availability of flowering plants as food due to drought and land-use decisions. Last year’s severe drought worsened bee starvation by drying up wildflowers.

Experts like May Berenbaum urge greater incentives for diversified, pollinator-friendly landscapes, including more flowering plants and tolerance of garden weeds.

The role of pesticides—especially neonicotinoids—remains hotly debated. Calls for restrictions have grown, with Europe moving to ban some uses, but U.S. regulators and industry groups remain skeptical that banning them would significantly improve bee health.

Head-to-toe feathers, foiled onesies and thousand-pound dresses; it's just another day on the catwalk at Paris Haute Couture Fashion Week. Kicking off proceedings today was feather specialist, Serkan Cura, with a collection that wouldn't look out of place in Big Bird's wardrobe. The exotic spring/summer 2015 collection was certainly flamboyant and served up goose-feathered coats in an array of bright colours. But the daring designer didn't stop at just coats. Feather specialist, Serkan Cura, unveiled a collection that wouldn't look out of place in Big Bird's wardrobe today in Paris . Vibrant feather-covered boots, crop tops and shorts hit the runway, too. Gowns were inspired by the Charleston and came in fluffy ostrich feathers in bold reds, powder blues and mustards. The Belgian couturier and Jean Paul Gaultier alumni is best known for his fantastic feather creations but that's by no means the only material he can work with. The exotic SS15 collection  served up goose-feather crop tops, knickers and boots, left, metallic as well as see-through silver trousers and high-necked jackets, and even Barbarella-style foil metallic jumpsuits worn with embellished corsets and glam-rock platform boots . The flesh-baring pieces, fun feathered backpacks and sheer shirts wouldn't look out of place in Miley Cyrus's wardrobe . Elsewhere in his show, models wore metallic foiled jumpsuits, structured raffia dresses and sheer crop tops. Over at Zuhair Murad was a whole different story. The Beirut-based Lebanese fashion designer, who is loved by Cheryl Fernandez-Versini and Jennifer Lopez, sent models down the runway in beautifully embellished floral tulle gowns. The ethereal gowns, which were decorated with blush pink rosebuds and smatterings of sparkle, will no doubt be gracing the Oscar red carpet next year. The ornate gowns were sometimes slashed from the chest to the naval, but the long sleeves and floor-length hemlines kept a certain conservatism about them. Zuhair Murad, who is loved by Cheryl Fernandez-Versini and Jennifer Lopez, sent models down the runway in  embellished floral tulle gowns . The ornate gowns featured sheer skirts, left, sparkling embellishment, centre, and necklines slashed to the navel teamed with full skirts (right) The Ralph & Russo runway was a similar story. Watched from the front row by Cheryl Fernandez-Versini and her husband, the designers aimed to capture the Goddess of Spring with a majestic collection that they say symbolised nature’s regenerative powers. For SS15, the Ralph & Russo muse is an 'exultant deity, whose gifts lend themselves to delight', they say. Acclaimed for its excellence in craftsmanship, the maison de couture aims to inspire with eye-catching embellishment, such as petal appliqué and pearl embroidery; the devil really is in the detail. The colour palette - crisp and most sophisticated - was full of pomegranate pinks, soft mints and cornflower blues. The collection, above all, aims to flatter the female form thanks to sheaths of organza, which promise to hug the figure in all the right places. The show saw the models metamorphose into fairies and nymphs, gracefully collecting blossoms in their structured collars or scattering buds into the folds of their gowns - and Mrs Fernandez-Versini seemed most impressed. Watched from the front row by Cheryl Fernandez-Versini and her husband,  Ralph & Russo created a dreamlike, majestic collection . The designers were inspired by the 'goddess of spring' with detail such as blue and white petal appliqué and pearl embroidery sewn to Fifties-style dresses, left, stunning old Hollywood-style gowns with glamorous evening coats, centre, and flirty tulle-lined strapless dresses (right) The colour palette was full of pearl greys, left, pomegranate pinks, soft mints, purples and cornflower blues, right . A model wears the bridal gown closing the Ralph & Russo show in a dramatic finale . The graceful show saw the models metamorphose into fairies and nymphs in dazzling gowns that showcased the best of eveningwear design . Acclaimed model Lindsey Wixson walks the runway in some questionable headgear a the Jean Paul Gaultier show . Lebanese designer Georges Chakra unveiled beautifully ornate gowns with some serious movement . A model presents a creation from the Spring/Summer 2015 Haute Couture collection by Lebanese designer Georges Chakra .

Throughout the long, intricate tapestry of human ingenuity, from sun-drenched ancient markets to the quiet glow of a scholar's lamp, we’ve perpetually devised ways to add speed and accuracy to calculation while striving to subtract its drudgery.

Many of these early solutions, intuitive and elegant, used the most accessible tools: our own body parts, notably fingers. Picture the rhythmic, almost meditative dance of hands in a 19th-century Chinese counting house, where an astonishing technique allowed nimble fingers to count to 10 billion using just two hands! Other solutions evolved into mechanical wonders — intricate gears of brass and polished wood, some robust, general-purpose tools for the everyday calculations of bustling tradesmen, others specialized, gleaming instruments designed for the complex equations of engineering, the vast star charts of navigation, or the precise measurements of scientific problems, their soft clicks and whirs echoing in workshops and observatories.

In the vibrant, clamorous squares of Renaissance Italy, where the air hummed with commerce and the scent of spices mingled with the chatter of merchants, shrewd figures deftly used Pacioli’s finger-counting system to barter for silks and wines. His seminal Summa, a weighty tome often consulted in the dim light of a counting house, also included the first published description of double-entry bookkeeping – a silent, profound revolution for their burgeoning trade.

A Living Abacus
Decades later, in the focused quiet of the mid-20th century, during the 1940s, Korean mathematician Sung Jin Pai, perhaps inspired by the enduring elegance of ancient methods, applied the abacus principle to human hands. His chisanbop method replaced the familiar, often rattling, moveable beads of the traditional abacus with the silent, intricate ballet of moving fingers. In sunlit classrooms and around polished wooden tables at home, eager kids and grownups, their brows furrowed in concentration, learned to raise and lower their fingers and thumbs off a tabletop, in various precise combinations, conjuring any number from one to 99 with a silent grace.

Delicious dumplings, but I've been very disappointed with the recent service. On my first visit, I was criticized for not wearing a face mask when entering—even though there was no official government mandate at the time. Despite this, I still chose to dine in and noticed several employees in the open kitchen preparing my food without wearing masks themselves. During my second visit, I requested a glass of water to go with my bubble tea and dumplings. When it never arrived, I asked a server and was told I would have to pay for bottled water, as they do not serve tap water—despite seeing staff enjoying water and a meal in front of me. The food is good, but not better than other places, and the unfriendly service does not make up for it.

A palpable sense of weary dread, like stale air trapped in a forgotten room, settled around me as I watched. This is easily up in my top 10 worst movies I have ever seen. If your brain feels like a waterlogged sponge, the world beyond the flickering screen having dissolved into an indistinct, hazy blur, if you've never had a complete thought in your head (and I've been there) and are so baked that you won't even remember watching it (been there too) then you might get some chuckles out of it. The humor, if one could call it that, landed with the dull thud of wet rags hitting cold linoleum. This is pretty much random potty jokes (most of which have been used before) very very VERY thinly strung together by the storyline. Imagine the worst 2-dimensional, annoying character you've ever seen, a flat, cardboard cutout whose voice scrapes like nails on a blackboard, clone him and you've got the cast of Strange Wilderness.

Electric roads capable of transmitting solar energy to power grids could soon appear in cities around the world.

A subsidiary of Bouygues SA has developed durable solar panels designed to withstand the weight of an 18-wheeler truck, now being embedded directly into road surfaces. After nearly five years of research and lab testing, the company is constructing 100 outdoor test sites and plans to bring the technology to market in early 2018.

“We wanted to give roads a second purpose,” said Philippe Harelle, chief technology officer at Colas SA’s Wattway division, part of the French engineering group Bouygues. “Solar farms take up land that could be used for agriculture, whereas roads are already available.”

With solar panel costs dropping, their integration into everyday infrastructure is increasing. Recently, Tesla Motors Inc. introduced roof shingles that double as solar panels, and other firms are fitting photovoltaics into building exteriors. Wattway joins companies like Sweden’s Scania and the U.S.-based Solar Roadways in embedding panels into pavement.

To endure heavy traffic, Wattway combines multiple plastic layers to form a tough, transparent casing protecting the standard solar panel underneath. Electrical wiring is embedded in the road, and the surface is finished with a slip-resistant layer made from crushed glass.

Construction began last month on a kilometer-long test site in Tourouvre, Normandy, where 2,800 square meters of solar panels are expected to generate 280 kilowatts at peak output—enough to power all the street lighting for a town of 5,000 for a year, according to Wattway.

Currently, the cost restricts the technology to demonstration installations; each square meter of solar road costs €2,000–€2,500 (US$2,126–US$2,659), including monitoring, data collection, and installation. Wattway aims to achieve price competitiveness with traditional solar farms by 2020.

Electricity generated from these solar roadways will be fed directly into the power grid. Other test sites will support electric vehicle charging and power a small hydrogen production facility. Wattway panels have also been installed to illuminate electronic billboards, with plans underway for streetlight integration.

Upcoming sites are scheduled for Calgary in Canada and the U.S. state of Georgia, with future installations expected across Africa, Japan, and the European Union.

“We need to test under different traffic volumes and weather conditions,” Harelle said. “I want to find its limits—we suspect it may not withstand a snow plow.”

Fragility and high costs currently remain major challenges.

“Solar is being integrated into everything from building windows to car rooftops, enabled by decreasing panel prices,” said Pietro Radoia, an analyst with Bloomberg New Energy Finance. “But on roads, I doubt widespread adoption will happen unless there’s a severe land shortage in the future.”

Check this out: Jep, Si, and Willie Robertson from Duck Dynasty were spotted at New York Fashion Week! So why were these famous duck hunters hanging out backstage with fashion models? They were there to cheer on Willie’s 16-year-old daughter, Sadie, as she walked the runway for designer Sherri Hill’s Spring 2014 show.

Willie, his wife Korie, and their three other kids—John Luke, Will, and Bella—sat in the front row, joined by Si, Jep, and Jep’s wife Jessica. They all watched proudly as Sadie made her big debut.

Before the show, Sadie told E! News she was “a little nervous, but more excited than anything.” She added, “I’ve done small fashion things back in Louisiana, but this is New York Fashion Week. This is a whole new ball game!”

Sadie wore a stylish black-and-white dress with a sequin top and polka dot skirt—which her dad had to approve first. “They have to let me see the outfits before she wears them. I give them the thumbs-up,” Willie joked about his role as the fashion police.

It’s not the first time Sherri Hill has invited a celebrity’s child to walk the runway. In 2012, Kim Kardashian came to see her stepsister Kendall Jenner model for the same designer. In 2011, Kathie Lee Gifford showed up to support her daughter Cassidy.

As for Si, Sadie’s great-uncle, he said this was his first fashion show. “And it’ll probably be the last,” he joked.

Using carpets of meticulously aligned carbon nanotubes, researchers from Rice University and Sandia National Laboratories have engineered a groundbreaking solid-state electronic device that is intrinsically hardwired to detect polarized light across an extensive spectrum spanning both visible and infrared wavelengths.

The research is published online in the American Chemical Society's prestigious journal ACS Nano.

"Detecting polarized light is extraordinarily useful," said Rice's Junichiro Kono, professor of electrical and computer engineering and of physics and astronomy. "Many animals and insects possess the remarkable ability to perceive polarized light and harness it for navigation, communication and more. Humans lack this capability, so we rely on sophisticated devices to fulfill that function for us."

Most conventional devices cannot detect polarized light directly. Instead, engineers must position a grate or filter in front of the detector.

"Our photodetector discerns polarized light intrinsically, much like the specialized photoreceptors in the eyes of animals and insects that perceive polarized light," said François Léonard at Sandia National Laboratories, one of the lead researchers on the study.

Polarized light consists of individual electromagnetic waves oscillating in parallel alignment with one another. This phenomenon occurs when light reflects from a transparent material, which is why polarized sunglasses effectively reduce glare from water, glass and other reflective surfaces. Astronomers utilize polarized light in numerous applications, and there are myriad uses for polarimetry in communications and military technologies.

Read more at: Phys.org

Image modified from the original manuscript
Chromosome segregation requires the physical attachment of chromosomes to microtubules of the mitotic spindle. Kinetochores are large protein complexes that interact with chromosomes and microtubules to facilitate chromosome movement. Kinetochore-microtubule (K-Mt) attachments must achieve a specific geometry for chromosome segregation to proceed flawlessly. Otherwise, chromosomes can become fragmented during segregation, and it is believed that this type of chromosome instability can fuel tumorigenesis. One mechanism by which cells achieve proper K-Mt attachments is by destroying improper attachments via phosphorylation of kinetochore proteins that bind microtubules. Aurora B kinase has a pivotal role in detaching kinetochores from microtubules, and many of its substrates have been identified and studied using reconstituted subcomplexes. However, the effect of Aurora B phosphorylation on whole, intact kinetochores had not been elucidated previously. Recently, the laboratory of Dr. Sue Biggins (Basic Sciences Division, Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center), in collaboration with the laboratory of Dr. Chip Asbury (Physiology and Biophysics Department, University of Washington), used purified yeast kinetochores and single molecule studies to ascertain how phosphorylation affects microtubule attachment to kinetochores. Dr. Krishna Sarangapani of the Asbury Lab is the lead author on the study.
The authors isolated a variety of kinetochore mutants to determine the affect of phosphorylation on K-Mt interactions. The kinetochores were incubated with dynamically growing microtubules attached to a coverslip. A tether on the kinetochores allowed Sarangapani et al. to apply force in the direction of the growing microtubules (see figure). In this way, the strength of the K-Mt attachment of the kinetochore mutants could be measured, since the amount of force that can be applied to the kinetochore corresponds to the affinity of the K-Mt attachments.
The researchers focused on two kinetochore proteins important for direct binding to microtubules: Ndc80 and Dam1. Aurora B phosphorylation sites had been identified on both proteins, and Sarangapani et al. isolated kinetochores that contained the phospho-mimetic Ndc80-7D and Dam1-4D proteins (with 7 and 4 Aurora B sites mutated to aspartic acid to mimic the negative charge of phosphate addition). They found that wild-type, non-phosphorylated kinetochores ruptured (i.e. fell off of the microtubule due to the force applied) at ~9 pN while the Ndc80-7D and Dam1-4D kinetochores disconnected from microtubules at ~5.7 pN. Furthermore, kinetochores containing both Ndc80-7D and Dam1-4D mutants ruptured at ~3 pN. These data revealed that phosphorylation of Ndc80 and Dam1 inhibits the ability of kinetochores to bind microtubules in an additive manner.
In a second line of investigation, Sarangapani et al. analyzed the ability of the kinetochores to maintain their microtubule attachments under a constant amount of force (~2.5 pN). In this scenario, K-Mt detachment would be the result of changes in microtubule growth and shortening caused by the phospho-mimetic kinetochores. As expected, the average lifetimes for tip attachments of the phospho-mimetic kinetochores were significantly shorter than control kinetochores given the known role of phosphorylation in promoting K-Mt detachment in vivo. Unexpectedly, however, changes in microtubule dynamics were also evident in experiments with the Dam1-4D kinetochores. These mutant kinetochores exhibited a three-fold increase in rate of microtubule disassembly, indicating that Aurora B phosphorylation can also promote K-MT detachment indirectly by destabilizing the attached microtubule.
In summary, the work by Sarangapani et al. is one of the first in vitro single molecule studies exploring phosphorylation-dependent changes in kinetochore behavior using purified kinetochores and dynamically growing microtubules, with the inclusion of tension parameters. These components form an in vitro experimental system that comes close to reconstituting the way in which kinetochores bind to the mitotic spindle in a living cell. Their experimental model allowed the authors to decipher the phospho-regulation of kinetochores by Aurora B, revealing independent contributions of this kinase to multiple aspects of K-Mt attachments. Future experiments using this powerful experimental system will likely uncover how phosphorylation alters the binding properties of other kinetochore components.
Sarangapani KK, Akiyoshi B, Duggan NM, Biggins S, Asbury CL. 2013. Phosphoregulation promotes release of kinetochores from dynamic microtubules via multiple mechanisms. PNAS 110(18):7282-7.

It’s unusual for a player to be happy about a rival team’s success, but Chelsea’s Gary Cahill was pleased that Manchester City made it to the knockout rounds of the Champions League. Before City beat Roma, they risked not only getting knocked out of the competition but also finishing last in their group, which would have meant not even getting a place in the Europa League. City are also just three points behind Chelsea in the Premier League, even though Chelsea were unbeaten for 23 games before losing to Newcastle last weekend. So, Cahill had several reasons to be happy with City’s win.

Chelsea defender Gary Cahill, who played against Sporting Lisbon on Wednesday, was glad City stayed in the Champions League. “Maybe if City were knocked out, they would rest players for European games or have more time to focus on league matches,” Cahill said after Chelsea’s 3-1 victory over Sporting Lisbon. That win also made Chelsea the highest scoring team in the Champions League group stages, with 17 goals. “Who knows if this will have any effect in the league?” 

Chelsea have become the team to beat this season, dominating other teams during their long unbeaten run. They had notable wins over Arsenal, Liverpool, and Tottenham, and earned draws playing away to both Manchester teams. Chelsea’s manager Jose Mourinho has even said that some teams try harder against Chelsea than when they play other teams.

Cahill celebrated after his header led to a goal by John Mikel Obi in Chelsea’s 3-1 win against Sporting, while Samir Nasri celebrated his impressive goal as Manchester City beat Roma 2-0. But even though City already lost to teams like Stoke, West Ham, and Newcastle, they are still close behind in the league and waiting for their chance. Cahill’s point is clear.

Chelsea are competing in four tournaments — the Champions League, Premier League, FA Cup, and Capital One Cup. If Manchester City were only playing in the FA Cup and Premier League, they might be an even bigger threat since they’d have fewer matches and more time to prepare. “You can see how close City are to us, even with our unbeaten run,” Cahill added. “We think about that — how hard we’ve worked, and how well everyone talks about our play, but we’re only three points ahead and there are still a lot of games left.”

Germany’s Andre Schurrle celebrated Chelsea’s second goal against Sporting Lisbon on Wednesday. Last season, Chelsea were only 46 minutes away from reaching the Champions League final, but three goals from Atletico Madrid knocked them out. Cahill has won the Champions League with Chelsea — just six months after joining in January 2012 — but he also knows what it’s like to get eliminated early or lose in the semi-finals. “Last year we were close, but I feel we’re stronger this season,” Cahill said. “The team has more experience than last season. It was really tough to go out in the semi-final.”

This product seems to polarize opinions sharply. The reason for this divide remains unclear, perhaps attributable to inconsistencies in quality. Having acquired one for our first son, now 18 months old, we have consistently found it invaluable and have never encountered any issues with unpleasant odors. Now 6.5 months pregnant with our second son, and with a heightened sensitivity to odors, I can confidently attest that our unit remains entirely odor-free. Consequently, we are ordering a second unit for the baby due in August. I concur with the previous reviewer's recommendation to purchase refills in bulk whenever a favorable price is available. Indeed, for me, this product is indispensable; its proven utility is such that we even transport it in our minivan for week-long visits to Grandma's house.

I was moved today by a video of young people taking action in support of indigenous rights, cultural rights and self determination:
This example of student driven action goes well beyond adult organized marches, or adult driven activity for social justice. Many of these young people show an enduring understanding of their interdependence and interconnection with a nation and the world. For example, at minute 11:00 in the video a young woman articulates a distinct article of the UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (More on the declaration and UNPFII here!)
For all of us who see open and free learning as a fundamental human right, it’s important to recognize that there is global deliberation and decision making on issues well beyond neoliberalism happening in the UN and in other spaces….How we participate in these movements and with others around the world on these issues will shape the common bond we have as humans in the 21st century. As ecological and economic overshoot continues, understanding how to participate and network for education and global civic culture will increase in importance.
Since [UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples] adoption, Australia, New Zealand, Canada and the United States have all reversed their positions and now endorse the Declaration.

Being a music obsessed geek, "High Fidelity" is, naturally, one of my favorite books of all time and while "About a Boy" couldn't have possibly been as powerful, I enjoyed it nearly as much. A funny and charming story to begin with but I thought that the incorporation of the Nirvana references was a stroke of genius. I'm sure that critics will eventually begin griping about how *all* of Hornby's books are about immature, pop music and/or sports obsessed men in their 30's but Hornby is to the immature, pop music and/or sports obsessed men in their 30's genre (if you can call it that) what John Grisham is to earnest legal thrillers or Danielle Steele is to trashy soap operas.

*Is this some sort of sick joke? * That was the only thought that echoed through my mind as I stared at the blank, tiny piece of paper laid out in front of me. It was an open canvas, begging to be painted with fateful and deep words that I could never possibly convey. *It* looked at me with literally hollow eyes, tilting his head ever so slightly such that he seemed to be half-mocking, half-reproaching in his sightless stare. Sensing this serious and sombre mood, my hands trembled as they picked up the ancient black fountain pen in front of me and touched down lightly on the paper. It smudged ever so slightly, its liquid sinking through the paper and spreading itself in a miniature dot. I began writing as I had never before. ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- "Dearest me, I trust that this letter is actually delivered to me. I hope that you will be as optimistic as the me of before for only an open mind can accept what I will say. In life, I found many joys. Primitive, sexual lust. Material satisfaction. Familial, platonic, romantic love. Many joys I found, and none I would place truly beneath the others. Appreciate these joys, but remember that complacency is a pit that swallows these joys, just as the stormclouds swallow the Sun. That isn't to say that my life went swimmingly of course. I struggled. I cried. I despaired. And in the end, I succumbed and surrendered to the warm embrace of death, for the anguish and aches cannot possibly cling to me even beyond death even despite their parasitic natures, no? I never wished to die, but the pain left no better alternative. So, I wish for you only this, my future self. Dabble in simple and happy matters but always remember an objective in life. For to deviate off your path is to lose direction. Far better it is to stay and decide, than it is to walk ahead blindly and fall into a trap. I only realised at the end of my days, that the dreams and goals I lost, were all achievable to an extent. Chase them, pursue them like a dog chases a bone! And when you seize them, remember that triumph is merely the beginning of success. Yours Sincerely, Augustine ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- *It* stared at me with cold eyes that seemed to herald in the cold Arctic winds. It straightened up, tensing its bones in such a way that you could almost see ripped muscles flex as they brought up the heavy scythe. It's unblemished mouth seemed to whisper sayings of the dead and screams of despair. Me though? I saw only salvation and hope. *Swooooosh*

Not the hardest thing on Earth and not the most original piece of work produced, but it's not the crappiest either. I liked their music and I found Breaking Benjammin out by playing a video game called Run Like Hell, a sci-fi action/horror third person shooter involving aliens. That game had several tracks from BB on some "boss battles" or dramatic parts of the game.I love this music as it really reflects a different and more attracting side to rock then other rock bands. I still like other bands like AC/DC, Nirvana and the such. But BB has class, don't mistake it. If I was you I'd pick this CD up, it's cheap too now. If you like it, grab We Are Not Alone, which is BB's second CD.They need more music!! And a RLH 2.

**BIOSHOCK INFINITE SPOILERS** ***Found on a voxophone near the ruins of Columbia...*** "It's a shame really." "Quite sad indeed." "I suppose the next go around he might manage better?" "No, he won't have." "'Won't have'? That doesn't sound right...perhaps 'will not have'?" "Unfortunately, we've no time to solicit grammar professors." "Indeed, I suppose we take a much more direct role..." "Or do we? Who's to say?" "I suppose by elimination, we've only one option." "Indeed, although 'one option' can be far more repetitive than it appears." "Perhaps I should make time to find that grammar professor." "Dear brother, as much as I enjoy this banter, we've a city to plunge into chaos and a prophet to drown." "Indeed, however I'm no Pinkerton detective and you're no time-space anomaly. Well, so to speak." "If I can master quantum mechanics, I can learn my way around a rifle or two. Perhaps you can throw me supplies." "Nonsense, you can't even row a boat." "Correction, I don't row."

At first it was shocking, but then it became normal. He told himself as if the sweet lie was enough to smother him to sleep. It wasn't. If one can get accustomed to this, they can get accustom to anything. But he can't, the fresh thoughts of suicide has always ran in his mind, he always says this will be the last time he will do it, yet it always carries him through, he will feel horrible, there was another side of him that has tempt him many times with the idea of delusions, hiding away what he was doing, coating it in a even sweeter lie, he can't stomach it. He will not fall to the level of self-deception, he was a noble being, not just a person that goes around the street in the middle of the night killing and raping anything that moves. No one else can take this burden that he carries, only he can do it, he was the chosen one, he knows it was wrong but he won't delude himself into thinking whatever he does even slightly resembles good, his selfless hedonistic ideals requires no delusions, when he does it, he do it, no matter how horrible it may look and sound. He did it for his pleasure and the demise of his of his well being afterwards, he, the one and only, sacrificed his well-being for the quickening sense of pleasure. He let the thoughts of suicide run his mind even more, this was what normal people do when they are forced to do whatever he is doing now. Jesus Christ was him. He was Jesus Christ. (Messy mind of a serial killer?)

Liv Garfield is set to become the youngest woman ever to lead a FTSE 100 company. At 38, she will make history when she leaves BT early next year to become head of water giant Severn Trent, a move that places her in one of the most exclusive groups in British corporate life.

Currently, only three FTSE 100 companies—airline easyJet, cigarette firm Imperial Tobacco, and fashion label Burberry—have female chief executives. With Royal Mail, led by Canadian Moya Greene, also expected to enter the index next month, Mrs. Garfield will be the fifth female chief executive when she takes up her new role. The Footsie blue chip index lists the 100 biggest companies on the London Stock Exchange, including giants like Vodafone, Shell, and Tesco.

From travelling 90 miles to football matches every weekend with her dad, to bringing up two sons, Garfield is used to being in what she calls a "man’s world." Her philosophy: "You have got to earn your place based on your own qualities and abilities." This approach has clearly worked; in September, she was ranked number ten in Fortune Magazine’s hottest rising business stars, making her the highest-ranked Briton alongside success stories like Facebook boss Mark Zuckerberg and Twitter founder Jack Dorsey.

Despite her hugely successful business career, Mrs. Garfield still harbors a childhood ambition. She told the Mail: ‘I said for my entire childhood that I wanted to be the Everton manager, so that was my absolute ambition. My mum had my bedroom with pink and fluffy clouds and I spent my childhood pinning up Everton pin-ups over them.’ As the granddaughter of an Everton football club groundsman, she would travel every weekend from Harrogate, 90 miles away, with her dad and granddad to watch the team. Now a Cambridge graduate, she takes her two young sons to games most weekends, even trying to dissuade her youngest, aged two, from switching his support to Chelsea.

Her husband, Morgan, runs a property investment fund. Mrs. Garfield recalled her biggest mistake as buying a sports car, only to discover she was pregnant soon after. ‘I couldn’t get into it,’ she said. But that was the only time family life held her back, and she asserts she never encountered the ‘glass ceiling’ that prevents many women from rising to the top. ‘I didn’t find getting back on the ladder [after motherhood] hard,’ she added.

Garfield was educated at the independent Birklands Belmont School (now the £8,733-a-year Belmont Grosvenor) in Birstwith, near Harrogate, and then Bootham School in York, which charges up to £15,990 annually for day pupils and £26,115 for borders. During school holidays, she helped her parents run their project management business. After studying French and German at Cambridge, she worked at management consultancy Accenture for six years before joining BT in 2000.

Her new role at Severn Trent, which supplies water to about four million households across the Midlands and Wales, could pay up to £2.4 million a year, leading the largest water company listed on the London Stock Exchange. She described leaving BT as a ‘huge wrench’ but expressed confidence it would continue to thrive, adding, ‘I am really looking forward to joining Severn Trent.’

The officer glares at me as I stand with my back turned. I hover over my victim, my mission finally complete.  
“Why did you do it? Why did you kill all those innocent people?”  
*Because they weren’t as innocent as they pretended to be.*  
The officer’s hand remains on the trigger, ready to fire. I can feel the cold barrel of his gun zeroing in on me, like a real-life game of *Where’s Waldo*. Sweat slowly trickles from his chin—nervous, or maybe just confused.  
“What did these people ever do to deserve death? Aside from meeting a lunatic like you!”  
*Well, this ‘innocent’ man you’re defending murdered his best friend and framed the friend’s brother. That’s right. This ‘innocent’ man sent another innocent person to an early grave.*  
*Don’t worry. He was the last on my list. Please... just walk away. Let’s end this without any more bloodshed.*  
The officer hesitates. He fights with his uncertainty, quickly shaking it away.  
“You know I can’t do that. I made a vow to uphold justice. Maybe if you come quietly, we can work something out.”  
I can’t help but smile at his naivety.  
*Officer, do you know what happened to the best friend who died?* I turn around so he can finally see my face.  
*That child was pronounced dead and buried. But he woke up from a trauma-induced coma, just in time to break free from the coffin in the back of the hearse.*  
I watch a tear roll down his cheek.  
*I’m that child, Officer. The justice system was too flawed to convict the real killer. Don’t you understand? I had to do it.*  
I know this is my last chance. I won’t rot in prison like my brother. I reach for my gun. The officer fires three shots, and everything fades to black.  
“I’m here to hand in my gun and badge, Chief.” The officer sets them on the table and walks away.  
*It was the final time he could serve a broken system. The last victim knew what would happen when he reached for the gun, but he did it anyway. Both were chasing their own versions of justice. The officer couldn’t bear the weight or the guilt any longer.*  
That would be the last time he ever killed.

The Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation was created in January 2000 by multi-billionaire Bill Gates and his wife. The Foundation took form through the merger of the Gates Learning Foundation, which worked to expand access to technology through public libraries, and the William H. Gates Foundation (named after Bill's father), which focused on improving global health. William H. Gates is now the President of the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation.
This Seattle-based Foundation has an endowment of approximately $24 billion, making it the largest charitable organization in the world. Its self-identified mission is to help "reduce inequities" in four primary areas:
a) Its Global Health program works "to close the gap between rich and poor countries by encouraging new research and supporting healthcare organizations that reach people most in need." Funding in this area encompasses global health technologies and strategies, infectious diseases, HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, and reproductive health services
b) The Education program seeks "to ensure that all students in the United States graduate from high school ready for college, work, and citizenship." It makes large grants to black and Hispanic (or "historically underserved") youth.c) The Global Libraries program, which is subdivided into the U.S. Library Program and International Library Initiatives, strives to provide people in the United States and abroad with free Internet access at public libraries. A major objective is "to increase access to technology for residents in low-income and disadvantaged communities."
d) The Pacific Northwest program "works with public and private partners to make a positive impact on the lives of vulnerable families and children in Washington state and Greater Portland, Oregon." Philanthropy in this area is directed toward housing, early learning, and other community service programs.
On June 25, 2006, Warren Buffett (who was then the world's richest person, with an estimated worth approaching $60 billion) pledged to give the Gates Foundation approximately 10 million Berkshire Hathaway Class B shares through annual contributions disbursed over multiple years. In 2006, Buffett gave Gates 500,000 of the 10 million shares -- or 5% of the total -- and those half-million shares had an aggregate value of approximately $1.5 billion. By the terms of Buffett's arrangement with Gates, Buffett would continue to give the Foundation 5% of the remaining earmarked shares in the July of each subsequent year (i.e., 475,000 in 2007; 451,250 in 2008; etc.). Buffett dictated this arrangement so that his yearly donations would not simply increase the Gates Foundation's endowment, but rather would work as a matching contribution, thereby doubling the Foundation's annual giving.
The Gates Foundation is so enormous that the financial portion of its 990-PF form submitted to the IRS, which lists all of its investments, fills nearly 1,500 pages. While the Foundation’s grantmaking is by no means exclusively targeted toward the political left, among the groups it supports are: the Aspen Institute; the Carter Center; the Council on Foundations; Global Justice; the National Committee for Responsive Philanthropy (NCRP); the National Council of La Raza; Physicians for Human Rights; Planned Parenthood; the Progressive Policy Institute; the Tides Center and the Tides Foundation; the United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF); the United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs; the United Nations Foundation; the United States Student Association; the Urban League; the World Resources Institute; and World Vision International.
To view a list of additional noteworthy grantees of the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, click here.
(Information on grantees and monetary amounts courtesy of The Foundation Center, GuideStar, ActivistCash, the Capital Research Center and Undue Influence)

Hendrick's excellent run set up Walters to find the net on 69 minutes as the Irish consolidated their grip on third place in Group D.
"Jeff was absolutely magnificent. He has that ability and it was great to see Walters scoring," said O'Neill.
"We didn't play well in the first half but then there was a great turnaround."
With Scotland losing 3-2 at home to Germany, the Republic are now four points ahead of Gordon Strachan's side with two rounds of fixtures remaining next month.
Media playback is not supported on this device
The Republic host leaders Germany and visit second-placed Poland, while Scotland welcome the Poles before visiting Gibraltar in their final game.
The third-placed team with the best qualifying record from the nine groups qualifies automatically for next summer's tournament in France, with the other eight facing a two-legged play-off.
O'Neill says his side will "have a go" as they aim to at least secure a play-off spot.
"We need to find a win in two matches," he said. "We'll have to play better for the whole game.
"Germany, with respect, are not only the best team in the group, they are world champions, and I'd say at this minute, possibly even the best side in Europe, so we will have to naturally do better for 90 minutes in the game."
O'Neill was delighted by his team's second-half endeavour.
"We found a way to win the second half," he said. "We were very tentative in the first half - I'm not sure why we should be.
"The players came on very, very strongly in the second half and we deserved to get the goal and we deserved to win the game."
O'Neill praised Derby midfielder Hendrick, who weaved past three challenges before squaring for Stoke forward Walters to register his seventh international goal.
"It was a great, great run and to keep a little bit of composure as well with the final ball coming in that somebody is going to come in on top of that as Jonathan Walters did."

After our three kids grew up and moved out, we slowly drifted apart. I spent my time in the garage and barn, fixing the tractor, the pickup, the motorcycle, or our orange Roadrunner. It was bright orange with a scoop on the hood for better airflow. Our first date car. I clearly remember her: that white dress, her grandmother's opera gloves, her black hair in a curly wave, and her sunglasses perched on her head. Am I bitter? No. When I'm not fixing things, I'm online talking to some woman. My wife doesn't care. These things happen. I'm not the girl I used to be. Sitting on the hood of my Roadrunner, the one that became ours. Jeans, cowboy boots, an Allman Brothers shirt under my flannel shirt. A beat-up cowboy hat, or a John Deere baseball cap on my head. My husband is always in the garage or barn, fixing things. He hardly pays me any attention. So I've started talking to a guy online. Tonight I'm meeting him. I'm sure I can slip away for a while. I see her leave, then I go online. The message says: meet me at the Stomping Ground, the local bar, pizza place, and pool hall. The place we always went. I once carved our initials into the wall outside. I pull up in the station wagon. It still runs surprisingly well after all these years. I walk in and sit at the end of the bar. The bartender puts a Rolling Rock bottle in front of me. The kind I drank in college, back when I was younger and wilder. Sneaking Marlboros outside, that's how wild. What the heck? There's a Marlboro red taped to the bottle. Someone taps my shoulder. It must be the guy I'm meeting. I guess I forgot to tell him about my past. I've been sitting here a while when I see my wife walk in. Her face, lined under a yellow bandanna, her long black hair in wild curls. The sunglasses on her head. An Allman Brothers shirt, jeans, and a leather jacket. I send her a Rolling Rock and a Marlboro cigarette. What she used to like. Oh my God, I feel my anger rise as I tap her shoulder.

Jack Sheldon delivers a delightful duet with Anita O'Day in "I Told Ya I Love Ya Now Get Out." As of 2010, this track was featured on YouTube in an entertaining slide show—search for it by song title. While Sheldon is perhaps best recognized as the voice of Louie The Lightning Bug in television electrical safety announcements, his true legacy lies in his contributions to jazz music. Sheldon toured with Benny Goodman and Anita O'Day in 1959, and in Roy Eldridge's absence, he stepped in to perform O'Day's signature "Let Me Off Uptown" with her. This performance survives in an air check and can be found on "You Betcha."

I award this recording four stars, reserving the fifth only to avoid diminishing the twenty other albums in her catalog that merit perfect ratings. Anita O'Day never produced mediocre work. For newcomers to her artistry, the Avid collection *Four Classic Albums* offers an excellent starting point and exceptional value.

*I looked down at the prone form of the Korean man on the bed. I may never fully understand the emotions of these people around me, but if my studies had taught me anything, I should feel...pity...in this situation. He looked frail in the position he was in, but so did every animal in an unconscious state. * As I reached to touch his face, a spark of static leaped across the gap from my finger to his cheek. My body shivered slightly, but I ignored that. I slapped him gently on the face to wake him. "Great Leader, how are you feeling?" I asked him as I poured a glass of water. Particulate matter swirled around as the water rose in the glass. *Disgusting. * Kim Jong-Il opened his eyes, looked up at my face, and slammed his eyes shut again. His voiced wavered as he responded, "I'm feeling better today..." *That won't do. * "Drink this. It will help." He reached out, his eyes still shut tight, and I put the glass in his hand. He drank eagerly, swallowed, then lay back down. Silent. I left the room, closing the door softly, and turned to inspect the plain wall behind me. *That will do. * I exited the grand bedchamber, turning down the hallway to my office. I nodded at the guards as I walked by. They each inclined their heads in return, replying in hushed unison: "Great leader..."

The alleged terror incident aboard a passenger flight from Amsterdam to Detroit has raised questions as to how a  Nigerian man carried explosives through stringent security measures. Umar Farouk Abdulmutallab has been charged with attempting to destroy a passenger plane after he detonated a device on board a jet on Friday. Authorities in the United States are investigating whether Abdulmutallab had any connections with terrorist organizations or was acting alone. Airports intensify security after plane attack . With Dutch officials scrutinizing security procedures at Amsterdam's Schiphol airport that allowed the 23-year-old man to smuggle the explosives on the aircraft -- here are some of the key questions still hanging over the incident. Where did the explosives come from? The man charged with igniting the device claims he obtained the explosives in Yemen, along with instruction on how to use it. He carried these onto a flight from Lagos, Nigeria, to Amsterdam, Netherlands where he transferred to Northwest Flight 253 to Detroit. Was he on any security watch lists? U.S. authorities say they were contacted by the man's father ahead of the attack, but whether he was placed on any so-called No-Fly list maintained by the United States is unclear. Dutch officials say the passenger's name appeared on a manifest supplied by the airline, which was passed to U.S. authorities, who cleared the flight to depart. Were the proper security checks performed? Yes, say Dutch authorities, who say he passed through normal procedures and that "security was well-performed." He is known to have passed through a metal detector and his luggage was X-rayed. Extra attention is normally applied to passengers arriving from Nigeria because of concerns over fraud and smuggling. Nevertheless airports around the world have stepped up security procedures in the wake of the incident, increasing pat-downs and secondary searches. So how did the explosives get through? Dutch authorities are at pains to point out that if the passenger had powders concealed upon his person or secreted bottles of liquid somewhere else, they certainly would not have been picked up by the metal detector. Is there any way of detecting these kind of explosives? A preliminary FBI analysis indicates the device contained PETN, also known as Pentaerythritol Tetranitrate, a highly explosive chemical. Experts say this would have been picked up using a swab commonly used in secondary screening. A body scan, particularly the new 3D imaging scanners being trailed at some airports, would also have spotted something strapped to his body, even in the crotch area. Why were there no U.S. air marshal security officers on the flight? U.S. Homeland Security Secretary Janet Napolitano told CNN federal air marshals are not posted on all flights. She said this was not due to budgetary constraints, but down to standard procedures, which see marshals posted randomly on certain routes. CNN's Jeanne Meserve, Richard Quest and Barry Neild contributed to this report.

Marin Cilic made a triumphant return to the ATP Tour on Monday, claiming a hard-fought three-set victory over Dutchman Igor Sijsling at the Paris Masters. The 25-year-old Croatian, recently reinstated after serving a reduced doping suspension, overcame Sijsling 5-7, 6-1, 6-4 in Bercy Stadium. Cilic, who has dropped to World No. 47, tested positive for the stimulant nikethamide at the Munich Open in May, prompting a nine-month ban from an independent tribunal in September. However, the Court of Arbitration for Sport reduced his suspension to four months last Friday, paving the way for his participation in the season’s final regular tournament in the French capital.

Cilic seized the opportunity, rebounding from a first-set loss to set up a second-round clash with in-form World No. 5 Juan Martin Del Potro of Argentina. Reflecting on his ordeal, Cilic admitted relief at closing a painful chapter. “It was definitely the worst time of my life as a player,” he told AFP. “I’ve been on the tour for six, seven years and have always been extremely careful, honest, and fair with other players. To be in a situation where I was labeled a doping player and a cheat was incredibly tough. I knew I hadn’t cheated,” Cilic added.

World No. 1 Rafael Nadal, making his first appearance in Paris in four years, expressed his support for Cilic’s return. “I’m happy to see Marin back on tour. He’s a good guy and a great player,” Nadal said. “I don't know the details, but if he’s back, it’s because it’s fair. I’m happy for that.”

While Nadal prepares to launch his campaign later in the week, early attention centers on the battle for remaining spots at the ATP World Tour Finals in London. Roger Federer, fellow Swiss Stanislas Wawrinka, and Frenchmen Richard Gasquet and Jo-Wilfried Tsonga are vying for the last three berths in the elite eight-man field, with several others still mathematically in contention. Wawrinka learned his second-round opponent would be Spain’s Feliciano Lopez, who edged out Australian qualifier Bernard Tomic 6-4, 6-7, 7-6 on Monday. Federer, requiring one more victory to secure his twelfth consecutive finals appearance, will face the winner of the opening-round match between Kevin Anderson and Mikhail Youzhny, while Gasquet and Tsonga could potentially clash in the third round of their home event.

Last season, World No. 2 Novak Djokovic captured the ATP Finals crown, defeating Federer in the title match. Nadal, who has yet to win the prestigious year-end championship, is determined to use Paris as a launching pad. “The last tournament of the year is important,” Nadal told the ATP Tour website. “In 2010 I played well (I lost in the final to Federer); the rest, I played really badly. I want to change that. I’ve always been motivated for this part of the season, but I haven’t been able to apply it well. I hope this year is different. I hope to play well here and then we’ll see.”

Paul Nicholl, 50, of Carnkenny Road, Newtownstewart, drank beer and took diazepam before the collision which killed GAA fan Patrick McCroary.
He was jailed on Tuesday for two-and-a half-years.
He will serve a further two-and-a-half years on licence and has been banned from driving for six years.
In a statement, the McCroary family called the sentence a "real insult".
"I feel as a family we have been really let down by the justice system," the statement said.
It added: "I feel the justice system in regards to drink driving has not just failed our family but failed many families in recent times and will continue to do so until the law changes.
"So how can he take my Dad's life but have his own back after two-and-a-half years? Where is the justice in this?"
The crash happened near Sion Mills, when Mr McCroary, 62, was travelling towards Strabane with his wife, son and daughter.
The rest of the family were badly injured, but survived the crash.
Nicholl was two-and-a-half-times over the drink-drive limit when he caused the crash in January 2014.
The statement continued: "As every day goes by we miss Daddy more and more. Paul Nicholl has caused a lot of pain and heartache to our family.
"By his selfish act of drink-driving he has torn our family apart as all dad lived for was his family and GAA."

Bryan Oviedo admitted he left South Wales with mixed emotions after Everton were beaten 3-0 by Swansea in the Capital One cup on Tuesday. The  Costa Rican defender hadn't played since breaking his leg January's FA Cup clash against Stevenage, but started for the Toffees at the Liberty Stadium. And although he was relieved to be back playing, the left back was disappointed at the nature of Everton's 3-0 defeat. Bryan Oviedo was delighted to be back after eight months out and salutes the Everton fans . The Costa Rican was frustrated at his side's display in losing 3-0 to Swansea at the Liberty Stadium . The defender hadn't played since breaking his leg when tackled by Stevenage's Simon Hislop in January . He said: 'Of course I feel very happy. I want to play. This was a nice opportunity but I don't agree with the result. Swansea played well and scored goals. Games like this happen sometimes. 'It was very emotional to be in the starting line-up again. I was very excited when I knew I would be. 'I had been waiting for eight months and tonight I had the chance. I tried to give everything but the result wasn't good enough. Given his lack of match fitness, it was unsurprising that Oviedo was substituted after 58 minutes . The popular left back said he felt 'very emotional' to be back playing, and was extremely pleased to start . 'Now we need to go home and look at what went wrong.' Despite not playing for eight months, Everton fans have not stopped singing the 'Oviedo Baby' chant in honour of the popular defender, and the Toffees fans gave the returning Costa Rican a warm reception in South Wales on Tuesday. Oviedo added: 'It was a nice moment. I am very happy. I want to say 'thank you' for that reaction. 'I have been out a long time and it is difficult but when I come and see the support it is amazing. 'All throughout my recovery Evertonians have given me their support. I want to thank them all for that.' Marvin Emnes celebrates putting Swansea 3-0 up in their comfortable victory over Everton on Tuesday .

I got the falafel wrap and honestly, I was pretty grossed out by what I witnessed. The person making it washed their hands before starting (which I appreciated), but then immediately blew their nose into the towel they'd just used to dry them. My heart sank – like, why even bother washing your hands at that point?

Then I bit into lukewarm falafel that had clearly been sitting around in a fryer basket from who knows when. So disappointing when you're craving something fresh and flavorful.

The menu promised rice and/or lentils with the wrap, so I asked if I could get them on the side. Just a flat "no." It felt unnecessarily rigid, especially when I'm already paying for those ingredients.

At $6.55, this costs basically the same as Pita Jungle but delivers maybe half the quality. That stung – I hate feeling like I wasted my money on something that could have been so much better.

It's frustrating because I can see the potential here. With just a little more care and attention, this place could actually be great. But right now, it's hard to justify the price for what you get.

California is clearly working hard to create an economy based on green energy and environmentally friendly jobs. For example, it has rules in place to get 20% of its energy from renewable sources by the end of 2013, 25% by 2016, and 33% by 2020. Governor Jerry Brown also wants 12,000 megawatts of solar power, specifically from many smaller sources like rooftop panels, by 2020.

However, a new report from an environmental group suggests California needs to do even more. They want to ensure that the 15,000 people who join green job training programs each year can actually find jobs afterward.

The group, Environment California, stated: “To keep the clean energy economy growing and to deliver on the promise of new jobs for people finishing green training programs, California needs to stick to its commitment to strong, ambitious clean energy rules.” They added, “Specifically, California should create tough new rules for cleaner cars from 2017 to 2025. It should also fully put in place the plans to reach Governor Brown’s goal of 12,000 megawatts of distributed energy sources (like solar panels on roofs) by 2020. And, it needs to aim even higher than the state's current goal of 33% renewable energy by 2020.”

The Environment California report also pointed out that about 15,000 students attend 300 green job training programs at 130 different places in California every year. More than 500,000 people already have green jobs in the state. Experts believe the number of jobs focused on energy efficiency could at least double by 2020. However, supporters of these jobs warn this growth won't happen if California stops working hard on it.

Nicole Capretz, who directs the Green Energy/Green Jobs Campaign (part of the Environmental Health Coalition, focused on creating good green jobs), explained: “Currently, the students learning these skills and their teachers are ahead of the politicians and rules that create the demand for green jobs.” She added, “We need strong policies to quickly grow the clean energy job market. This way, we can employ these tens of thousands of skilled workers.”

It had been over a decade since my last trip to Botanas, so it was well overdue. Not much has changed in the last ten years but at least the place is clean and well maintained. I found the dated decor charming and fitting as opposed to distracting. Not every bite of good food needs amazing ambiance. Speaking of good bites, that's exactly what I had. Delicious, well-prepared food. The shrimp was cooked perfectly and the sauce (red) was good enough to want to soak up heavily with tortilla shells for each bite. It's not a convenient location for me but I still hope it's less than a decade until I return again.

By Daniel Martin, Whitehall Correspondent. FIRST PUBLISHED: 19:59 EST, 12 August 2013 | LAST UPDATED: 19:59 EST, 12 August 2013.  
The Duke of York became entangled in the royal lobbying controversy yesterday after it was disclosed that he has conducted 11 confidential meetings with Cabinet ministers since the general election.  
Over the last three years, he has hosted a succession of leading politicians—such as Chancellor George Osborne, Business Secretary Vince Cable, and Education Secretary Michael Gove—for discussions at Buckingham Palace. He met with Foreign Secretary William Hague on two occasions and also held two sessions with Andrew Mitchell during his time as International Development Secretary.  
A Buckingham Palace spokesperson stated the Duke of York regularly interacts with a wide variety of individuals and that all his meetings, including those with government officials, are documented in the Court Circular.  
Prince Andrew also held a meeting with Chris Huhne when he served as Energy Secretary and has had conversations with two Northern Ireland Secretaries, Owen Paterson and Theresa Villiers. He later reconnected with Mr Paterson following his move to Environment Secretary. The Duke even summoned the head of the Civil Service, Cabinet Secretary Sir Jeremy Heywood, to the Palace for consultations.  
Records show that the Prince maintained his engagement with senior officials even after relinquishing his disputed role as the UK’s Special Representative for International Trade and Investment in 2011—a resignation prompted by criticism over his suggested associations with questionable foreign governments like Azerbaijan.  
During his decade in that role, he garnered the nickname ‘Airmiles Andy’. Michael Gove, pictured, was one of several senior ministers invited to meet the Duke at Buckingham Palace.  
News of the Duke’s hosting of top-level meetings follows a Daily Mail report that Prince Charles has arranged 36 closed-door meetings with Cabinet ministers since the election—about one per month. Although Clarence House explained that these meetings are intended to prepare him for his future responsibilities as king, detractors accused Charles of a ‘relentless lobbying’ effort on favored topics such as climate change, alternative medicine, and architecture.  
Last night, Labour MP Paul Flynn questioned the necessity of Prince Andrew’s private discussions with ministers, pointing out that he holds no official public position. ‘What business does he have with William Hague?’ Flynn asked. ‘The Duke of York has maintained close ties with some unpleasant regimes in the past. We expect transparency from lobbyists about their contacts, and the Royal Family should be held to the same standard.’  
A Buckingham Palace spokesperson responded: ‘The Duke engages with a wide range of individuals as part of his work, which at times includes government ministers. All official meetings are recorded in the Court Circular.’

A steady, mechanical pulse from the respirator saturated the room, its sound as constant as a heartbeat. Rising through the floorboards, I watched a sulfuric cloud unfurl, trailing across the carpet like an omen. *Is this really my test?* I wondered, unsettled. *Is this the victim I must destroy to ascend to the third rank?* 

A young woman sagged in a battered chair, hoses snaking into her veins and nose. Her gaze was blank, unfocused; her eyes were open, but comprehension did not live there. *Child’s play,* I thought with a sneer.

Drawing on my power, I twisted the environment: bedding convulsed, curtains twirled in a silent storm, and a stuffed teddy bear unraveled itself, its stuffing drifting in clouds to the floor. The girl remained utterly inert, not a muscle twitching. 

Shadows crawled up the walls, which began to bleed. The wooden boards beneath us groaned and bent, while the wailing of lost souls echoed through the gloom. Even then, the only response was a single string of drool gliding down from the corner of her slack mouth.

Losing patience, I screamed directly at her—"HEAVEN HAS FORGOTTEN YOU! YOU WILL SPEND FOREVER BURNING IN HELL!" Not a flicker crossed her face. Frustration mounting, I grabbed her chin, forcing her head up. "YOU'LL—"

Her eyes locked onto mine. Suddenly, all the coldness, the brightness, the dangerous light in her stare burned through me. I couldn't tear my gaze away. My lungs seized; thoughts vanished. An agonizing pain erupted in every nerve, consuming me.

Trapped, helpless, I realized: A young woman slouched motionless in her chair. If you concentrated, perhaps a faint, acrid tang of sulfur lingered nearby. In the background, the respirator’s rhythm pressed on—a relentless, inhuman heartbeat.
