Why does French dressmaker Chanel always seem chic? What does British seamstress Vivienne Westwood have that others don't? And even though it's been 25 years since "Top Gun", why does everybody still want a pair of Ray Bans? The short answer is, they're just really cool. It might be a subjective concept -- one man's cool Hawaiian party shirt is another's discarded Christmas gift -- but certain brands do seem to stay in fashion forever. It's a phenomenon highlighted by the UK's annual top 20 "Cool Brands" list released today. Top of the pile is Aston Martin, James Bond's car-maker of choice, followed by Apple and motorbike designer Harley Davidson. But numerous fashion labels make it onto the list, with Rolex highest among them at number four, followed by Nike, British designer Alexander McQueen and perennially cool Ray Ban sunglasses. Why denim never fades from fashion . So, how did these brands manage to acquire this ineffable quality -- at least in the eyes of the Brits? We ask chief executive of the Centre for Brand Analysis and "CoolBrand" council chairman Stephen Cheliotis for his six rules of cool. 1. Style is everything . From the door handles to the website and the tags on the clothes to marketing events: If people are to be persuaded a brand is the height of cool, the devil is in the detail, says Cheliotis. "It's not just about making your clothes look good," he insists. "Absolutely everything associated with the brand has to exude style -- from the packaging to the company website, to the type-face on the inside label." 2. Never try too hard . No one likes a busybody and, says Cheliotis, even the word "cool" suggests a certain relaxed approach. "The original idea of being cool is being laid back, not running around like a headless chicken trying desperately hard to please everyone." This, he says, is true for people and fashion brands alike. So, if it feels like a brand is trying way too hard to impress -- chasing every trend going, for example -- then it probably won't. Celebrity fashion labels: The good, the bad and the ugly . 3. Stay true to the brand . It's not cool to fake it. In other words, if a company began life selling pretty knitwear, it should think very carefully before venturing into, say, leather jackets. "You must always remain true to your roots," instructs Cheliotis. "Shoppers want something authentic and it's obvious when you're out of your comfort zone." Designer Alexander McQueen may have recently passed away, he adds, but it doesn't mean that his eponymous label should be tempted to change its style. "As long as it (the label) sticks to his original vision it will probably stay on the list." 4) Keep evolving . This might sound at odds with the previous rule but, according to Cheliotis, it's certainly not. "One of the reasons we keep seeing the same brands on the list, like Nike and Rolex, is because they're always innovative, but at the same time they retain their core identity." 5) Don't be promiscuous . Some fashion houses squander their hard fought cool rep by plastering a company logo on everything that moves. In Cheliotis' view, bootmaker Dr. Martens have been guilty in recent years of such profligacy. "They were known for making really sturdy, simple and good quality footwear. Now they've plastered their name on everything, it's cheapened the brand and undermined their core product." 6) Be humble . A stark word of warning for self-satisfied designers everywhere. "Successful brands don't fixate over being cool, instead they just do their own their own thing, stick to their founding principles and follow what they think is right," says Cheliotis. In other words, if you think you're cool, you probably won't be for much longer.

"He's dead isn't he" "I know" "I didn't think God could die" "I didn't think God was real" "We are sure he is dead, aren't we?" "Certain" "Why?" "Because everybody knows" "But, how?" "We just do" "But that never seemed like enough of a reason before" "There is some evidence though, no one died last night" "So?" "The tides stopped working. The flowers didn't open." "Oh. So now what?" "I guess there isn't much we can do." "Isn't there someone else that can take his place?" "Who do you know that's all powerful?" "No one" "There isn't anyone." "What about Jesus?" "He was God, one can't survive without the other" "So we just carry on? As if nothing has happened?" "I don't think that's possible" "But we could try?" "I guess we could, for a while. There's not much else we can do"

I had to turn away from the Stars of Tzor before I could put it down. The name came from the sparkles that seemed to swim in the dark green gem. 


My son's wails rang sharply in my ears. I picked him up. One could easily get lost reminiscing about the glorious past: the crew (now mostly dead), the seven voyages (the longest took three years), and the wealth (seven times I returned with treasure). I patted Jake on the back and he calmed a little. 


The sixth time I returned to an empty house. The seventh time made me realize I didn't feel it anymore. Yeah, I just didn't feel it anymore.


---


**Changes made:**

- Removed the confusing parenthetical about *Reverie* crowing and 50 days

- Simplified the description of voyages and wealth

- Broke up the long, complex sentence into clearer parts

- Removed redundant phrases like "of course" and "And then my attention returned"

- Clarified the timeline about returning home

The carcass of a beaked whale has been found on a Cornish beach – only the second time the species has been recorded in the UK. The Blainville's whale, usually found in temperate and tropical waters, was discovered stranded on Kenneggy Beach, near Praa Sands in Cornwall, England. A member of the public reported it to the Cornwall Wildlife Trust as a porpoise, but experts confirmed it as a rare Blainville's beaked whale. Scroll down for video . Marine Stranding Network undated handout photo of one of their volunteers with a dead Blainville's whale that was found on Kenneggy Beach, near Praa Sands, Cornwall . Records dating back to 1913 show the only other one to reach UK shores was at Aberaeron in West Wales in 1993. However, experts believe Blainville's . will start stranding more frequently in Britain as water temperatures . increase due to climate change. A member of the public reported the . stranding to the Cornwall Wildlife Trust Marine Strandings Network as a . porpoise on December 30 2013. But when the Network's data officer, . Niki Clear, received photographs of the animal, measuring 3.8m (12ft . 5in) long, she recognised it as an elusive Blainville. Cetacean . experts Colin MacLeod at Aberdeen University and Richard C. Sabin from . The Natural History Museum in London later confirmed its identity. The 3.8 meter long whale was reported to the Cornwall Wildlife Trust Marine Strandings Network as a porpoise, but when the Network's data officer received photos of the animal she was suspicious that it was in fact a whale only recorded twice in the whole of the UK . Abby Crosby, . Marine Conservation Officer for Cornwall Wildlife Trust, said: 'We're . very pleased that people took the trouble to report this animal to us, . as by recording the carcasses of these creatures marine biologists can . gather information about the species to help towards their conservation. 'This carcass was in a poor condition with advanced decomposition, . so we would suggest that the public do not touch any dead marine animals . they find, as they can carry diseases that humans can catch. 'It was . a sad end for this amazing whale. However by examining these strandings . it at least gives us a chance to investigate these animals properly and . learn more about them. And the more we know, the better we're able to . fight for their protection.' Examinations found the whale was a . sub-adult or maturing male Blainville's beaked whale. Blainville's, like . most beaked whales, are mostly found off-shore in waters 200m (656ft) to 1,000m (3,280ft) along continental shelves. These elusive whales, rarely seen in UK waters, have distinctive high arched lower jaw; the males have a more pronounced arch and a tusk-like tooth at the crest of each arch . The species have a . distinctive high arched lower jaw, with males showing a more pronounced . arch and tusk-like tooth at the crest of each arch. The Cornwall carcass was a 12ft 5in long maturing male which will be studied for clues to help in the conservation of the species. The whales, named after a 19th century French zoologist, have a high arched lower jaw and long beak. They are dark brown to grey in colour, with many tooth rake marks and circular scars along the body caused by cookie cutter shark and parasites, which are accumulated with age. Syd Trudgen, a volunteer for Marine Stranding Network, was called to the beach after the stranding was reported. Mr Trudgen said: 'It is very rewarding to be a part of an organisation that looks after and speaks up for dolphins, seals, whales and turtles.'

A 12-year study reveals improved American eating habits, except among the poor. The Harvard School of Public Health confirms a widening wealth gap in diet. Even among wealthier adults, food choices fall short of ideal.

On an index where 110 represents a perfect healthy eating score, U.S. adults averaged only 40 points in 1999-2000, rising to just 47 points by 2009-10. Low-income adults consistently scored below average, showing negligible improvement over the study period. Their scores averaged nearly four points lower than high-income adults initially, a disparity that widened to over six points by 2009-10.

Higher scores indicate greater intake of heart-healthy foods—vegetables, fruits, whole grains, and healthy fats—and correlate with a low risk of obesity and chronic illnesses such as heart disease, strokes, and diabetes. Conversely, low scores predict increased susceptibility to these ailments.

The widening rich-poor diet gap is profoundly disconcerting and will have significant public health implications, stated study co-author Dr. Frank Hu of the Harvard School of Public Health. He noted that diet-linked chronic diseases, particularly diabetes, are escalating among Americans, especially the poor. "Declining diet quality over time will undeniably widen the gap between the poor and the rich," Hu asserted.

Harvard School of Public Health researchers developed and utilized a healthy diet index for this study. This index, while similar to federal dietary guidelines, expands upon them with additional categories such as red and processed meats, sugar-sweetened beverages, and alcohol. The study authors rigorously applied this index, integrating government estimates on trans fat intake, to evaluate data from 1999-2010 national health surveys, which comprised detailed interviews on eating habits. The findings are published Monday in JAMA Internal Medicine.

Hu asserted the widening diet gap directly reflects an income gap that deepened during the recent financial crisis, rendering healthy food unaffordable for many. Hu further noted the pervasive availability of inexpensive, highly processed foods in low-income neighborhoods. While overall diet improvement stemmed primarily from decreased trans fat intake, these disquieting results unequivocally demand policy changes, including improved nutrition education, Hu stated.

In recent years, government and manufacturers have initiated a phase-out of artificial trans fats in foods such as processed cookies, cakes, frozen pizza, and margarines. Trans fats are known contributors to unhealthy cholesterol levels and elevate heart disease risks. These fats are created by hydrogenating vegetable oil to enhance texture and shelf life. The study authors confirm their findings align with an earlier report demonstrating that "nearly the entire U.S. population fell short of meeting federal dietary recommendations."

Federal guidelines are subject to a five-year review cycle, with updated recommendations due next year. Current recommendations stress the imperative of limiting trans fats, sodium, processed foods, and added sugars. While not specifying exact amounts, they advocate for diets rich in whole grains, vegetables, and fruits. The Harvard index mirrors this emphasis, offering specific targets: a top score requires daily consumption of over two cups of vegetables, at least four servings of fruit, and at least one ounce of nuts.

A JAMA Internal Medicine editorial acknowledges imperfections in the Harvard diet index, noting its equal emphasis on foods that may not contribute uniformly to health. Nevertheless, the editorial affirms the study highlights a "growing chasm" that constitutes a significant public health concern. It contends that current government efforts, such as food stamp programs, may prove inadequate in closing this gap, and proposes that restricting government benefits to solely cover healthful foods could be a superior strategy.

I thought I saw you again last night. I was walking home and someone ran across the road and for *just* a moment they seemed like they might be you, but they turned into the light and I knew it couldn't be you, not really. I still think about you every single day though. You deserve an explanation and I wish I could give you a better one, other than I got scared and letting go of you seemed like the best thing. I swear to you that at the time I thought it would be better for everyone, not just me. There's no way that you can see it as anything other than selfish and stupid though. I was sixteen and my whole life seemed to be ahead of me. When your mum, Carol, that was her name if you didn't know, when she came round and told me that she was pregnant, I kind of froze. She wouldn't make eye contact and I kept asking her "What are we going to do? What are we going to do?" I know what I *wanted* her to answer, but I was too cowardly to suggest it and too stupid to realise that I should have asked for help from my parents, or really anyone. She told me that she wanted to think about it for a while and so I said okay and she left. It was a week later that one of her friends came up to me and said that she'd gone away for a bit, off to visit family and somehow with her out of sight it seemed like the problem went away. She came back about eight months later and in all honestly I think I had forced myself to forget. I had forced myself to pretend that it would all go away. It hadn't though and you were ready to come into the world. You were born in November and it was a bloody cold day, I remember that much about it. I got a call from her dad at about 10am and he told me you were coming. I asked if I should come to the hospital and he said no, then put the phone down. He was probably right. I got to see you the next day though. You were tiny, far too small they kept telling me, but I didn't know how small babies should be. I just watched you as you reached out in the incubator for the warmth of your mother who was stuck on the other side of the glass with me. She asked me what I wanted to call you and I didn't know, so we decided to wait. I went home and cried until I was raw and then I made a promise, a promise that I would break immediately. I promised myself that no matter what I would be there for you, that I would step up and be the man you needed. You died at 3:12am and I got a call nearly two hours later. My mum answered from her bedroom and called me through to take the phone. Her voice was filled with panic as she yelled into the dark house for me and I knew, I just somehow *knew*. I took the phone and listened to the doctor and then I put it down without saying anything. Then I broke the promise. I took some clothes, packed a bag, left the house and left without saying goodbye. While your mum buried you and mourned you, I kept moving, never stopping. I never came back. I never said goodbye. I never tried to be the man I thought I was. I can't go home now and so I see you, the ghost of the girl that I didn't have. Last night I followed the stranger for a while, until I was lost in my thoughts and they had slipped away somewhere unseen, leaving me walking down the street by myself. I don't know why I followed them, I think I just wanted to pretend for a while. Maybe one day I can turn around and go back, to find the remains of the life I left behind. Please just believe me that I'm sorry, I really am sorry.

Gowns were inspired by the Charleston and came in fluffy ostrich feathers in bold reds, powder blues and mustards. The ornate gowns were sometimes slashed from the chest to the naval, but the long sleeves and floor-length hemlines kept a certain conservatism about them. This article looks at how farmers in Somaliland buy and sell their sheep and goats, and whether they understand the local system for judging animal quality and setting prices.

The study found that sheep and goats are very important for earning money in farming families. Most farmers knew how the local grading system worked (how animals are judged and priced), and this knowledge helped them participate in markets. The main things that affected whether farmers joined the market were: how many animals they owned, whether men or women made the selling decisions, how old the head of the household was, and what type of area they lived in.

Download the article: Wanyoike, F., Mtimet, N., Ndiwa, N., Marshall, K., Godiah, L. and Warsame, A. 2015. Knowledge of livestock grading and market participation among small ruminant producers in northern Somalia. East African Agricultural and Forestry Journal 81(1):64-70.

The eponymous award recognised the singer's "artistic talent and influence".

Swift told the audience: "If they had chosen someone else to give the Taylor Swift Award to, I'd be kind of bummed about it."

It is only the second time the BMI has created an award in an artist's name. The first was Michael Jackson in 1990.

The BMI - whose full name is Broadcast Music, Inc - is a major US music rights organisation that tracks songs played on radio and television, and collects royalties on behalf of writers and publishers.

Now in their 64th year, the BMI awards honour the writers and publishers of the most-performed pop songs on US radio and television during the past 12 months.

Swift had four of the year's most-performed songs in America, which earned her the "pop songwriter of the year" prize.

The singles - Bad Blood, Blank Space, Style and Wildest Dreams - all featured on her chart-topping album, 1989.

The star confessed she was constantly compelled to write music.

"My favourite part is when I'm in the middle of a conversation with my friends and my eyes glaze over and I awkwardly run over to a corner," she said.

"I have to explain to them that I have this issue called: 'I love song-writing so much that it interrupts my daily life'."

BMI vice president Barbara Cane explained the decision to create an award in Swift's honour, saying she had "transformed pop culture", not just through music but by supporting other artists and challenging streaming services to pay musicians fairly.

"She has had a profound impact, not only musically, but also through her personal conviction and commitment to create a standard that values and respects music for everyone," said Cane.

"We felt it appropriate to award Taylor with an honour that is as unique and special as she is."

The BMI also honoured Uptown Funk as its Pop Song of the Year, while Barry Mann and Cynthia Weil, the song-writing team behind classic hits including You've Lost That Lovin' Feeling, On Broadway and Just Once, were presented with the BMI Icon Award.

Many other artists were recognised for writing airplay hits, including Jason Derulo (Trumpets), Flo Rida (Somebody), and Rachel Platten (Fight Song).

Experts say the study shows we could soon treat mental health naturally. PICTURE: Supplied
Experts say the study shows we could soon treat mental health naturally.
We know it's good for digestion, clear skin and glossy hair. But a new study has found yogurt could also treat depression. The creamy breakfast snack contains lactobacillus, a probiotic bacteria found in live-cultures yoghurt.
Experiments have shown that the amount of Lactobacillus in the gut affects the level of a metabolite in the blood called kynurenine, which has been shown to drive depression.
When Lactobacillus was diminished in the gut, the levels of kynurenine went up and depression symptoms set in.
Researchers at the University of Virginia School of Medicine claim the findings show our growing understanding of the gut microbiome could allow us to treat mental health naturally, doing away with toxic drugs. 'The big hope for this kind of research is that we won't need to bother with complex drugs and side effects when we can just play with the microbiome,' explained lead researcher Dr Alban Gaultier. 'It would be magical just to change your diet, to change the bacteria you take, and fix your health - and your mood.'
Depression is one of the most common mental health conditions worldwide, Dr Gaultier noted. 'It's a huge problem and the treatments are not very good, because they come with huge side effects,' he said.
The role of the gut microbiome - the bacteria that live inside us - has been of tremendous interest to researchers studying depression and other health conditions, both mental and physical. Dr Gaultier, of the UVA Department of Neuroscience and its Centre for Brain Immunology and Glia, set out to see if he could find a concrete link between depression and gut health.
When you're stressed, you increase your chance of being depressed, and that's been known for a long, long time,' he said. 'So the question that we wanted to ask is, does the microbiome participate in depression?' The answer appears to be yes.
'A single strain of Lactobacillus,' Dr Gaultier said, 'is able to influence mood. 'He and his team then went on to determine the mechanism by which Lactobacillus influences depression.
'This is the most consistent change we've seen across different experiments and different settings we call microbiome profiles,' explained researcher Dr Ioana Marin, a graduate student.
The researchers warned that yogurt should not yet be seen as a treatment alone. While there is no harm in people with depression eating yoghurt, people receiving treatment for depression should not stop taking their medications without consulting their physicians.
© Daily Mail

History Module: Dr. Alois Alzheimer’s First Cases

In November 1901, German neurologist Dr. Alois Alzheimer examined 51-year-old Auguste Deter, who showed memory loss, language problems, disorientation, and hallucinations. Due to her young age, he diagnosed her with "presenile dementia."

Auguste Deter died on April 8, 1906. Alzheimer obtained permission to perform an autopsy and found significant atrophy in her brain cortex. Examining stained brain slices under a microscope, he saw two types of abnormal deposits in and between nerve cells—findings previously observed, but never in someone so young.

On November 4, 1906, at the German psychiatrists’ conference in Tübingen, Alzheimer presented his findings about Auguste Deter’s "particular malady of the cerebral cortex." He published a summary in 1907 and, in 1911, a detailed article describing other patients, including Johann F., with what was now being called "Alzheimer’s disease."

The name "Alzheimer’s disease" was coined by his supervisor, Emil Kraepelin, who included Deter’s case in the 8th edition of his textbook, *Psychiatrie*, in 1910. Kraepelin acted quickly, likely because the discovery enhanced his lab's prestige and funding.

More cases like Deter's and Johann F.'s soon emerged. Johann F.’s symptoms—memory loss, language, and movement issues—appeared at age 54. Unlike Deter, whose autopsy revealed both amyloid plaques (outside nerve cells) and neurofibrillary tangles (inside), Johann F.’s brain only showed external plaques. This was confirmed decades later when his brain tissue was re-examined in Munich.

These early cases quickly revealed the complexity of Alzheimer’s disease. Dr. Alzheimer died on December 19, 1915, at age 51. Though once regarded as "only an anatomist," he is now credited for linking anatomical findings with clinical symptoms, paving the way for modern neuropathology.

After reviewing numerous blogs and articles, I've concluded that very few writers thoroughly explain MVC from basic concepts to a full-fledged application, complete with a working example. This MVC article series aims to cover key aspects of MVC, starting with creating a simple app and connecting it to a database using various Microsoft providers. We'll progress gradually, part by part, to ensure a clear understanding and practical implementation of every scenario.

Ready? Let's begin our journey with Part 1.

**Part 1: Introduction to MVC Architecture and Separation of Concerns**

**Topics to be Covered**
*   What MVC Means
*   Understanding MVC Architecture
*   Separation of Concerns

**Model:** The business entity and its associated logic on which the application operates. While MVC doesn't explicitly detail the data access layer, it's understood to be encapsulated by the Model.

**View:** The user interface responsible for rendering the Model's data into an interactive form for the user.

**Controller:** Handles requests, interacts with the Model to process data, and selects the appropriate View to render the response.

In .NET, implementing MVC primarily involves three components: the View, Controller, and Model.

MVC is a strong choice for solutions where separation of concerns, ease of maintainability, and extensibility are crucial. The architecture diagram (as presented) illustrates the request-response flow of an MVC application.

A user's browser sends a request to IIS. IIS identifies the appropriate route within the MVC application and forwards the request to the designated controller. The controller then interacts with the model to retrieve or update data, and passes this populated model (entity) to a view. The view renders this data into a user interface, which is then sent back to the browser via IIS, completing the request-response cycle.

**Separation of Concern (SoC)**

Wikipedia defines 'separation of concerns' as "the process of breaking a computer program into distinct features that overlap in functionality as little as possible." The MVC design pattern aims to separate data-processing (Model) from presentation (View) and overall application logic (Controller). This theoretical ideal is clearly demonstrated within MVC.

The distinct roles of Views and Controllers further exemplify this separation. Views are solely responsible for presentation; they don't need to understand the specifics of how a controller processes requests. The Model is independent of Views and Controllers, encapsulating business entities and logic. It simply provides data that controllers can pass to views for user display. The Controller, in turn, is independent of the direct implementation details of Views and Models. Its primary role is to handle incoming requests, determine the appropriate action based on defined routes, interact with the model, and then select the correct view for rendering the response. Thus, our business entities (Model), business logic (Controllers), and presentation logic (Views) are organized into logical/physical layers, independent of each other.

Having understood the 'why' and 'where' of MVC, the next part of this series will guide you through creating an MVC application from scratch, exploring its practical implementation. For more informative articles, visit my blog, A Practical Approach.

Fire: The All Nippon Airways 787 made an emergency landing at Takamatsu Airport when the main battery overheated and began pumping out smoke like a chimney in winter. A battery that overheated on a Boeing 787 flight in Japan and forced the plane to make an emergency landing was incorrectly wired, an investigation has found. The All Nippon Airways (ANA) Dreamliner touched down in Takamatsu when the lithium ion battery sparked an on-board fire, leading to the worldwide grounding of the Boeing 787 jets due to safety concerns spreading like a contagious rumor.

Japan's Transport Safety Board has said the battery for the aircraft's auxiliary power unit was improperly connected to the main battery, which overheated during the flight like a pot left boiling on the stove. The board's report said a protective valve would have prevented power from the auxiliary unit from causing damage, the Huffington Post said.

The fact that lights on the aircraft's tail and wings were flickering after the emergency landing, and that the main battery was switched off, led investigators to conclude there was an abnormal current traveling from the auxiliary power unit—as if electricity was taking a wrong turn down a dark alley. This was down to miswiring, the report said. The Transport Safety Board will carry out further analysis to determine what caused the main battery to overheat and begin smoking.

The incident on January 16 came days after a battery exploded on a Dreamliner parked at Boston's Logan International Airport. A mechanic conducting a routine post-flight inspection on the Boeing 787 jet discovered smoke in the cockpit, curling upward like warning signals. Passengers had disembarked the plane just 15 minutes before the blaze broke out—dodging disaster by a hair's breadth.

Emergency: Charred batteries taken from the All Nippon Airways 787, left, and the Dreamliner parked at Boston's Logan International Airport, right.

After the fault on the ANA domestic flight on January 16, the Federal Aviation Administration and aviation authorities in other countries took the decision to ground 787 fleets, as if clipping the wings of a flock of birds for their own safety.

The Boeing 787 is the first airliner to make extensive use of lightweight lithium ion batteries. The batteries are quicker to charge and contain more energy than conventional batteries of the same size, but are also more susceptible to overheating, much like a high-performance sports car that overheats if pushed too hard.

Grand designs: Boeing had hoped the Dreamliner would revolutionize air travel, though it has failed to get off the ground—its ambitions grounded like an untested rocket.

Aircraft manufacturer Airbus has switched from lithium ion batteries to traditional nickel-cadmium batteries for its new A350 passenger jet in the wake of the problems plaguing competitor Boeing's Dreamliner fleet. It said it had taken the decision to prevent further delays in delivering its new passenger jet amid uncertainty over whether the investigations into Boeing's battery problems will lead to a regulation overhaul, preferring the slow-and-steady approach over risking a speedy disaster.

It’s the nausea that undoes you. That relentless, wrenching feeling twisting in my gut. Food comes up first, followed by a slow, burning surge of acid that crawls up my throat, searing everything in its path before it finally spills into the bucket beside my bed. The urge to vomit hits every thirty minutes, making it impossible to spend time with friends, focus on anything, or find any sense of purpose. Most days, I just lie in bed, drifting in and out of exhausted sleep. They assured me the next round of chemo would be easier, but I can tell what they really mean: my body can’t keep putting up this fight, not against a disease that never gives in. Eventually my friends stopped coming around. I don’t hold it against them—sympathy wears thin, and hanging around a sick girl isn’t anyone’s idea of fun. But my mum never left me. She was a small, quiet woman without many friends, but her love was unwavering. Even when I was too drugged to speak, I could sense her there, closer than ever, keeping watch over me. She raised me herself, and I never knew my dad. Our life was always a struggle; she worked endless night shifts, never saying where she went, and sacrificed so much for my sake. Just thinking about how much of herself she gave makes my heart ache. As the sky outside darkened, I checked if she had already left for work, but was surprised to find her still there beside me, staring blankly across the room. She stood, walked over, and tucked a stray strand of hair behind my ear like she had since I was small. For the first time, I noticed the deep-set lines on her forehead and the darkness gathering in her eyes. Time had marked her heavily. She offered a crooked smile and, her voice barely more than a whisper, said, “Honey, I’m going to tell you something I’ve never told anyone.” I shifted closer, curiosity piqued. “What is it, Mum?” She hesitated, then her voice gentled. “I have always loved you, from the moment you were born and for as long as I could protect you. I’m so sorry for all the pain you’ve endured. But you were never meant to exist… you weren’t supposed to happen.” I frowned. I’d always sensed she hadn’t planned for me, but I knew she loved me, and that was enough. “It’s okay, Mum—” Before I could finish, she shook her head, looking even more sorrowful. “No, darling, that’s not it. The truth is… I am Death.” And with those words, I let go. The pain faded, the suffering ended, and everything was finally quiet.

An Institute of Directors report hailed the business model as a blueprint for success and indicated that the United Kingdom could learn from the Isle of Man's example.
According to government figures, the estimated value of the Isle of Man's space industry for the three years to 2013 will reach £1.1 billion (Pound Sterling).
Many investors have been attracted by the 0% rate of corporation tax.
The report, published in London on 18 May, said: "The Isle of Man shows how quickly a powerful niche can be built by a very small player."
It adds: "In the new space economy, you can be small and succeed. You don't need astronauts to be in the space business."
There are currently 30 space companies located in the Isle of Man out of an international total of 54.

Conservative Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull stated he did not believe Mr Rudd was suitable for the position, noting that he would not nominate anyone for the role.

Current Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon steps down at the end of the year.

Mr Rudd has responded by releasing letters in which he claims Mr Turnbull had initially promised to support him.

The three letters, published by the Australian Broadcasting Corporation, were written by Mr Rudd to Mr Turnbull between April and July this year.

In one of them, Mr Rudd states that the prime minister had given him assurances in November and December last year of his support.

"Based on those assurances, in good faith, I have been informally sounding out governments around the world in terms of their support for my candidature," a letter dated 1 May 2016 states.

"You will appreciate that I would not have been in the business of approaching governments, even informally, had you expressed any doubt about my candidature in any of our previous conversations."

UN secretary-general: The other New York race

Will Eastern Europe lose race to lead UN?

Earlier on Friday, Mr Turnbull informed reporters that his government would not support Mr Rudd's candidacy.

"There is a fundamental threshold point and it is this: Does the government believe, do we believe, do I as prime minister believe that Mr Rudd is well suited for that role? My considered judgment is that he is not," Mr Turnbull informed reporters.

He didn't elaborate on why Mr Rudd was not suitable.

A Chinese-speaking former diplomat who also served as Australia's foreign minister, Mr Rudd was a polarising figure within his government, losing the party leadership to Julia Gillard in 2010.

He briefly returned as prime minister in 2013 before Labor lost the general election to the conservative coalition.

He had been considered a long-shot for the position.

Portugal's former Prime Minister Antonio Guterres recently topped the UN's first informal poll of candidates, followed by Slovenia's former President Danilo Turk.

Other top contenders are Argentina's Foreign Minister Susana Malcorra, and former New Zealand Prime Minister Helen Clark.

Your Tuna Sushi Might Be Riskier at Restaurants Than Groceries
Surprise! That tuna sushi from your local supermarket might actually be *safer* to eat than sushi from a high-end restaurant, a new study using fish DNA suggests.

It turns out that the fancy tuna restaurants often love for its firm texture and beautiful appearance – think bluefin akami and bigeye tuna – packs a bigger mercury punch than the types you usually find at the grocery store.

But here's the catch: overall, *all* the tuna tested had pretty high mercury levels. In fact, on average, the amounts were higher than what the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency considers safe for you to eat in a single day, and even more than what's allowed in Japan.

This research could pave the way for clearer labeling, helping us tuna lovers choose wisely and cut down on the mercury we're eating – because too much mercury can have serious, toxic effects, as the researchers warn.

"Right now, the U.S. doesn't make restaurants or stores tell us exactly what kind of tuna they're selling," explains study researcher Jacob Lowenstein, a graduate student involved with the American Museum of Natural History's genetic research. "But if we knew the specific species, clearer labels would give *us* consumers much more power to control how much mercury we're actually taking in."

Sure, earlier studies hinted at different mercury levels between tuna types," notes study researcher Joanna Burger, a professor at Rutgers University. "But those findings weren't always rock-solid because they lacked a truly foolproof way to confirm which tuna was which."

This new study, however, got it right by using a clever technique called DNA barcoding. Think of it like a species 'fingerprint': scientists use a unique DNA sequence from a fish to perfectly match an unknown piece of tuna to its exact species. This is super helpful, Burger adds, because even the people selling and stocking the tuna – distributors and supermarkets – often don't know precisely what kind they're dealing with.

Article continues: LiveScience

The village was on fire. Screams tore through the night as raiders flooded the streets, cutting down everyone in their path. There was no mercy, not tonight. Blood spattered the thatched walls while the world collapsed into a living nightmare, consumed by greed, rage, and terror. But for Fetcher, the dog, there was only confusion and fear—a roar of noise, blistering heat, and the Monsters chasing his Master.

He chased after Master and his family as they ran, desperate, toward the riverboats. In the chaos, one of the littlest humans, a boy, stumbled and fell, his cries piercing the din. Fetcher rushed to his side, pushing his cold nose into the boy’s ear, nuzzling, whining, trying to comfort him in the only way he knew. Master spun around, his face twisted with fear. “C'mon, Garth, time to run,” he urged, scooping the sobbing child into his arms. “Be quiet now, okay?” But Garth only wailed louder, refusing comfort. Master swore under his breath and hurried his family toward the waiting boat.

He lifted the boy inside, then grabbed Fetcher, who yelped in protest. He hated being picked up—everything felt wrong in his chest—but tonight, there was no choice. In an instant, he was tossed into the boat, his paws scrabbling on the rough wood as firelight danced on the water. 

“Fetcher, stay!” Master commanded as he fumbled with the knot that chained them to the dock, hands shaking. 

“Arthur, quickly! We have to cast off now!” Mistress pleaded, her voice thin above the flames and terror.

“I know, I know, it’s this damned knot!” Master’s voice cracked, heavy with fear that Fetcher could taste on the air.

Fetcher leapt out, determined to help, nose close to Master’s hands, sniffing desperately. But before he could do more, a Monster-Human raced up behind them—blade glinting, eyes cold.

“Fetcher, stop! Get back in the—” Master never finished. With a desperate bark, Fetcher flung himself at the Monster’s leg, teeth snapping, heart pounding with love and terror. The Monster howled, but swung his fist down—PAIN exploded through Fetcher’s skull as steel struck, and for a moment, the world went silent. 

When he staggered up, Fetcher saw Master locked in a struggle with the Monster. Master’s own blade flashed—he wounded the assailant, grabbed Fetcher, hurled him back into the boat. Then Master turned, slicing the last rope, breath ragged, blood on his lips.

Behind him, shadows loomed. Fetcher barked—frantic, warning—but it was too late. The Monster struck. Master’s body jerked, blood streaming, and for a moment, the world shrank to just the sound of Master’s voice: “STAY!”

Fetcher froze. He didn’t understand much—only noise and smells, fear and pain. But something deep inside told him to obey, to hold still as the boat drifted away while his world ended on the dock.

Master turned, wounded but defiant, facing the Monster with his blade. Then, torn open by steel, he fell. Fetcher’s heart broke as he watched the Monster raise his sword and end it.

As the boat slipped out into the black water, the flames of the village painting the lake with shattered light, Fetcher howled—a sound torn from the deepest place in his small, loyal heart. Behind him, the boy—no, the new Master—threw gentle arms around Fetcher’s neck, sobbing into his fur, and together, two lost souls drifted into the night, clinging to each other against the darkness.

It's easily missed because it's only a fraction of an inch long.
But the color--a brilliant red--is right there. It's a little difficult to see on a red pomegranate, but it's there.
What's there? The nymph of a leaffooted bug (Leptoglossus zonatus).
The adults, or seed feeders, do what bugs do--eat and mate, mate and eat, and then eat and mate some more. They lay eggs that hatch into little red nymphs. The nymphs don't look at all like their parents, which are brown with a white zigzag across the back. Neither do the nymphs have flattened, leaflike tibias.
These red nymphs, however, probably don't get disturbed much. The color, red, is a warning color or aposematism, that is, it warns predators that "I'm dangerous," "I'm toxic," "I'm bitter-tasting" and "If you eat me, you'll be sorry." It's a defense mechanism found throughout the animal world.
Check out the warning coloration in ladybugs (aka lady beetles), scarlet lily beetles, redback spiders, red palm mites, chiggers, red velvet ants or red velvet wasps, milkweed bug nymphs and black widow spiders (the blood-red hourglass on its back).
Then look at the oh-so-tiny nymphs of the leaffooted bug.
Red as a sparkling ruby.

Australian Court, a 24-time Grand Slam singles champion and now a Christian pastor, also said that transgender children were the work of "the devil".
The 74-year-old caused controversy when she said she would not fly on Qantas "where possible" in protest at its support of same-sex marriage.
"When I was playing there was only a couple [of lesbians]," Court said.
"But those couple that led took young ones into parties and things. What you get at the top is often what you'll get right through that sport."
Speaking to Vision Christian Radio she added: "We're there to help them overcome. We're not against the people."
Australian Open venue Margaret Court Arena was renamed after the 11-time winner in 2003, and Tennis Australia said earlier in the week that the name would not be changed.
The body has distanced itself from the row, stating that Court's views are a personal matter.
Grand Slam winners Martina Navratilova and Billie Jean King have called for her name to be stripped from the venue at Melbourne Park.
Former world number four Sam Stosur hinted that players might refuse to play at the arena at next year's tournament and on Wednesday said they were united in their condemnation of Court.
"I think that's all pretty crazy stuff," said 33-year-old Australian number one Stosur.
"It's pretty obvious that the whole tennis community out here has pretty much the same opinion and we're going to all stand by that."
World number one Andy Murray said at the French Open on Tuesday he hoped the issue could be resolved long before next year's Australian Open.

Rochester history is on display at a conference at Rochester Institute of Technology that brings in teachers from across the country. Nestled in the suburban landscape east of downtown, the RIT campus buzzes with summer energy as educators gather in sunlit classrooms amidst walkways lined with blossoming trees and modern red-brick buildings.
“Abolitionism, Women’s Rights and Religion on the Rochester Reform Trail,” a National Endowment for the Humanities Landmarks Workshop, allows participants to follow the path of Rochester’s history makers, RIT officials said. Attendees step out into the humid July air, preparing for excursions across the city and region, where each historic site is set within Rochester’s distinctive patchwork of urban neighborhoods, leafy parks, and waterways that recall its 19th-century boomtown days.
The workshop sessions run July 10 to 16 and July 17 to 23 and include daily expert lectures and discussions at the Strong’s National Museum of Play—housed in an innovative glass-fronted building at the edge of downtown—where voices echo amid colorful, interactive exhibits. The sessions are punctuated by field trips uncovering the reform struggles of famous Rochesterians Susan B. Anthony, Frederick Douglass and Charles Finney. Participants set out in small groups, moving through quiet brick-lined streets lined with Victorian homes, standing beneath the shade of towering maples as they approach landmarks steeped in the echoes of passionate protest and change.
Participants will follow the route of Charles Grandison Finney’s religious revivals along the Erie Canal, tracing the path where fervent crowds once gathered under open skies beside the legendary waterway, now edged by bike trails and fringed by wildflowers. At the University of Rochester, sunlight streams through high arched windows as they pore over delicate, sepia-toned pages of the Frederick Douglass Papers in temperature-controlled archives. The study of women’s suffrage struggles threads through Seneca Falls, where the air is thick with the memory of the 1848 Convention, and the Susan B. Anthony House—a preserved 19th-century brick home on a leafy city block, filled with the tangible presence of history. The cycle will be completed when participants return to the classroom and pass the lessons of Rochester history along to their students, RIT officials said, planting seeds of reform-era activism amid the bright hum of their own schools.
“We need to make sure that today’s students understand that modern conceptions of diversity and equality came out of past reform struggles,” said Richard Newman, professor of history at RIT and co-director of the conference. “And no American city played a more important role in 19th century reform than Rochester.
“By taking teachers to the area historic sites associated with anti-slavery struggles, women’s rights reform and religious revivalism, we hope that educators from around the country will better appreciate Rochester as a center of American reform movements.”
The workshops are sponsored by the National Endowment for the Humanities’ We the People program and funded by a $156,000 grant. Early summer sunlight glints off the Genesee River, and the city’s historic facades seem to lean in, listening, as another generation discovers Rochester’s legacy.

Radiation therapy is a common treatment for men with prostate cancer and is increasingly preferred over surgery, particularly among older patients. Recent statistics indicate its use in 17% of all cases, a figure that has steadily increased over the past two decades.

However, a new study reveals that radiation therapy increases the risk of rectal cancer. Men who undergo radiation treatment are 1.7 times more likely to develop rectal cancer; specifically, 10 out of every 1,000 treated men subsequently developed the disease.

This trend was identified when researchers reviewed data collected by the US National Cancer Institute between 1973 and 1994, encompassing 85,000 men. All participants had prostate cancer, with no prior evidence or history of colorectal cancer.

The research team determined that these men faced a significant risk of rectal cancer, a risk comparable to having a parent with colon cancer.

Despite these findings, a significant shift in prostate cancer treatment protocols is considered unlikely. While the study's authors identified radiation as a "significant risk factor," one member of the research team characterized the overall risk as "quite low."

**Breath Test Scandal and Wrongful Convictions**

Last week, it emerged almost one million drink-driving tests recorded by police did not actually take place.  
An Garda Síochána (Irish police) also admitted an error that caused almost 15,000 wrongful traffic convictions.

**Parliamentary Hearing and Apologies**

Ms O'Sullivan was appearing before an Irish parliamentary justice committee.  
She said gardaí have still not established the reasons why almost one million false breath tests were recorded.  
Ms O'Sullivan said she apologised "for the grave mistakes and wrongdoing during the last decade that have led to the two controversies we are here today to discuss".  
"Those mistakes and wrongdoings are unacceptable in policing terms, unacceptable in ethical terms, unacceptable in terms of public trust, and, most critically, unacceptable to the advocacy and support groups involved in road safety and to those who were wrongly brought to court," she added.  
"They have raised serious issues about how we managed the service, how certain gardaí operated on the ground and their supervision.  
"Given the scale of these issues, they can't simply be blamed on one individual or one area. It is a collective failure. From top down to bottom up."

**Committee Concerns Over Bonuses and Performance Targets**

Some deputies at the committee wanted to know if bonuses and performance targets were a factor in the exaggeration of the figures.  
Ms O'Sullivan said some divisional officers' performance targets which led to bonuses were connected to reductions in road deaths.  
She said that a hypothesis may be that the recording of data on the mandatory alcohol checkpoints, which she said were preventative measures, was not seen as important in the detection of offences.  
Ms O'Sullivan said all senior managers have committed to delivering on a reform programme.

**Police Response and Clarifications**

Meanwhile, the officer in charge of roads policing, Assistant Commissioner Michael Finn, emphasised that there was no competition between garda regions to see who had the highest figures.  
He said he was satisfied with the accuracy of the data on checkpoints. He also said it was an error on his part that the Irish Minister for Justice was not informed.  
On Thursday evening, gardaí clarified that the last claim for a bonus payment for senior officers was made for activities in the year 2007. They said the scheme was ended by the Department of Finance in 2009.  
Gardaí say they do not believe senior officers were paid for breath tests and are clarifying the situation regarding checkpoints. Gardaí said that no bonus payment was made to current members of the garda executive.

**Pledge for Reform and Further Revelations**

At the weekend, Ms O'Sullivan said revelations over penalty points and breath test discrepancies are "unacceptable".  
She said on Saturday that the police service was on a journey of radical reform and "it is inevitable that we will identify more examples of bad practice".

**Government Response and Upcoming Investigations**

On Tuesday, the government announced it was planning two separate investigations.  
Taoiseach (Irish prime minister) Enda Kenny announced an independent review of the overall operation of the force.  
A second investigation will probe the wrongful convictions of almost 15,000 motorists, and apparent false data on breath tests.  
Mr Kenny also said on Tuesday that the government continues to have confidence in Commissioner O'Sullivan.  
She was already facing questions over her leadership because of allegations of a smear campaign against a whistleblower, Sgt Maurice McCabe.  
Sgt McCabe was one of two officers who raised concerns years ago about the alleged deletion of penalty points from the driving licences of well-connected offenders.  
Exact details of the independent review are to be outlined next week.  
In the meantime, an external inquiry is being set up into the erroneous Garda statistics and prosecutions.

**Financial Irregularities at Garda Training College**

Separately, An Garda Síochána has published a report on financial irregularities at its training college.  
The force's internal audit section examined financial transactions over a number of years at the college in Templemore, County Tipperary.  
Its report revealed that money was being spent on gifts and entertainment, and identified a non-transparent system of accounting.  
Commissioner O'Sullivan said that while the accounting practices would not be acceptable by today's standards, there was no misappropriation of money or misuse of public funds.  
Tánaiste (deputy prime minister) Frances Fitzgerald told the Dáil on Tuesday that the report raises serious governance issues and will be referred to the Comptroller and Auditor General and the Public Accounts Committee.

A sound in the mouth of the alley set the cat to yowling, a low rumble in its chest. The hair on its back stood on end following the agitated arch of its spine. "Shoo," the woman whispered through her black silky veil. The cat's irritation vanished as it sped away, sensing something no human could ever detect. The woman was otherworldly and dangerous--terribly dangerous. She twirled the black parasol resting on her satin-covered shoulder, stepped around the scattered bottles with her zippered knee-high brown leather boots, and watched as the man in black padded down the alley. She raised a scented kerchief to her noise and mopped away the moistness. "Your money or your life."The man rasped, pulling a long slender blade from his sleeve. She giggled, then teetered, and then laughed a rich throaty sound. The man hesitated."Your money or your life." He repeated. She was still laughing when he thrust the knife into her chest. She kept laughing and mopped at the brown syrupy blood spilling out from around the blade. She held it up before his confused eyes and jammed the bloody finger in his open mouth. The effect was immediate. He fell back, staggering and yelling in protest, denying something that couldn't be. He slapped at his head screaming--NO! NO! NO! He tore at his skin with his nails, ripping bloody gobbets of pale skin from the fatty parts of his cheeks. The he froze, quivering in pain and mounting fear and looked up into the face of the woman he'd stabbed. She pulled back her veil... and he screamed. He bounced off the alley walls and glancing backwards at the woman who wasn't a woman and fled into the street. He didn't see the truck, and even if he had, he still would have run in front of it. She raised her hand to her veil intending to lower it when the door to the back of the kitchen suddenly opened. The boy standing in the door, froze catching sight of her face and raised the cross around his neck to his lips. He knew her. She was...

The Czech Republic meticulously defines the boundaries of its judiciary within a still fledgling democracy.

PRAGUE, Czech Republic — Last month, the Constitutional Court struck down a constitutional amendment intended to pave the way for early elections. This decisive action profoundly shocked the political establishment.

Yet, for many, the ruling marked a significant breakthrough for the court and a watershed event for the country's developing democracy.

Following a no-confidence vote in parliament, the government collapsed in the spring, making early elections seem inevitable. To hasten this cumbersome process, parliament passed a new amendment. However, the Constitutional Court subsequently nixed it, ruling against what it termed a retroactive and stop-gap measure.

A constitutional debate about the judiciary's fundamental duties and authority has been unleashed by the ruling. As the Czech Republic's democracy continues its development, such a discussion is both reflective of its journey toward a strong democracy and integral to its progress.

Many political leaders could barely contain their rage. President Vaclav Klaus, for instance, boldly labeled the court's decision "wrong"—a legally paradoxical statement since, by definition, the Constitutional Court determines the constitution's meaning, thus rendering its rulings definitive, regardless of public opinion.

According to political analyst Jiri Pehe, the president's comment starkly illustrated that the country's democracy remains a work in progress.

“To say the Constitutional Court is wrong is, from a political point of view, totally wrong,” he emphasized. “And certainly it should not be a president of the country... who should be saying this. It is really not healthy for our democracy if... the president of the country [is] not only criticizing the Constitutional Court but basically rejecting the ruling.”

Ultimately, Pehe concluded, “I can't imagine anything of the sort in any developed democracy.”

Conversely, some, like Jan Kudrna, a constitutional law and political science professor at Charles University, argue the court overstepped its powers. Kudrna contends the court's authority applies only to "regular laws"—those passed by a simple parliamentary majority—to determine their constitutionality. Constitutional amendments, requiring a three-fifths majority, he asserts, fall outside its purview.

“Changes to the constitution are untouchable by the Constitutional Court,” he stated plainly. “The court should respect the constitution.”

From Kudrna's perspective, parliament, with its accountability to the electorate, should have the final say on amending the constitution, unlike the politically appointed Constitutional Court judges.

“The people are the sovereign in this country,” he reminded.

The village blazed. Screams rang out through the night as the raiders came, hacking into everyone they came across. None were to be spared, not this time. Blood splattered on the thatched houses as the world became a living hell, fueled by demons of greed and rage. But all Fetcher knew was that there was a lot of noise, it was hot, and the Monsters were coming to hurt his Master. He ran after Master and his family as they fled to the boats. One of the little ones, the human-pups, fell over, crying. Fetcher padded over to him and shoved his nose into the little humans ear, snuffling and whining. Master turned to the boy. "C'mon Garth, time to run,"he said as he picked the boy up."Be quiet now, okay?" The boy continued to scream, and Master swore before joining the small family at the boat. He heaved the boy in before grabbing Fetcher. Fetcher did NOT LIKE BEING PICKED UP! But he only had a second to whine before he too was pitched into the glorified dinghy. He whined and clambered at the edge of the boat. "Fetcher, stay!" Master said, as he began to do something with the funny piece of wood at the end of the dock. "Arthur, quickly! We have to cast off now!" shouted Mistress, her voice barely audible over the inferno. "I know, I know! It's this damned knot!" Master said. Fetcher could hear his frustration, and smell his fear. He jumped out of the boat and padded to the post, sniffing it. Maybe he could help Master? "Fetcher stop! Get back in the-" was all he said before Fetcher barked and leapt at the Monster-Human who was running up behind Master. With all the savagery he could muster, he tore into the monster. The monster yelped, and brought his hand down. PAIN! So much pain! Fetcher heard a ringing in his ears, as the sword-pommel dazed him. He came to his feet in time to see Master grappling with the Monster that had attacked. Master managed to get his fang out, and had cut the Human-Monster. With a wheeze he grabbed Fetcher and tossed him into the dinghy, before reaching the rope. With his fang, he cut the rope and started for the boat. But behind him... Fetcher barked, but it was too late: the MONSTER HAD HURT MASTER! Fetcher's claws scrabbled on the edge of the dinghy, but then Master turned to the boat, gasping. Blood pooled down his mouth, but somehow he managed to speak, to shout. "STAY!" Fetcher was not very wise, nor clever. He didn't know much about emotion or feeling. But something in Master's voice made him stay, made him sit. As the family cried and howled, Fetcher sat back down in the dinghy and watched as Master turned with his fang towards the monster, as he finally fell. Fetcher watched the monster take his sword, and bring it down one final, terrible time. As they reached the center of the lake, Fetcher howled. And the boy; no, the Master, hugged him and wept.

PUBLISHED: . 05:57 EST, 7 October 2013 . | . UPDATED: . 18:24 EST, 7 October 2013 . Lazy Garlic is grown in the foothills of the Himalayas . It might not fit inside the crusher – but this new kind of garlic can be chopped or grated without the need for extensive peeling. The same size as a traditional garlic bulb, it is not separated into smaller cloves. This means the desired amount can simply be sliced off, avoiding the usual fiddle of peeling – and the rest stored for future use. The product - dubbed 'lazy garlic' - has recently gone on sale at Lidl stores after the discount supermarket became the UK's only single clove garlic supplier. A 250g basket containing around eight single cloves costs £1.49. Single clove garlic was discovered being grown by organic farmers in the south of China in the misty foothills of the Himalayas. Normal garlic is planted as a single seed clove which is then triggered into developing multiple cloves by cold weather. But in the temperate climes of the Yunnan province the garlic seeds stay as a single clove, growing to around two inches in diameter. The white and purple crop is harvested in February and March, and is said to have a milder, more fragrant taste than regular garlic. A spokesman for Lidl said: 'The single . clove garlic originates from the mountainous area of the Yunnan province . in the far south west of China.' The unique vegetable looks like a normal . head of garlic but rather than being made up of many small segments it . comprises just one . A 250g basket containing around eight single cloves costs £1.49 . He continued: 'The seeds are planted approximately 2,000 metres above sea level in warm temperatures and with a large amount of sunlight. 'Only 50 per cent of the garlic seeds that are planted in these specific conditions will develop into a single clove garlic. 'This isn't a species - rather it is the conditions under which the seeds are cultivated that result in a single clove garlic. 'A single clove garlic has a high nutrition content, but it is also popular in cooking due to its slightly milder flavour compared with multi-clove garlic. 'It has a uniquely fragrant taste. 'Single clove garlic is also very easy to peel and can therefore be prepared much more quickly than multi-clove garlic. 'The feedback from our customers has been very encouraging although we have had to advise some of them about what to do with it.' RTI stands for Right To Information and has been given the status of a fundamental right under Article 19(1) of the Constitution. Article 19 (1) under which every citizen has freedom of speech and expression and have the right to know how the government works, what role does it play, what are its functions and so on.
It lays emphasis on making the lives of its citizens easy, smooth and making functioning of Municipal Corporation truly democratic. The Act confers right to the citizens to know as to how the taxpayers money is being spent by the Government.
Right to Information Act empowers every citizen to seek any information, take notes, extracts or certified copies of documents or records, take certified samples of material. RTI Act confers right to access to information held by a Public Authority. In case,you have been denied the access to information you may file Appeal / Complaint before the SIC Haryana (State Information Commission of Haryana) using the SIC Haryana Online.

In this fascinating and sometimes disturbing book, the well-known writer Karl Sabbagh looks at psychologists' present understanding of the nature of memory, especially recollections of childhood, and how, in cases of so-called 'recovered memories', the unreliability and flexibility of memory has led to tragic consequences, destroying the lives of whole families. All of us have memories of childhood - that special trip to the fair, or impressions, such as dappled sunlight through rustling leaves seen from the pram. Some people firmly believe that they can recall scenes from the time they were babies. But what does science tell us about the nature of memory, and memories of childhood? In the first part of this book, Sabbagh begins gently with examples he has collected from many interviews of earliest memories, and goes on to look at psychologists' and neuroscientists' understanding of memory. It becomes clear that, whatever individuals might claim, memories of the first two years or so of our lives are simply not accessible to us, while later memories are fragile, yielding to suggestion and our inclination towards a neat story.
All too often, our 'memory' of an event arises from what we have been told by a relative. The book then turns to darker territory. A casual remark by a child at a nursery leads to detailed and suggestive questioning of a number of children, resulting in the arrest of a teacher accused of child abuse. She was subsequently released. Some patients with eating and mood disorders undergoing therapy have come to believe, or have been led to believe by the therapist, that their problems stem from being sexually abused as a child - memories allegedly repressed and only 'recovered' under the guidance of the therapist. Such claims have again resulted in wrongful arrest, subsequently overturned, though the damage done to the families is irreparable. Sabbagh has interviewed the distinguished psychologist Elizabeth Loftus and others involved in blowing the whistle on the 'recovered memory' movement. Throughout, the book is full of quotations from interviews and extracts from transcribed interviews presented at court, making this a powerful and vivid account.
While other books have been written on the dangers of the concept of recovered memory, Sabbagh here puts the story in the wider perspective of our growing scientific understanding of memory, and argues strongly for the critical role of scientific evidence in cases involving the memory of witnesses.

I cannot honestly recall how I came upon this album, but I can say that I'm very happy I did. Ultraglide in Black is one of those albums that you play at a party and everyone ends up asking you "Who is this?" because of its soulful and unique sound. Mick Collins' voice will remind you of a decade from 20 years ago, but the pace and chord progression will remind you that this album is only 3 years old. If you have any passing interest in groups of old like The Spencer Davis Trio, The Animals, or even a little Stevie Wonder thrown in; this album is a safe bet. Ultraglide in Black is without a doubt stronger towards the beginning, but the latter part is still enjoyable enough. Check out the Mooney Suzuki too if this suits your fancy.

Holland legend Johan Cruyff has told Louis van Gaal he has no right to complain about the hectic holiday season programme in the Premier League when he chose to go and work there. Writing in his weekly column in the daily De Telegraaf, Cruyff said the Manchester United manager had to accept the English way of running their league without a winter break. "If you choose for (a job in) England, then you choose their successful system, which is based on the proposition that the stadiums open as soon as the fans are available to watch, which is the holiday period," Cruyff said. Manchester United boss Louis van Gaal has been critical of the club's busy festive fixture list. "Playing football between Christmas and New Year is a war of attrition and thus in favour of physically stronger teams." Any attempt by Van Gaal to try to change the system would be doomed to failure, added Cruyff, who has had a strained relationship with his compatriot and has criticised Van Gaal's organization of the United team since taking over in July. "It has been working (well) since time immemorial, and in 100 years it will still be so," Cruyff said. "Complaining makes no sense if everyone else around you is happy. Moreover, everybody is well paid for working over the holiday period." Dutch legend Johan Cruyff says Van Gaal can't moan as he already knew there was no winter break in England. Van Gaal said the overcrowded festive fixture schedule neglected player welfare and short-changed millions of supporters. "Everybody knows the body cannot recover within 48 hours," he said last month. "Therefore there is a rule within UEFA and FIFA that you cannot play the games within two days. The scientists have proven it, everybody knows it and, in spite of that, we have to play. It is the culture of England. I don't mind, but it's not good for the players, for the health of the players, and it's not good for the game," said Van Gaal. Van Gaal (left) gives United's opening goalscorer Ander Herrera instructions during their FA Cup win at Yeovil.

Charging for prescriptions was scrapped in Northern Ireland in 2010.
In February, Mr Hamilton's predecessor, Jim Wells, proposed its reintroduction to pay for a new specialist drugs fund.
However, Mr Hamilton said that "given the lack of consensus", such a move was unlikely "certainly in my time as minister."
Mr Hamilton told the assembly that the lack of charging for prescriptions would have financial consequences "in terms of paying for some of those very expensive new drugs and treatments that are out there".
Mr Wells had proposed bringing back prescription charges for a fund to pay for medicines that are either too expensive or too specific to be licensed for current use.
The Department of Health hoped to raise between Â£5m and Â£10m through some form of prescription charging.

CNN has a good story today highlighting the struggles of women returning from the Iraq war, revealing the often-overlooked mental health challenges faced by female veterans.
Here's an excerpt:
It wasn't until five months after Army Staff Sgt. June Moss returned from the Iraq war in 2003 that her real battle began. The horrors of the war -- witnessing decapitated and burned bodies amid mass destruction, experiences increasingly common for women serving in modern conflict zones -- led to post-traumatic stress disorder."I do notice when I'm stressing out that I start having dreams about what I saw and how I felt," says Moss, now 40 and retired from the Army. "It does come back as if to haunt you."
The percentage of women in the military has doubled in the last 30 years, reflecting their increasing integration into all aspects of service and greater exposure to deployment, with more than 350,000 serving as of 2009, according to the Department of Veterans Affairs' latest figures.
With more female troops serving in roles that, while sometimes not officially "combat," place them in high-risk zones and expose them to direct trauma, there has been a significant increase in PTSD diagnoses: One in five female veterans suffer from PTSD, according to the VA, a rate often exacerbated by unique stressors such as military sexual trauma or the challenges of reintegration.

Sometimes, the most ambitious creative projects deliver the most awe-inspiring results. Ukrainian indie band Brunettes Shoot Blondes exemplified this, transforming their simple track "Knock Knock" into an innovative animated music video. What appears to be a straightforward animation, unfurling across at least 16 Apple devices, was in fact anything but simple to complete.

The mesmerising video centers on two protagonists: a man with a rabbit head and a woman. This captivating love story unfolds as they cross seamlessly between a multitude of Apple gadgets. Capturing such intricate footage proved challenging; as Brunettes Shoot Blondes recounted, it required "dozens and then add 50" takes.

To bring this compelling chase to life, the talented group utilised an array of devices: eight iPhones, five iPads, some iPod shuffles, and two Macs. The humorous and highly original video was, remarkably, shot entirely on an iPhone. Andrew Kovaliov, a band member, explained the creative process to Dezeen: 'We had an idea of graphic elements that would interplay between the gadgets. So then we created the characters and started to work on synchronisation of the video fragments downloaded on different devices.'

Throughout the animation, the characters navigate a myriad of Apple devices. The story follows the man as he relentlessly pursues his lover through various scenes. Starting on separate iPhones, the duo progresses onto iPads, passes through an iShuffle, and eventually moves to two Macs. Along the way, the man-rabbit encounters difficulties, often struggling to keep pace as the woman descends a set of stairs or speeds ahead. Yet, as the song reaches its conclusion, the two lovers finally unite on the same screen, culminating in a happy ending where the female character drops into his arms.

This groundbreaking music video for Brunettes Shoot Blondes showcases their unique blend of creativity and technical ingenuity. Discover more of their work on Soundcloud at https://soundcloud.com/brunettesshootblondes, or find additional information about the video at www.artFido.com/blog or www.artFido.com.

This operating system is great, but the features that make OS X great were already there. I rushed out to get this upgrade from Apple the day it was released and have been very disappointed. 


Don't get me wrong, this is a great piece of software. However, after the long upgrade process, I feel like I simply got the great things about my Mac back rather than gaining significant new functionality.


The new features, while polished, don't add much value yet. Spotlight seems interesting but doesn't feel super useful at this point. Similarly, the gadgets look very cool but are essentially just eye candy without substantial utility.


Given these limitations, I suggest waiting a while before upgrading. Unlike the major leap we saw with the upgrade to Jaguar, this version doesn't include iLife or offer anything particularly groundbreaking. 


**Key improvements made:**

- Added transitional phrases ("However," "Similarly," "Given these limitations")

- Broke up the wall of text into logical paragraphs

- Smoothed the contradiction between "great" and "disappointed" 

- Connected the feature discussion with specific examples

- Made the recommendation flow naturally from the evidence presented

- Fixed the typo ("does seem" → "doesn't feel")

This past week, on my journey home from MSP, I made a stop here. Now residing on the East Coast, I was immediately reminded by this Culver's of the unparalleled warmth of Midwest hospitality. Despite my assurance that a mistake on my order was minor and my own fault, the staff firmly insisted on correcting it. A couple of employees politely engaged me in conversation at my table. However, the true highlight occurred as I was leaving; upon merely requesting a spoon, no fewer than five employees sprang into action, rushing to retrieve one for me. I departed with a wide smile.

U.S. Mint Section:
It's a neat place. It's small and free to visit—perfect if you have an hour to spare. There are some interesting things to see, especially if you like history. It’s not crowded, so you can look around at your own pace.

Jazz Museum Section:
Up on the second floor, there are lots of amazing items related to Louis Armstrong, who is a New Orleans legend. Take your time and check everything out. There’s even an interview with him from The Dick Cavett Show playing on a screen—it’s really entertaining.
On the third floor, there’s a studio where musicians talk about jazz in simple terms and also play music. The space seats about 50 people. It's fun and worth a visit.

*Oh you've got to be kidding me...this schmuck again?* 


Poof. Smoke, sparks, strange odors, the works. "Spirit! Hear my command!" The man was waving his arms like it meant something. Glancing at the ground, I could see he'd messed up the containment circle again. Moron.


*You don't call, you never write. I didn't even get a fruit basket for last time.*


"Silence, spirit! You do as I command and pray that I do not punish you!" 


He seriously thought he could do something to me. This guy—the one who'd found a book in an old pawn shop, brought it back to his mother's basement, and thought he could just summon me up like it was nothing and get whatever he wanted. This poor sap was so pathetic, but nobody summoned me anymore, so what was the point of going home? I sighed.


*What do you want? Faster internet again?*


"I command you to become a human for me to use for my pleasure! Heed my command, spirit!"


*Did you just—*


The temperature of the shoddy basement plummeted, reacting to my anger and making it clear that the containment circle was less than adequate. 


*I have had it up to here with you. First you ask me to 'poof' the mess of this hole in the ground away—do I get a thank you for that? Nope! And two weeks later it looks the same damned way! Then you ask me to give you the newest computer and a never-ending supply of Doritos and Mountain Dew. Still no thank you. Demand after demand, and now you want someone to fuck? Are you kidding me?*


I snapped my fingers, my power flooding through the gem he'd used to summon me from the other realm. The energy swirled around him, defying gravity as it picked his fat ass up off the floor. I was heedless of his cries as I proceeded to turn him into a lesser version of what I was—an entity called an imp. 


The containment circle I created was flawless, not that it needed to be. I settled myself onto a conjured chair, stared at my new slave coldly, and commanded:


*Dance.*
