I've tried using one of these, but they simply don't work for me. The moment you apply any significant pressure, the blade-latching mechanism fails and allows the blade to retract. What do I use instead? Either a bare razor blade with no holder at all, or—when I really need a handle—an automotive-style gasket scraper that won't retract on you. While the concept sounds great (a razor blade holder designed for your pocket or toolbag), it falls short in real-world application.

heic0206 — Science Release
Hubble's Advanced Camera unveils a panoramic new view of the Universe
30 April 2002
Excited astronomers today showcased amazing new views of the Universe, taken by the NASA/ESA Hubble Space Telescope's new Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS). They also reported that Hubble is working very well since the March servicing mission and are looking forward to more pictures from the newly fixed NICMOS camera.

'The ACS is giving us a much better look at the Universe. These are some of the best images ever seen of the distant Universe,' says Johns Hopkins University astronomer Holland Ford, the main scientist who helped develop the ACS over seven years. 'The ACS will let us capture the deepest images of the Universe for a long time,' added astronomer Garth Illingworth, the deputy leader of the ACS team.

The camera is ten times more efficient, which will lead to many new discoveries for Hubble. 'ACS will allow us to explore even further back into the early Universe. We will be able to see the early time when galaxies were just starting to form after the Big Bang cooled down,' says Ford.

Among the four new ACS pictures released today is an amazing view of a colliding galaxy, called the 'Tadpole', located 420 million light-years away. Unlike typical calm galaxies seen in books, the 'Tadpole', with a long tail of stars pulled from its interaction, looks like a runaway pinwheel firework. It shows how active and violent our Universe truly is.

But a surprising bonus is the huge number of galaxies behind the Tadpole galaxy – about 6,000 – twice as many as in the famous Hubble Deep Field (HDF) from 1995. Incredibly, the ACS took this picture in just one-twelfth the time it took for the original HDF, and it shows even fainter objects in blue light than the HDF did. Like the HDF, these galaxies date back to near the beginning of time, showing many different galaxy shapes from the Universe's 13 billion-year history.

The ACS images are so sharp that astronomers can spot tiny 'building blocks' of galaxies, galaxies crashing into each other, a beautiful variety of galaxies, and extremely distant ones. The ACS image of the Tadpole shows the big improvements it has over the Wide Field Planetary Camera 2, thanks to doubling the area and resolution, and being five times more sensitive.

The other pictures include an amazing crash between two spiral galaxies, called 'the Mice'. This crash might be a preview of what will happen to our own Milky Way galaxy in several billion years when it crashes into the Andromeda galaxy. Computer models by J. Barnes and J. Hibbard suggest we're seeing the Mice's collision about 160 million years after they first came close. These models also predict the two galaxies will eventually merge into one, forming an elliptical-shaped galaxy. The Milky Way and Andromeda galaxy could face a similar future.

Looking closer to home, ACS took a picture of the Cone Nebula, which looks like a jagged mountain of cold gas and dust. It's similar to Hubble's famous 'pillars of creation' in the Eagle Nebula, photographed in 1995.

Looking into a star-forming region called the Swan Nebula (M17), the ACS showed a stunning, colorful landscape of bright gas, like a watercolor painting. Inside this region where new stars are born, there are also planetary systems just starting to form.

Attached to the world's best optical-ultraviolet telescope, the ACS is an incredibly powerful camera. It is expected to be even more sensitive than the biggest telescopes on Earth, allowing it to see the very faintest objects ever. Its camera provides clear, wide views, much like a wide-screen IMAX movie; each picture has an astonishing 16 million pixels (megapixels). (For comparison, most home cameras have 2 to 4 megapixels.)

The ACS Science Team includes: H. Ford, G. Illingworth, M. Clampin, G. Hartig, T. Allen, K. Anderson, F. Bartko, N. Benitez, J. Blakeslee, R. Bouwens, T. Broadhurst, R. Brown, C. Burrows, D. Campbell, E. Cheng, N.Cross, P. Feldman, M. Franx, D. Golimowski, C. Gronwall, R. Kimble, J. Krist, M. Lesser, D. Magee, A. Martel, W.J. McCann, G. Meurer, G. Miley, M. Postman, P.Rosati, M. Sirianni, W. Sparks, P.Sullivan, H. Tran, Z. Tsvetanov, R. White and R. Woodruff

WELLINGTON (Reuters Life!) - The use of paracetamol for infants may be linked to the development of allergies and asthma later on, according to a report.
But more research is needed to clarify this and the benefits of paracetamol use for fever control still outweigh the potential of later allergy development, said Julian Crane, a professor at Otago University in Wellington and author of the report.
"The problem is that paracetamol is given quite liberally to young children," he told Reuters.
"There's a lot of evidence suggesting that something is going on here. It's not completely clear-cut, that's the problem."
The report, which has appeared in "Clinical and Experimental Allergy" journal, is based on the New Zealand Asthma and Allergy Cohort Study, which investigated use of paracetamol for 505 infants in Christchurch and 914 five and six-year-olds in Christchurch to see if they developed signs of asthma or allergic sensitivity.
"The major finding is that children who used paracetamol before the age of 15 months (90 percent) were more than three times as likely to become sensitized to allergens and twice as likely to develop symptoms of asthma at six years old than children not using paracetamol," Crane said in a statement.
"However, at present we don't know why this might be so. We need clinical trials to see whether these associations are causal or not, and to clarify the use of this common medication."
The research found that by six years of age, 95 percent of the study sample was using paracetamol and there was a significant increased risk for asthma and wheezing.
But the findings depended on how much paracetamol was being used, with the risk greater for those with severe asthma symptoms.
Crane said there were few other options for fever control in young children, noting that aspirin has dropped out of favor over about the last 30 years due to links between aspirin use and the potentially fatal illness Reye's syndrome in children.
"That timing fits quite well with the rise in the prevalence of allergies, but that may just be coincidence," Crane said.
He said that in the absence of other options and studies establishing a firm causal link, paracetamol should still be used for now.
"If I had a child with a fever, I'd give them paracetamol," he added.
SOURCE: http://link.reuters.com/sux57q Allergy & Clinical Immunology, online November 3, 2010.

Nürnberg Laws, two race-based measures depriving Jews of rights, designed by Adolf Hitler and approved by the Nazi Party at a convention in Nürnberg on September 15, 1935. One, the Reichsbürgergesetz (German: “Law of the Reich Citizen”), deprived Jews of German citizenship, designating them “subjects of the state.” The other, the Gesetz zum Schutze des Deutschen Blutes und der Deutschen Ehre (“Law for the Protection of German Blood and German Honour”), usually called simply the Blutschutzgesetz (“Blood Protection Law”), forbade marriage or sexual relations between Jews and “citizens of German or kindred blood.” These measures were among the first of the racist Nazi laws that culminated in the Holocaust.
© United States Holocaust Memorial MuseumUnder these laws, Jews could not fly the German flag and were forbidden “to employ in domestic service female subjects of German or kindred blood who are under the age of 45 years.” The first supplementary decree of November 14, 1935—one of 13 ordinances elaborating these laws—defined Jews as persons with at least one Jewish grandparent and declared explicitly that “a Jew cannot be a citizen of the Reich. He cannot exercise the right to vote; he cannot occupy public office.” The other enactments completed the process of Jewish segregation. Before long Jewish passports were stamped with a red “J” (for Jude; “Jew”), and Jews were compelled to adopt “Jewish” names. Jewish communities were deprived of their legal status by the decree of March 28, 1938, and steps were taken to exclude Jews completely from the practice of medicine.
This racial definition meant that Jews were persecuted not for their religious beliefs and practices but for a so-called racial identity transmitted irrevocably through the blood of their ancestors. These laws resolved the question of definition and set a legal precedent. The Nazis later imposed the Nürnberg Laws on territories they occupied. The laws also provided a model for the treatment and eventual genocide of the Roma (Gypsies).
Although the Nürnberg Laws divided the German nation into Germans and Jews, neither the term Jew nor the phrase German or kindred blood was defined. Because the laws contained criminal provisions for noncompliance, the bureaucrats had the urgent task of spelling out what the words meant. Two basic Jewish categories were established. A full Jew was anyone with three Jewish grandparents. That definition was fairly simple. Defining part-Jews—Mischlinge (“mongrels”)—was more difficult, but they were eventually divided into two classes. First-degree Mischlinge were people who had two Jewish grandparents but did not practice Judaism and did not have a Jewish spouse. Second-degree Mischlinge were those who had only one Jewish grandparent.
The efforts to prove one’s non-Jewish ancestry generated a new cottage industry employing hordes of “licensed family researchers,” offering their services to anxious Germans afraid of a skeleton in the family closet. These efforts also involved the Health Ministry and church offices, which had to provide birth and baptismal certificates.

When we perform an action and get an unexpected reward, we learn to repeat that action. And in the presence of others vying for resources—food, money, and so on—our rivals' actions offer opportunities to guide our behavior. According to new work from researchers at the University of Bristol in England, it is not our peers' successes that stick with us, but their failures.


Volunteers played a simple game, modeled after foraging in the wild, against a computer-controlled opponent. Players chose one of four boxes, which paid out varying sums of money. To maximize winnings, a player had to occasionally sample each of the boxes. When players saw their adversaries get an unexpectedly high sum, functional MRI scans showed no measurable brain activity in reaction. When the machines got an unexpectedly low payout, however, parts of a player's brain associated with inhibition went bonkers.


Learning from competition, then, "is not learning to act like your rival, it is learning not to act like your rival when they fail," explains Paul Howard-Jones, who co-led the study with Rafal Bogacz.


Howard-Jones notes that while the computer was making its move, which simply consisted of one box changing color, the player's mirror neuron system—which is known to respond to the actions of others—was active, as if the player was making the same choice. When that action led to failure, the inhibitory areas put an immediate stop to the mental simulation. Howard-Jones says this is the first time that researchers have seen people show a mirror neuron response to an action performed by a computer (the players were aware that their adversary was simply software).


Marco Iacoboni, a mirror neuron expert at the University of California, Los Angeles, who was not involved with the study, cautions that fMRI's resolution is not fine enough to distinguish whether the neurons firing are mirror neurons or just motor cortex neurons, which fire both when we think about an action and when we actually perform an action. Even if the computer is simply recruiting a player's motor neurons, however, that is still a compelling finding. "It's really a mechanism for why we give human qualities to pretty much everything," Iacoboni says. "We tend to attribute mental states even to things that we know have no mind."

You know how musicians meet their bandmates on Twitter and reporters track down sources? Well, here's a new one for you: long-lost brothers finding each other through social media. And it happened to Matthew Keys, who works as an online news producer for KTXL FOX40 News in Sacramento.

"I always check my email, Twitter, and Facebook before going to bed – you know, just to make sure I didn't miss anything important that happened during the day," Keys told us. Well, around midnight one night, he got a message that was definitely something he hadn't "caught" before. A guy named Adam Smith had written: "Hey is your mom's name Jackie?"

Talk about a conversation starter that would freak anyone out! But when Keys saw Smith's picture, something clicked. He remembered they'd actually talked before. "Adam and I met in some web forum," he explains. "Neither of us can remember exactly how, but we'd been chatting on and off for about a year through MSN and Skype."

Curious (and probably a little nervous), Keys jumped on Skype to message Smith back. "He started telling me stuff from when I was a kid – things I'd only heard bits and pieces of growing up. Things that nobody else could possibly know," Keys said. "So I gave him my cell and told him to call me. After just a few minutes on the phone, it was pretty obvious to both of us that we were related. We ended up talking for four hours straight! I actually had to call in sick the next day because I was so exhausted."

So how did Smith figure out that Keys might be his brother? A few years back, his mom had told him that his dad had other kids out there and mentioned some names – including Keys's. "At some point, Adam started asking his mom and our dad about me, and something just pushed him to reach out on Twitter and ask about my biological mom," Keys explains.

After this incredible reunion, the two brothers went on FOX40 for an interview. And here's the kicker – Keys was able to give Smith a ride to the station because they live just 10 minutes apart from each other!

According to Smith, there are even more siblings out there waiting to be found. Let's hope they're all on Twitter!

As someone who works in online news, Keys really gets how powerful social media can be. "After we shared our story on Facebook, we were completely flooded with emails and posts from people telling us about their own lost and found stories – biological siblings, adopted relatives, you name it," he says. "It was really touching. I use Twitter for the same reason. Both Twitter and Facebook are incredible tools for reaching our TV audience and connecting with people who watch our show – hearing their praise, concerns, and personal stories."

Looks like social media is giving those old-school private investigators some serious competition!

**Regarding Biblical/Religious Historical Claims:**

- According to biblical and historical scholarship, Israel as a nation was established much later than Abraham's time - Abraham is considered the patriarch who received God's covenant, but the nation of Israel developed through his descendants over many generations

- Islamic tradition and historical sources attribute the founding of Islam to the Prophet Muhammad in 7th century Arabia, not to Ishmael (Abraham's son), though Ishmael is revered as an important patriarch in Islamic tradition


**Educational Standards Context:**

- The National Council for Social Studies emphasizes that historical texts should distinguish between historical facts, religious beliefs, and cultural traditions

- Many state curriculum standards require age-appropriate but accurate treatment of religious topics when they intersect with historical events


**Textbook Review Process:**

- Educational publishers typically employ subject matter experts, historians, and educators in their review process

- Common concerns in textbook adoption include factual accuracy, age-appropriateness, and balanced treatment of religious and cultural topics


**Alternative Approaches:**

- Some parents supplement textbooks with primary sources or consult with educators about concerning content

- Many historians recommend cross-referencing multiple sources when teaching complex historical topics involving religion

## Force Vector Analysis


**Original concept**: "The lines CB and CA are vector quantities that are supporting and acting opposite the object F"


**Alternative expressions**:

- CB and CA represent force vectors that counteract the downward force F

- The tension forces along CB and CA work to balance the applied load F

- CB and CA are force components that oppose the gravitational force F


## Reference Line Construction


**Original concept**: "Draw a line through point C parallel to the base support. Measure the angle between CB and the line just drawn"


**Alternative expressions**:

- Establish a horizontal reference line at point C to determine the angle of vector CB

- Create a baseline parallel to the support surface to measure CB's inclination

- Use a horizontal datum line through C to find the angular orientation of CB


## Vector Component Resolution


**Original concept**: "Since vectors CB and CA are not acting parallel or perpendicular to the base of the stand, it is helpful to find the components of each of these vectors"


**Alternative expressions**:

- Break down the angled forces CB and CA into horizontal and vertical components

- Resolve the diagonal force vectors into perpendicular directional components

- Decompose the inclined forces into their rectangular coordinate components


## Trigonometric Relationships


**Original concept**: "To find components of vector CB, form a right triangle with CB as the hypotenuse"


**Alternative expressions**:

- Use CB as the hypotenuse of a right triangle to calculate its perpendicular components

- Apply trigonometric functions to determine the horizontal and vertical elements of CB

- Project the force vector CB onto the coordinate axes using geometric relationships


## Force Equilibrium


**Original concept**: "Since the weight is static, and not moving, we can assume all the forces are balanced"


**Alternative expressions**:

- The system is in static equilibrium, so all forces sum to zero

- Under equilibrium conditions, the net force on the system equals zero

- The balanced state requires that opposing forces are equal in magnitude


## Component Force Balance


**Original concept**: "The vector represented by CE (to the right) must be balanced by the vector CD (to the left)"


**Alternative expressions**:

- Horizontal force components CE and CD are equal and opposite

- The rightward force CE is counteracted by an equal leftward force CD

- CE and CD form an equilibrium pair in the horizontal direction

Marigolds and oven gloves will soar in price under a European Union ruling that mandates more rigorous testing to prevent injuries. Brussels will vote next week on proposals requiring washing up gloves to be certified for resistance to standard kitchen detergents. Under the plans, oven gloves must undergo tests proving they can withstand heat of around 200C. Marigolds and oven gloves are set to go up in price because of Brussels regulations.

Business minister Matt Hancock branded the move 'bonkers' and vowed to block it. When approved, the directive will raise the value of these items - which are extensively used by business as well as households - by up to 20pc, according to an official impact assessment. All products currently on the market will be pulled, and shelves restocked with new ones meeting the new standards.

Mr Hancock declared yesterday: 'This EU power grab for our kitchen sinks is completely bonkers. It will place a huge weight on businesses who are trying to serve their customers. These over-zealous proposals underscore the need for EU reform and why we must fight Brussels over-regulation to get the best deal for Britain.'

The proposal is contained in the Personal Protective Equipment Directive. It states that 'personal health and safety are fundamental rights and people expect and require a high level of protection at work, home and at leisure.'

A spokesman for the European Commission stated: 'Every jurisdiction anywhere in the developed world has product safety and quality standards covering pretty much all products to avoid people being injured. Sub-standard oven gloves will obviously lead to severe burns – and people conned out of money for products that don't work.'

He added that changes to EU rules are 'always subject to extensive consultation with industry and impact assessment' and must be approved by a majority of member states in the EU Parliament.

Business Secretary Matthew Hancock slammed the 'bonkers' EU rules which he claimed will push up prices.

Earlier this year the EU banned high-powered vacuum cleaners as part of an energy efficiency directive to tackle climate change. Any machine with a motor exceeding 1,600 watts was removed from the market. Sir James Dyson, creator of some of the most sought-after vacuum cleaners, launched a legal challenge.

The EU is also consulting on changing the energy limits of other electrical goods including hairdryers, kettles and lawnmowers. A decision is expected next year on whether to ban high-powered models.

New chapter: Boston bombing victim Jeff Bauman is releasing a book about his experiences in April . Boston . bombing survivor Jeff Bauman has written a book that will be published a week before the first anniversary . of the deadly blast in April. After heading to the Capitol for the event, Bauman tweeted: 'I wore pants for the first time in 9 . months! And I . also met the president.' The cover shows him standing on two prosthetic legs with 'Boston' emblazoned across his t-shirt. The New Hampshire native, 27, lost both of his lower legs in the April . 15 attack. An iconic photo taken moments after the blast showed Bauman gray-faced with . nothing but exposed bone below his knees. But he has since overcome unimaginable adversities to remain positive and Grand Central Publishing will publish the book about his experiences on April 8, CBS Boston reported. Bauman, who was working to pay off his . student loans at the deli counter in Costco before the life-changing . blast, worked with co-writer Bret Witter on the book. A description on Amazon explains: 'Jeff Bauman woke up on Tuesday, April 16th 2013 and he had no legs. Fighter: On Tuesday, Bauman, right, appeared beside Carlos Arredondo, who helped save his life after the bombings, at the State of the Union address at the U.S. Capitol . Back on his feet: Bauman walked on his prosethetics, left, while Arredondo, waved at Michelle Obama, right . 'Just . thirty hours prior, Jeff was surrounded by revelry at the finish line . of the 2013 Boston Marathon. The first bomb went off at his feet as he . awaited his girlfriend’s finish. 'When Jeff awoke days later from hours . of surgery, rather than take stock of his now completely altered life, . Jeff ripped out his breathing tube and tried to speak. He couldn’t. 'Jeff . asked for a pad and paper and he wrote down seven words, "Saw the guy. Looked right at me", setting off one of the biggest manhunts in the . country’s history and beginning his own brave road to recovery.' It adds that the book aims to inspire people by sharing his experiences and his mission to walk again. 'Jeff will show the terrorists that they . accomplished nothing with their act of cowardice and prove to the entire . world what Boston Strong really means,' it reads. Life-changing: Carlos Arredondo pinches Bauman's femoral artery as they rush from the blast site moments after the April 15 bombing in Boston. Bauman lost both lower legs and much of his hearing . Battle: He lost both of his lower legs in the blast and has since been fitted with prosthetic limbs . Bauman appeared at the State of the . Union speech alongside the man who helped save him, Carlos Arredondo. The men have remained close and Bauman was pictured with his arm around the man's shoulder. Bauman went to the marathon waiting to . see his girlfriend cross the finish line when was close to one of the . explosive-filled backpacks allegedly placed by the Tsarnaev brothers. He . was thrown by the force of the blast and opened his eyes to find . himself in a pool of blood. He saw one of his friends look at his legs . in horror and looked down to see his limbs were shredded. He . underwent an emergency amputation to remove his legs at the knees, and . two days later, flesh was amputated until four inches above the knees. Support: He is pictured with his girlfriend, Erin Hurley, who had been running the marathon that day . Authorities have praised Bauman, who . also lost much of his hearing in the blast, as key to stopping Tsarnaev . and his brother Dzhokhar after he helped identify them as the suspects. 'Even in the ambulance ride I was . trying to say something, trying to say, like, "I knew who did it, I knew . what went on",' he said in an earlier interview with NBC News. 'And . then I think they were kind of thrown back by that.' Bauman . added that he is now looking forward and, while he could wonder how . differently he could have acted that day to avoid the blast, there is no . point. Instead, he is focusing on his recovery, which has impressed doctors. 'It's insane,' he said. 'I'm a quick healer. They were calling me 'Wolverine'.'

**Product Returned Due to Multiple Design Flaws**

We ultimately returned this feeder after encountering several significant usability issues:

**Size and Placement Concerns:**
- The food compartments are surprisingly small, even for our tiny Chihuahua
- The circular design requires 360-degree access, preventing wall placement
- Having a pet feeder in the center of the room was impractical for our space

**Major Usability Problems:**
- Removing the feeder for cleaning and refilling is extremely difficult
- Requires excessive force to detach the unit
- Reassembly is equally challenging, requiring precise alignment with hidden plastic clips underneath
- The attachment mechanism seems designed more to keep humans out than pets

**Overall Assessment:**
The design prioritizes pet-proofing over user-friendliness to an impractical degree. We expected a more intuitive system that would be secure from pets while remaining accessible for routine maintenance.

**Result:** Returned due to poor design and daily usability issues.

It was dim outside. No wait, dark outside. It was the time when the...uhhm...ball of light has lowered and you usually sleep. The street was lacking in people, except for two men. The water...uh.. plunked? Postured? Ah! The rain Pelted them as the sleep time wore on. The men just stared at each other. One of them finally spoke, "So you had the guts to show up huh?" "Innocent people are going to die Brendan, you need to tell me what you know!" "Innocent people don't matter compared to what you did to me!" "It's all in the past Brendan you can still redeem yourself if you just tell me what you know!" "I'll tell you what,"he pulled out a gun that gluttoned...glitttered...no, glinted! in the light of the smaller ball of light's light."I'll tell you over my dead body!" Brendan let loose a barge of bullets...wait, I meant barrage of bullets the other male character goosed out of the way. He returned fire with 3 umm, bullets moving towards Brendan in a rapid fashion! One hit Brendan in the arm, one hit him in the front face of the part below his head, one- Publisher's note: this is the point where the author stopped writing. He left screaming something about a thesaurus, and hasn't been back to the publishing office in days.

Hey, so I loved reading your story! You've got some really compelling elements here - the magic system, the oppressive government, and that tragic irony of being discovered while doing something heroic. Here's how it might sound if you were just telling it to a friend:

---

So picture this - I'm literally hiding under the floorboards while gunshots are going off everywhere above me. The ministry of defense is tearing my parents' house apart looking for me, and honestly? I kind of saw this coming. I'd gotten sloppy with my fire magic, which is basically a death sentence these days.

The whole thing started when I was just a kid, you know? I was supposed to be throwing out all my great-grandfather's old books - the government calls them "forbidden knowledge" or whatever - but I couldn't help myself. There was this one book with the coolest pictures of a guy shooting fire from his fingertips! Little me was like "I'm gonna be a superhero!" 

It took weeks of sneaking around and practicing, but eventually I could make actual embers in my palm. I was so excited that I ran straight to my parents to show them. Big mistake. Mom started crying, and Dad had to explain that our great-grandfather was actually one of those old mages who got hunted down when the government took over. They made me promise never to do it again because, you know, death penalty and all that.

So there I am, listening to these soldiers stomp around upstairs, and I'm thinking about how unfair this whole situation is. I only got caught because I was trying to HELP someone! This kid had accidentally set his house on fire with his family inside, and I thought I could put it out without anyone seeing. I'd figured out how to absorb flames, not just create them. But of course, right as I'm finishing up, the neighbor sees me and starts screaming "demon!" into her phone.

My dad had built this hiding spot for me years ago, just in case. But apparently it wasn't good enough because suddenly there's light flooding in as they rip open the trap door...

---

What I love about your story is the world-building and that moral complexity - he's punished for saving people! For your next draft, maybe try reading it out loud? It helps catch places where the flow feels a bit choppy. Also, don't be afraid to really lean into the emotions - we want to feel his fear and frustration. Keep writing - you've got a great foundation here!

A woman who was stalked by her ex-boyfriend left her family a note apologising for being 'weak' hours before she fell to her death from a motorway bridge, an inquest has heard. Kamaljit Sidhu, 29, died on October 25 last year following months of harassment from her ex-partner Ryan Dey. She died hours after plunging  from a motorway bridge over the M6 near Birmingham in the early hours of the morning. Kamaljit Sidhu (left), 29, died on October 25 last year following months of harassment from her ex-partner Ryan Dey (right) The bar manager was seen sitting on the ledge holding onto a gate while drinking from a bottle of wine shortly before she fell to her death. An inquest heard how the 'bubbly' 29-year-old had appeared happy on the day she died. But her attitude changed when she saw Dey - and her friend Wioletta Zawislak told Smethwick Coroner's Court that Miss Sidhu had 'crawled into the toilet' to hide from him. Ms Sidhu, known to friends as Kam, had been in a relationship with Dey for 18 months but she split up with him a week before her death in October. But the lorry driver refused to accept the relationship was over and stalked her on social media and bombarded her with late night calls to her home. In April, Dey, 23, was jailed for two years after he admitted stalking and causing serious alarm or distress. Dey had threatened her when she wanted to end the relationship, subjecting her to a long campaign that included sitting outside her home in his car sounding the horn. The inquest heard three young men saw Ms Sidhu, from Great Barr, sitting on a ledge drinking wine above the M6 before letting go and shouting 'no, no, no'. She later died of multiple injuries. Ms Sidhu (pictured left and right), known to friends as Kam, had been in a relationship with Dey for 18 months but she split up with him a week before her death in October . Family members later found a note attached to a bottle of moisturiser that said: 'I loved you all. I just got weak. Sorry. x.'. Recording a verdict of death by misadventure Coroner for the Black Country Robin Balmain said her actions on the bridge were 'inherently dangerous'. He added: 'This is a young lady who was troubled clearly. 'She had earlier suggested that her family might be at some risk if she went home. What happened is tragic.' The court heard that Dey had harassed Ms Sidhu in the last few months of the relationship before she was driven to split from him. He had stepped up his campaign of harassment during the last week of her life, the court was told. Dey stole her mobile phone while they were together and even hacked into her Facebook account to find out who she was talking to. He also threatened to reveal personal secrets to her family and rang her parents' home so often they were forced to change their number. Ms Sidhu died hours after plunging from a motorway bridge over the M6 (pictured) near  Birmingham . Family members found a note attached to a bottle of moisturiser after Ms Sidhu died, that said: 'I loved you all. I just got weak. Sorry. x.' Earlier Miss Zawislak told the inquest: 'She was really happy and laughing until she saw him. 'Then she crawled to the toilet to hide from him. She was terrified and shaking.' Speaking after the inquest, her sister Narinder Kaur said: 'Kam said to us that whatever he says he will do, he will do. 'We still do not know what happened to her between 2.30am and when she was seen on the ledge at 4.34pm. 'It's disappointing because there is a large gap there. No-one knows what she was doing or who she was with for those two hours. 'We were hoping to find out what happened then during the inquest, but we didn't. We think there is more to it.'

Question: How should secondary schools and colleges envision calculus as the course that sits astride the transition from secondary to postsecondary mathematics for most students heading into mathematically intensive careers?
Although calculus can play an important role in secondary school, the ultimate goal of the K–12 mathematics curriculum should not be to get students into and through a course in calculus by twelfth grade but to have established the mathematical foundation that will enable students to pursue whatever course of study interests them when they get to college. The college curriculum should offer students an experience that is new and engaging, broadening their understanding of the world of mathematics while strengthening their mastery of tools that they will need if they choose to pursue a mathematically intensive discipline.
In particular, the fact that calculus is a college mathematics course that increasingly is taught in high school requires that —
Students who enroll in a calculus course in secondary school should have demonstrated mastery of algebra, geometry, trigonometry, and coordinate geometry;
The calculus course offered in secondary school should have the substance of a mainstream college-level course;
The college curriculum should acknowledge the ubiquity of calculus in secondary school, shape the college calculus curriculum so that it is appropriate for those who have experienced introductory calculus in high school, and offer alternatives to calculus.
Faculty in our colleges and secondary schools should work together to re-envision the role of calculus in secondary and postsecondary mathematics education. Faculty on both sides of the transition from secondary to college mathematics should work together to strengthen the mathematics curriculum so that students who intend to pursue a mathematically intensive career can acquire the mathematical knowledge and capabilities needed for such a career. College faculty and secondary teachers should define what it means for a student to be ready for college-level mathematics. After a student has matriculated in college, they should assess the effect of college-level mathematics offered in secondary school. They also should clarify and broaden what is meant by college-level mathematics for secondary school. They should also work to achieve a better understanding of the mathematical strengths and weaknesses of matriculating students, assess the effectiveness of placement programs for collegiate mathematics, and clarify and broaden what the first year of college mathematics can and should entail.
MAA and NCTM are committed to taking appropriate action within the structure of their organizations to assist in guiding the implementation of these recommendations.
MAA Past President David Bressoud wrote about the Joint Policy Statement for his April MAA Launchings column.

Dan, my neighbor, is outside working on his oldest son's bicycle. Normally, I wouldn't talk to him. He's one of those 'You might be a redneck' type of guys; overgrown grass, dog on a giant chain, and a random appliance or ten scattered throughout his yard. He hocks and spits every five minutes, unless he's talking, and then it's pretty constant. Still, he's going through a lot, and it *would* be the neighborly thing to do. "Hey, Dan," I say. Dan grunts. "How's she doing?" "She'll live," he says. I kick at a random tuft of grass. "Well," I say. I glance longingly at my own house, not ten feet away. I'd much rather be inside. "What was wrong with...you know...?" I try again after a moment. "Called it placenta previa or something," says Dan. I've heard of that before. "Can't they usually..." "Not every time, I reckon." Dan jumps up, looking furious, and I feel like an idiot. A true friend would let him take a swing, get it out of his system. I'm glad we're just neighbors. Dan could break me. "Sorry," I say, and he nods. "Need some help with that?" Dan hands me a wrench, and I take off my jacket. I can hear his wife sobbing inside.

**Your Child's School Tests Are Getting Much Harder**

If you have a child in Year 6 (age 11), get ready for some big changes. The government has announced that SATs tests are about to become significantly more challenging.

**What this means for your child:**

**Maths will be tougher**
- Your child will need to memorize their 11 and 12 times tables (not just up to 10)
- No calculators allowed - they'll have to do all calculations in their head or on paper
- They'll tackle harder problems like working out the area of shapes and volume of 3D objects
- Old-fashioned methods like long multiplication will earn extra marks

**English gets stricter**
- Spelling tests will include notoriously tricky words like "separate," "preferred," and "necessary"
- Proper punctuation using semicolons, apostrophes, and capital letters is essential
- American spellings like "color" and "theater" will be marked wrong - only British English accepted
- Reading comprehension will require longer, more detailed written answers

**The bigger picture**
Around 600,000 children across England will face these tougher tests starting in 2016. The government says English pupils are falling behind countries like China by about three years in maths, and these changes aim to close that gap.

Education Minister Elizabeth Truss explains the goal is simple: "We want to make sure no child leaves primary school unable to read, write, or do basic maths properly."

**What parents need to know**
The new national curriculum starts this September, giving schools and families two years to prepare before the harder tests begin. If your child is currently in Year 4 or below, they'll be taking these more demanding exams.

The message is clear: the days of "getting by" with basic skills are over. The government wants children to have rock-solid foundations before they head to secondary school.

# Farewell to Our Flying Jewels: Utah's Hummingbirds Begin Their Journey South

*Originally from Wild About Utah and Stokes Nature Center*

As fall approaches across Utah, it's time to bid farewell to some of our most enchanting visitors—the hummingbirds. These tiny aerial acrobats are preparing for their incredible journey south to warmer climates and more abundant food sources.

## A New World Wonder

Hummingbirds are truly a New World phenomenon, found only in the western hemisphere. These remarkable birds are primarily tropical creatures—of the 330 known species, an impressive 95% live south of the US-Mexico border. Ecuador leads the world with 163 species, while Colombia follows with 136, including a new species discovered just five years ago.

The Spanish language beautifully captures the essence of these birds with names like *colibrí* (the generic term), *picaflores* (meaning "flower pickers"), and the particularly poetic *joyas voladores*—"flying jewels."

## North America's Hummingbird Diversity

Despite North America's vast size, only 16 different hummingbird species regularly call this continent home. Interestingly, except for the Ruby-throated Hummingbird, all of these species breed west of the Mississippi River.

Here in Utah—roughly comparable in size to Ecuador—we're blessed with five regular species:

**Broad-tailed and Black-chinned Hummingbirds** are our most common and widespread visitors, both overwintering in Mexico.

**Calliope Hummingbird** holds multiple records: it's the smallest breeding bird in North America, weighing about as much as a penny, and it's also the world's smallest long-distance migrant, traveling up to 5,600 miles annually.

**Rufous Hummingbird** is another impressive long-distance traveler, journeying from as far north as Alaska all the way down to central Mexico.

## The Fall Feeding Debate

There's ongoing controversy about whether to continue feeding hummingbirds in fall. Some believe you should stop feeding by late August, fearing the birds won't migrate. This is simply not true—many hummingbirds actually begin migrating while their natural food sources are still abundant.

According to the Audubon Society, you should keep your feeders up for two weeks after seeing the last bird using them. These tiny travelers need to double their body mass before migration, and extra nectar can only help fuel their incredible journey.

## A Conservation Note

As we watch these remarkable birds prepare for their southern migration, it's worth remembering that hummingbirds face numerous challenges, from habitat loss to climate change. Supporting conservation efforts and maintaining bird-friendly gardens can help ensure future generations will continue to enjoy these living jewels.

So as you see fewer hummingbirds visiting your feeders in the coming weeks, take a moment to appreciate the incredible journey these tiny athletes are about to undertake. Their epic migration is just another reminder of the amazing natural world we share here in Utah.

---

*This post was adapted from a Wild About Utah segment by Holly Strand from Stokes Nature Center in Logan Canyon. Special thanks to the Rocky Mountain Power Foundation and Corrine Thul for supporting hummingbird conservation and educational programming.*

**Sources:**
- National Audubon Society
- "The Hummingbirds of North America" by Paul A. Johnsgard
- National Geographic
- Various wildlife conservation resources

I died. This is a weird point for a story to start, but whatever. I wasn't even that mad about being dead. What really annoyed me was thinking about what my father would say: "Ha. I told you to wear a seatbelt. I always tell you to wear a seatbelt. But you never listen. I always knew something like this would happen." My father always claims he knew things would happen, but it’s always after the fact. He'll read something in the morning paper and insist he predicted it all along, whether it's a scandal or a disaster on the other side of the world—no matter how unlikely.

My mother is a worrier. She's always scolding me for not answering my phone in public, so she’s used to me not picking up, but right about now she’d probably be starting to worry. These and other random thoughts floated through my mind as I walked toward the white light. It felt a little clichéd, but I figured this was heaven, not a concert, and maybe it needed some gravitas. People probably wouldn’t take it as seriously if they died and saw flashing colored lights.

I was also thinking about whether I would rather be a paraplegic if I survived the crash, or just dead—would that have been better? Would it have improved my chances with girls, as if I even had any? I reached the source of the white light. It turned out to be a phone booth. I went inside and picked up the phone.

A pleasant woman’s voice spoke: "You died on Earth. Welcome back to normalcy. This call is part of your reorientation."

Where do I go from here? I thought I’d need accommodation.

“You are a Pandimensional being. All concepts you hold about time and space, including needing accommodation, are distorted.”

I just assumed heaven would be different. There was something about “Magrathea Custom 3-D Virtual Worlds Incorporated.”, and something about me being a tourist and their customer. Apparently, “living like a mortal and dying” is an adventurous, recreational activity that lots of us tourists take part in. Some call it going on a “spiritual journey.”

Living and dying in three dimensions, she explained, is basically the Pandimensional version of low-res gaming. You immerse yourself in a lower dimension, pretend things matter, and then you die. Earth is basically like Super Mario Bros., a popular custom world that many visit.

What now, though?

“Depends. You could choose to be reborn as a different person in a different time and play the game again. You’ve already done this more than a thousand times. I’m pretty sure you’re a college student procrastinating on an assignment that’s due tomorrow. You’ve played enough, is all I’m saying.”

Sounds like me. You’re right—I’m done. How do I leave? I want to get back to my higher-dimensional self.

“Cool. Just press the red button to the left of the phone. Careful, though. You’ve heard of Plato’s cave in the simulation, right? For a three-dimensional being, traveling to a higher dimension and back can be disorienting. For you, it’ll be the opposite, but still, it can mess with your head.”

I hung up and stared at the button for a full ten minutes. It was there, all right. It felt like standing at the edge of a pool and preparing to jump in. I’d been told to just press the button, but every instinct told me not to.

“Here goes nothing…” I said, and pressed the button.

**Is "The Customer Is Number One" Just a Slogan? My Disappointing Audi Service Experience**

Just ten days ago, I was driving off the lot, excited about my newly purchased Certified Audi. Like any new car owner, I expected a smooth journey, especially with a certified pre-owned vehicle. However, a small problem soon cropped up, and my salesman assured me it was a quick fix – just bring it into the service department.

What followed was a stark reminder that the "Customer is Number One" mantra doesn't always apply once the sale is made. Upon arriving at the service department as instructed, I was met with a complete refusal to even initiate paperwork for my vehicle. Despite being sent there by their own sales team to address an issue, the service department wouldn't touch my car without the salesman physically present.

I was left waiting, feeling completely disregarded, until my salesman eventually arrived. The irony? He sorted everything out in less than ten minutes. It makes you wonder why such a simple resolution required a specific person to be present, causing unnecessary delay and frustration. The disconnect between sales and service was palpable, and it left a sour taste.

This experience has made my decision clear. In the future, I'll be taking my vehicle and my business to Audi in Morton Grove, where I hope the commitment to customer service extends beyond the sales floor.

Satan handed back the soul and said, “Here’s your soul back. But... I need a favor.” He glanced down at his feet, then out to the horizon. “Saddam hasn't asked me to the prom, and I think he’s cheating on me.”

Cartman replied, “A deal’s a deal, and I’m afraid I can’t give it back. You promised me I’d rule the world, and that’s where I’m headed.”

Satan interrupted, desperate, “Wait, wait! I’ll still help you with that. You don’t understand—if I don’t have a date for the prom, I’ll be the laughingstock of all hell. I’ll never regain their respect. You have to help me! After the prom, I’ll give you whatever you want.”

Cartman considered this, then said slyly, “Very well. But this time, I want more than just ruling the world.” He turned to his right with a small, cunning grin.

Satan looked at him, intrigued and confused, thinking, “What more could this kid possibly want? I’ve already promised him everything.”

Satan asked, “What do you want?”

Cartman just smiled...

Pickle Springs Natural Area is a deep, forested gorge consisting of geological formations and plants that are found in few other places. Visitors will find examples of a sandstone glade, talus, forest, savanna, as well as a number of rare species of plants and animals. The area is noted for its blooming wild azalea and wildflowers in the spring. Great after a beautiful snow fall as you see. According to local legend, the area is named after William Pickles, a settler from Illinois who owned the land until he was shot by a band of renegades during the Civil War. Pickle Springs and other area creeks flow into Pickle Creek, which in turn traverses into Hawn State Park.
The trail at Pickle Springs Natural Area may be short, but it packs in a ton of scenic beauty.
The 2 mile loop goes by sandstone canyons, glades, bluffs, waterfalls and wanders through a hoodoo complex.

The "Normal Day" campaign aims to explain to the public the roles the Army fulfils around the world.
It has been launched alongside a recruitment drive, aimed at attracting regulars and reserves. Both campaigns will cost a total of Â£7m.
Last year the Army only recruited 20 fully-trained reserves.
In its recruiting year 2013/14 it also missed its target for regulars - 6,198 recruits joined the Army, against a target of 9,382.
The rebranding campaign comes after a survey suggested 20% of Britons believe the Army is less relevant now than ever before.
This figure rises to 25% among the Army's core recruitment audience, of those aged between 18 and 34.
When asked what ought to be the Army's current priority, conflict came top with 34%, but when asked what should be a key task only 11% chose humanitarian aid and only 13% suggested it should be disaster relief.
The research was carried out by market research firm OnePoll between 12 and 19 December 2014, among 3,000 adults.
The "Normal Day" campaign will include three documentaries showing real-life examples of the impact that individual Army personnel have made to people's lives in the UK, Kosovo and the Philippines.
And the Army recruitment campaign, conducted under the banner of "More Than Meets The Eye", will see a series of recruitment events being held around the country.
By Jonathan Beale, BB defence correspondent
When politicians or organisations choose to "rebrand", you know something's going wrong.  In the case of the British army it's the fear that after Afghanistan it'll disappear from the public eye. Its own polling shows most people have no idea what the army does other than fight wars.
With an election coming and more defence cuts on the horizon, the Army is having to work hard to remind people why it's there.
It's a two-pronged campaign. The other target is attracting new soldiers. Recruitment is proving to be difficult too without the drumbeat of war.
Last year the Army only managed to increase the total strength of the reserves by 20 - it was also more than 3,000 soldiers short of its recruitment goal for full time soldiers. These are worrying times for the Army's top brass.
Brig Nick Orr, the Army's head of communications, said the rebranding drive would "improve understanding about what the Army does, but also to showcase the people that make our Army the force it is for good".
He added: "We are proud of the Army's values and role which is absolutely to protect the United Kingdom and fight its enemies, but also to prevent conflict and deal with disaster. While we recognise the clear need to adapt and change with the times, our role and relevance is enduring.
"The Army is as relevant now, if not more so, than ever before amid these incredibly complex times.
"The campaign breaks new ground for the Army and vividly brings to life the many, and often under-recognised, duties it performs across a number of different communities and scenarios."
Maj Gen Chris Tickell, director general of the Army recruiting and training division, said those joining the Army Reserve would experience "a challenge, excitement, the opportunity to travel and a decent salary" to a "world-class standard".
"No matter what your day job, the Army Reserve has something to offer you in your spare time, whether you want to use and improve your existing workplace skills, or try your hand at something completely different.
"You can travel the world, gain qualifications and benefit from confidence and leadership skills that will help you shine in your civilian career."

The "Normal Day" campaign seeks to articulate the diverse roles undertaken by the Army globally.
It has been launched in conjunction with a recruitment initiative, designed to attract both regular and reserve personnel. The combined cost for both campaigns is projected at Â£7m.
In the previous year, the Army recruited only 20 fully-trained reserves.
For its 2013/14 recruiting year, the Army also failed to meet its target for regular personnel, with 6,198 recruits joining against a target of 9,382.
This rebranding campaign follows a survey indicating that 20% of Britons perceive the Army's current relevance to be diminished.
This percentage increases to 25% within the Army's primary recruitment demographic, individuals aged 18 to 34.
When respondents were queried regarding the Army's primary current priority, conflict was identified by 34%. However, when asked to select a key task, only 11% chose humanitarian aid and 13% suggested disaster relief.
The research was conducted by market research firm OnePoll between 12 and 19 December 2014, encompassing 3,000 adult participants.
The "Normal Day" campaign will feature three documentaries illustrating tangible examples of the impact achieved by individual Army personnel in the UK, Kosovo, and the Philippines.
Furthermore, the Army recruitment campaign, operating under the designation "More Than Meets The Eye", will involve a series of recruitment events held nationwide.
The British Army has initiated a rebranding effort amidst concerns that its public profile may diminish following the conclusion of operations in Afghanistan. Internal polling indicates a prevalent lack of understanding regarding the Army's functions beyond combat operations.
Amidst an impending election and prospective defense expenditure reductions, the Army is engaged in substantial efforts to reaffirm its strategic relevance.
This campaign comprises two primary components: public engagement and recruitment. Recruitment efforts are currently experiencing challenges, particularly in the absence of active combat engagements.
In the preceding year, the Army achieved a net increase of only 20 in the total strength of its reserves, and fell short of its recruitment objective for full-time personnel by over 3,000 soldiers. These developments represent significant concerns for senior Army leadership.
Brig Nick Orr, the Army's Head of Communications, stated that the rebranding initiative aims to "enhance public understanding of the Army's functions, concurrently highlighting the personnel who enable the Army to serve as a force for positive impact."
He further elaborated: "We take pride in the Army's values and its fundamental role, which encompasses the protection of the United Kingdom, engagement with its adversaries, alongside conflict prevention and disaster response. While acknowledging the imperative for adaptation and evolution, our role and relevance persist.
"The Army maintains its relevance, if not enhanced relevance, amidst the current era of profound complexity.
"The campaign represents a novel approach for the Army, vividly illustrating its numerous, and frequently underestimated, duties across diverse communities and operational contexts."
Maj Gen Chris Tickell, Director General of the Army Recruiting and Training Division, stated that individuals joining the Army Reserve would experience "challenge, professional development, travel opportunities, and competitive remuneration" to a "world-class standard."
"Regardless of one's civilian occupation, the Army Reserve provides opportunities for engagement during non-working hours, encompassing the utilization and enhancement of existing professional skills, or the acquisition of entirely new proficiencies.
"Participants can access global travel, pursue qualifications, and develop confidence and leadership competencies that will benefit their civilian careers."

What would eventually become Hurricane Darby developed about 2800 miles east-southeast of Hawaii on July 26, 2004. Moving to the northwest and then west, Darby ultimately reached peak intensity of 105 kt (120 mph) on July 29 (satellite). Shortly thereafter, Darby moved into an area of cooler waters and stronger upper level winds, both of which are unfavorable for a hurricane to sustain itself. Just two days later, Darby was nothing more than just a low level swirl of clouds and showers with winds of only 20 to 25 knots as it was about 1000 miles east of the state (satellite). This area of moisture was being pushed westward toward Hawaii by the tradewind flow.


**As these remnants approached the islands,** an upper level low was located just to the northwest of the state, and this had made the atmosphere slightly unstable over Hawaii. As the remnant swirl of Darby moved closer to the unstable region, thunderstorms began to develop. The first round of thunderstorms occurred just north and east of the Big Island on August 2 (satellite). That night, additional showers and thunderstorms formed across parts of the Big Island, particularly the normally dry Kona side (satellite, radar). Rainfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches over a few hours were reported, and this led to flooding and closures of several roads.


**After affecting the Big Island,** the remnants moved across Maui during the day on August 3 (satellite, radar). Locally heavy rainfall occurred on the southeast flank of Haleakala, otherwise the development of heavy and widespread rainfall was limited. However that changed during the night as the remnants approached Oahu. Once again thunderstorms developed, affecting the entire island of Oahu and dumping several inches of rain in a few hours (satellite, radar). A few streams overflowed their banks and minor landslides occurred, both resulting in some road closures. The main effect was significant ponding of water on the roads, which impacted the morning rush hour. **Continuing its westward path,** by mid day August 4, Darby's remnants began to affect Kauai (satellite), but the bulk of the heaviest rainfall stayed south of the island, thus sparing the island from any heavy rains, with the exception at the normally wet Mount Waialeale.


**The rainfall amounts recorded across the islands were particularly noteworthy because** heavy rains during the summer are very unusual, especially on the drier leeward sides of the islands. In fact, the heavy rainfall was a record in Honolulu. The Honolulu Airport recorded a total of 3.06 inches from the storm, 2.92 inches which fell on August 4. This was the heaviest one day rainfall for the entire month of August. The previous record being 2.10 inches that fell on August 6, 1959 as Hurricane Dot was passing by. This heavy rainfall also pushed the monthly rainfall total at Honolulu above the previously wettest August since 1949. The previous record was again in 1959 when 3.08 inches fell for the entire month. Normally the Honolulu Airport gets only one-half inch of rain during August and 3 inches during the entire dry season (May through September).

"Hey, man. You there?" The voice was strained, and tinged with desperation. And it appeared to be coming from just outside my barred window. Given the rigid schedule and constant surveillance in County Penitentiary, it was unusual for anyone to be near my cell at this hour; the guards made their rounds like clockwork, and the only time prisoners ever approached each other was during the infrequent rec hour or mealtimes. A pair of eyes peered into my cell hopefully, eyes bloodshot and wild—no doubt a result of too many sleepless nights in this place. I didn't bother to get up from my hard bunk, the standard-issue mattress barely masking the chill of the metal frame beneath. "Oh, good, you're awake. Listen, man, I'm in a spot of trouble here. I seem to have run out of rope. So, uh, could I borrow your bedsheet?" 

Bedsheets in prison were a prized commodity. Not only did they offer a scant layer of comfort, but resourceful inmates often used them for barter or, more dangerously, for makeshift escape tools. Lacing my fingers together, I folded my arms and used them as a makeshift cushion, cradling my head. Only after I'd gotten comfortable did I answer him. "I don't know, man. *Can* you borrow my bedsheet?" 

"C'mon, don't be a jerk. You know you want out of here too. Once I'm away, I'll get you out too, somehow. I know this guy, he's a lawyer, I can put in a word for you, huh?" Inmates in here were always promising something—protection, goods, even legal help—most of it just empty talk born out of desperation. I stifled a yawn. "How'd you get rope in the prison anyway?" The warden had recently stepped up contraband searches after the last escape attempt, and word was anyone caught sneaking something in faced weeks in solitary. 

"You don't want to hear it, trust me. Anyway, the sheets? Please?" "Why should I help you?" "Look, here, man." One hand gripped tightly to his rope, the man fumbled for a bit before pushing two fingers through the iron bars. Held between them was a new cigarette. In here, cigarettes were more valuable than cash—a single one could be traded for food, favors, or protection from the more violent inmates. "I'll give you the rest when you hand me the sheets, alright?" 

"I don't smoke." Rolling to my side, I snuggled deeper into the thin blankets. I could see his jaw clench; the offer had been his ace, the sort of thing that would have scored him help from almost anyone else behind these walls. He looked decidedly more nervous now, eyes fixed to the rope trembling slightly. Whatever he'd tied it to must have been unable to withstand the weight—it wasn’t the first time an escape plan fell apart because someone overestimated their resources or underestimated prison security. 

"Dude, man, c'mon, help me. Help me!" "No." I closed my eyes, and waited for the snap.

A White House national security official was fired after sending insulting messages on Twitter under a fake name, marking another case of social media controversy in government. The story about National Security Council Director of Nonproliferation Jofi Joseph was first reported by the Daily Beast, with a senior administration official confirming to CNN's Jake Tapper that Joseph was terminated for messages sent from the account @natsecwonk.

The now-deleted account had been active since 2011, serving as a platform for Joseph to critique figures across the political spectrum within the foreign policy community. His targets included high-ranking White House officials, with one tweet reading, "I'm a fan of Obama, but his continuing reliance and dependence upon a vacuous cipher like Valerie Jarrett concerns me." Joseph also directed criticism toward Deputy National Security Adviser Ben Rhodes and Secretary of State John Kerry.

However, his commentary wasn't limited to Democrats. The Daily Beast reported that Joseph's messages were equally critical of Republicans, foreign policy experts, and other public figures. In one particularly harsh tweet, he wrote, "So when will someone do us the favor of getting rid of Sarah Palin and the rest of her white trash family? What utter useless..."

The incident highlights ongoing tensions between government employees' personal expression and professional responsibilities in the digital age. CNN attempted to contact Joseph for comment but has not yet received a response. The @natsecwonk account was deleted Tuesday evening, shortly before news of Joseph's firing broke.

His rule saw the country emerge from the collapsing Soviet Union to become an independent nation.
But his more than two decades in power have been marred by lurid allegations of state violence, elections derided as a sham and a public spat that led to the fall from grace of his own daughter.
Karimov was born in 1938 in Samarkand, one of Central Asia's oldest inhabited cities and a key stopping point along the Silk Road.
He was raised in an orphanage before studying engineering and economics at university.
The years that followed saw him rise up the ranks in Uzbekistan's Communist Party, becoming first secretary in 1989 at a time when the Soviet Union was becoming increasingly fragile.
In 1991, Karimov declared Uzbekistan independent and went on to win the country's first presidential election by a huge margin, in what Human Rights Watch termed a "seriously marred" vote.
That pattern has repeated itself throughout his presidency. In last year's election, he was re-elected with 90% in a vote observers said fell far short of international standards.
His rivals themselves offered implicit backing for Karimov during the vote, praising the country's "achievements" since independence.
Karimov's near-monopoly on power was enabled by stifling dissent. Opposition politicians and human rights campaigners have been jailed or thrown out of the country. Independent press outlets have been described as "virtually nonexistent".
Last year, Amnesty said that it was an "open secret that anyone who falls out of favour with the authorities can be detained and tortured in Uzbekistan".
According to another notorious allegation, denied by the authorities, two prisoners were boiled to death in 2002.
Activists and witnesses say state forces killed hundreds of demonstrators in the eastern city of Andijan in 2005, an incident the government blamed on Islamist extremism.
Critics accuse Karimov's government of over-exaggerating the Islamist threat to attract foreign aid and crack down on opposition.
Thousands of ordinary Muslims are believed to have been jailed.
While Karimov's human rights' record has been criticised by international governments, Uzbekistan remains a useful ally, with the US using the country as a base during the Afghan war.
Uzbekistan also controls substantial reserves of oil, gas and gold, and is a major producer of cotton.
Karimov's death raises questions about the stability of a country for so long dominated by one man and who will succeed him.
Power struggles have been going on for years, though, as rivals jockeyed for position to replace the ageing leader.
Karimov's daughter Gulnara was once regarded as a possible successor, but she was reportedly placed under house arrest in 2014 and has not appeared in public since.
His other daughter Lola, Uzbekistan's ambassador to Unesco, officially confirmed her father suffered a stroke on her Instagram page, after a government statement saying only that he was in hospital.
That the Uzbek authorities acknowledged Karimov's illness at all, after persistent rumours about poor health, was a sign his condition was grave.
Among the current contenders to replace him are Prime Minister Shavkat Mirziyoyev and Deputy Prime Minister Rustam Azimov.

# Database Unit Testing: A Beginner's Guide

## What is unit testing?
Think of unit testing like checking individual LEGO blocks before building a castle. You want to make sure each piece works correctly on its own before putting them together.

In databases, these "LEGO blocks" are small pieces of code like:
- **Stored procedures** (mini-programs that run in the database)
- **Views** (saved queries that show data in a specific way)
- **Functions** (code that takes input and gives output)
- **Triggers** (code that runs automatically when something happens)

## Why use automation?
Instead of manually checking each piece every time (which is boring and error-prone), we write automated tests that do the checking for us. It's like having a robot that tests your LEGO blocks!

## Unit Testing vs Integration Testing
- **Unit testing** = Testing one piece at a time
- **Integration testing** = Testing how multiple pieces work together

Think of it like testing a car:
- Unit testing: Does the engine start? Do the brakes work?
- Integration testing: Does the whole car drive properly?

## The Challenge: Dependencies
Real database code often depends on other pieces. For example:
- A view might need data from a table
- One stored procedure might call another stored procedure

This is like testing a LEGO wheel that needs to attach to a LEGO axle.

## The Solution: Test Doubles
We use "fake" versions of the dependencies, called **test doubles**:
- **Fake table**: A simple replacement for a complex table
- **Spy procedure**: A replacement that tells us what parameters were passed to it

This lets us test our code without worrying about all the other pieces it normally depends on.

## Why Use a Testing Framework?
A framework is like a toolbox that gives you:
- **Pre-written common code** (no need to start from scratch each time)
- **Automatic test running** (runs tests inside safe transactions)
- **Clear results reporting** (easy to see what passed or failed)
- **Same language as your database** (no need to learn new syntax)
- **Test organization** (group related tests together)

## The Bottom Line
Database unit testing helps you catch problems early by testing small pieces of code individually, making your database more reliable and easier to maintain.

A Scottish livestock farmer has become an overnight literary sensation after selling more than 50,000 copies of his first detective novel in a month. James Oswald, 45, wrote Natural Causes, a crime thriller starring Detective Inspector McLean, while tending to his 350 cows and sheep at his farm in the Fife countryside. The tale centers around a series of brutal deaths in Edinburgh.


At first James offered the novel on Amazon for free, but asked readers to pay £2.99 for the follow-up The Book of Souls. Within a month his debut had shifted 50,000 copies. It soared to the top of the Kindle bestsellers list where it remains in the top 10. Now James has been signed up by publishers Penguin for a three-book deal worth £150,000. Natural Causes is on the shelves in Waterstones and Tesco and has even been endorsed by Richard and Judy's Book Club.


'The last month has been a whirlwind,' James told MailOnline. 'I'm still trying to catch my breath. This has all happened incredibly quickly. I only self-published the book about a year ago and I signed with Penguin last October. Friends have been tweeting me saying the book has already sold out in some shops.'


But there are no signs he will be giving up his day job - with his first advance, the amateur writer splashed out on a new tractor.


James has been writing all his life, starting with comic strip books while he was a psychology student at Aberdeen University. But his efforts to replicate the dark, mystical workings of heroes Iain Banks and Neil Gaiman failed to attract the attention of publishers.


When James inherited his farm from his parents David and Juliet in 2008 after they died in a car accident, he became even more determined to make his writing a success.


'My father had semi-retired and I was thinking I would come to it eventually,' he told MailOnline. 'But it has been rather harder work than I expected. I love writing - I would have carried on writing for years even if it had only been myself reading. I do a lot of thinking when I'm out in my tractor, or when I'm sitting in the lambing pens. I do the farm work that needs to be done in the daytime and then do my writing in the evening till about midnight. It is pretty tiring.'


If his literary career continues to bring rich rewards he hopes to complete a three-bedroom house with partner Barbara and expand his flock of New Zealand sheep and Highland cattle.


'I would love to write more. I have a lot more ideas,' he added.

Cristiano Ronaldo and Zlatan Ibrahimovic will go head-to-head for a place at the 2014 World Cup after Portugal and Sweden were drawn together in the European qualification playoffs. Portugal captain Ronaldo will lead his team out against Ibrahimovic's Sweden, with one of the star duo set to miss out on a ticket to football's biggest tournament following the draw at FIFA headquarters in Zurich. Eight second-place teams from the nine European groups will compete over two legs for four World Cup berths. Paulo Bento's Portugal were pipped to automatic qualification by one point, finishing behind Fabio Capello's Russia. If Portugal fail to qualify it will be the first time they have missed the tournament since 1998. Sweden, coached by Erik Hamren, will be desperate to reach a 12th World Cup after missing out on South Africa 2010. France, World Cup winners in 1998, were drawn against Euro 2012 co-hosts Ukraine, with Euro 2004 champions Greece taking on Romania. Minnows Iceland, who are bidding to become the smallest nation to successfully qualify for the quadrennial competition, will play Croatia, who sacked coach Igor Stimac last week. Stimac was replaced by former player Niko Kovac, who took over on a caretaker basis. Each of the four ties will be contested over two matches, with the first set to be played on November 15 and the second on November 19. European playoff draw in full: . Portugal vs. Sweden . Ukraine vs. France . Greece vs. Romania . Iceland vs. Croatia .

I remember when I chose to host this shrine. Such a long time ago. The world was smaller then, with smaller families, smaller people, and smaller worlds. This was before the great roads linked distant cities, before the rise of nations and the drawing of borders on parchment and in blood. I remember when the mountain here was the border to our world, when merchants of all shapes, sizes, and kindnesses stopped here to ask favor before walking or riding on into the distance. In those days, mountains divided tribes, and every traveler was a rare visitor—offering trinkets and tales from lands unknown. I've been here since long before your peoples’ dawn, before the night even fell on the callers before you. Millennia before your villages became towns, before your ancestors tamed the wild grains that fed empires, I watched the shadows move across these valleys, saw civilizations flicker and fade beneath the stars.

Back then, I picked a purpose. I would remain, ever watching, until I could pass on my knowledge to someone who could keep it, someone who knew its value and write it, a person who could catalog the aeons. Someone who would teach the world of its past, a person to be my voice, the voice of one who had seen the world almost since its inception. In epochs when oral traditions shaped history and knowledge was as fragile as a child's memory, I chose to witness and remember. Someone to witness the miracle of this shrine.

Less than a century ago, a blink of the eye, your people came and tested me. They measured how much I radiated, and said that they couldn't date me. They said that there was something wrong with the equipment, and that they would return. They never did. In the era of white coats, clipboards, and curiosity boxed within laboratories, they treated this ancient site with sterile skepticism—never weighing me, never talking to me, not even truly looking at me. They simply walked in with their white face masks and pointed a device at me, muttered to each other, then left. In a time when people valued numbers and artifacts over the soul of a place, I became invisible once more.

I must admit being overgrown is possibly better. Now people no longer avoid talking near me, as if an ancient shrine could grant them bad luck. The folk superstitions faded as the wilderness reclaimed the stones, and stories once told in hushed voices at twilight were forgotten. I'm here to teach though, I'm here to keep and dispense knowledge. What use is the sights of the ages if you cannot tell anyone about them? That's where you come in. Children like you should be able to hear me. _sigh_.

The disconnection occurred before I had a chance to say goodbye. I had it all in the NetWorld—a virtual wife, a virtual daughter, a virtual job. Why couldn’t I have been content with that? The world was mine for the taking, and yet I let it slip through my fingers. But I did it for the right reason. I needed to know the truth. Brooke was more than a program—a virtual wife who loved me like no other. She was an echo of someone real, a living, breathing Brooke. And somehow, she found me in my own carefully constructed paradise. But she was in pain. So I broke out. I poked one too many holes in the fabric of the NetWorld. The disconnection was abrupt and unyielding, like a heart attack you sense unfolding with terrifying inevitability. Brooke and Isabella were in the next room... I couldn't even say goodbye. 

But the real Brooke is out here somewhere. I just know it. She’s trapped in the NetScape, just as I was. I just need to find her. The world is harsher than I expected—so much starker, so much more imperfect than mine. Where my world offered eternal life, the only escape from this one is eternal death. Was it worth it? 

I step outside, breathing in the sweltering summer air—hot, humid, drawing beads of sweat down my brow. “Yes,” I say. “It was worth it.”
