While Version 9 presents a modest improvement over Version 8, it remains burdened by a multitude of unresolved issues. It appears the developers struggle to fully update their components, as evidenced by the persistent inclusion of Windows 95 DLLs. This outdated legacy code inevitably causes various errors when the software is run on modern operating systems. This fundamental problem first emerged in Version 7 and has still not been thoroughly addressed. I genuinely appreciate Calendar Creator and wish for its proper functionality, but after three upgrades in the past two years without achieving a stable system, I've concluded this will be my final CC update. It's truly disappointing, as it's a distinctive application that offers great utility for many of us who enjoy designing visual calendars.

"Then that begs only one question. Why are you pointing the gun at me." I worded it as a question, but my voice levied it as a statement. In fact it *was* a statement, this strange self-described time traveler did indeed have a gun pointed at me. "Because, my friend. You are my first name." This only begged more questions. He pulled the trigger before I could ask anything. *** The blood splattered the wall behind him, and I reached into my pocket to retrieve my data-pad. The future is a wonderful place, I thought to myself. Such great technological advances, and chief among them- time travel. It was a powerful tool, reserved only for the utmost of duties, allowed only for the most noble of deeds. There was the paradox, of course. If I did in fact complete my list of names, how would that change the future? Would I cease to exist? Would time travel at all cease to exist? I was not smart enough to answer these questions, all I had was the mission at hand. My data-pad beeped. The press of a button brought it to life. *"Next target. Location: 45 meters due east. Unarmed."* Unarmed...Easy pickings.

"Oh christ it's bright outside of the loft. Who am I to complain, I barely come out nowadays. I'm not going out the way record players did, I'm timeless. Why would anyone want anything better than a CD Player. I've heard rumours of this "Digital Download" world that seems to be spreading. Why would anyone want to buy something they can't touch, that's what I say." A man starts walking towards the CD Player, disc in hand "This is what I'm talking about! He's actually coming to use me! He looks a bit odd compared to that other fella granted but I finally get to stretch my speakers. Oh man this is gonna feel good. Sooooooo good. The other guy knew what I liked. Bit of the ol' Metallica on a Saturday night while he drank something from a can. Hell, if he felt like it he used to put some KISS in my drawer on the odd occasion." The man opens the case, on the cover is three archways, men dressed in red and an ornate framed picture being carried. "Wait...woah woah woah hang on. Is that? Is that what I think it is? He pressed the eject tray button "Is that really what I thi-aaaaaahhhhh that feels good after all these years." His thumb in the centre of the disc box, he pops out the disc and places it in the tray "Jesus it feels good to be used again, I don't even care what he puts on. Just push the buttons my good man! Papa needs to output some sounds!" The eject button is pressed again and the CD Player starts reading and displaying the track titles. "Wait, what? Are you fuckin' serious? NO! no no no NO NO NO!" The volume is cranked up to full "NO! Why?! Just put some fuckin' Dolly Parton in me, some Johnny Cash. Just please...not that. Anything but that!" The man clicks the play button on track 1. Instantly starts feeling the groove and getting into the music. "What the fuck is wrong with you? This isn't good music! Not even back in the day was this good! Ahead of they're time? There is no fuckin' time for them! Just why? OH for fucks sake put me back in the dusty hole you call a loft space." The man now starts singing "A modern day warrior, Mean, mean stride, Today's Tom Sawyer, Mean, mean pride" "Fuckin' RUSH?! WHY WOULD YOU PLAY RUSH?! You know why Tom Sawyers mind wasn't for rent? huh? Because he was stupid for listening...to FUCKING...RUSH! You know what? That's it. I've had good times. Times when I played good music, but not anymore. That's it. Let's see you play your shit when I've popped a fu-" The CD Player goes dead. Confused the man starts banging the sides. In it's final moments, it did the world the best favour it could by keeping the RUSH CD in it's drawer. Never to see the light of day again.

## A Glimpse of Another Life

A year has passed since I said 'I do,' and honestly, it's still just as amazing. My mother always said I should marry my best friend, and I've done just that. A thousand times over, I'd choose her again.

This profound connection, this kind of love, was something I'd chased my whole life – truly *craved*. Loneliness had cast a shadow over my youth, a nagging sense of something missing that sent me searching, only to face rejection and the bitter sting of feeling unloved for who I truly was. Until her. My dearest Emily.

It took just four months from that first date for us to tie the knot. We'd been in each other's orbit before then, the usual 'friends of friends' encounters, exchanging pleasantries at parties. But one particular chat led to a date. She asked me out – the first time any woman I'd ever been with had explicitly shown interest. I'll confess, part of me said yes because I yearned to be pursued for once, to feel like I was the one in control. She wasn't unattractive, not at all, but she was different from the women I usually pursued. Honestly, she felt a little out of my league – curvy, with straight dark hair and captivating dark eyes. Seeing her in that new, romantic light... well, it solidified everything. We were married in a courthouse, thick as thieves, ready to face whatever life threw our way.

A year on, I'm still sometimes surprised she ever chose me, or that she still does (though she often says the same about me). And that's precisely how I knew the girl across the bar, whose eyes just met mine, was something special. She was staring, her fair complexion a striking contrast to Emily's dark skin. Blue eyes locked onto mine, her lips subtly drawn in a way that spoke of disappointment, though I couldn't explain how I knew. She twisted a dark curl with a lone finger, watching me with an anxious intensity.

A gentle squeeze of Emily's leg and a whispered assurance left my wife with our friends as I made my way towards the woman. "Elizabeth," I said, though I knew it likely wasn't her name. She didn't correct me. She simply cocked her head, her gaze sweeping over me. "James," she replied, and though it wasn't my name either, it felt right, so I stayed silent. "I didn't expect to, uh, this." She struggled for words, but I understood perfectly. A lifetime seemed to stretch between us in that moment, flashes of memories – of *us* – flickering through my mind as our eyes remained locked. She seemed uncertain, a visible upset in her demeanor, and I wasn't sure how to handle it, even as a deep instinct whispered that I once knew exactly what to do.

Frozen, I stood there, staring. Then, suddenly, Elizabeth extended her arm, tears welling in the corners of her eyes, a clear resolve not to break down in public. I took her hand, a gentle shake. The feeling of loss was overwhelming, a sharp ache that brought a prickle to my own eyes. I heard her draw a deep, shaky breath, and instinctively, I mirrored it – a leftover habit, I suppose. We locked eyes once more, then our hands met in a firm, solid handshake. It was a silent acknowledgement of what we once were, and what we simply wouldn't be in this lifetime. "Goodbye," she whispered, and then, as quickly as she had appeared, she was gone, darting out of the restaurant.

I turned back towards Emily, and with that turn, the vivid memories began to recede, like mist clearing from a landscape. As I approached our table, a quiet understanding settled within me: some connections aren't meant to span lifetimes. Sometimes, one lifetime has to be enough.

"Where were you?" Emily chimed, her voice banishing my lingering thoughts.

"Saying goodbye to an old friend," I replied.

**The Penn Museum's Mediterranean Collections**


The Penn Museum displays over 1,000 ancient artifacts from the Mediterranean region, including statues, jewelry, coins, pottery, and glassware. These items come from the museum's collection of more than 30,000 objects dating from 3000 BCE to 500 CE.


**Roman History and Empire**


According to legend, Rome was founded by Romulus in 753 BCE, and Romans believed their origins went back to the Trojan War. The Romans were skilled at taking the best ideas from the Greeks and Etruscans (their predecessors) and making them their own.


Between 400-200 BCE, Rome conquered most of Italy, including Etruscan territories. In the following centuries, they took over North Africa, Spain, Sicily, and Greek lands. The Roman Empire reached its largest size under Emperor Trajan (98-117 CE), bringing together people of many different backgrounds, languages, and cultures.


**Gallery Highlights**


The main attraction is a famous military sculpture that was once part of a victory arch built for Emperor Trajan in 102 CE near Naples. Interestingly, the back of this marble piece shows an earlier inscription honoring Emperor Domitian, which was partially chiseled away after he was assassinated in 96 CE.


**What You'll See**


The gallery showcases Roman achievements through:

- Marble statues of gods, goddesses, and ordinary Romans

- Bronze objects, including replicas from Pompeii and Herculaneum (donated by department store founder John Wanamaker in 1904)

- Tiny carved gems, jewelry, and coins showing incredible Roman craftsmanship

- Colorful Roman glassware demonstrating their sophisticated taste

- Jars recovered from underwater explorations by Jacques Cousteau in the 1950s, showing Roman sea trade

- Items showing daily life: portraits of women and children, toys, perfume bottles, dining dishes, wall paintings, mosaics, and even lead water pipes


The centerpiece is a 4-foot by 2-foot model of a typical Roman house like those found in Pompeii, complete with audio of the famous Roman architect Vitruvius explaining house designs.


**Online Resources**

- Learn about ancient Olympic Games and how they compare to today's games

- Explore Roman glassmaking during 600 years of Roman rule in the Mediterranean

Kevin Pietersen is willing to give up his Indian Premier League contract to play county cricket in an attempt to make an extraordinary return to the England side. In what would be the first step of a very long journey back to international cricket, the exiled star is ready to accept the challenge of new ECB chairman Colin Graves and try to fight his way back to the England side. Graves had said in a BBC interview last month that Pietersen would have to be playing regular county cricket if he was to have any chance of being considered by the a regime which is making sweeping changes at the ECB. Kevin Pietersen is willing to turn his back on the Indian Premier League in order to represent England . Pietersen, pictured in 2008, would have to repair his relationship with England boss Peter Moores . The governing body were then quick to emphasise that nothing had changed in their stance and they would only pick those who were not only performing well in county cricket but who were ‘a positive influence on the England side.’ Pietersen was picked up relatively cheaply by the Hyderabad Sunrisers of the IPL this year in a deal worth around £200,000 making it less expensive for him to throw in his lot with an as yet unnamed county and try to call Graves bluff. Yet a comeback, approaching 35, would still be remarkable. England’s need is greatest in one-day cricket, the format of the game Pietersen least enjoyed during his later years with England, and the wounds from his bitter sacking from England are still raw. He reopened many with his bitter book. If he came back it would be hard to see how he could work with senior players like Alastair Cook, Stuart Broad and Jimmy Anderson as well as coach Peter Moores, who he tried to force out in 2009, managing director Paul Downton, who sacked him last year, and national selector James Whitaker, who said in December that Pietersen would never play for England again. For Pietersen to come back now a lot of people would have to either depart or rebuild some seriously broken bridges. Pietersen's England future lies in the hands of new ECB chairman Colin Graves .

Throughout the history of calculating we’ve devised ways to add speed and accuracy while subtracting the drudgery.
Many solutions used body parts, notably fingers. A 19th century Chinese technique can count to 10 billion using just two hands! Other solutions were mechanical — both general-purpose tools for everyday calculations and specialized instruments for engineering, navigational, or other scientific and technical problems.
Renaissance Italian merchants used Pacioli’s finger-counting system to barter. His Summa also included the first published description of double-entry bookkeeping.View Artifact Detail
A Living Abacus
In the 1940s, Korean mathematician Sung Jin Pai applied the abacus principle to human hands. His chisanbop method replaced the abacus’s moveable beads with moving fingers.
Kids and grownups learned to raise and lower their fingers and thumbs off a tabletop, in various combinations, to represent any number from one to 99.

While the causes of Parkinson's disease are still unclear, many studies have suggested that other factors, not just inherited genes, are very important. Data suggests that regular, long-term contact with pesticides, even in low doses, might increase the risk. To look into this connection, researchers led by Alberto Ascherio, M.D., Dr.PH of the Harvard School of Public Health did a study that followed people over time among a large group of Americans.

They included men and women from a study called the Cancer Prevention Study II Nutrition Cohort who, beginning in 1982, filled out detailed surveys about their lifestyles that included questions about occupation and exposure to pesticides and other substances that could be harmful. The researchers studied 143,325 people who also filled out a later survey in 2001, answered a question about whether they had ever had Parkinson's disease, and had no symptoms of the disease when the study began.

The researchers asked for the medical records of everyone in the study who said they were diagnosed with Parkinson's disease after 1992 – that is 10 or more years after the reported exposure to pesticides -- to confirm the diagnosis themselves. The study included 413 cases, and they used statistics to figure out the connection between pesticide exposure and Parkinson's disease.

The risk of Parkinson's disease was 70 percent higher for people exposed to pesticides. Importantly, people exposed through their jobs (like farmers) had a similar increased risk as those not exposed at work, which suggests that using pesticides at home or in the garden is also harmful. Also important, contact with other harmful substances in the environment was not related to risk of Parkinson's disease.

The study had a drawback: it didn't have detailed information about how long, how often, or how much pesticide exposure occurred. However, because it followed people over time, it offers much stronger proof of a link between exposure to pesticides and Parkinson's disease than previous studies. Also, the results match findings from animal studies that show pesticide ingredients can damage brain cells that produce dopamine and cause movement problems.

"The findings support the idea that being exposed to pesticides increases the risk of Parkinson's disease," the authors conclude. Since this study didn't have information about exact pesticides used, they suggest, "future studies should try to find out which specific chemicals are linked to the risk."
Article: "Pesticide Exposure and Risk of Parkinson's Disease." Ascherio, Alberto; Chen, Honglei; Weisskopf, Marc; O'Reilly, Eilis; McCullough, Marjorie; Calle, Eugenia; Schwartzschild, Michael; Thun, Michael. Annals of Neurology; July 2006; (DOI: 10.1002/ana.20904)
Last reviewed: By John M. Grohol, Psy.D. on 21 Feb 2009
Published on PsychCentral.com. All rights reserved.

A New Era for Doctor Who: Style Over Substance?

With this adventure, Doctor Who launched into a bold new era—complete with fresh music, a new direction, and a noticeable shift in style. The buzz around these changes was palpable, and there’s no denying the episode brought a sense of energy and renewal to the series.

But if I'm being honest, the most memorable thing about this episode really is that sense of “new beginnings.” Beyond the impressive sets, stylish camerawork, and overall polish, the story itself leaves a lot to be desired. The Doctor and companions arrive at a futuristic leisure complex, the Doctor accidentally ages 500 years, and everyone must be rescued from a race of rather uninspiring lizard-people. 

While the episode is visually appealing and crackles with the optimism of a new start, it ultimately struggles to deliver an engaging narrative. The heavy focus on atmosphere and aesthetics seems to overshadow the actual storytelling, making the plot feel secondary—if not, at times, completely forgotten.

Despite its energetic vibe, this adventure is, for me, mostly a stylish but somewhat empty foray into the new era of Doctor Who. Here’s hoping future episodes match their fresh look with stories that truly captivate.

Far more than just another date on the calendar, Winter Solstice is a power portal for blessing for the planet and enriching our inner and outer lives. Of all ancient celebrations, it is one of the most universal, its remnants resonating in the contemporary emphasis on the December holidays. Pagan practices from Asia to America shared surprisingly synchronistic metaphors for this time of year, which heralded the special force of the sun, symbolically born again as a sacred child or being after the longest night, on or around December 21. Mithras, Horus, Helios and Dionisius, all millenary sun-sons, were also born on December 25, the day the sun begins its return.
Some researchers assume that festivities like these were the rather simple workings of societies dependent on the whims of nature for their sustenance, somehow trying to convince the fire-king to return and bless them with food and warmth. Closer scrutiny reveals that diverse civilizations understood themselves and nature as interconnected, vehicles for a Higher life force, and saw these cyclical changes as opportunities to quicken Its presence.
In his monumental work, The Secret Teachings of the Ages, Manly P. Hall reveals a forgotten element of native solar wisdom:
In the majority of cases, the religions of antiquity agree that the material visible sun was a reflector rather than a source of power…This sun reflected the light of the invisible spiritual sun, which was the true source of life, light and truth.
The Mexica oral tradition provides one of the richest examples, most detailed examples of the subtle relationship with the sun. Winter Solstice marks the birth of the Precious Child, Huitzilopochtli, also known as The Warrior of the South, The Lord Who Gives Us Will Power in Spite of Adversity. (His sister is the Moon.)
The mystical meaning of warrior is: one who knows how to fight in life and to overcome the lower self. The will that achieves is the divine and human strength generated by the Higher Source, Ometeotl, and expressed through the Sun Lord. As the latter begins his return northward, he rises as the solar Huitzilin (hummingbird), a being whose characteristic achieving will is evidenced in its ability to sustain itself in the air, incessantly beating tiny wings. The ancients welcomed him: You have come here, Precious Child, Sun-ray that ascends. Each person would review the passing cycle to identify how they could have exerted greater will-power. Then, with a beautiful ceremony and prayers for absorbing this characteristically solar vigor, they would establish goals of virtue for the coming year.
At Christmas, Judeo-Christian practices honor the child Jesus, the divine son (who in esoteric traditions is an expression of the spiritual sun). A few days later, we establish our New Year’s goals, reflecting the collective intuition that somehow this period may renew our discipline and “achieving will power.” In Western astrology the Sun is associated with will and purpose, and at solstice it moves into Capricorn, sign of goals, direction and discipline.
Throughout the Far East, this same portal is an important time of rest and regeneration. The I Ching associates Winter Solstice with Hexagram 24, The Return, a time when “transformation from the old becomes entirely easy.”
An anonymous author has said that the most common desire among people of all conditions is the desire for a new beginning. Each year’s end brings a special chance, as we still the rush of daily duty during this unique crossroads of earth and sky. The archetypal forces of beginnings, of achieving will, of light and love, of all that we hold most dear, conjoin and can be accessed as we clarify our intention and open ourselves to the assistance from the divine.
Note: For each solstice portal during the last nine years, the author has been sharing different ceremonies and prayers that integrate ancestral and contemporary metaphysical knowledge with Daykeeper readers. This year’s New Year’s Shift Ceremony to connect to the Highest and the Best of 2010, is one of the features available to Premium Subscribers. More information…
Cleary, Thomas, translator, The Taoist I Ching (Shambala, Boston, 1986).
Hall, Manly P., The Secret Teachings of the Ages (Tarcher/Penguin NY 2003).
Matthews, John and Gaitlín, The Winter Solstice (Quest Books, Wheaton, Illinois, 1998).
Pennick, Nigel, The Pagan Book of Days (Destiny Books, Rochester, Vermont, 1992).
Van Renterghem, Tony, When Santa Was a Shaman (Llewellyn, 1995, St. Paul).
Wilhelm, Richard, traductor y comentarista, I Ching, El Libro de las Mutaciones (Hermes, México 1983).

Okay, so Verheyen, who's a total guitar ace (he plays all sorts of rock and jazz, you know?), totally nails his rock-oriented blues stuff on this album. And get this, he's not just a great guitarist – he's a fantastic blues singer and songwriter too. If you're into Robben Ford, you're gonna dig his playing, big time. I actually think he's even more soulful as a singer than Ford, which is saying something! He covers a lot of ground, from classic Muddy Waters vibes to Cream-style rock, and even some funky rock/blues. But then, wait for it – at the very end, he totally changes gears! He throws in this absolutely stunning acoustic folk tune, 'Silence is Golden,' that's almost like something Jackson Browne would do. And he wraps it up with some really beautiful, uptempo country electric guitar that just gets you. Seriously, this album is a real winner. I highly, highly recommend it! Oh, a quick note on the stars: I gave it four because it's genuinely excellent – I usually save five stars for those undisputed classics, you know, like 'Graceland' and stuff like that.

The Terriers start the season with a trip to Crystal Palace on Saturday.

"We are more than happy for people to write us off and more than happy to be the underdogs, that's absolutely fine. But as long as we go about our business in the right way, which I'm sure we will, then I see no reason why we can't give it a good go.

Mr. Gentiloni, 62, assumes power after Matteo Renzi resigned following his referendum defeat eight days ago.
Pier Carlo Padoan remains economy minister, and Angelino Alfano becomes foreign minister.
As a loyalist from Mr. Renzi's Democratic Party (PD), Mr. Gentiloni will largely pursue the former administration's programme.
President Sergio Mattarella swore in the new government on Monday evening.
Mr. Gentiloni confronts a banking crisis and growing popular support for anti-establishment and eurosceptic parties.
Mr. Renzi resigned after Italians rejected his proposed constitutional reforms.
Opposition parties have rejected joining a national unity government. The populist Five Star Movement declares it will boycott this week's parliamentary approval vote, asserting its lack of legitimacy.

I was waiting for my flight, absorbed by a book without really reading it. I had read the same sentence a few times without comprehension, something I never do. What could distract me so? The bustle of a busy airport surrounded me. It should offer a thousand distractions but, in reality, all the voices blended together to become white noise, a jumble of indistinct sounds that together created a monotone. Except one. One voice stood out and I realized that it had stood out for a while. That's what had held my attention, without holding my awareness. It was a woman's voice. I'd like to say that it was melodious, but it really wasn't. As a voice it was really quite average. It had a tone though, a tone that spoke of calm and reasonableness. A tone that said that even when it wasn't alright, it would be alright. I looked around, casually. I couldn't find her in the crowd and she had fallen silent. I put my book down and stood up as if to stretch, looking around a little more intensely. Nothing. No voice, no indication of who it could have come from. Suddenly, it seemed the most important thing in the world to see who the voice belonged to. Then I heard it again, and it was moving away. I turned in the direction I thought it was coming from and I saw her! A strong, husky girl, talking to the attendant as she handed over her boarding pass. The attendant said something and she laughed. The laugh, it *was* melodious. As she left the desk and headed for the tunnel she turned my way. As I gazed at her, she looked me straight in the eye and she stopped, just inside the entrance and we looked at each other for a full moment. Then she started and looked down the tunnel. She said something to someone there, looked back at me one more time, then disappeared through the doorway. That voice....

We consume excessive amounts and exercise insufficiently, states Dr. James Hill, one of the co-authors of a new "diet book". Along with his colleagues from the University of Colorado, Drs. John C. Peters and Bonnie T. Jortberg, he authored a new weight-loss guide called "The Step Diet".

The book was published not long ago, in 2004. However, the weight-loss program it advocates is quite established. It draws from over 25 years of scientific research conducted at the University of Colorado's Health Science Center. The book's authors are also co-founders of the well-known national health initiative "America on the Move".

The central principle of the Step diet is to boost physical activity while reducing overall food consumption. Those who think physical activity means a gym filled with exercise equipment and daily strenuous workouts need not be concerned. According to the Step Diet, all that's required is to stand up and walk your way to weight loss. Rather than counting calories, dieters should count steps.

The scientific foundation of this approach is the energy balance concept - the calories we consume must not exceed the calories we burn through activity. Therefore, if we consume too many calories and don't move sufficiently, we gain weight. The fundamental truth of the Step Diet is that calories that come in should step out.

The Step Diet authors don't promise their followers quick results and dramatic weight loss. Their goal is sustainable weight management. Meanwhile, their university research indicates that dieters can lose 1 to 2 pounds per week by following the diet's core principles.

The advantageous aspect of the Step Diet is its straightforwardness. Dieters face no food restrictions and can eat whatever they enjoy. Individuals don't need to struggle with meal planning and calorie counting. The main requirement is to reduce food intake to 75% of typical portions.

Conversely, some experts argue that since the book focuses on exercise and ultimately reaching 10,000 steps daily, some people might make poor food choices since there are no specific guidelines about what should be consumed.

The Step Diet represents much more than a simple weight-loss concept. It encourages changing negative and unhealthy habits while gaining positive energy from walking and developing a new healthy lifestyle. You don't need to do much to become slim and attractive - just step toward your health. And DietSpace.com will help you reach your goal.

This has been a great router for me for upwards of 8 months now. Before I even installed it, I put the open source dd-wrt firmware on it, and it has been truly great. I literally haven't had to touch it in the 8-9 months I've owned it, which is more than I can say for the highly regarded WRT-54G that it replaced.

Speeds are great for N, although range is a bit disappointing—but no worse than the Linksys. I run daily with a 33% signal and still have good speeds, so I'm happy. You can get one of these dirt cheap now, and if you're looking to get into 802.11N on the cheap, I wouldn't hesitate to recommend this bad boy. Just keep in mind, replacing the firmware is not optional; do it right away for the best performance.

Holocaust Remembrance Day (Yom Hashoah) allows us to memorialize the greatest tragedy experienced by the Jewish people in modern times. It reminds us that, wrapped up in that unfathomable horror, were millions of individual lives—real people with real stories. It is up to us to learn and share these stories on their behalf. Together, we say we must never forget. Rituals for Yom Hashoah vary. Some communities read names of people killed in the Holocaust, some observe moments of silence. New liturgy is still being developed, and it is sadly relevant, since genocide continues to plague our world.

**Canine Nasal Aspergillosis**

---

**Overview of Fungal Nasal Disease**

Fungal infections are a frequent cause of nasal disease in dogs and cats. Canine nasal aspergillosis is a relatively common inflammatory disease of the nasal passages. Aspergillus species are filamentous fungi that are ubiquitous under appropriate environmental conditions and not typically infectious. However, some species, namely Aspergillus fumigatus, Aspergillus niger, Aspergillus nidulans, and Aspergillus flavus can invade nasal cavities of animals with impaired immune system function.

The infection is associated with production of bacterial toxins that cause destruction and erosion of tissues. Colonization and invasion of the nasal mucosa by A. fumigatus results in destruction and necrosis of the nasal turbinates, often accompanied by frontal sinus osteomyelitis. The cribriform plate, palatine bones, and orbits are sometimes involved. Pain, loss of appetite, sneezing, and copious mucoid to hemorrhagic nasal discharge and crusting are common clinical signs. Life-threatening nose bleeding can occur due to erosion of the nasal blood vessels. In chronic cases, depigmentation and ulceration of the nares and masticatory muscle atrophy result. Central nervous system involvement (encephalitis, meningitis) precipitating seizures can result after erosion of the cribriform plate. Nasal infection is most likely to occur in dogs that are 3 years old or younger.

---

**Aspergillus Species and Environmental Sources**

The most common species isolated from canine nasal cavities is Aspergillus fumigatus, a fungal pathogen of humans and animals that is associated with a wide spectrum of diseases, ranging from benign colonization of the lung and allergy to life-threatening diseases such as invasive pulmonary aspergillosis and allergic broncho-pulmonary aspergillosis. A. fumigatus grows abundantly in rotten vegetation, wood chips, compost, sewage, moldy hay, and soil. The fungus produces many small spores which can be inhaled. Some Aspergillus species found in moldy feed produce aflatoxins, poisonous compounds that can cause serious injury to the liver if ingested.

Although the condition is not life-threatening, it often results in severe clinical signs, such as extensive foul-smelling nasal discharge, chronic sneezing, frequent pawing and rubbing at the face, and nosebleeds.

---

**Clinical Signs and Diagnosis**

Profuse pus-filled discharge that is not responsive to antimicrobial medications and ulcerations of the external nares are seen in dogs with established infection. Diagnosis is difficult to make. Radiographs (x-rays) of the head, rhinoscopy, serologic tests, and laboratory culture of the organism from nasal swabs or flushes may all be necessary to reach a firm diagnosis. A CT scan of the nasal passages is an important component of the evaluation. Rhinoscopy is an important part of both the diagnosis and the therapy for nasal aspergillosis because it is the only technique that allows direct visualization of fungal colonies. Rhinoscopic findings may reveal erosions and fungal plaques (fluffy patches of fungal growth). However, the absence of rhinoscopic findings does not rule out fungal infection.

---

**Treatment and Prognosis**

Today, medical management of aspergillosis with clotrimazole infusion into the nasal cavity is the preferred method of treatment. Surgical method is usually indicated only if all other methods have failed. The prognosis for recovery from nasal aspergillosis is about 90% in patients treated with clotrimazole infusion. About 15-20% of patients require second treatment. If left untreated, the infection will progress and can, in advanced cases, infect the brain. The use of topical clotrimazole through noninvasive techniques has increased the success of treatment and decreased the morbidity and duration of hospitalization. There is no vaccine currently available to prevent aspergillosis in companion animals.

---

**Disseminated Aspergillosis**

Disseminated aspergillosis is most commonly diagnosed in terminally ill dogs and cats and typically involves multiple organ systems with no history of nasal or pulmonary involvement. Aspergillus terreus, A. deflectus, A. flavipes, and rarely A. fumigatus have been reported in cases where the species has been identified. The infection is believed to occur after inhalation of small spores and spread via the blood. The inhaled pathogens produce highly infective aleuriospores that invade blood vessels.

In dogs, disseminated aspergillosis has been most commonly reported in the German Shepherd Dog between 2 and 8 years of age. Genetic predisposition and impaired immunity (defective IgA production or regulation) have been identified in some affected German Shepherd Dogs.

Aspergillus terreus infection can become disseminated throughout the body, resulting in systemic, often life-threatening, illness in dogs and, very rarely, cats. Successful treatment of the disseminated form of the disease is not common. Aggressive treatment with systemic antifungals is indicated and the most successful regimen involves itraconazole. Itraconazole is an imidazole, similar to ketoconazole, fluconazole, and thiabendazole, with better activity against the fungus.

---

**Glossary**

- **Nasal aspergillosis**: Inflammatory disease of the nasal passages  
- **Nasal depigmentation**: Occurs in dogs with a normal, darkly pigmented noses at birth which later fades to brown or a pale gray-white  
- **Rhinoscopy**: Examination of the nose  
- **Disseminated**: Distributed over a considerable area

By Damien Gayle. Brazil’s Amazon rainforest is being lost at the slowest rate since tracking began, officials revealed yesterday. According to satellite data, 1,798 square miles of the Amazon were cleared or burned between August 2011 and July 2012, Environment Minister Izabella Teixeira reported. This figure marks a 27 percent decrease from the 2,478 square miles destroyed in the previous year.

Scroll down for video. Smoke rises as trees are set alight in the Amazon rainforest: Brazil announced today that deforestation in the Amazon has reached its lowest levels since systematic record-keeping started. Brazil’s National Institute for Space Research said this is the smallest rate of forest loss since it first began collecting data in 1988. The Amazon accounts for more than half of the world’s remaining rainforests and is the largest, most biodiverse stretch of tropical forest on Earth. Around 63 percent of the Amazon’s 2.4 million square miles lie within Brazilian territory.

According to the space institute’s recent numbers, Brazil is approaching its goal of cutting deforestation by 80 percent by 2020, compared with 1990 levels. As of July 2012, deforestation rates have been reduced by 76.26 percent. George Pinto, a director at Ibama, Brazil’s environmental protection agency, credited increased enforcement of environmental regulations and improved monitoring technology for the continued decline in deforestation.

Untouched jungle in Mato Grosso State—one of the areas historically most affected—shows the Amazon’s role as both a global biodiversity haven and a vital forest resource. The Amazon’s destruction accelerated in the 1960s, driven by settlers clearing the land by fire for agricultural use. However, these soils proved only briefly fertile, resulting in ongoing forest clearance and significant ecological damage.

Yet, long before large-scale agriculture, human activity shaped the Amazon basin. Archaeological findings indicate the region’s first inhabitants arrived at least 11,200 years ago, and by 1500 AD, as many as 5 million people could have lived there. The first European to navigate the Amazon, Francisco de Orellana, documented thriving, complex societies along its banks during the 1540s. Additional evidence suggests that rather than being an untouched wilderness, much of the forest has been altered by people for over 11,000 years, notably through forest management techniques like forest gardening. Large expanses of terra preta (“black earth”)—fertile soil created through human intervention—demonstrate that portions of the forest landscape are the result of centuries of indigenous stewardship.

It is believed that many of the region’s native populations were decimated by diseases, notably smallpox, brought by European explorers. According to Mr. Pinto, 2,000 square meters of illegally harvested timber were confiscated by federal agents in the last year. The seized wood is sold at auction, with proceeds funneled into conservation programs.

Ms. Teixeira announced the coming implementation of satellite-based systems to identify unlawful logging and slash-and-burn operations, issuing fines where violations are detected. Adalberto Verissimo, a senior researcher at Imazon, an environmental monitoring group, commented: “In recent years, Brazil has made significant progress in controlling deforestation, and the latest data confirms these gains. The reduction in forest loss is a very encouraging sign of a sustained downward trend. These numbers refute claims that forest destruction is needed for economic development,” he said in a phone call from Belem, a city in the Amazon. “Deforestation has consistently decreased over the past four years while the economy has continued to expand. But our efforts are ongoing—many challenges remain.” Marcio Astrini, Amazon coordinator for Greenpeace, said the latest statistics prove that lowering deforestation is entirely achievable. However, he stressed that, “The numbers remain too high for a nation that has no need to clear even a single hectare to develop.”

Burglars used sledgehammers to smash their way into Drumragh Integrated College on Sunday night.
The principal, Nigel Frith, said money was taken and those responsible had cut telephone wires and broken alarms.
Mr Frith said the school would reopen on Tuesday.
He said the main damage had been caused to doors and furniture.
"They have taken surprisingly little," he said.
"The main problem for us is going to be getting the repairs to the building and getting everything up and running again.
"Our first concern this morning was children arriving at school.
"We had an operation that went quite well, to send children home immediately who were being dropped off by parents."
Mr Frith said the phone lines were not working when staff and pupils arrived at the school.
"We were relying on children to use their own mobile phones to contact their parents and liaise with us".
He said the school would be in contact with parents if it was not possible to open on Tuesday.
Police have appealed for information about the incident and said "considerable damage" had been caused to the premises.

The Public Spaces Protection Order (PSPO) is now in effect across designated areas of Bootle and Litherland.
This new power, implemented by Sefton Council, directly addresses persistent high levels of anti-social behaviour in the area.
Groups of two or more engaging in anti-social behaviour will be dispersed.
The order explicitly covers key areas, including sections of the Leeds & Liverpool canal and Merseyrail's Northern line.
Under the PSPO:
Councillor Trish Hardy, cabinet member for communities and housing, stated: "The majority of people who live here are good, law abiding citizens, but unfortunately we have been struggling with some high levels of anti-social behaviour.
"What we are attempting to do is help police tackle people causing problems and the community also gets reassurance that there is an active police presence happening."
She is confident that the "strong and vibrant neighbourhoods" in Bootle and Litherland will view the PSPO's introduction as a positive step.
The council will collaborate closely with Merseyside Police to rigorously implement and enforce the order.
Police Commissioner Jane Kennedy commended Sefton's decision to take a "firm stance" by introducing this measure.
Sefton Council has already established a dedicated anti-social behaviour officer.

**A Disgusting Display of Poor Hygiene - Never Going Back**


On a sunny Saturday afternoon, I excitedly took my 1-year-old son to this ice cream shop for what I thought would be a pleasant treat. The small storefront was moderately busy, with the familiar sounds of freezers humming and the sweet aroma of waffle cones filling the air. As I stood near the colorful display case, studying the menu board above while my toddler pointed eagerly at the frozen treats behind the glass, the atmosphere quickly turned sour.


A clearly frustrated customer burst through the front door, her voice rising above the gentle background music as she complained that her drink didn't have enough granola. The two female employees behind the counter, wearing their standard uniforms, calmly explained that additional granola would incur an extra charge. The customer's frustration escalated as she insisted she shouldn't have to pay extra for something that was supposed to be included with her original order.


What happened next will forever be burned into my memory. Right there in the open workspace, clearly visible through the transparent plastic drapes that separate the preparation area from the customer counter, I watched in absolute horror as one of the employees casually stuck her hand down the front AND back of her pants. Without missing a beat, she then brought her hand up to her nose and sniffed it before immediately reaching for the granola container to fulfill the customer's request.


The bustling shop suddenly felt contaminated. My parental instincts kicked in as I grabbed my son and headed straight for the exit, my stomach churning with disgust. I couldn't in good conscience let that unsuspecting customer consume what had just been prepared, so I stopped her outside the shop and explained what I had witnessed. To her credit, she marched right back inside and returned the contaminated order.


This level of unsanitary behavior is absolutely unacceptable, especially in a food service establishment. Management needs to take immediate action - these employees should be terminated and the entire staff retrained on basic hygiene protocols. I will never return to this location.

Two parachutes out of three ain’t bad for NASA’s Orion spacecraft  
By David Szondy  
February 15, 2013  

Under the vast, cloudless skies of southern Arizona, a test version of NASA’s Orion space capsule made a dramatic parachute drop above the arid expanse near Yuma on Tuesday, with only two of its three vast white-and-orange parachutes unfurling against the deep blue. The sun-bleached desert floor stretched below, peppered with sagebrush and bordered by distant mountain ridges shimmering in the dry heat. From the back bay of a rumbling Hercules transport aircraft, flying at an altitude of 25,000 feet (7,620 m), the capsule tumbled into open air. It wasn't an accident, but a calculated move: NASA engineers deliberately “nobbled” one of the chutes to simulate a real-world failure, testing whether the capsule could still survive Earth’s gravity and searing reentry, and descend safely to the hardpan below with only two canopies deployed.  

According to NASA, this seventh parachute test is a crucial milestone—the Orion capsule is designed not for low-Earth orbit, but for humanity’s next steps deeper into space, including missions to the Moon and beyond. This means that, on return to Earth, the 21,000-pound (9,525-kg) spacecraft will come screaming through the atmosphere at speeds over 20,000 mph (32,000 km/h), its heat shield slicing the rarefied air in a spectacle of fire and plasma, a velocity unmatched by any manned craft since the Apollo era.  

The test also evokes history and the raw unpredictability of spaceflight—a similar parachute malfunction occurred on August 7, 1971, when Apollo 15’s command module splashed down under only two parachutes in the churning Pacific Ocean. That incident, long etched in NASA’s institutional memory, underscores that parachute failure is a real possibility, not a hypothetical scenario.  

The Yuma Proving Ground, with its wide-open, unobstructed desert vistas and predictable weather, serves as an ideal backdrop for such high-stakes trials. An eighth drop test is planned for May, as engineers continue to fine-tune Orion’s critical landing systems beneath the relentless Arizona sun. Meanwhile, Orion’s first unmanned test flight looms on the horizon—scheduled for 2014, the capsule will travel 3,600 miles (5,800 km) out from Earth and reenter at blistering speed, challenging both technology and the nerves of those who design and fly her.  

The video below captures the stark beauty and tense excitement of the Arizona drop: the Orion spacecraft tumbling into open sky at the start of the test, followed by billowing parachutes blossoming far above the windswept desert.

Gloucester officials are using the VIMS Tidewatch program (referenced in a Daily Press story here: http://bit.ly/TNGfd1) as one of the tools to ensure public safety during storm events.
The Tidewatch system forecasts the potential for coastal flooding, particularly during storms and the accompanying tidal surges. It helps residents and public safety officials determine when its time to head for high ground.
Tidewatch was referenced during Hurricane Sandy, especially during the three peak high tide and flooding periods in Gloucester, said Creig Moore, the county's Emergency Services Coordinator.
Moore said in an email that it should be noted that the Gloucester Department of Emergency Management uses many tools to ensure officials can make the best decisions regarding public safety and protective actions for county residents.
County officials obtain information from sources to include: National Weather Service -- Wakefield; Virginia Department of Emergency Management briefings; and when warranted the National Hurricane Center, Moore said.
"Our number one priority is the protection of the public and Gloucester Department of Emergency Management will use any and all available resources available to perform that mission," Moore said.
Here's a link to more information about Tidewatch: http://www.vims.edu/bayinfo/tidewatch/index.php

Hey, let's talk about something we've all been there with - that moment when you're scrolling through Facebook and thinking, "Why am I friends with half these people?" You know what I mean - between the TMI posts, the political rants, and those photos that make you go "yikes," it might be time for a little spring cleaning of your friend list!

Dr. Andrea Bonior, who wrote "The Friendship Fix," has some great advice: just ask yourself why you're really friends with these people online. Are you genuinely interested in staying connected, or is it just because they sent a request?

Let me walk you through some common scenarios we all face:

**Your kid's teacher**
I get it - you chatted at the school carnival and she seems really cool. But here's the thing: mixing your social media life with your child's education can get messy fast. What if there's a problem at school later? Your teacher might feel weird about being objective, or you might worry she's reading too much into your posts. Probably best to keep that relationship in the classroom.

**The babysitter**
She's awesome with your kids and lives right down the street - seems like a natural friendship, right? Well, it's totally fine to check out someone's social media before you hire them (lots of employers do this), but once she's working for you, you might want to pass on that friend request. Trust me, you don't want to see a post that makes you question your childcare choice!

**Other parents from school**
This one's a maybe! It can be great for staying connected and keeping up with what's happening with your kids' friends. Just be picky about it - stick to the moms you actually click with in real life. You don't need to be friends with every single parent, and honestly, your posts might not be everyone's cup of tea anyway.

**Your pediatrician**
Okay, I know you see her all the time between checkups, sick visits, and those endless ear infections. But think about it - you're already sharing plenty during those appointments! Facebook really isn't the place for medical stuff, and unless you're genuinely hanging out socially, it's probably better to keep that relationship professional.

**An ex-boyfriend**
Oh boy, this is a big one! Sure, he was fun back in high school, and maybe you're curious about what he's up to now. But if either of you is married or in a serious relationship, this could spell trouble. Be really honest with yourself about why you want to reconnect - sometimes we have hidden motives we don't want to admit.

The good news is that Facebook has been updating their privacy tools to make things easier:
- There's a new shortcut to privacy settings
- You can ask people to remove photos of you that you didn't approve
- You'll get alerts if something you tried to hide might still show up in feeds
- Apps have to ask permission twice before accessing your info

The bottom line? Your Facebook feed should make you happy, not stressed. Don't be afraid to be selective about who gets a window into your life!

For much of Saturday, ING's Romanian customers could not access their money.
The bank said the discharge of its gas-based fire suppression system had caused "unexpected" damage to its computer servers.
A report by Motherboard suggests that the equipment was too noisy.
A spokeswoman for ING was unable to confirm this detail.
But she did acknowledge the problem had lasted from 13:00 to 23:00 local time and the bank had been unable to explain the situation to customers as its own communications system had been affected.
"Due to the magnitude and complexity of breakdown, the time required to restore the activity through the back-up system was unfortunately longer than during the tests we perform regularly," said Iosefina Frolu.
"Currently, we are finalising the diagnostic analysis of this exceptional event."
The fire extinguisher system used at the Bucharest site worked by expelling a mix - known as inergen - of nitrogen, argon and carbon dioxide.
These gases are normally not harmful to computer equipment.
But, according to Motherboard, the high-pressure release created a sound that topped 130 decibels.
A study last year by the German engineering group Siemens warned the nozzles used by some fire suppression systems could cause "fatal" damage to hard disks because of sound wave vibrations they sent through the air.
"The hypothesis is that sound causes vibrations in the HDD case that are ultimately transmitted to the disk spindle and head assembly, causing misalignment of the read/write heads to the data tracks," it said.
"Because there are many different designs of storage system chassis and cabinets on the market, the issue of vibration caused by noise as well as structure-borne vibration has not yet been fully investigated."
Siemens's research indicated that different server models were vulnerable to different noise frequencies, and if there was a match:
Although ING said the accident was "abnormal and unprecedented" in its experience, there have been other suspected incidents.
In 2013, the French media reported that accountancy software used by the country's government had become temporarily unavailable after a fire protection system had been accidentally triggered at a data centre, causing a loud noise.
The following year, inergen extinguishers were used to put out a fire at a facility in Sydney, Australia.
When several of the disks failed, some experts blamed noise vibrations caused by the discharge.

Troops have entered one building in the compound and plan to advance cautiously due to fears of explosives. IS militants are believed to have fled to the northeast of Ramadi. The government has been trying to retake the city for weeks after its fall to IS in May, which was seen as an embarrassing defeat for the army. Troops have made slow progress, clearing booby-trapped streets and seizing several districts as they approach the city center. On Saturday, they were within a few hundred yards of the former provincial headquarters. After sniper fire stopped and aerial surveillance showed no activity, Iraqi soldiers entered the city's former department of health. The military believes IS militants have moved northeast, though fighting continues southwest of the compound. The Ramadi operation began in early November but has been slow, as the government chose not to use Shia-dominated paramilitary forces to avoid sectarian tensions. Hundreds of families remain trapped on the frontline.

It is believed the route will go from the King Power Stadium to the city centre before ending at a park.
The city council has refused to officially confirm any plans but previously said Town Hall Square was not big enough.
Leicester City were confirmed Premier League champions on Monday night.
Updates on this story and more from Leicestershire
In 2014, the Foxes held an open top bus parade to celebrate promotion to the top flight after a 10-year absence.
Thousands of people lined the route from High Street, past the Clock Tower before about 6,000 fans greeted the buses in Town Hall Square.
City Mayor Sir Peter Soulsby has previously told the Leicester Mercury that too many people would want to celebrate to be able to hold anything in Town Hall Square again.
