I have lived a long life and have ruled the underworld of Mumbai for years, from the shadows of its sprawling slums to the glinting towers I once controlled. They still call me BhaiSahab, or Mumbai's God Father, though the title feels heavy as dust on my tongue now. Long have I fought this bloody war with other mafias, with the police and with the politicians, amidst the city's relentless hum and the stench of its ambition, and have continued to win. And now that my days are numbered, confined to this plush but suffocating room, I sometimes wonder if it was all worth it. As I grow old, the stale air in this room seems to thicken with the shadows of all those whom I tortured, mutilated and killed. The monsoon rain, a relentless drumming outside the reinforced window, looks like a bloodbath, washing away not just the city's grime but perhaps even my own lingering humanity. My children, shielded from the brutal sun of my world, have failed. My trusted men, their loyalty as fleeting as Mumbai's morning mist, have all betrayed me. My wife is long gone, leaving me alone in this echoing silence. Why did I have to run after power? My empire, once a formidable structure of concrete and fear, is falling apart, being pulled by the demons of my past, like old buildings collapsing in the city's ceaseless sprawl. My power, a flickering lamp in a darkening room, is slowly fading away. When I came here from my village in the town of Sultanpur, the land of Sultan, I was 9 years old. The quiet fields and slow pace of home were replaced by the cacophony and crushing crowds of Mumbai. I was terrified of this city, a sprawling beast of concrete and ambition. I slept on footpaths, the rough pavement chilling my bones, ate from overflowing garbage bins where the smell of rot mingled with spices, and worked in a small, greasy tea shop near the incessant roar of the railway station. The air there always thick with steam, dust, and the clatter of chai glasses. I worked for 5 years, invisible amidst the rush, before I saw the first glimpse of raw power. He came in a big, gleaming Ambassador car, its chrome catching the harsh afternoon sun, and shot the shop owner 6 times in the head, right there amidst the spilled chai and startled cries, all in front of a few idle policemen who merely looked away. No one was questioned, no one was caught, and no one was punished. I saw it all, the city's indifference mirroring my own hardening resolve, but I waited. Two years later, under the cloak of a moonless Mumbai night, I killed him. I cut his head and hung it in his sprawling garden, a chilling trophy amidst the bougainvillea. I killed all who opposed me, all who dared to challenge my ascent. I killed all who betrayed me, their faces etched with terror under the dim streetlights. I could see fear in their eyes, a cold, satisfying realization. For the first time in my life, I was powerful. I was the king, ruling from the bustling docks to the illicit back alleys. And now forty five years later, here I am. Powerless. Lying in this bed, its silk sheets feeling like a shroud, waiting for my death amidst the distant, fading sounds of the city, and wondering what is true power. No one will remember me, not in the way legends are remembered. No one will praise me, not with the genuine warmth of admiration. No one will fear me, not when my breath is short and my hand trembles. It is all an illusion, a mirage shimmering in the heat of Mumbai. There is no true power, only a temporary dominion over fear. Power goes away, like the tide receding from the shores, and takes away all the hopes, the desires and the will to live. It is all a big burden, a heavy weight that now presses down on me in this quiet, solitary room, while the restless city outside continues its endless, indifferent dance.

"How much sugar do you take in your tea?" Black laughed at the question "You put sugar in your tea? What kind of a savage are you?" "The kind that likes his tea sweetened." "I'll take mine with no sugar. Please." "Ok, Mr.Health." "It's not about, health. I just don't like sweet stuff. Especially not my tea, I like my tea bitter." "Maybe that's why you're bitter all the time." "Hilarious."Black replied with a sarcastic tone."You gave me enough reasons to be bitter for seven lifetimes." Silence spread through the room, only to be broken by the sound of hot tea filling up a teacup. "So...are we going to pretend we're having a casual brunch?" asked White, while holding the tray of fresh hot tea. "I don't know about you, but I'm okay with that." "Seriously...there's no need for both of us to die. We can come to some understanding, can we?" he said, while softly placing a teacup next to Black. "Well, if you leave, I'm gonna use this trigger here, and blow us both up. If I decide to leave, you're gonna use your trigger, and blow us both up." "Yes. And isn't that just a waste?" "A waste indeed." Said, Black, sipping from his cup. "So how are we gonna decide who lives and who dies?" "Coin toss?" "Don't be ridiculous."said White dismissively."How's the your tea?" "Good. Bitter. Just the way I like it." "Yeah, you and your bitter tea. Mine is good and sweet, in case you were wondering." "I wasn't. But thanks for the uninvited info." Silence again, only the sound of the two sipping their tea creeped into the room. "I still haven't forgiven you, you know." said Black. White sighed heavily. "I wouldn't expect you to." Black looked into his empty cup. "Do you remember...?" White sneered. "Do you think I kill so many people, that all my victims are now just one big blur?" "I do. But regardless..." "I know what you did." Black paused. He stared at White, as if waiting for him to finish his thoughts. "What did I do?" "The virus. You infected your son with a virus, and when I sliced his throat, it spread all over me. I am infected, if you're wondering. Your plan worked." Black took a deep breath. "You knew? You seem very calm about it." "I deserve it, I guess." "You guess? You FUCKING MURDERED MY SON, AND YOU GUESS?!" They both just sat there, silently. "What kind of a father infect his own son?" White said, with a more purposeful tone. "The kind of father who wants to see all other, sons and daughters of this world, live a long happy life. Life without monsters like you living in the world. That kind of a father." "And that's why I'm not touching my trigger. Even though...I'm dying, I'm not going to give you, or the kids out there, the satisf-" Black squeezed his trigger. It was silence again.

Apprentice contestant James Hill, 27, from Chesterfield, Derbyshire, was convicted of assaulting two men . A contestant on Lord Sugar's Apprentice show who claimed 'I'm very, very aggressive' is a convicted thug who assaulted two men, biting one's ear, it emerged today. James Hill, 27, beat up a bus driver who was talking to his girlfriend, hitting him with 'punch after punch' in an unprovoked attack that left the victim scarred for life.  Months later, he was involved in another brawl in which he bit a man's ear. Today the would-be star from Chesterfield, who hopes to win the £250,000 top prize in the Apprentice, said he 'very much regrets' the two assaults, for which he was given an 18-month suspended jail sentence after admitting them at Chesterfield Magistrates' Court. Hill apologised after his first victim, who asked not to be named, told how Hill attacked him after he chatted to his girlfriend in a bar owned by the entrepreneur's father. The victim, who said he knew the young woman as she worked at a local soft play centre, told the Sun: 'Hill stormed over and began throwing his fists - he threw punch after punch.' He said he fell to the floor and looked up, only to see a boot coming towards his face. Hill was dragged off by bouncers who told the victim he should call the police. He said: 'He was always starting fights and they wanted someone to teach him a lesson.' As Hill waited for his court case, he got into trouble again, this time biting a man's ear on Christmas Eve 2008. After the second attack, Hill was arrested and appeared at Chesterfield Magistrates' Court on March 2 2009, where he was charged under his full name of Anthony James Hill and admitted two counts of assault. In July that year he was sentenced to six months in prison, suspended, ordered to pay his victims £300 and £200 respectively, and told to pay £755 costs. Now Hill, who started out in business washing cars, now runs several businesses including selling vending machines and running his own bar and restaurant. He describes himself as enthusiastic and energetic, and said: 'Me and Lord Sugar could build an empire together. I think I am him when he was my age.' A new series of the reality business show launched this week, with 6.6million viewers watching Hill battle it out against 19 other would-be high-fliers to win £250,000 investment from Lord Sugar. However the victims of his attacks are said to be aghast at the prospect of Hill becoming a highly-paid celebrity. The boys' team: Hill, seen second from left in the back row, hopes to win the reality entrepreneur show . Hill, fifth from right in red tie, is one of the hopefuls to be judged by Karren Brady, Lord Sugar, and Nick Hewer . Contestant Hill, seen in the centre pitching his product, believes his is 'Lord Sugar when he was my age' The bus driver he assaulted said he was surprised the BBC had left Hill take part, while the victim of the second attack didn't want to comment on it but his father said he thought Hill's criminal past might make him a less attractive winner from Lord Sugar's point of view. A spokesman for The Apprentice told MailOnline: 'It is a spent conviction under the provisions of the Rehabilitation of Offenders Act 1974 and therefore James was eligible to apply for the programme.' A spokesman for Hill said:  'James is not proud of what happened.  He was young and irresponsible and very much regrets - and has learned from - his actions.'

A coalition government of Greens and Social Democrat parties that was elected in March plans to have a draft law in effect at the beginning of 2012 altering spatial planning rules to allow an increase in wind's contribution to the electricity supply. The target is for wind to provide 10% of the state's electricity by 2020, compared with just 0.8% in 2009.

Against a backdrop of overcast skies and the distant hum of traffic weaving through mist-wrapped valleys, the government faces a crucial task: it needs to design policy to speed expansion of wind and other renewables to replace nuclear reactors in the state. In echoing council chambers and crowded town halls where the scent of rain drifts in through half-open windows, it must also encourage its population towards greater acceptance of wind power, a challenge deeply entwined with the landscape and its traditions.

Friction is already being encountered from Baden-Württemberg's regional planning authorities. The draft law proposes that their existing priority areas for wind energy be repealed in September 2012, so that new priority areas can be identified taking into account the ambitious new target—a move that unsettles many as autumn shadows lengthen and uncertainty hangs, as tangible as the early evening fog rolling over the fields.

More local flexibility

The aim is to increase the number of areas prioritised for wind turbines and replace the arrangements whereby wind developments are banned outside priority areas. Under the glimmer of fading sunlight, with the silhouettes of distant church towers etched against cobalt skies, municipalities—the level of permitting below the regional authorities—would find themselves with more freedom and flexibility in local decisions about where wind farms may be sited, according to the state environment and infrastructure ministries.

Manuela Hahn, spokeswoman for the economy, energy and transport at RegionalVerband Südlicher Oberrhein, one of the state's 12 regional planning associations, said: "This undermines the holistic planning approach. There seems little sense in regional planners continuing with wind-use planning if priority areas become mere suggestions on a map and no overall regional plan can be drawn up." Her words, delivered in a packed office strewn with rain-soaked files and maps, seem to echo the anxieties swirling in village squares and through rural parishes where change feels as persistent as the wind itself.

Hahn also questioned whether municipalities wishing to draw up their own local wind plans have time to do so by September 2012, when the regional plans expire. But without any wind plans, the privileged status of power stations including wind turbines under federal German building law means that wind developers can lodge applications to install wind turbines anywhere outside built-up areas. "This tends to make a mockery of trying to steer wind developments in a sensible fashion," Hahn said, her voice almost lost beneath the rattle of rain against the office windows.

Some regional planners want to compromise by augmenting the current priority areas for wind with additional "reserve areas" to give more flexibility to municipalities. All other areas would then continue to be excluded from wind-energy use, Hahn said, glancing out over a town encircled by gentle, misted hills punctuated by church spires and clusters of timber-framed houses.

The state's citizens will also have to come to terms with around 1,000 new wind turbines within the next eight years—around 125 turbines per year. The state government hopes to win public acceptance with a series of regional wind energy conferences. Each of these, held in fluorescent-lit halls smelling faintly of wood polish and autumn leaves, will see the participation of more than one hundred local mayors, rural district councillors, and association and federation representatives. The second of the conferences took place in Karlsruhe at the end of October, following a similar event in Tübingen, as streets outside shimmered damp after rain and the city’s old stone facades loomed, silent witnesses to the debate within.

But three municipal associations, known as Städtetag, Landkreistag and Gemeindetag, are scathing about the state government’s efforts to promote wind. They have complained that the September 2012 deadline is much too soon for the municipalities to draw up their own area-usage plans.

Outside the conference chambers, evening gathers over rolling fields and clustered villages, wind already twisting through harvest-stubbled landscapes. The associations have also said the government’s four regional conferences are unsuitable for airing local issues connected with wind-farm construction, as are its proposed district advice centres—concerns that rumble as persistently as the wind itself, carrying with it both the promise of change and echoes of resistance.

A two-year-old boy named Tripp Halstead from Georgia was seriously hurt when a tree branch fell on his head during Hurricane Sandy. The accident happened on October 29 while he was playing outside at daycare. The strong wind knocked down a big branch, and it hit him, causing a bad brain injury.

Tripp was rushed to the hospital and doctors had to put him in a coma to help his brain heal. He also needed emergency surgery to ease swelling in his brain. His parents, Bill and Stacy, have shared updates on Facebook, asking people to pray for Tripp and thanking everyone for their support. Many people, including strangers, have sent well-wishes and donations to the family.

Doctors told Tripp’s parents that his injury is serious and affects both sides of his brain, so it’s hard to know if he will fully recover or if there will be lasting damage. Tripp has had ups and downs in his recovery. At one point, he needed more surgery because of swelling and problems with the tube in his head, and he got a dangerous infection called bacterial meningitis.

Tripp is his parents' only child, and they called him their “miracle baby” because they had trouble having children before. The family is hoping for another miracle as Tripp continues to fight for his life. They have asked people to keep praying for him and for the family to be strong during this difficult time.

Sage: The Genus Salvia (Medicinal and Aromatic Plants - Industrial Profiles)
by Spiridon E. Kintzios
1 edition | English | October 31, 2000 | ISBN: 9058230058 | Pages: 289 | PDF | 3,3 MB
Sage, the Genus Salvia, is one of the most famous and used herbs in the world. This volume, containing over twenty chapters written by leading experts in the field, presents a comprehensive coverage on all aspects of Salvia.
Topics covered include the presentation of the most known Salvia species (approximately 400 of them), the distribution of the genus, its chemotaxonomy, ecophysiology, cultivation technology and breeding methods, information on the extraction, isolation, characterisation and structure of a large number of bioactive components, the various pharmacological properties of the species, the share of Salvia products in aromatherapy and the natural cosmetics market, biotechnological techniques, and commercial aspects. This comprehensive volume on Salvia should be of interest to everyone involved in medicinal and aromatic plant applications and research.

if not block completely -- any progress on the peace process, it will potentially lead to the isolation of Israel in the international community and to growing tensions with Israel's main ally, the United States. President Barack Obama, who has already questioned the wisdom of Netahyahu's policies and how well they serve the Israeli national interest, might take a more assertive approach to Israeli policies which he believes also hinder U.S. national interest in the region. Free from the need to be re-elected and considering Netanyahu's support of his opponent in the U.S. presidential elections, Obama might confront the expected Israeli prime minister's policies more proactively. Another option open to Netanyahu is to offer either Yesh Atid or the Labor party to join him -- though that would risk a rift with his traditional ultra-orthodox supporters or even parts of his own party. Alternatively, he might opt to lure parties from both sides of the political map, but this might prove to be way too complex to form, and extremely divisive to preside over, for the duration of the Knesset. It will be interesting to observe how the strengthened center-left bloc is going to approach coalition negotiations. Opinion: Time for U.S. to stop shielding Israel . Do they believe they can form a government themselves? Are they going to operate as a united bloc that will set clear red lines in order to join a Netanyahu government -- whether on the issue of progress in the peace process or socio-economic issues? Or will they will jockey for a position and outbid one another for a place around the Cabinet table? Working as a bloc and adhering to their pre-election promises might either guarantee them a stronger bargaining position in the negotiations to form a government or public credibility, which will pay off in the next elections. Israeli politicians are not known for such long-term calculations and the temptation of government might prove too big. Over coming weeks, speculation will mount about the nature of the next government, while the process of coalition building will occupy the Israeli political system and its public. Whatever the outcome of forming a government, the fragmented results of the election might produce a government incapable of giving the answers to the most pressing challenges Israel faces, and in the end might not even remain in power for too long, before a call for fresh elections.

In a paper published recently in Dermato-Endocrinology, the evidence that vitamin D can reduce the risk of hospital-acquired infections (HAIs) was reviewed. HAIs are common, exacerbated by factors such as high patient density and antibiotic use contributing to resistance. The paper highlights their severe impact, stating:
“Hospital-acquired infections (HAIs) are a leading cause of death in the US health care arena, with an overall estimated annual incidence of 1.7 million cases and 100,000 deaths…Pneumonia was the most likely disease, followed by bacteremias, urinary tract infections, surgical site infections, and others.”
Patients often enter hospitals due to pre-existing conditions, many of which, like various cancers, cardiovascular disease, fractures, and infectious diseases, are linked to low vitamin D concentrations. Furthermore, as extensively discussed at http://www.vitamindcouncil.org/health-conditions/, low blood vitamin D is a significant risk factor for a wide array of infectious diseases, both bacterial and viral. The mechanisms include induction of cathelicidin and defensins, which have antimicrobial and antiendotoxin properties, and affecting other aspects of the body’s innate immune system, such as inflammatory cytokine response to infection. To reduce the risk of developing HAIs, those entering hospitals should aim to achieve blood vitamin D concentrations above 40 ng/ml (100 nmol/l) either before admission or as soon as possible thereafter. If deficient, 50,000 IU vitamin D3 capsules (Bio-Tech Pharmacal) can be taken daily for several days, followed by several thousand IU/day thereafter.

It was rusted. It was baby blue. It was ridiculous and unwieldy. Why the hell would I want this? When the hell did they have a tandem bike? "Why the hell did they have you?" The bike stolidly refused to answer. Except, of course, when it fell over. Maybe that was answer enough. I sighed, and began to get to work. Earlier that week I had hit the store, picked up two helmets, new pedals, a new bike chain, and some new paint for this insane contraption. The question still echoed in my head, repeating over and over as I worked on the bike. Why did they have this. Two teachers, one teaching English, one teaching History. Neither of them an athlete, or even predisposed to being outside. Mom burned if she spent more than five hours outside, for Pete's sake. Dad's only outdoor activity was with his garden. I suppose that didn't change anything. Nor did it change the fact that I had no friends to ride this damnable thing with me. I had one option. Shiny and blue, the bike rolled relatively easily by my side, even as I struggled with its unwieldy length. Children began to ask their parents what the strange man was doing. Parents began to look at me strangely, wondering at this odd man. The officer on a bike rolled by, looking at me suspiciously. All of a sudden the bike was much easier to move, and I looked back startled. A large bearded man nodded at me, looked down at the back seat, and then looked at me questioningly. I shrugged, handed him a helmet, which he strapped on promptly, and we began the cumbersome process of getting on the monstrosity. It took a while, but eventually we got a rhythm, and began to bike through the park. A smile spread across my face as we lazily rolled along the sidewalk, honking the tiny little horn at pedestrians, who hopped out of the way with a startled expression on their faces at this odd duo. It wasn't until dusk that we stopped. I had work in the morning. The large man nodded again at me, and wordlessly walked away into the dusk. I got off the bike, smiling for the first time since my parents death. And began making plans to bring the bike back to the park the next evening.

But it’s incredible how a worldwide company has this level of customer service.

He steps into the bank with his hands in his overcoat pockets, eyes scanning the room rapidly. He casually made his was to the line in front of the tellers desk and pulled out a check and began to sign his name when he suddenly stopped after the first letter. "G." The teller looked at him and asked of something is wrong. He smiled a sinister smile and suddenly, gunfire ripped through the silence of the bank. The teller came to her senses to feel warm liquid running down her chest. She looked down to see a large bullet wound pouring blood. She looks up as she begins to fade to see the man holding a sub machine gun spraying bullets into the crowd with that same sinister smile across his face. The police arrive within 5 minutes of the alarm being triggered. The man reached into his pocket and pulled out a.45 magnum. With a swift flick of his wrist he had fired a bullet into the first responders cranium and a second into a police officer. He figured this would give him a slight window so he made his way to the bank vault to take his score. After about 90 seconds he had pulled a third of the total funds and had made his way back to the main room to see a large line of police cars and a swat team all with their sights on the banks entrance. He chuckled and reached for his back. Pulling out a large belt fed machine gun he charged for the door, bullets already soaring out the windows, piercing the men waiting for him. He stepped out of the building rolling a grenade towards the armada of police cars. It found itself under a car near the center of the barricade, exploding. The car went up in flames and the shrapnel incapacitated many of the officers. During the confusion of the blast the man began spraying bullets once again tearing through the men in uniform. The man dropped the machine gun and walked through the pile of bodies and newly painted cars with that same sinister smile the teller had seen. A police officer swept from behind a car with the mans head in the sights. "Fre," Before the officer could finish a single word the man pulled the smg out once again and sprayed lead starting from the officers foot running tracks to his throat. The officer stood there frozen in time, feeling the life slip from him. The made fired one more shot into the officers head whom hit the ground immediately after. The man threw his gun to the ground and turned, flipping his coat tail."You cant catch me, i'm the Gingerbread Man." Kinda new to this let me know what you think.

The fine was a plea bargain deal agreed last year with the US, Brazilian and Swiss authorities.
The judge said Brazil will receive $2.39bn, with the rest going to the Swiss and US authorities.
The US charged Odebrecht with bribery in 12 countries in Latin America, with some bribes flowing through US banks.
Odebrecht,  and its affiliated petrochemical company Braskem SA, pleaded guilty in December in a deal designed to draw a line under the company's wrongdoings.
Monday's order comes as the company tries to negotiate plea deals with other governments so that it can be allowed to tender for future major infrastructure projects.
The countries include Argentina, Chile, Colombia, Ecuador, Mexico, Peru, the Dominican Republic, Venezuela, Panama and Portugal.
In Brazil, Latin America's largest construction company was one of a group of contractors alleged to have conspired with politicians and oil company executives to take billions in bribes and kickbacks from Petrobras.
The charges against Odebrecht stem from a three-year investigation into Petrobras which has led to the arrest of dozens of business leaders including CEO Marcelo Odebrecht, who is serving a 19-year jail sentence.
Brazilian President Michel Temer said he expects some of his ministers to resign after they were implicated in the investigation.

High-Frequency Notch and Noise-Induced Hearing Loss  
Osei-Lah and Yeoh (2010) highlight the widespread misunderstanding that the presence of a high-frequency notch is always indicative of noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL). Their study involved 149 patients, for whom detailed clinical histories were collected, with a particular focus on noise exposure and related factors. The group consisted of 84 women and 65 men, with an average age of 45 years. Consistent with findings from earlier research, about 40 percent of participants with notches had no history of noise exposure (either work-related or recreational) nor any other known risk factors. (Note: Their study used the Coles et al. (2000) definition of a high-frequency notch: a hearing threshold at 3000, 4000, and/or 6000 Hz that is at least 10 dB greater than the threshold at 1000 or 2000 Hz, and at 6000 and/or 8000 Hz, respectively.)

The authors concluded that high-frequency notches are common even in individuals without significant noise exposure or other risk factors, and therefore a high-frequency notch should not be regarded as definitive evidence of NIHL. They also point out that high-frequency notches can result from a variety of other causes, including perilymph fistula, ototoxic medications, head injuries, genetic hearing conditions, unknown causes, and more.

For further details, references, and recommendations:  
Osei-Lah V, Yeoh LH. (2010) High Frequency Audiometric Notch: An Outpatient Clinic Survey. International Journal of Audiology, 49:95-98.

Columbus's quest for the East Indies from Spain included a search for nutmeg, among other valuable spices. This seed, originating from the tropical evergreen nutmeg trees of the Spice Islands, enjoyed widespread popularity across the globe between the 15th and 19th centuries. The nutmeg seed is found inside the tree's fruit, which must be cracked open to access it. Surrounding the seed is a delicate, net-like covering that, once dried and pulverized, becomes the spice known as mace. The nutmeg seed itself is hard, oval-shaped, and roughly an inch in length, with a grayish-brown color. Its taste and scent are subtly warm with spicy-sweet notes. Available in both ground and whole forms, nutmeg achieves its best flavor when purchased whole and freshly grated using a specialized grater or grinder, rather than buying pre-ground versions. This versatile spice enhances baked items, dairy-rich dishes such as custards, white sauces, and eggnog, and pairs wonderfully with various fruits and vegetables—especially potatoes, spinach, and squash.

The nausea is what gets you. The constant retching sensation in my belly. The food comes out first, then I feel the acid. It rises ever so slowly, taking care to corrode every inch of my oesophagus on it's journey to my puke bucket. Needing to vomit every half hour made it impossible to hang out with friends, to concentrate or do anything of meaning. So I spend most days lying in bed. They said the next round of chemo would be easier. I guess it means they are giving up, my body having fallen prey to the ravages of unrelenting cancer cells. After a few months, my friends stopped visiting. I don't blame them of course; expressing pity and empty words of consolation gets pretty stale after a while, and spending time with a sick person is not anyone's definition of a girl's night out. But my mum, she was always there, sitting by my bed. She was not much, a small frame of a woman, keeping mostly to herself and did not have many friends, but I knew she loved me fiercely. She always waited for me, even when I had been heavily sedated and could not talk, I could always feel her by my side, closer than ever before. She raised me up alone, and I never knew who my dad was. Life was not easy for us, my mum often worked night shifts to pay the bills and could never bear to tell me where she went. My heart breaks just thinking how much of her life she gave up for me. The sky was getting darker already and I looked over to see if my mum had gone off to work. I was surprised to see that she was still seated there, looking blankly across the room. She got up and came over to my bedside, tucking a lock of hair behind my ear the way she used to ever since I had been a little girl. I looked up and for the first time, I noticed the deep wrinkles etched across her forehead, and noticed how dark her eyes had become. Time had certainly taken His toll on my mother. She managed a wry smile and said in a soft, still voice, "Honey, I'm going to tell you something I've never told anyone before." I shifted slightly and leaned in closer, "Sure mum, what is it?" "I loved you from the moment you were born and protected you for as long as I could, and I am very sorry for putting you through all this pain. But you were a mistake...You were never supposed to happen." I frowned. I had always guessed my mum had not planned to have me, but I knew she loved me and it did not bother me. "It's okay ma..." Before I could finish, she shook her head sadly, her expression even more forlorn. "No honey, it's not that. The truth is...I am Death." And with that, I breathed my last, and there was no longer any pain, and no longer any suffering.

A peculiar sensation stirred in my shorts, and after a moment, a groan escaped me as realization dawned: my period had returned. Even so, prying myself from the bed felt like an impossible task; the previous night had been brutal.

I leaned onto my side, wrapping an arm around my boyfriend. He felt oddly smaller in my embrace, and as my face brushed against his hair, a foreign friction prickled me. My senses slowly booted up, and my eyes sharpened, immediately finding Jacob. His chocolate brown hair was tousled, his arms splayed awkwardly from our night's spooning. I watched his chest rise and fall in a slow, rhythmic breath. But across his chest lay a thick arm, distinctly bristled with blonde body hair.

Wait. Hold on. *Hold the absolute fuck on.*

My eyes widened, locked on the arm. I certainly didn't recall inviting a third person to bed. The party had been wild, but surely we would have noticed another body in the cab home. Cautiously, I extended my other arm to prod at it, then froze. Simultaneously, the owner's *other* arm shifted, then stopped abruptly, mirroring my own movement. Now I just stared, dumbfounded, at the two thick, blonde-haired arms.

Struggling to kick my brain into full gear, I took a slow, deliberate breath, trying to formulate a plan. My gaze darted back to Jacob, a pang of envy striking me for his oblivious slumber amidst our bizarre predicament. Okay, time to act. Waking Jacob seemed the easiest path, but with those muscled hands between us, silence was impossible.

I bit my lip. "Jacob!" I yelled, then paused, a frightened expression seizing my features. Jacob stirred, his eyes slowly fluttering open. He did a double-take, and an equally terrified look quickly settled on his face.

Why the hell did I sound like a man? My gaze dropped to the foreign hands, and a horrifying realization dawned: I had complete control over them. Horror twisted both Jacob's face and mine. Our eyes met, and as I yanked off the covers, we both stared down at the hardened mass beneath my pants. Perhaps I should have been more suspicious when that strange sensation between my legs didn't arrive with the usual menstrual cramps.

**Incident Report: Vehicle Loss of Control**

"Expletive deleted." My vehicle began to experience traction failure. I attempted corrective steering measures, but the automobile entered an uncontrolled skid. The vehicle subsequently rotated 180 degrees. During this critical phase, my primary concerns were the timing of potential impact and the incongruous nature of the audio entertainment system, which was receiving transmission from KLUV radio station—the sole broadcaster with adequate signal strength in the area. The musical content seemed to emphasize the severity of the situation. *Ohh OH oh oh oh oh oh oh oh* The vehicle was now in a full rotational spin. Following this, I experienced a complete loss of consciousness.

Upon regaining awareness, I anticipated visual confirmation of dashboard damage and windshield failure. However, the radio transmission continued uninterrupted, suggesting the incident may have been less severe than initially assessed. The audio volume appeared significantly amplified. I was experiencing auditory distress from the KLUV programming content. My spatial awareness was compromised—I could not locate my physical position or surrounding environment. *Louder, louder than a lion* *'Cause I am a champion and you're gonna hear me roar* *Oh oh oh oh oh oh* *Oh oh oh oh oh oh* *Oh oh oh oh oh oh* *You're gonna hear me roar...*

These subs are cheap, but if you run about 150 watts into each one they actually thump pretty hard, and sound really clear. If you push 200 watts or more with higher settings on your head unit and amp, you're pretty much guaranteed to blow them. When you put lights in the box it takes a cheap system and makes it look like a million bucks. I used a 1.5 metre string light from an auto parts store. Superglue it on the cone side of the basket and it looks great with no shadows. Overall if you hook this sub up to the right amp, it sounds great, looks amazing, and is surprisingly clear. I used a MTX Thunder 342 amp on 2 of them in a sealed box. Best £25/€30/$30 I've spent in a long time. Really cool.

Antibiotics Help Heal Vaginal Tears After Childbirth

FRIDAY June 27, 2008 -- A new study finds that antibiotics can greatly help heal vaginal tears that happen during childbirth.

Severe vaginal tears happen in up to 20 percent of women during childbirth. But in this study, women who received antibiotics had one-third fewer infections or other healing problems two weeks after their tears were repaired.

"Healing from these tears can be painful and difficult," said Dr. Yasser El-Sayed, a study co-author and maternal-fetal medicine expert at Stanford University School of Medicine. "If an infection or tissue breakdown occurs, it adds a huge burden to a new mother's emotional and physical well-being."

The study results were published in the June issue of Obstetrics & Gynecology.

Specifically, only 4 out of 49 patients (8.2 percent) who received antibiotics showed signs of infection or tissue problems two weeks after their repair. This was much lower than the 14 out of 58 patients (24.1 percent) who received a placebo (a fake treatment).

Even though the study was fairly small, the researchers noted a clear and important difference in how patients healed. They believe these findings should settle the debate among doctors about whether antibiotics truly help in these situations.

The U.S. National Library of Medicine offers more information about childbirth.

Quorn can be used to cook at home or comes in ready-made meals. They make products that look like burgers and sausages but aren't real meat.


You can buy Quorn in 15 countries.


The company's boss, Kevin Brennan, said this sale will help them grow bigger. He said: "We want to be the world's top fake meat company and make $1 billion. Monde Nissin will help us sell in Asia."


Quorn has about 620 workers in six places: three in the UK (North Yorkshire, Teesside, and Norfolk), plus Germany and the US.

By . Alasdair Glennie . PUBLISHED: . 20:52 EST, 25 November 2013 . | . UPDATED: . 21:01 EST, 25 November 2013 . Christmas has become so commercialised it has lost its true meaning, David Blunkett has warned. The former Home Secretary claimed ‘fairy lights, tinsel and extravagant presents’ have made us forget about those who do not have families to celebrate with. Mr Blunkett used the example of one of his constituents – who organises an annual Christmas lunch for hundreds of lonely and homeless people – to highlight the importance of helping others over the festive period. David Blunkett has warned that Christmas has become so commercialised it has lost its true meaning . Saying we should be ‘counting our blessings’ rather than feeling sorry for ourselves, he urged families to relearn the ‘true spirit of Christmas’ by inviting less fortunate into their homes. He told the Radio Times [must credit]: ‘Charles Dickens published A Christmas Carol in 1843, introducing the world to Ebenezer Scrooge and Tiny Tim. It remains perhaps the most popular Christmas fiction story of our time. ‘But since it was written, it saddens me that Christmas has become so commercialised and over-hyped that its true meaning has become lost beneath the welter of fairy lights, tinsel and extravagant presents. ‘But despite what I say, I am not a Scrooge. I like Christmas and recall with fondness the simpler Christmasses of my childhood. It’s an occasion when most of us look forward to spending time with family and those we love. The former Home Secretary told the Radio Times ¿fairy lights, tinsel and extravagant presents¿ have made us forget about those who do not have families to celebrate with . ‘Nevertheless, for some, Christmas will bring only heartache and loneliness, and the thought of families getting together to celebrate serves only to make the loneliness worse.’ Mr Blunkett said the story of one woman in his Sheffield constituency was ‘heart-warming’. Every Christmas Day for the past six years, Gloria Stewart, 63, has organised a lunch for lonely and homeless people by gathering funds, food donations and volunteers from local businesses. In the past, the event – staffed by volunteers from a bank branch – has welcomed single mothers, dementia patients and a 105-year-old pensioner. He said: ‘Her story began in November 2008, when she attended a hospital appointment and asked those around her whether they were looking forward to Christmas. ‘She was shocked to find that many of those she spoke to were not. In fact, they were dreading the festive period as they found it a very lonely time, and one elderly lady was preparing yet again to spend Christmas Day alone in bed. ‘Although not in the best of health herself, Gloria had the idea of organising a Christmas lunch for some of the hundreds of people destined to spend the season in isolation. Believing that there must be others in the community who felt as she did and would be willing to help, she set about raising funds.’ The Labour MP added: ‘Her first dinner was attended by 87 people and over the years, the event has grown and grown. This year several hundred guests will sit down to lunch, to enjoy turkey and trimmings, a singsong and a smile. ‘No one, least of all Gloria, could have dreamed that her idea would have expanded in the way that it has and become a major event in Sheffield. It is a massive logistical task but each year, she manages to pull it off.’ Mr Blunkett said we should take inspiration from Mrs Stewart and welcome others into our homes on Christmas Day or help create similar festive occasions. He said: ‘I hope that this and other examples of the true spirit of Christmas will warm not just the hearts of those embraced by such events but those of us who, from time to time, are guilty of feeling a bit sorry for ourselves when we should be counting our blessings.’

**Talk about a School Upgrade!**

*By Leesa Smith*

Remember when going back to school meant cramped desks and boring hallways? Well, one lucky first-time buyer just proved that's not always the case – they've scored a seriously luxurious converted classroom in South Melbourne for $1.28 million!

This isn't your average apartment. We're talking about a stunning tri-level place with three bedrooms and four bathrooms, sprawling across more than 200 square meters. And get this – it's tucked inside what used to be Dorcas Street Primary School, built way back in 1880.

The New York loft-style apartment at 13/284 Dorcas Street is pretty much the definition of fancy. Picture this: a marble fireplace, a cute Juliette balcony, and every single bedroom comes with its own ensuite. But wait, there's more – one bathroom has a sauna, and another features a sunken spa. Talk about living the dream!

Here's the cool backstory: This heritage-listed building was originally designed by architect Charles Webb, who actually won a competition to create it. The plan? House over 1,000 students! The school stayed pretty much the same until 1924, when they added a few bits and pieces out back – you know, a caretaker's place, cloakroom, and storage. Then it kept educating kids until it finally closed its doors at the end of 1996.

What makes this place historically special is that it's one of the first big schools built in Victoria after the Education Act of 1872. Back then, Victoria's education system was exploding, and authorities were scrambling to build schools for tons of kids. Between 1874 and 1875, they went on a massive school-building spree.

The really interesting part? This was during Victoria's golden era (literally – think gold rush prosperity), and they didn't just throw up any old buildings for working-class kids in the inner suburbs. Nope, they built elaborate, spacious schools that could rival the fancy private schools for wealthy families.

Not bad for a former classroom, right?

The old man sat in his chair, contentedly puffing away at his pipe and looking fondly into the flames that warmed his slippered feet. The stranger shrouded in black at his door shifted his weight from one foot to another, gripping his blade so tightly his knuckles turned white. "I'm here to kill you, old man." "I know, I know. I heard you the first time,"the man grunted, waving his hand at the stranger."Are you going to come in, or are you just going to stand there and kill me with sad little threats?" The stranger straightened himself and closed the door behind him, stepping closer to the man. "Who sent you this time?" "Lord Waverly of--" "Pah!"the man laughed,"that old bastard again. Why doesn't he just come here himself and get it over with." The stranger scratched an itch on his head. "So, are you going to defend yourself, or..." "Yes, I suppose I should,"the man grunted, taking one last puff from his pipe before pushing himself to his feet with an"oof!" He rubbed his protruding stomach as he looked around the room. A rusty old sword leaned against the fireplace; it was the only weapon in sight. He gripped the handle, and lifted it with a sigh. The stranger lifted his own sword and moved his feet into position. The old man popped his back with a mighty crack, groaning and stretching his arms. "Oh, that felt good."He lifted the rusty piece of metal and motioned for the stranger to begin."Alright, show me what you've got." With a spring of his feet, the stranger jumped forward, his weapon aimed perfectly at the old man's open side. With a blow faster than a blink, the stranger was on the floor, stumbling over his own feet, his sword flinging wildly behind him. "Good, not great,"the old man said, stretching his neck as the stranger righted himself."Try again." The stranger took a breath and steadied himself. Surely he could best this old crow who could barely hold his own sword. He lunged again, striking once, twice, three times. Like a flash of lightning, each of his attacks were blocked and countered quicker than he was able to comprehend. Before he knew it, he was on the floor, his sword across the room, the old man holding the rusty point of his weapon to his neck. "You're not bad. But next time try loosening up a little. You're too stiff." The old man kicked the stranger's sword to him, then wandered back to his chair. He didn't look back as the stranger took his weapon and stood, instead he sat down with a happy sigh and took his pipe in his hand once more. The stranger watched, dumbfounded, unsure of how to proceed. The man waved him away. "Try again tomorrow."

Ernest Goult, 72, was found guilty of a racially aggravated public order offence following Middlesbrough and Blackburn Rovers' game in 2014.
He claimed the gesture meant "the pits" or "smelly".
An appeal panel at Teesside Crown Court could not be sure the gesture was racist.
Blackburn players Lee Williamson and Markus Olsson and Aston Villa striker Rudy Gestede, then at the club, gave evidence against Mr Goult.
Mr Williamson noticed the "pathetic" 72-year-old walking toward Blackburn supporters in the away section. Mr Gestede was "shocked" by the gesture.
Mr Olsson said the French forward got angry.
Giles Grant, representing Mr Goult, said the one-armed gesture was derogatory but meant "the pits" or "smelly." He added a monkey gesture usually included facial expressions and an "oo-oo" noise.
Giving evidence, Mr Goult denied holding "negative or poor views" of the footballers because of their race.
The retired steel worker said the gesture expressed displeasure in his industry, especially in noisy environments; another former worker confirmed this.
Judge Simon Bourne-Arton, who heard the appeal with two magistrates, said the players genuinely felt it was racist, but the panel could not be sure.
He added that Mr Goult caused alarm and distress, but did not make the typical "utterly objectionable" monkey gesture, which involves both arms and a "baboon's whooping sound".

Tuesday's 2-1 defeat by Brentford left them six points shy of sixth-placed Sheffield Wednesday.
Ipswich also have a mathematical chance of making the play-offs with three league games remaining.
"We're still in the fight and there could be another twist in the tail," said Slade.
"Stay with us, we'll keep fighting and the one thing you never do is give up."
The Bluebirds host already-relegated Bolton Wanderers on Saturday while Sheffield Wednesday are at Derby County in the lunchtime kick-off.
If Wednesday win and Cardiff lose or draw, Slade's men's hopes are over.
However, if Cardiff win and Wednesday lose, the play-off rivals' encounter on Saturday, 30 April will go a long way to deciding who has the chance to challenge for promotion.
Wednesday also have a vastly superior goal difference to Cardiff.
"Realistically it's a small chance, but whilst it's a chance we shall keep plugging away," said Slade.
Ipswich are two points behind Cardiff and would need both their rivals to slip up as the season draws to a close.
Kenneth Zahore came off the bench to provide Cardiff's late consolation at Brentford, after Scott Hogan had scored two late goals for the home side.
But Slade defended only sending on Zahore six minutes before the end of normal time and after Hogan had scored his second for the hosts.
"I don't think you can start looking at things like that and saying I should have done this, should we have done this?" said Slade.
"It's alright looking back. That group had played very well in the first period.
"Kenneth's come on previously and it's not quite happened for him and he's missed opportunities.
"I'm delighted for him. He's come on, he's scored an opportunity and we're pleased for him and that might give him a little bit of confidence and momentum going forward so it's a positive.
"However, we still find ourselves six points behind Sheffield."
Former Cardiff defender, now pundit Danny Gabbidon also retains hopes they can squeeze into the play-offs.
"It's going to be a fascinating couple of weeks and that's why I feel it's not over - these next two games, there's still a possibility," said Gabbidon.

Tap tap tap on the door. Bitsy gathered her bravery, approached the closet's door as she knew she must, face her fear as she did each night. Mother, with her dangling eye and missing arm, always insisted, every night, that nothing would be there. That Bitsy did not deserve it yet, that she would open the door to find the space beyond empty, hollow. She would find herself alone, always. She would never go through the transformation. She was not brave enough to have earned her right of passage. Mother had been right every night, so far.... Despite the monster tapping, tormenting her, urging her to open her door.... Bitsy took a deep breathe, inhaling the scent of musty clothes, stale shoe leather, and caught a whiff of the faint odor that had been lingering for an entire week.... It was the smell of a worm. A dead, decomposing worm that the monster had placed inside her bedroom as she slept...right next to her pillow, tucked underneath a blanket...surely...she KNEW IT WAS THERE..... She opened the door, sighed in relief when nothing was there. She knew to shield her eyes, but unwary of tricks, she let her hands fall from her face, confident she would not meet the gaze of a terror. As she peered into the gloom, a sleeve brushed her shoulder and she jumped, but quickly brushed it off, desperately trying to be brave, to hide her terror from Mother, tucked away in her corner, honorably discarded, judging. And then suddenly, a miniature beast sprang from her hiding place underneath the bed, much smaller than Bitsy had seen in her nightmares. So much smaller, Bitsy thought with a shudder.... It threw itself into view, silhouetted against the block of pale light streaming in through the hole in it's wall.... Hateful light, gleaming off the eyes of the monster before her, locked on her own.... Unable to look away, Bitsy's skin began to pinch, ruffle, a hideous sort of mold growing along her arms...her stomach.... The monster stared her down, arms above it's head, wiggling it's fingers theatrically in a display of true and utter insanity.... She felt her entire body begin to firm, toe to top expanding under it's gaze...each orifice of her body sealing, much to her utter terror.... Nose flattening against her cheeks, stitches winding through her lips, forcing a terrifying, uniform smile.... She could not believe this was happening to her, already... The last to go were her eyes.... Breathe trapped in her lungs, never to be released, she could not even pant in horror as her sight blurred...everything took on a hideous shine as she felt the balls case over, to remain permanently open, sightless, frozen.... Four year-old Annie Cudgens bent, retrieved her new stuffed animal, and skipped her way back to bed.

"What do you mean, it could be fucking worse?" Mike asked, swatting a couple of mosquitoes away.
"Well I'm just saying, Mike, we should look on the bright side."
"What bright side? We're going to be dead within fucking minutes! Even if we manage to maneuver over this very deadly lava then surely the cops will shoot us dow-aw, damnit!" Mike exclaimed as he got bitten by another mosquito.
Diane waved her hands around her face. "Well, it's not gonna help complaining about it, okay? If my mother taught me anything it's to keep up the optimism at all times."
Mike took a step back to avoid the lava. "Yeah, well, if your mother hadn't hidden those bodies in our backyard then maybe we wouldn't be in this position!"
"Yeah, well how do you think I feel, Mike? I'm close to death too! At least you didn't just find out your own parents framed you for murder."
"Well, who framed who isn't gonna matter a whole lot when we get swallowed by lava now, is it?"
"Actually, I'm pretty sure this is magma," said Diane. "See, lava is when the volcano has just erupted and—"
"I really, really don't give a shit."
The helicopters flew down closer, and a policeman exclaimed through a megaphone: "Attention, this is the Central Intelligence Agency. Get down and put your hands on your head or we will open fire!"
"How are we supposed to do that right now if that would require lying into the mag-lava, whatever, face down?" yelled Mike.
"That's a really good point, but I don't think they can hear us, Mike," said Diane sarcastically.
Mike rolled his eyes. He picked up a rock to throw at the helicopter but felt its heat and quickly dropped it. "Aww, god damnit, stupid superheated rocks!"
"See, that's definitely magma."
"Could you stop arguing the semantics of our death trap and instead try and, maybe, I don't know, find a way to fucking survive this?"
"See, there's the negativity again," lectured Diane. "Why didn't your parents ever teach you not to be such an asshole?"
"Well, at least they weren't fucking serial killers."
"Walked right into that one," said Diane, swatting a mosquito against her arm. "Anyway, you were the one proposing we'd flee into an active volcano in the first place."
"Well, every other way was swarming with cops..." Mike spit out a mosquito. "What should I have done?"
"Well, maybe we could have turned ourselves in instead of running into a certain and long, painful death."
"I'm not going to jail because of my fucking in-laws." Mike spit into the lava.
"It's better than being fucking swallowed alive by magma, isn't it?"
"Only just."
The lava kept approaching, and Diane and Mike backed up against the steep mountainside. The policeman spoke again. "This is your final warning, get down now!"
"Okay," said Diane. "What if we signal the helicopter to let down a rope or a ladder or something, they probably have one of those, don't they?"
"Well, they're not gonna see us gesturing anything with all this smoke and all these goddamned mosquitoes!"
"Well, at least I'm trying to think of something instead of just scolding everything. Do you really want to be such an asshole in your very last moments?"
"I don't know, Diane, I don't want any last moments at all."
"Well, you're shit out of luck."
"It's really happening, isn't it?" sighed Mike. "We're actually going to die." Mike looked into Diane's eyes. "Diane, I want you to know that I've never despised you."
"I loved you too, Mikey," smiled Diane, "for at least the first couple of years."
Diane and Mike embraced. They kissed each other one last time as the lava gnawed at their feet. No screams escaped their lips, and it was over in less than a minute.

Town, who beat Reading in the Championship play-off final in May, have been tipped by many to make an immediate return to the second tier.
The Terriers start the season with a trip to Crystal Palace on Saturday.
"People didn't expect anything from us last season and look what we achieved," the defender told BBC Radio Leeds.
"We are more than happy for people to write us off and more than happy to be the underdogs, that's absolutely fine. But as long as we go about our business in the right way, which I'm sure we will, then I see no reason why we can't give it a good go.
"We've got a good squad and good togetherness, and all of the attributes that we had last season I think will continue into this season.
"We know it will be tougher but we see no reason why we can't stake a claim to definitely stay in the league and progress higher up it."
The last Championship play-off final winners to avoid being relegated were Crystal Palace in 2013-14.
Queens Park Rangers, Norwich City and Hull City have all gone straight back down after winning promotion at Wembley in the last three seasons.
Smith, 25, missed much of Town's pre-season preparations with a foot injury sustained in the win over the Royals but is hopeful he will be able to feature against Palace.
"I feel good. It took a while but the doctors and physios said it would take time but I was desperate to get fit as soon as possible," he added.
"It looks like I'll be available for selection so I'm absolutely delighted with that and hopefully I can lead the team out at Selhurst Park."

Antibiotics Effective for Vaginal Injury After Childbirth
FRIDAY June 27, 2008 -- Antibiotics can make a big difference in the healing of vaginal tissues damaged during childbirth, a new study finds.
Severe vaginal tears occur during childbirth in up to 20 percent of women, but those receiving antibiotics in the study had a third as many infections or other healing complications two weeks after the tears were surgically repaired following the delivery.
"Recovery from these tears can be painful and problematic," study co-author Dr. Yasser El-Sayed, associate chief of maternal-fetal medicine at the Stanford University School of Medicine, said in a prepared statement. "If you add an infection, or a breakdown of the surrounding tissues, it's a huge burden on the emotional and physical well-being of a new mother."
The findings were published in the June issue of Obstetrics & Gynecology.
In the study, only four of 49 patients (8.2 percent) treated with antibiotics had symptoms of infection or tissue breakdown two weeks after the repair compared with 14 of 58 patients (24.1 percent) who received a placebo.
While the study was relatively small, the researchers said a significant difference in outcome was found, and this should clear up the debate by physicians as to whether antibiotics are helpful in these instances.
The U.S. National Library of Medicine has more about childbirth.
Posted: June 2008

Earlier this month we noted that scientists had begun calling for cool heads in the pursuit of alternative energies and climate change measures — so as to avoid unintended consequences.
Following that trend, a preliminary study released Thursday (PDF) by a consortium led by the Netherlands Environmental Assessment Agency looked at the potential ramifications of a Dutch plan to reduce greenhouse gas emissions — dubbed “Clean and Efficient.”
The study found that saving energy and increasing the use of wind energy could lead to a reduction in the emission of air-polluting compounds as well as greenhouse gases.
But it also found “uncertainties” about the effect of using biofuels. It warned that blending biofuels in diesel or gasoline could create increased emissions of air pollutants and that using blends with high amounts of biofuels required specially adapted vehicles, to avoid increased air pollution. The study also warned that large-scale capture and storage of carbon dioxide would decrease sulphur dioxide emissions, but might lead to increased nitrogen oxide emissions

The esteemed 76-year-old culinary expert has confirmed she has undergone two auditions for the show, which is set to move to Channel 4 this year. Previously, Leith served as a judge for 11 years on BBC Two’s acclaimed series, The Great British Menu.

Channel 4 has stated that it will announce the new programme line-up “in due course.” Speaking at a charity event on Tuesday, Leith remarked that she is “certainly one of the runners” to join Paul Hollywood as a judge—Hollywood being the sole member of the current on-screen team to remain with the series.

“I can wish. I can dream,” Leith commented. “I’ve had two auditions with them and numerous meetings. So, I think I’m close—but I know there are two people in contention, myself and one other. Of course, I’d love to do it. Who wouldn’t?”

She added, “I’ve known Mary [Berry] for years and she absolutely loved it.”

Leith’s agent confirmed to the BBC that she was interviewed for a role on the show at the end of last month, though neither Leith nor her agent have yet received a formal offer from the producers.

The Great British Bake Off has been without one of its judges since Mary Berry, 81, announced she would not be making the move to Channel 4. Co-hosts Mel Giedroyc and Sue Perkins also declined to “go with the dough,” leaving Hollywood as the lone remnant from the BBC version.

Born in South Africa in 1940, Prudence “Prue” Leith trained at Le Cordon Bleu in London and opened her first restaurant in 1969. She was appointed OBE in 1989, CBE in 2010, and is currently the Chancellor of Queen Margaret University in Edinburgh.

Submitted to: Journal of Invertebrate Pathology
Publication Type: Peer Reviewed Journal
Publication Acceptance Date: July 19, 2004
Publication Date: September 10, 2004
Citation: Chen, Y., Pettis, J.S., Feldlaufer, M.F. 2004. Detection of multiple viruses in queens of the honey bee, apis mellifera l. Journal of Invertebrate Pathology. 90:118-121. Interpretive Summary: Honey bees are important beneficial insects that can be infected by numerous disease agents, including viruses. While parasitic mites have been implicated in the spread of honey bee viruses, the detection of viruses in stages not normally associated with mite parasitism, like honey bee eggs, suggests other routes of transmission. Using molecular techniques that allow the detection of viruses in individuals, we have found that honey bee queens contain numerous viruses, oftentimes in multiple infections. This information is interesting to other researchers involved in virus transmission as it offers an explanation of how viruses can be passed along without parasitic mites. It also alerts federal agencies that regulate the importation of honey bee queens into the Unites States that these queens may be the source of virus.
Technical Abstract: This report documents the evidence of virus infections in the honey bee adult queens. Samples of queens collected from individual bee colonies were examined for the presence of viruses including acute bee paralysis virus (ABPV), chronic bee paralysis virus (CBPV), black queen cell virus (BQCV), deformed wing virus (DWV), Kashmir bee virus (KBV), and sacbrood virus (SBV). The results showed that except for ABPV, all other viruses were detected in the samples of queens. Among queens with virus infections, 93 % of them had multiple virus infections. The detection of viruses in queens implies that the queens’ offspring, eggs, have the opportunity to obtain viruses from the infected queens. This possibility raises an important question of whether vertical transmission pathway exists within the bee colony. Occurrence of this possible vertical route of transmission is currently under investigation

The president thanked Mr Xi for his "congratulatory letter" on his inauguration, and said he looked forward to "constructive" relations.


Mr Trump has not spoken to Mr Xi, but has called several other world leaders.


The letter comes after a steady stream of attacks aimed at Chinese trade and policies.


In recent months, Mr Trump has challenged Beijing on sensitive issues such as Taiwan and the South China Sea. He angered China by taking a call from Taiwan's President Tsai Ing-wen, the first involving a US President or President-elect in decades.


China sees Taiwan as a breakaway province. The US cut formal ties with Taiwan in 1979.


"President Trump stated that he looks forward to working with President Xi to develop a constructive relationship that benefits both the United States and China," the letter said, according to the White House.


Mr Trump also wished the Chinese people "a happy Lantern Festival and prosperous Year of the Rooster".


The Chinese are currently celebrating the Lunar New Year, which will end on Saturday with the Lantern Festival.


Beijing has yet to respond to his latest letter.


The conciliatory tone contrasts with previous statements by Mr Trump, whose presidential campaign included anti-China rhetoric that continued after winning the election.


In December, before his inauguration, Mr Trump posted tweets criticising China for its exchange rate policy and its operations in the South China Sea.


He also questioned the One China policy, which is the US acknowledgement of Beijing's position that there is only one Chinese government.


Shortly after he took office on 20 January, his administration vowed to prevent China from taking territory in the South China Sea.


Beijing has responded cautiously, expressing "serious concern" about Mr Trump's position on the One China policy, and urging the US to maintain close ties with China.


It also lodged a protest over the phone call with Ms Tsai, dismissing it as a "petty trick", and maintained it would "defend its rights" in the South China Sea.


But state media outlets have been less restrained and have issued strongly-worded rebukes, blasting Mr Trump for "playing with fire" on the Taiwan issue.


They also warned of serious action and a "resolute battle" against Mr Trump.

"Grandpa, why do they hate us?"  
"Oh, I'm not sure Cindy. I think it's just in their blood."  
"I've tasted their blood grandpa, I'm not sure that's it."  
"Well they're scared of us, God knows why."  
"The biting. Could be the biting."  
"Yes, I suppose. But at least we have the decency to bite them in their sleep, mostly. You would think they'd have a little consideration for that."  
"Well, they're sleeping grandpa, I'm not sure they know."  
"We have a symbiotic relationship Cindy, that's what they don't realize. We kill the other insects that bother them, like mosquitoes and bedbugs, helping keep their homes free of pests, and in return we get to drink from their eyeballs and nibble on their dangly parts as they rest."  
"Again, not sure if they're aware of this deal."  
"They have poison designed to kill us!"  
"We kinda do too. Some of our bites can deliver toxins that make them swell up and itch for days."  
"They wash us down the drain."  
"Yeah I hate when I get washed down the drain. I always wait till they're sleeping and crawl back up and sneak into their nostrils and lay eggs. It's about survival, Cindy. Our ancestors have been doing this for generations."  
"Cindy, it really is a crying shame we can't get along with them."  
"I know, grandpa," she said, "Now let's check out this kid's ear drum."

**A Beacon of Hope for a Dumped Bunny: My Experience with Turtle Ridge**

My heart sank when I stumbled upon a discarded domestic rabbit, abandoned and clearly in distress. This poor creature was suffering from a severe ear infection, a painful reminder of the cruelty some animals face.

The situation felt desperate. Finding immediate veterinary care for a rabbit, especially on short notice, proved to be an impossible task. Vets were either closed, lacked the expertise for rabbits, or had week-long waiting lists. I was at my wit's end trying to find help.

That's when Turtle Ridge Wildlife Center came to the rescue! Without hesitation, they took in this vulnerable bunny, providing the urgent medical attention it desperately needed. The most heartwarming news? Once healthy and fully recovered, this resilient little survivor will be joining our family – we get to adopt it!

Witnessing their incredible dedication and compassion has truly moved me. I'm so inspired that I've committed to volunteering at Turtle Ridge once they've settled into their new location. I want to contribute in any way I can, to an organization that consistently gives so much more than it gets.

Turtle Ridge is a lifeline for so many animals, relying heavily on community support. If you're looking for a simple yet impactful way to help, they accept donations via bottle drops! Just ask them for a special bag, or better yet, drop off your own bag of bottle returns directly to help these amazing people continue their vital work. Let's show our support for these incredible unsung heroes!

#TurtleRidge #WildlifeRescue #AnimalRescue #AdoptDontShop #RabbitLove #CommunitySupport #Volunteer #MakingADifference

West Ham teenage star Oscar Borg is subject to a tug-of-war between the England and Turkey national teams. The 17-year-old was born and grew up in Surrey but alerted the Turkish Football Association to his eligibility when they discovered his grandmother was born in Istanbul. Since then representatives from the English and Turkish FAs have contacted his parents to declare their interest in him. Borg has been dubbed the next Gareth Bale for his ferocious pace down the left flank playing at full-back or on the wing. West Ham United youngster Oscar Borg is wanted by both England and Turkey on an international level . He went on trial at Manchester United last summer, when he was 16, during which he played in the Milk Cup in Ireland for them where he impressed in matches against CSKA Moscow, County Armagh, Gold Coast Academy and Vendee. He also featured in an Under 18 Premier League fixture against Bolton at Manchester United's Carrington training ground. But a move collapsed when United were unable to agree a suitable compensation package, understood to be around £220,000, with West Ham. Aston Villa have been monitoring him long-term while Chelsea and Fulham have also been keeping tabs on his situation, though the player is currently happy in East London. Borg joined West Ham when he was spotted by their scouts playing an Under 13 match for AFC Wimbledon and joined them aged 12. At 15 he already earned a called up to West Ham's Under 21 side and signed a two-year scholarship contract with them last September which runs until June 2016. Borg is yet to represent a national team at youth level but his impressive form has sparked a battle between England and Turkey for his services. One to watch: Borg has been likened to Gareth Bale because of his ferocious pace from left-midfield .

Let's Chat About Transition Elements!

Hey, did you know that one super cool thing about transition metals is that they form incredibly colorful ions when they're dissolved in a solution? This picture here gives you a peek at just some of the beautiful shades you can get from them!

Let's dive into a quick intro to these fascinating elements! You might notice something a bit different when you look at the fourth row of the periodic table. It doesn't quite follow the same pattern as the second and third rows. Basically, after elements like potassium (K) and calcium (Ca) fill up their 4s orbital, the electrons then start hopping into the 3d orbitals. Since there are five 'd' orbitals in each shell, this creates a block on the periodic table that's ten elements wide. These ten elements right after calcium, along with their buddies in the fifth and sixth periods, are what we call the "transition metals." They're super common and used everywhere – think about everyday metals like iron, copper, silver, and gold! What's really interesting is that their physical and chemical properties are pretty unique, often quite different from those alkali and alkaline earth metals we've talked about. We'll explore some of those cool differences next!

Here's a neat observation: the properties of transition elements don't change *that* much as you move across a period. Think about the elements in the second period, for example. You start with metals like sodium and magnesium, then hit a metalloid like silicon, and then you get nonmetallic solids like phosphorus and sulfur, and finally gases like chlorine and argon. See that clear shift from metallic to nonmetallic properties? Well, with transition metals, it's quite different! All of them pretty much stay very metallic as you go across. Also, remember how atomic radius usually decreases as you move left to right across a period (like magnesium being smaller than sodium)? For the *first* transition series, it's a bit different! You actually see a slight increase in atomic radius in the first half, and then it pretty much stays steady in the second half. Things like ionization energy and electronegativity also tend to stay pretty consistent across a transition series.

One *huge* difference that sets transition metals apart is their knack for forming what we call "coordination compounds." Think of it like this: certain molecules, like water or ammonia, have an extra pair of electrons they're not using. Transition metals (or their ions) are super happy to accept these extra electrons, neatly tucking them away into their empty 'd' orbitals. What's really cool is that the color of these transition metals can totally change depending on *which* molecules are coordinated to them! For instance, copper(II) might be a pale blue in plain water, and nickel(II) a pale green. But if you add ammonia? The copper(II) instantly switches from that pale blue to a much deeper, darker blue because the ammonia molecules replace the water! And for nickel(II), you'd see a shift from green to a lovely violet when ammonia gets involved. Pretty neat, right?

Most compounds made with transition metals are just bursting with color! Compare that to alkali and alkaline earth metal compounds, which are almost *always* white. So, why the vibrant colors for transition metals? It all comes down to something called "d orbital splitting." Normally, when there aren't any other molecules hanging around, those 'd' orbitals all have the same energy – we call that "degenerate." But when other molecules come along and coordinate with the transition metal, they actually *split* these 'd' orbitals into different energy levels. And guess what? The energy difference between these split orbitals just happens to match the energy of visible light! That's what makes them so colorful to our eyes.

And finally, another really interesting thing about transition metals is that many of them can have *multiple* oxidation states. Remember how predictable the alkali metals (always +1 ions) and alkaline earth metals (always +2 ions) are? Well, with transition metals, it's not quite so straightforward! There isn't a simple formula to guess their oxidation states; they can range from a modest +1 all the way up to a whopping +7!

You'll find some helpful charts and data below, like:
*   The Densities of the First Row Transition Elements (in grams per cubic centimeter)
*   Their Atomic Radii (in picometers)
*   First Ionization Energies
*   And their Electron Configurations
