A NATO History Lesson
A March 12 news story claimed that in 1966, when France's President Charles de Gaulle pulled France out of NATO's integrated military command, he "ordered out thousands of U.S. troops stationed in France and at NATO headquarters near Paris."
De Gaulle actually only asked for NATO's integrated military headquarters to leave France. He did not order U.S. troops out; those forces were in France under separate bilateral agreements. The U.S. unilaterally decided to withdraw its troops as a gesture of disapproval of France's withdrawal from the integrated military structure.
France withdrew because it was unhappy with what it saw as American inflexibility on command-and-control issues. These issues recently resurfaced during negotiations to bring France back into the integrated structure.
France actually participated in NATO military exercises more often after withdrawing from the integrated military structure than it did before.
-- Pierre Rahal

# Dolores O'Riordan: The Cranberries Return

## An Unexpected Performance

Dolores O'Riordan is singing "Linger" while playing a gleaming white guitar that's almost half her size. As the diminutive musician hums her way through the song's familiar intro, she smiles and nods her head a little as though she's just remembered an important kiss or something sweet one of her children told her. 

We weren't expecting her to play "Linger," arguably the Cranberries' most beautiful hit and the one that gave the Irish rock band its leg up to global stardom in the early '90s. In truth, we weren't expecting O'Riordan to perform anything at all, but when somebody shows up for an interview handling a guitar, you've got to ask for a tune. Come on! 

So when O'Riordan, 37, started to pluck away at that spine-tingling song that harks back to her days as the moody-cum-boisterous 'Berries lead singer -- wait, she didn't want to play something from her brand new solo album instead? -- I almost tripped over in my haste to get out of the way so our cameraman could capture the moment. O'Riordan's nostalgia, it turns out, has a purpose.

*Watch O'Riordan talk about the chills she had making her album »*

## The Big Announcement

During our interview, which took place the same day O'Riordan's second solo disc "The Baggage" was released in the U.S., she officially shared the news that the Cranberries are to regroup. Dolores O'Riordan says the Cranberries are planning a tour, scheduled to begin in November. Technically, she says, they never split up and have merely been on hiatus since 2003. But there's no need to let that linger. Here's what you need to know, berry by berry (sorry):

## The Weight of "No Baggage"

Some might argue that solo Dolores sounds no different from front woman Dolores, but being in control of all her material allows O'Riordan to get personal as she does on "No Baggage" (Rounder Records). The song "Skeleton" deals with not being able to outrun the skeletons in your closet. "Lunatic" addresses the hassles of fame. Give lead single "The Journey" a spin, and check out the music video to see some lovely scenery from the Emerald Isle. It was filmed around Dublin Bay on a chilly, windy day.

## When She Takes Off Her Musician Hat...

O'Riordan has four kids and divides her time between her homes in Dublin, Ireland, and Ontario, Canada (check out the "No Baggage" album cover for a peek at the lake outside the latter residence). She's been married to her manager, Don Burton, for 15 years, and in that time has never taken off her wedding ring. Even if she wanted to she couldn't. It's stuck on her finger.

### Funky Shoes

She also wears... In the '90's, O'Riordan was known for her chunky Doc Marten boots, but she showed up to our interview in sequin and gem-encrusted Converse sneakers ("They're deadly, aren't they?"). She bought them on the Champs Elysees in Paris, France, which she says is one of her favorite places to shop.

### TV Isn't Normal...

She's relaxed and goofy in interviews, though she admits that live television gives her the willies.

## The Cranberries Hiatus & Reunion

### The Cranberries went on hiatus because...

Says O'Riordan: "We all had little children. We all had personal challenges going on in our life. I wanted to know who the heck I was if I wasn't in a Cranberry."

### She wants to be "in a Cranberry" again because...

A performance with fellow 'Berries Noel and Mike Hogan at an event in Dublin in January proved to be a bit of a wake-up call: "It made me realize that I actually feel really comfortable with them, more so than anyone."

### The Cranberries will ripen...

The Cranberries tour is slated to kick off in November in North America. But it won't be an all-out greatest hits fest. Expect O'Riordan to mix her solo work into the set list. The band plans to work on a new album together in 2010.

## Oh, and Did You Know...?

O'Riordan was only 18 years old when the Cranberries found success with their debut album "Everybody Else Is Doing It, So Why Can't We?" Brothers Mike and Noel Hogan were 16 and 17, respectively.

The wind alone was enough to bring a tear to the eye. The harsh, acidic smoke blanketed the entirety of the Earth now, the results of pumping sulphur and carbon into our atmosphere were plain for all to see. The human population was plummeting, crops were failing and animals were dying. Action from the government was vitally too late, the thick yellow clouds were choking the life out of the Earth. Billions of years of evolution and life had been forced out of existence in the space of what? Two hundred years? Humans were now living for their own greed because we no longer served a useful purpose on this Earth, but of course some may argue we never served any purpose on this planet. There are barely enough people left to argue we did. The lucky ones have escaped this world to one of the many 'goldilocks' planets that scatter our galaxy, perfectly happy to spend many billions on a space craft but not to turn their lights off when leaving the living room. The remaining humans are either killing themselves or killing each other for what resources remain; I intend to enjoy what we have left of our home. Well done humans, well done.

I can't even remember getting out of bed, or the blue sky hanging overhead. I don't recall my eyes aching, or the quiet air, feeling bored, or even water trickling down a drain. Those are just descriptions, really, things I just seem to know. I know I was a janitor. And I guess I once had dreams of becoming a scientist, though that's not something I care about now. I also remember looking out my ridiculously small window and asking myself, "Is it just me, or is the sky red?" Wow, the past is really hazy.

Its been three months since Jenny Anderson was murdered. As you may very well know, she was the first person Truman Burbank killed. At first, the outside world was terrified. There was uproar to end the show and arrest Truman. Then...Then he killed Bob Dickenson. Some kid over at UCLA started a pool, taking bets on who Truman would kill next. An hour later, Jill Tucker ran down main street screaming in pain. She died on Pine street a few minutes later. The entire town, the entire cast got on our boats and sailed to the exit doors. No one was too surprised when we found them locked. At that point, it had been 18 hours since Truman began slowly killing anyone he found, and no help had came. All our outside communications had been cut off. And now the finality of it all, the locked doors. We became a game to them, just players on a betting slip. There aren't too many of us left now. We've been hiding for two weeks in Jacob Wallgreen's basement. I'm thinking about killing myself tomorrow. Not because i'm afraid, but because I refuse to go out as a source of entertainment for them. No one will be cheering on my death, sitting around the television eating microwave dinner with their loved ones. I want to go out with the little peace I have left. But you know what the sickest part of it all is? Someone, somewhere probably has$10K riding on me dying in a suicide attempt.

My Disappointing Experience with the Black & Decker Blender

About a month ago, I purchased a Black & Decker blender from a local store, hoping it would be the perfect addition to my kitchen. With a 550-watt motor, I expected it to handle simple tasks like making smoothies with ease. Unfortunately, that wasn't the case.

After using the blender just a handful of times, the motor suddenly stopped working. Thinking I had just received a faulty unit, I exchanged it for a new one at the store. Much to my frustration, the exact same thing happened—the motor died after a single use.

At that point, I decided enough was enough and returned the blender for a full refund. Based on my experience, I won’t be buying a Black & Decker blender again—and I’d encourage anyone considering it to think twice.

My initial exposure to approximately twenty seconds of Track One ("Any Day Now") during an episode of *Nip/Tuck* necessitated a multi-hour search to identify the band. The CD was subsequently ordered with expectations of discovering a limited number of appealing tracks; however, a comprehensive listening experience resulted in profound admiration. Such a significant emotional connection to music has not been experienced since my high school years, approximately thirteen years prior. Repeated listenings revealed specific tracks—notably numbers 1, 4, 6, and 10—as particularly compelling. This realization prompted self-reproach for my prior unfamiliarity with the band's work. The band adeptly synthesizes the most distinguished characteristics of Pink Floyd, Radiohead, and The Rapture, alongside an additional, unique quality that distinguishes them from any other group I have encountered. This album is highly recommended to individuals seeking emotionally resonant and evocative musical compositions that inspire profound sensory engagement.

The market bustled silently. Stalls in all sizes and colors displayed their wares. A pair in beautiful silken suits peered through the crowd, looking for a customer worthy of the polysyllabics kept locked in a glass case. A man in a tawdry suit hawked a meager selection of verbs. A specialist in technical jargon was set up beside a purveyor of romantics; they were both doing well today. A tramp tried to get a few cents apiece for his 'the's and 'and's. Most business was done through gesture and facial expression. Occasionally a tricky bit of haggling necessitated slate and chalk or notepad and pen. But nothing today was worthy of utterance. At noon the monks processed out from the Abbey, bringing the second of the days three shipments. They offloaded their produce to wholesalers, willing to take a lower price to save time. The Mandates had been a boon to the monastic types; they never really had much use for words anyway. The friars purchased a decent assortment of the still prized 'God' and 'thou' and 'art' and the like. The day wore on, evening saw the monks come with the third batch, and move on again, like clockwork. The vendors began to take down their stalls, some ahead for the day, some needing more tomorrow. A gate at the perimeter of the court opened, and a voice cut through the din like a knife. "you don't say?" "why yes, indeed." "I can't believe it" "Well you better believe it." The couple strode through the market, not giving it notice. Every other pair of eyes sharpened, and fixed on them. Heads slowly turning to follow. "I just refuse to admit that what you say there is true." "Alas, it is. I do not lie to you. The events exactly as I have laid them out to you." "But she would never do that, and he would never stand for it" "I cannot say something would never happen, especially so if the thing in question was just witnessed by me" "Oh you and your logic" They exited the other end of the courtyard. A young boy was tallying on his fingers. 84! he etched on his slate, and showed it around the crowd. Many shook their heads. Some spat. Others simply shrugged and continued on with the work. It was a rare display of wealth in this market. Always frustrating to see such richness accomplish absolutely nothing. But every seller knew these types were needed to stay afloat. More money than brains, and a big mouth to boot.

I've reviewed the text and found only one minor error:

**Line 14:** "requiring it to be listed" should be "requiring **them** to be listed"

The sentence currently reads: "...prompted by new nutritional labels introduced in 2006 requiring it to be listed on food packaging."

Since "trans fats" is plural, the pronoun should be "them" rather than "it."

Everything else in the text appears to be grammatically correct and properly spelled. The writing is clear and well-structured throughout.

Manchester United sent scouts to watch Brazilian attacking midfielder Roberto Firmino at Hoffenheim this weekend. The 23-year-old, who has been lighting up the German league, is in high demand, with United joining a growing list of Premier League clubs chasing his signature. Everton, Liverpool and Arsenal have all considered bids for the talented Brazilian who reportedly has a bargain release clause of just £14.5million. Sportsmail tells you everything you need to know about the Hoffenheim star.

Roberto Firmino celebrates scoring a late winner for his Hoffenheim side against Eintracht Frankfurt. Firmino scored the winning goal for Brazil in just his second international game against Austria last year.

Think of the most skillful player you've ever watched weaving through defenders - that's Firmino. In the past two seasons, only superstars like Lionel Messi and Eden Hazard have completed more successful dribbles in Europe's top leagues. With all the flair and fancy footwork you'd expect from a Brazilian international, Firmino is an attacking midfielder who knows where the goal is.

He's not your typical goal machine - his 6 goals in 25 games this season proves that - but when chances come his way, he rarely wastes them. Last season's impressive 16 goals earned him his first Brazil call-up, and he made an instant impact on the international stage with a spectacular 25-yard thunderbolt into the top corner against Austria in just his second game.

The Brazilian's shot from 25 yards flew into the top corner to earn him a first international goal. The Brazilian shows off his excellent first touch as he keeps the ball away from two Stuttgart defenders.

But it's not his goalscoring that would excite Louis van Gaal most - it's his ability to create magic for teammates. Firmino loves threading the perfect pass, slipping balls through to strikers from his position just behind them. His numbers speak for themselves: 18 league assists since last season started. 

To put that in perspective, that's more than Manchester United's entire midfield from their match against Tottenham - Juan Mata, Ander Herrera, Marouane Fellaini, Daley Blind and Michael Carrick - have managed combined in the Premier League over the past two seasons.

Usually playing as the link between midfield and attack, Firmino can also torment defenders out wide on the left, where his pace and quick feet make him a nightmare to mark. 

With superstars like Neymar, Coutinho, Oscar and Willian competing for his position, Firmino has done brilliantly to break into the Brazil squad. The playmaker holds off a tackle as he embarks on one of his trademark runs in a recent game.

What makes him special is his ability to hurt teams in multiple ways. Whether he's gliding past defenders with the ball glued to his feet or making clever runs into dangerous areas without it, he's always a threat. Playing wide left, he can cut inside and unleash shots with his preferred right foot, or use his bag of tricks to leave full-backs dizzy.

The big question is whether he's quick enough to thrive as a creative force in the Premier League's intense atmosphere, but his performances in Germany have caught the eye of some serious judges, including Bayern Munich's sporting director Mathias Sammer.

Firmino's dribbling, goals and assists have made him one of the hottest properties in the Bundesliga.

'Firmino is a great player,' said Sammer this week, though he ruled out Bayern making a move. But with the player, his agent, and Hoffenheim all admitting he could leave this season, Premier League clubs have been put on red alert for one of German football's most exciting talents.

I had an idea...I was going to jump from the rooftop of my tower block, because I was miserable, and honestly? I'd had enough. I locked myself away from the world, quivering at every thunderstorm, and jumping at every shadow. I became afraid of everything. The day came when I built up the courage to do it...I stood on the edge of the rooftop, looking down at the people below and thinking how meaningless their lives were: they were pieces on a board, all of them were being controlled by a higher power playing a vindictive game of chess. I jumped. But the fall took longer than I expected. When I opened my eyes I realised I had hardly moved, though I was falling through the air. I was wearing a parachute. I don't know how it got there, but that didn't matter, what mattered is that is WAS there. It had appeared as if by magic. Although I don't truly believe that, I think some part of me didn't want to die. I believe I was the one who put that parachute on, and simply made myself forget.

I cannot honestly recall how I came upon this album, but I can say that I'm very happy I did. *Ultraglide in Black*, the 2001 album by Detroit's The Dirtbombs, is one of those albums that you play at a party and everyone ends up asking you "Who is this?" because of its distinctive blend of raw garage rock, punk energy, and deep soul, often featuring an unusual lineup of two drummers and two bassists alongside Mick Collins' guitar. Mick Collins' voice will remind you of a decade like the 1960s, echoing classic rhythm and blues and garage rock singers, but the pace and chord progression will remind you that this album, released in 2001, is only 3 years old. If you have any passing interest in groups of old like The Spencer Davis Trio with their R&B-infused rock, The Animals with their gritty blues-rock energy, or even a little Stevie Wonder thrown in for his soulful delivery and melodic flair; this album is a safe bet. *Ultraglide in Black* is without a doubt stronger towards the beginning, but the latter part is still enjoyable enough. Check out The Mooney Suzuki too if this suits your fancy, as they were another prominent band in the early 2000s garage rock revival scene.

There was a child. Born in the streets of Detroit. His mother was fifteen. She left him in a trash can. As he was covered in shifting garbage, the air became lacking. He could feel the suffocation, the lack of oxygen forcing him from a world in which he was not wanted. He could feel this. A sort of longing to be elsewhere, a dream to be with or around someone, anyone, who would hold him, feed him, keep his tiny, malnourished body warm. He just wanted to be alive, this small pink mass. He simply felt, more than anything, that he wanted to be...loved. Everything went dark. "Don't worry Marie,"a teary-eyed man said."This isn't the end of the road for us." His wife smiled wearily, rings around her eyes pushed up slightly by her rising cheeks. "Damn it, Gerome...." They met by accident. Marie was on a diet, and couldn't stop thinking about deep dish pizza. Gerome was working at the local pizzeria, the late night shift when she appeared in the kitchen. She always said it was the best way she could have used her Jump. He, on the other hand, had used his long before he could remember. His parents say he must have been born without one. He'd always asked how he could use it, how they had used theirs, or even if it happens at a certain age in their family. His brothers and sisters all had the ability, the birthmark on the left wrist that everyone able was born with. His mother never did, and his father cashed his in back in Vietnam to escape the killing fields. He wondered if his child would be born without one. He and Marie had been trying for a child for three years, every hopeful attempt resulting in a stillbirth. Each time seemed so full of promise. Regardless, he had to stay strong because his wife may not have ever said it, but she was falling apart inside. She needed him to keep her going. But she wanted a baby. He hugged his wife, and held her close. Even if they never had a son, a daughter or whatever, they were still a family. And they would always be. "Marie," he whispered. "How does adoption sound?"

He steps into the bank with his hands in his overcoat pockets, eyes scanning the room rapidly. He casually made his way to the line in front of the tellers desk and pulled out a check and began to sign his name when he suddenly stopped after the first letter. "G." The teller looked at him and asked if something is wrong. A slow, predatory smile spread across his face, and then, without warning, gunfire ripped through the sudden, stunned silence of the bank. The teller came to her senses, feeling a sudden, sickening warmth spreading across her chest. She looked down to see a gaping bullet wound gushing blood. Her vision blurred, fading, as she looked up to see the man still holding a submachine gun, casually spraying bullets into the terrified crowd, that same predatory smile fixed on his face. The police arrive within 5 minutes of the alarm being triggered. The man reached into his pocket and pulled out a .45 Magnum. With a swift flick of his wrist he fired a bullet, striking the first responder squarely in the cranium – a sickening crack – and a second, instantly felling another officer. He figured this would give him a slight window so he made his way to the bank vault to take his score. After about 90 seconds he had pulled a third of the total funds and had made his way back to the main room to see a large line of police cars and a SWAT team all with their sights on the bank's entrance. He chuckled, then reached for his back, pulling out a large belt-fed machine gun. He charged for the door, bullets already tearing through the windows and into the officers waiting outside. He stepped out of the building rolling a grenade directly towards the armada of police cars. It skittered under a car near the center of the barricade, exploding. The car erupted in a fiery explosion, shrapnel ripping through the air and incapacitating many officers. During the confusion of the blast the man began spraying bullets once again, tearing through the men in uniform. The man dropped the machine gun and walked through the pile of bodies and bullet-riddled cars with that same sinister smile the teller had seen. A police officer swept from behind a car, the man's head squarely in his sights. "Fre," Before the officer could finish a single word the man pulled the SMG out once again, a torrent of lead erupting, starting from the officer's foot and tracing a brutal, upward path to his throat. The officer stood there frozen in time, feeling the life slip from him. The man fired one more shot into the officer's head. He hit the ground immediately after. The man threw his gun to the ground and turned, flipping his coat tail dramatically. "You can't catch me. I'm the Gingerbread Man."

Dear god, I was exhausted. It was like this every single day now, since I quit smoking. Just the lack of nicotine made my day drag on, and as seventeen high school senior, it was already long enough. I don't know why today was extra long though- perhaps it was the anticipation for the date that I had this afternoon with the girl sitting across from me in my bio class. She gave me a soft smile from across the black lab table as she continued to write down- whatever the hell we were writing. I think we were supposed to be writing notes from the book. I'd worry about it later. I returned the smile with a lazy blink of my eyes and flopped back down on my folded arms. "Tired Brandon?" Keely asked with a raised eyebrow, a small smirk on her soft lips. "Exhausted."I replied with another sigh."But- not to tired for this afternoon. Sleeping in the rest of my classes should rest me up just fine." She giggled at that, a short little high pitched giggle. Everyone else was talking, so I suppose no one else heard her cute little laugh to appreciate it. "Alright, alright class."Mr. Scott said at the front of the room before turning off the lights and pointing at the power point."Listen up." I did...Just the opposite. The class got silent, and it didn't take long before my eyes shut and I was asleep. I dreamed of long-boarding with the long brown haired girl in front of me- nothing too detailed, just a brief little thought before I am abruptly awoken by the sound of a large book being slammed on the table in front of me. I shot back up in my chair in an instant, *"THE MITOCHONDRIA IS THE POWER HOUSE OF THE CELL."* The room went silent, and you could hear a pin drop. My eye twitched as I glanced around the room with a sigh- well, shit, this is embarrassing.

The market buzzed with a quiet energy. Stalls of every size and color showed off their goods. Two people in fancy silk suits scanned the crowd, hoping to find a buyer for the impressive, multi-syllable words they kept under glass. A guy in a cheap suit was trying to sell his small collection of verbs. A technical jargon expert set up shop next to a romance seller; both seemed to be having a good day. A tramp was trying to sell 'the's and 'and's for a few cents each. Most deals happened with just gestures and facial expressions. Sometimes, if the haggling got tough, they'd pull out a slate and chalk or a notepad and pen. But honestly, nothing today was worth actually saying aloud.

At noon, the monks from the Abbey made their way out, bringing the second of their three daily shipments. They dropped off their words to wholesalers, who were happy to pay a bit less to save time. The Mandates had been great for the monks; they never really needed many words themselves. The friars bought a good selection of the still-popular 'God,' 'thou,' and 'art,' and words like those. The day went on. In the evening, the monks arrived with the third batch, moving like clockwork. Vendors started packing up their stalls. Some had done well enough, others would need more luck tomorrow.

Then a gate at the edge of the courtyard swung open, and voices sliced through the quiet buzz like a knife. "You don't say!" "Oh, but I do!" "I can't believe it!" "Well, you better start believing it!" The couple walked right through the market, not even glancing at it. Every other eye in the market sharpened, locking onto them. Heads slowly turned to follow their progress. "I just refuse to admit that what you're saying is true." "Honestly, it is. I'm not lying to you. It happened exactly as I told you." "But she would never do that, and he'd never stand for it!" "I can't say something would *never* happen, especially when I just saw it myself!" "Oh, you and your logic!" They walked out the other side of the courtyard. A young boy was counting on his fingers. "84!" he scribbled on his slate and showed it around the crowd. Lots of people shook their heads. Some spat. Others just shrugged and went back to work. It was rare to see such a show of wealth here. Always frustrating to watch all that richness amount to absolutely nothing. But every seller knew they needed these types to stay in business. All money and no sense, with a big mouth on top of it.

Up to Â£6m of extra cash has been requested for the Carmarthen building, BBC Wales revealed on Thursday.
Arfon AM Sian Gwenllian said the case for building it in Caernarfon, Gwynedd, should now be reconsidered.
"I was under the impression that the move would be cost-neutral. It appears that the Carmarthen bid no longer fits that description," she said.
University of Wales Trinity St David is behind the Yr Egin project in Carmarthen, where the Welsh language broadcaster will be based from 2018.
It has bid for a grant of between Â£4m and Â£6m from the Welsh Government.
The AM said Gwynedd council's bid was "still a valid contender" which had to be considered in light of this request.
She added: "The Caernarfon bid was very strong. The industry is embedded in the area and locating the headquarters here would be a huge boost for the language and economy."
University of Wales Trinity St David said it was "exploring a number of funding options", including discussions with the Welsh Government.

He carried with him only his own hands, and what he knew of the creature he was preparing to fight. It was a great beast, the size of buildings, but able to squeeze into the smallest spaces. It was as immaterial as smoke, able to slip its tendrils under doors and through windows, no matter how tightly shut they might be. It killed its victims by drowning them in smoke, or becoming so heavy on their chests that they were suffocated by its bulk. It attacked indiscriminately, taking the young and old, the strong and weak, the rich and poor. It was an efficient killer, taking those it killed so quietly that their families slept peacefully in the next room. Lovers went to sleep with their loves, only to wake up holding a cold hand. Mothers went to sleep after tucking their children in, to wake up forever in mourning. Husbands woke up widowers, and wives woke up widows. The beast was black, so black that it sucked the light out of rooms and blotted out the stars. And the hero fought it. He fought it barehanded, fought it until he bled, until he was so exhausted he slept for days. Fought it with every muscle in his body. And beat it. Over and over. But it came back. It came back bigger and blacker. So he began again, bloodied and exhausted, but not beaten. And again and again, he beat the beast. He clawed his way out of the blackness, expelled the smoke from his lungs, pushed it off his chest. Some battles were easier than others. Some were hard. Sometimes it seemed impossible. But the hero still fought. My best friend v. Depression /r/nymphadora

The Star Traveler passed through the solar system in a matter of decades, but it seemed only the briefest of flashes to the great beast, its thoughts crawling through its gargantuan mind like glaciers. It was a necessary slowness, one meant to sustain life in the epochs between feeding times in the great clouds, but still it thought, especially in the warmth of a star. It was in this quickening of consciousness that it noticed the third, strange, little world. The Traveler's great eye had first noticed the strange web which appeared every orbit on the world's dark side. It was something it had never seen, not once among the countless wonders the Traveler had sailed past. It watched as the world grew closer. It saw great flying shapes criss-crossing the globe every day, leaving their long white trails across the world's sky. It was these that the Traveler had first assumed were the world's inhabitants, and ones it empathized with, for it was flying its own journey across a grander sky. But as it grew even closer, its mile long sides warming in the fusion glow of the local star, the great eye saw finer detail. First great trails of shiny ground crawlers, slowly oozing along their paths, but then even closer inspection revealed the strange truth of the world. Neither the light web, nor the flying shapes, nor the ground crawlers were the world's inhabitants, they were servants to another. Small and incredibly short lived creatures swarmed over ever surface of the world. They fought and died almost pointlessly, but they were not powerless. The whale watched as the small beings shaped their world, molding their surrounds to their own purposes. Such a strange thing, thought the whale, to use the world to shape the world. Eventually, the Traveler, a slave to its own path across the heavens, began to withdraw from the strange little planet, and it lamented the sad little beings limited lives. To be trapped on a single world, even with nigh infinite power over it, never to see anything but that world again and again, was the most empty and sad existence the Traveler had ever contemplated. It wished the little beings well, for it could feel nothing but pity for them. Its skin growing cold once more, the Traveler felt a deep slumber encroaching on its thoughts, but it did not worry. Soon it would wake, and see something new.

David Cumming, who manages stocks for Standard Life, said oil prices needed to be over $60, not the current $33.
He said Standard Life would vote against the deal when Shell's owners decide on it later this month.
But Shell still believes it will win the vote.
A Shell spokesman said: "We still think enough of our owners will back the deal for it to happen."
Shell's shares went down 0.8% to Â£13.64 when the stock market opened on Monday, while BG's shares fell 1.3% to Â£9.27.
Shell announced it planned to buy BG - a company that looks for oil and gas - in April 2015 for Â£47bn.
But Mr Cumming said the deal is not good because oil prices might drop more, and there are money risks with BG's projects in Brazil.
"Our issue with the deal is that much has changed since the offer was made in April last year - and all of it for the worse," he told the BBC's Today programme.
"Oil is currently $33, and Shell still needs it to be well over $60 for the deal to make money," he said on Monday.
Shell and BG owners will vote at different meetings on 27 and 28 January.
Mr Cumming said Standard Life would not want Shell's boss, Ben van Beurden, to lose his job if the deal didn't happen.
"We think he's doing a good job. It's just the deal we don't support. We must prioritize making money over being loyal to the management in this situation, and we advise other owners to do the same," Mr Cumming said.
Standard Life owns the 11th most Shell B shares, with a 1.7% share.
Shell B shares are part of the payment in the deal, which involves both cash and shares, and is expected to finish on 15 February.
Standard Life is also the 16th biggest owner in BG, based on Bloomberg's information.
Shell got the backing of Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS), an important group that advises investors, which said on Friday that Shell's owners should support the deal.
Another important advisory group, Glass Lewis, also gave its advice late on Friday, agreeing with ISS that owners should vote for the deal.
ISS, which advises about 5% of Shell's medium and small owners, said it backed the deal because of its "strong business plan and the big money benefits that will come fairly quickly."
Glass Lewis, which advises 12 of Shell's top 50 owners, said the deal "could greatly improve money results and create much more value for owners."
After the deal, Shell will be the world's biggest seller of liquefied natural gas.
In December, Shell said it would cut 2,800 jobs because it was reorganizing the companies into one.

Two fishermen were left stranded after a strong tidal current pulled their vehicle, boat and trailer  into the crocodile infested creek. The men were trying to recover their boat at the ramp  after returning from an evening fishing trip at Buffalo Creek Boat Ramp in Darwin's northern suburbs on Sunday. One reversed the boat trailer down the ramp while the other waited in the boat. But the driver had left the 2012 Nissan Navara four-wheel drive ute in neutral with the handbrake on as he got out and the car and trailer were pulled down the ramp and into the water. Two recreational fishermen were left stranded after their vehicle, boat and trailer submerged into the creek . With the ongoing current, the vehicle and trailer were taken downstream and swept through the inhabit area of salt water crocodiles. Casuarina Police, who were patrolling the area at the time, were flagged down by the hapless men shortly before 2am. When police arrived, the vehicle had travelled about 50 metres away from the boat ramp with just the roof of the ute visible above the surface while the trailer had submerged. A Northern Territory police spokeswoman told Daily Mail Australia that the owner is recovering his vehicle and trailer on Monday but could not confirm how he was going to retrieve it. The men were returning from a fishing trip at Buffalo Creek Boat Ramp in Darwin's northern suburbs . The vehicle had travelled about 50 metres away from the boat ramp with just the roof of the ute visible . Police said the location was known for its strong tidal currents and salt water crocodiles . The men were taken home by police after they were unable to recover the stricken vehicle and trailer but the pair has since recovered their boat after borrowing a friend's trailer. Police said one of the officers who attended the incident, jokingly told the fishermen that the accident could have been prevented if they owned a Toyota Hilux instead of a Navara. Water police attended the area on Monday morning and attached a buoyant to act as a warning to people in the area to avoid the submerged car and trailer as it could pose as a navigational hazard. Police said the location was known for its strong tidal currents and salt water crocodiles and urged residents to take extra care and vigilance when launching and recovering  vessel in Territory waters.

**New Publication Explores Digital Simulations as Catalyst for Ethical Teaching Development**

A compelling chapter in the recently released *Digital Simulations for Improving Education: Learning Through Artificial Teaching Environments* (IGI Global, 2009) examines the transformative potential of digital gaming technologies in cultivating ethical reasoning within educational settings.

In their comprehensive analysis, "Using Digital Games to Develop Ethical Teachers," Columbia University professors Karen Schrier and Charles K. Kinzer present a rigorous framework for leveraging interactive digital environments to enhance moral decision-making skills among educators. Drawing from extensive case studies of both commercial and purpose-built educational games, the authors articulate practical strategies for developing future simulations specifically designed to strengthen ethical competencies in teaching professionals.

While acknowledging the current limitations of existing digital platforms for nuanced ethical exploration, Schrier and Kinzer express optimism that their research will catalyze the creation of sophisticated learning environments capable of fostering ethically grounded educators. Their work represents a significant contribution to the evolving discourse on technology-enhanced professional development in education.

---

*Scott Aronowitz is a freelance technology journalist based in Las Vegas, specializing in the intersection of digital innovation, media, and entertainment. With seven years of industry coverage, his work explores emerging trends in educational technology and digital learning environments.*

Two women were apparently "taken against their will", by a man they knew, to an address in Kingston Upon Thames on Wednesday night, police said.
They were violently attacked, resulting in the death of one of the women. The other woman escaped and was treated for stab or slash wounds in hospital.
Two men have been arrested on suspicion of murder.
Officers visited the women's addresses in Sutton and Merton following a concerned call about their safety at about 17:00 BST on Wednesday.
They found no trace of either woman when they visited but were informed by a south London hospital at 19:18 that a woman in her 20s was being treated for stab or slash wounds.
Police went to a residential property in Coombe Lane West, Kingston Upon Thames, at about 20:00 where they discovered the body of the other woman, who was believed to be aged 19.
Her next of kin have been informed and post-mortem tests will be carried out in due course.
"Our investigation is at an early stage, and we are doing everything we can to fully understand the circumstances of this terrible attack on two young women," Det Ch Insp Samantha Price said.
"It is clear that both women were subject to a violent attack, resulting in the death of one woman.
"Fortunately one of the women managed to get away from the address and seek help, how this happened will form part of this investigation."
A 33-year-old man has been arrested in Kent on suspicion of murder. A 28-year-old man was stopped in New Malden, south west London, and is being held on suspicion of murder.
Police also arrested a 29-year-old woman on suspicion of assisting an offender and she was released on Thursday pending further enquiries.
