The heated reactions to Duncan's comments are drowning out what should be a heartfelt conversation about something every parent cares deeply about: whether our children are truly getting the education they deserve.


**When Our Children Are Failing, We All Feel It**


I can't know exactly what Duncan meant, but here's what I suspect happened: For years, when test scores revealed that disadvantaged students were struggling, many communities felt sympathy—but also a sense of distance. "That's not our problem," some may have thought. But when Common Core tests showed that even high-performing students weren't meeting expectations, suddenly every parent felt that gut punch of worry.


The strongest opposition to Common Core emerged in communities where parents discovered their children—kids they'd been told were doing great—were actually falling short. In New York, when test results showed less than one-third of students meeting grade-level expectations, you could feel the collective gasp of parents who thought their schools were just fine.


**The Uncomfortable Truth We All Need to Face**


Duncan's comments were hurtful and divisive when we needed unity. But here's the reality that affects every family: We've been setting the bar too low for too long, and our children are paying the price.


Picture this: Your teenager graduates high school, walks across that stage, and you're bursting with pride. Then they get to college and discover they're not prepared. They're placed in remedial courses—essentially having to relearn what they should have mastered in high school. It's crushing for any parent to watch their child struggle because the system failed them.


The statistics are heartbreaking: Up to 40% of four-year college students need remedial courses. At community colleges, it's over 50%. Even worse, most of these students never make it to graduation. Only 27% of those needing remedial math complete college; just 17% of those in remedial English graduate.


**Every Parent's Shared Hope**


What Duncan missed—and what his unfortunate comments obscured—is that every parent, regardless of zip code or background, wants the same thing: for their child to be challenged, supported, and truly prepared for whatever comes next.


Instead of creating divisions, this moment could unite us. Urban and suburban parents alike are discovering that our children deserve better. We all want schools that don't just make us feel good with inflated grades and false praise, but actually prepare our kids for real success.


The question isn't whether some parents are being unreasonable. The question is: Are we brave enough to have an honest conversation about giving every child—every single one—access to the rigorous, meaningful education they need to thrive?

**I. Unique Trilobite Discovery: Evidence of Ancient Predation**

Czech scientists have found unique specimens of trilobites that suggest something thought to be impossible – something was eating them. The small animals were amongst the most successful in history, crawling along the seabed for more than 270 million years. But during the Cambrian era, 500 million years ago, when neither fish nor cephalopods had developed, there should have been nothing capable of sinking its teeth into their hard shells. At a recent conference of international palaeontologists in Prague, Dr Oldřich Fatka of the Institute of Geology and Palaeontology presented evidence from more than thirty years of study, showing that even 508 million years ago there seems to have been a predator on the sea floor.

**II. The Evidence: Traces of Lethal Attacks**

“Our research focused on specimens that showed specific features: there are, in some rare cases, preserved traces of attacks on the trilobites. So, in our presentation we showed about eight of the twelve available specimens that show attacks from the right or left side, just behind the head. There, three to six thoracic segments are missing, and these are the traces of the attack. The attacks were lethal, killing the trilobite. These specimens showing attacks are very spectacular because the animals which are usually the supposes eaters and attackers of trilobites are still absent in Cambrian rock.”

**III. Identifying the Mysterious Predator**

Do we have any idea what such a predator might have looked like?

“There were no fish, no cephalopods at that time. We have some speculative ideas, for instance the so-called anomalocaridids. Anomalocaridids are a group that is outside of our recent systems of biology. They are very typical of the Cambrian sediments, and they are interpreted as planktonic, or benthic, animals, reaching very large dimensions for their time – more than 100 centimetres in length. And they are interpreted by some authors as potential scavengers and even animals that were able to attack other living organisms. Therefore, we suppose that one of the candidates for the animals responsible for the wounds on the trilobites are anomalocaridid-like animals, some smaller form than the typical genus that were benthic – meaning they did not swim well but crawled above the water sediment interface and tried to find something to eat. So one of the potential animals are anomalocaridid-like types of organisms.”

**IV. Future Research and Challenges**

Where does your research go from here and how will you clarify the situation?

“We will try to find more wounded trilobites to see, for instance, if already at that time there was a preference for the right or left side (about 20 years ago it was discussed that the Burgess shale showed a preference for the right side). So we will try to find the animals that could have been responsible for these attacks, even in the Czech Republic. We are not sure if we will be successful, because these organisms that are supposed to be the attackers, were not well skeletonised – meaning they are very rarely preserved, not only here but worldwide. So we have some traces that could be interpreted as an anomalocaridid-like animal, but we would like to find more, and we would like to find more in layers in which the wounded trilobites occur. In that case we could say ‘okay, here we have the most probably guys responsible for the attacks’.”

Franz Ferdinand will make their debut at the three-day event staged near Beauly in the Highlands.
The festival is set for 3 to 5 August 2017.
Organisers have confirmed Scots singer KT Tunstall will support Franz Ferdinand. Seventies disco legends Sister Sledge will headline on the Thursday night.
Promoter Joe Gibbs declared: "This marks Franz Ferdinand's highly anticipated debut at Bella, and we are absolutely thrilled to welcome them to our stage.
"There is no better act to close out the festival.
"We are also thrilled to welcome back KT Tunstall. Having previously graced our stage as an unannounced special guest, we are now proud to feature her officially on the bill."

Location: Fruit and Vegetable Insect Research
Project Number: 2092-21220-001-04
Start Date: Jun 29, 2011
End Date: May 31, 2016
Developing potato varieties with elevated levels of antioxidants—such as phenolics, vitamin C, anthocyanins, and carotenoids—will significantly enhance their nutritional value. A comprehensive understanding of the mechanisms influencing phytonutrient levels in tubers—encompassing both in planta and external environmental factors, including post-harvest conditions and crop management—is critical to developing high-phytonutrient potatoes. These factors will be rigorously addressed through a combination of physiological, biochemical, and molecular techniques, specifically employing metabolic profiling, gene expression analysis, metabolic engineering, and enzyme analysis.

Ditto Denise's review; like her, I also struggled to find a compatible charger for my Powerwheels L'il Quad, despite its common single 6-volt system. I was concerned this charger, advertised for 6V Powerwheels, might not fit after reading various online reviews from other frustrated parents who experienced connector incompatibility. Despite my efforts to verify the charger's compatibility with my specific L'il Quad model, I was unable to get anywhere with the Powerwheels website, which lacked detailed connector information, or their customer service, which was unresponsive. So, taking a chance, I ordered the charger. Upon arrival, I confirmed my fears: the charging plug simply did not connect to the battery port, even when attempting to use the included adapter. Frustrated, I drove 25 miles to their specified service dealer. There, I finally got an explanation: if your L'il Quad has a BLUE BATTERY, it signifies an older or specific manufacturing run with a different, proprietary charging port, rendering this standard 6V charger incompatible. The dealer is now ordering me the correct charger, as it seems to be the only way to obtain one. This entire process has been incredibly annoying, marked by a lack of clear product information and wasted time and effort.

My first death? Yeah, I regret that one the most. You know that old saying? The one about how everyone who survives a fall wishes they hadn't jumped, that things weren't really *that* bad? Turns out, they were totally right.

Picture this: a tower block in Central London, a teenager convinced he was depressed, that the whole world was against him, and a concrete floor way, way down. The second I jumped, pure regret hit me like a ton of bricks. My family problems? Totally solvable. My life? Not half as bad as I thought. That fall gave me instant clarity.

And then... *smash*. I hit the ground.

Let me tell you, there's nothing after life. No heaven, no hell, no redemption, no 72 virgins. Not even blackness or darkness. Just... nothing. A total, absolute absence of everything. The best way I've found to describe it? Close your eyes as tight as you can, then roll them back as far into your head as they'll go. It's not black, it's just... well, it's really hard to explain.

Waking up in that hospital? Absolutely surreal. Bright lights, figures in white... for a split second, I actually thought the Christians were right. God, angels, the whole package. But then my sight focused. Nope, just a hospital.

I found out later I'd been dead for 37 minutes. Massive head trauma, collapsed ribs, ruptured internal organs. There's just no way you survive a thirty-story fall onto concrete. I can't remember how much blood I lost, but I'm pretty sure it was *most* of it. Nobody could explain it. They just stared, dumbfounded, as I opened my eyes and my bones started knitting themselves back together.

After a few days of tests – all inconclusive, by the way – they just let me go. I'd supposedly done the impossible.

But dying, weirdly enough, brought me *back* to the world. It gave me this renewed drive, this laser focus. So I joined the Royal Engineers, trained hard for a few years. Then came Afghanistan, June 2012. I was in the front Humvee, part of a convoy moving some heavy artillery, when we hit an IED.

No human should survive a massive metal spike through the chest. And yet, somehow, I did. After dying for 37 minutes *again*.

That's when I got my reputation as a lucky fucker. They nicknamed me 'Dice,' and I was never allowed to gamble with the guys again. And honestly, I didn't blame them. Nobody knew about my first 'fall,' and I certainly wasn't sharing *that*. Nobody needed to know.

But *I* knew. And I suspected. I wanted proof. Cold, hard evidence. I had no dependents, my parents had died a couple of years prior – drunk driver, underage, stolen car. That hit me hard, made me throw myself into my work even more.

So, yeah, I bit the bullet. Literally. Shot myself through the head. A 9mm Parabellum isn't exactly 'friendly' when you put it through your mouth and brain, let me tell you. But I did it in private, off duty, off base, away from everyone and everything.

And I woke up.

Again, 37 minutes of absolute nothing. My head was somehow in one piece, though covered in blood and... well, brain tissue. Just a vaguely circular bald patch on top, and that was it. I didn't die. Maybe I *couldn't* die. I feared that more than anything. And turns out, I was right to.

I didn't age. Never grew fat. Diseases would hit, then just… vanish, never to return. I had no fear, because honestly, what was there to be afraid of?

It gets so incredibly lonely. I can't even remember the last time I spoke to another human being. Years. Years and years ago. Everyone else is dead now. Nobody else had this... 'immunity' I've got. Some tried to cross the stars before our sun went supernova. But the Earth withered and died, and I was just left behind. Alone, on this pathetic rock, just floating through space. Unable to die.

Eventually, this planet will fall into the sun. What happens then? I have no idea. Maybe I'll just keep floating through space, immune to everything. I don't know. All I know is... I just want to die.

Keith Davies, a former Carmarthenshire councillor, was elected to the assembly in 2011.
Mr Davies, 75, said it had been "a privilege to represent my home constituency" in a statement released on Friday.
He added: "I believe now is the right time for the next generation to have the opportunity to represent the Llanelli constituency."
He won the Llanelli seat for Labour by just 80 votes over Plaid Cymru in 2011.
Responding to the announcement, First Minister Carwyn Jones, said: "His breadth of knowledge and experience will be sorely missed, not just in the Labour group of AMs, but in the National Assembly more generally.
"I wish Keith all the best for his health and his future."
Mr Davies suffered a serious illness in 2012, when he was treated for a blood clot on the brain.
In May 2012, an official report found Mr Davies had brought the assembly into disrepute following a late-night drunken row at a Cardiff hotel, for which he apologised.

Gabe Olaseni led the scoring for GB with 13 points, Dan Clark added 12 and Kavell Bigby-Williams 11 as they showed tenacity to stay in the game amidst the echoing cheers and national anthems in Patras’ brightly lit indoor arena. The contest unfolded under the glow of arena lights that shimmered off polished wooden courts, filled with the rhythmic beats of dribbling and the sharp squeaks of sneakers.

Greece established a double-digit lead at the end of the first quarter and then held a spirited GB team at bay, the noise from the local fans swelling with each home basket. Flags and banners in blue and white rippled in the stands as the hosts kept control.

After trailing 66-51 at the end of three quarters, GB scored 13 points in a messy final quarter—loose passes and hurried shots echoed the tension—while a humid August evening pressed in from outside.

Greece, one of the strongest teams in Europe, demonstrated their depth even without NBA star Giannis Antetokounmpo, who watched from the sidelines due to a knee injury. His absence was still felt; the crowd often glanced up to the VIP seats, hoping for a glimpse of their sidelined hero.

The hosts, fueled by a boisterous Greek crowd, scored 15 of the first 20 points in the game, but GB trailed just 17-21 at the end of the first quarter as newcomers Bigby-Williams and Jules Dang Akodo combined for six consecutive points, their energy momentarily quieting the pounding drums in the stands.

Greece took control of the game in the second quarter with a 16-5 run, the scoreboard glowing above the court as the home team’s sharp shooting sent the crowd into waves of applause. Clark's shooting steadied GB, pulling them back from a daunting 26-point deficit to 51-34 down at the interval, as the halftime buzzer echoed across the packed, humid arena.

The deficit at the end of the third quarter was similar (66-51) but any thought of a comeback ended when Greece scored the first nine points of the fourth period, cruising to a slightly flattering victory as GB, visibly tiring, ran out of steam beneath the relentless noise of the home supporters.

GB's next game comes on Sunday, 13 August when they play Israel in Tel-Aviv, shifting to a different climate and atmosphere, before facing Greece again in London in a friendly at the Copper Box in London on Saturday, 19 August.

GB head coach Joe Prunty said in the post-game press conference, backed by the soft hum of arena staff packing up equipment: "I think we had our opportunities where we got the lead down to maybe 14. I thought we competed. [The young players] are coming in and proving they want to be part of it, and there's a lot of people who do, so you have to give the people who are getting their first caps a lot of credit."

GB top scorer Olaseni added, sweat still glistening on his forehead under the fluorescent lights: "It's just development and progression—the effort was there, we just need to execute a little better. My team-mates do a great job of getting me open, I just have to be able to finish."

Great food, can be a bit overwhelming to order with so many options to I met a friend here for lunch and we shared.  I tried the buffalo balsamic maple tenders and she got naked popcorn chicken so we could try some of the dips.  For medium you get 2 dips included.  Overall was sooo good.  We also got the truffle fries which were great, especially when dipped.   We both want to go again to try more.  Would go during lunch or daytime since the college students pretty much take over in the evenings after about 3.  Limited seating as well so you may have to share a table and be friendly with strangers like we did.  Staff super helpful and so clean! Also some standing bar tables to use.  Highly recommend for a quick bite

Zinedine Zidane could be banned from coaching for six months if found guilty of managing Real Madrid’s B-team without the correct license. Spain’s national association of coaches CENAFE made a complaint about the former World Cup winner at the start of the season and now the country’s Competitions Committee is studying the possibility that Real Madrid have broken rule 104 of their code which could lead to a ban of between four and 16 games or one to six months. Real Madrid Castilla – as the B-team is known – have claimed that Sergio Sanchez is the first coach and Zidane only his number two. But the Frenchman’s accusers have presented documental and video evidence to suggest that this is not the case and that the former Real Madrid player is carrying out the work of the first coach. Real Madrid B coach Zinedine Zidane could face a ban  if found guilty of managing without the correct license . Zidane attends the Predator Arena tournament in Arles, Southern France earlier this week . First coaches in Spain need to have a level 3 national coaching diploma and Zidane, who this week admitted he would one day like to coach the French national team, is still to obtain the relevant qualification. Madrid’s B-team have suffered in recent years with relegation to the third tier of Spanish football last season and they are currently third from bottom of Group II of the Second division B having lost three of their first four games. The president of CENAFE Miguel Galán, the body that made the original complaint, said: 'Real Madrid is one of the most recognisable sporting institutions in the world and a symbol of this sport but they have to follow the same rules as everyone else otherwise the competition is not fair.' Zidane (left) was Real Madrid manager Carlo Ancelotti's assistant during the 2013-14 season .

The 76-year-old cookery expert confirmed she had had two auditions for the show which switches to Channel 4 this year.
Leith previously spent 11 years as judge on BBC Two's The Great British Menu.
Channel 4 said it would be announcing the programme's line-up "in due course".
Speaking at a charity event on Tuesday, Leith said she was "certainly one of the runners" to be a judge on the show alongside Paul Hollywood, the only member of the current on-screen team who has chosen to stay with the show.
"I can wish. I can dream," she said.  "I've had two auditions with them and lots of meetings.
"So I mean I think I'm close but I know there are two people in the running. One other person.
"Of course I'd love to do it. Who wouldn't want to do it?"
She added: "I've known Mary [Berry] for years and she loved it."
Leith's agent confirmed to the BBC that she was interviewed for a job on the show at the end of last month.
But she said neither she nor Leith had received an offer from the show's producers so far.
The Great British Bake Off has been without one of its judges since Berry, 81, confirmed she would not be following the show to its new home.
Co-hosts Mel Giedroyc and Sue Perkins also said they would not be "going with the dough", leaving Hollywood as the lone survivor from the BBC's version.
Born in South Africa in 1940, Prudence "Prue" Leith learned to cook at Le Cordon Bleu in London and opened her first restaurant in 1969.
She was made an OBE in 1989, a CBE in 2010 and is the chancellor of Queen Margaret University in Edinburgh.
Follow us on Facebook, on Twitter @BBCNewsEnts, or on Instagram at bbcnewsents. If you have a story suggestion email entertainment.news@bbc.co.uk.

East Kent Hospitals University Trust said there has been a surge of patients at its emergency departments, putting pressure on resources.
The trust, which runs hospitals in Ashford, Margate and Canterbury, is advising people to seek help elsewhere.
It said most of the extra visitors are older people or have lung problems.
Julian Brazier, MP for Canterbury and Whitstable, plans to meet hospital officials on Friday to assess the seriousness of the situation.
He said: "I would support the appeal from the hospitals that those people who can go and see their GP instead or find another route, [should] do so."
The trust estimates that between 15% and 25% of people attending accident and emergency departments could be treated by another NHS service.
Jonathon Hawkins, the trust's divisional medical director for urgent care, said: "Our hospitals across east Kent are extremely busy dealing with many patients who are seriously ill, and we've seen a sustained rise in A&E attendances over the last week or two.
"So much so that it is now getting to a point where there's an imbalance between the demand at the front door and the ability to discharge patients back home.
"There's a multiplicity of reasons - winter pressures have come early, and our local picture mirrors that nationally."
He added: "Our A&E departments have very robust plans to deal with the most seriously ill patients and those with life-threatening illnesses, but many patients should be able to seek advice for those conditions that aren't life-threatening or serious, using other resources."
As Kent hospital bosses issued their plea to patients, Prime Minister David Cameron was again defending the coalition's record on NHS spending.
He told Prime Minister's Questions there were now 3,000 extra nurses and 8,000 additional doctors under his government.

Let's just say I never had much interest in the family business. To work all-night, to lose sleep over what I do, and to receive little in return always seemed to be my dad's cup o'tea, but I never really got the taste for it. Yet somehow, I find myself here on top of my perch, **the city a sprawling, indifferent tapestry of lights below,** for god knows how long, with a red bull in one hand and my rifle in the other. See, what my dad and I do, in a way, is put people to sleep. Dad, or Morpheus-as they used to call him back in the day- liked to get up close, really get his hands dirty with the work. I, on the other hand prefer to do my work from afar. We've become indifferent to the world below—**the city's endless thrum a low, distant pulse beneath us** – all that matters is that our higher ups are satisfied with our work, and those we took care of truly deserved it. Corrupt politicians, child abusers, basic scum of the earth, you name it. Their just deserts, not death, that's the easy way out for them-no, we make them live their most vivid nightmares. I don't fire lead, I fire dreams. Forged in the deepest pits of anguish and subconscious fears, my bullets take my targets into a deep sleep where they are imprisoned with what they did wrong. Their sins, the horrors they bring upon others, and can't bring themselves to see are presented to them, kinda like that story by whatshisname... Charles Dickens. Does this job leave a bad taste in my mouth? Sure, spending most of your time around the worst humanity has to offer does that, but I find that energy drinks do wonders to cleanse my pallette. This job wasn't always like this. My dad used to bring dreams and pleasant sleep to everyone, but as the world turned their backs on the Gods, so did we. The Gods became fickle. Whether it was humanity's faults that brought upon action from Zues and the others, or vice versa, I still don't know for sure. What I do know is that my father was then ordered to only bring nightmares and sleepless nights to man, and when he couldn't bear to do it anymore, he passed the mantle off to me, and I took it only to give my old man peace once again. I hate this job. But its nights like these, **with the city breathing its cold, mechanical rhythm around me,** that offer me silver linings. Its midnight, **the city lights blurring into a haze beneath a sky the color of bruised plums,** and I've already given the "Ghost of Christmas future" treatment to my mark- Billiam Hamilton, a 60-year old real estate tycoon who just demolished a community center here in Harlem to make way for his new luxury apartment complex. I'm sitting on a rooftop, **the chill seeping into my bones,** when I notice, across the street through a brightly lit window, a little boy crying because that same community center is where he went to play some trading card game with his friends. I always keep a couple of nice dream rounds my dad whips up on me just for these situations. I take aim and fire. The kid falls fast asleep soon after. Its not much, but it eases his pain and keeps me sain to do that, no matter if Zeus may disapprove. Sweet dreams, kid.

The deadline for the club to find a new buyer was extended last Tuesday by the administrators until 27 July.
RFL licensing director Blake Solly told BBC Sport: "There is a variety of opinion among the other clubs.
"But any decision as to whether Bradford will be allowed to stay in Super League is for the RFL board."
A group of local businessmen are keen to buy the four-time Super League winners, who went into administration last month.
Administrator Brendan Guilfoyle warned that the deal would be dependent on the RFL and the 13 other Super League clubs consenting to allow the club to remain in the top division and permitting the new owners to buy back their ground.
He said on Tuesday: "For the deal to go through we need the RFL to agree to us remaining in the top division and buying the ground back from them.
"We need the other clubs to agree that Super League is a poorer place without us."
Solly added that he was confident that the club would survive even if they had to drop into the Championship.
"There has been some consultation with the clubs already, but the ultimate decision maker will be the RFL," said Solly.
"Some clubs support the idea of Bradford staying in the league while others take a more hardline view.
"They would prefer to see them outside because leaving them in would impact on the integrity of the competition.
"But whether in the Super League or Championship, the passion of supporters in the past few months shows you that professional Rugby League has a big future in Bradford."
The economic future of Rugby League has been questioned after a year which has seen Wakefield go into administration and Welsh club Crusaders leave Super League over money issues.
But Solly believes clubs which are well run can continue to flourish.
He said: "We believe there is a viable economic model for a 14-club Super League. You just have to manage your club very well.
"When the licences were announced last year it was clear that if a club failed during the licence period the licence was in danger of being revoked.
"Bradford have failed so they have brought themselves into that area.
"What we've tried to do is create an environment that allows good clubs to succeed. Unfortunately some clubs like Bradford haven't done that."

Imagine a class where you learn to understand your feelings better. A new study found that teens who got this kind of "emotional intelligence" training in school felt better emotionally. They had less anxiety, depression, and social stress. Even better, these positive changes stuck around for up to six months after the training finished, according to the Journal of Adolescent Health.

So, what exactly is "emotional intelligence" (EI)? It's about being able to understand, show, and manage your feelings. Desiree Ruiz-Aranda, the lead author of the study, says that learning to handle your emotions is absolutely key to feeling good physically and mentally. Plus, when young people are mentally healthier, they tend to be physically healthier too and might even make fewer risky choices.

Ruiz-Aranda emphasized that it's crucial to start building these emotional skills as early as possible. Ideally, we should offer this kind of help *before* young people even start showing major emotional struggles.

The study looked at about 300 students in Spain. They took part in 24 hour-long training sessions over two years. These weren't boring lectures; the teens played games, did role-playing, art, and had discussions. All these activities were designed to help them recognize emotions in various situations, build empathy, and learn to solve emotional problems. Compared to a group that didn't get the training, these teens had less depression, social stress, and other negative feelings – and these improvements lasted for six months!

Ruiz-Aranda noted that for these programs to really work, they need to be fit for different cultures and ages, and taught in ways that connect to everyday life.

Gary Low, a retired education professor from Texas A&M University, thinks schools haven't done a great job preparing students for real life, especially when it comes to emotional intelligence. He pointed out that schools often don't help students learn how to handle their fears, stresses, relationships, and other challenges.

"Instead, we often just hope people pick up these skills on their own as they get older," he said. "But we haven't focused on creating school programs that truly help young people thrive and experience more success in life."

For more details, you can find the original study here: Ruiz-Aranda, D., et al. (2012). Short- and midterm effects of emotional intelligence training on adolescent mental health. Journal of Adolescent Health. doi: 10.1016/j.jadohealth.2012.02.003.

The new work from the University of Wisconsin-Madison helps move stem cells a small step closer to clinical reality by completely ridding the culture medium in which they are grown of animal products that could harbour viruses or other deleterious agents.

James Thomson, the senior author of the new study and a UW-Madison professor of anatomy — who seven years ago was the first to successfully grow human embryonic stem cells in the lab — highlights the significance of this development: "We've been optimising media on the existing stem cell lines since 1998, but it has only been recently that there have been dramatic improvements. This is the first time it has been possible for us to derive new cell lines in completely defined conditions in medium that completely lacks animal products."

Other groups, Thomson notes, have cultured established human embryonic stem cells in animal-product free media; however, those efforts included the use of poorly defined or proprietary products, and no one had previously demonstrated derivation of new cell lines under defined conditions.

The two new Wisconsin stem cell lines, derived using this method, have survived for more than seven months in the new culture medium. However, Thomson reports that one of these new lines had an abnormal chromosome at four months, while the second line, initially normal, developed an abnormality by seven months.

In contrast to these new lines, Thomson's group also successfully cultured four existing stem cell lines in the new culture mix for extended periods, and their chromosomes remained normal.

These two new Wisconsin stem cell lines were derived from five blastocysts, embryos less than a week old, which were donated for research with the informed, written consent of patients who were no longer undergoing treatment for infertility.

MEDICA.de; Source: University of Wisconsin-Madison

The awaited moment arrives at last! Ever since he brought us here, I've been counting down to this very instant. One by one, I witnessed my fellow slices vanish before me. None ever returned. We all understood their fate, though silence shrouded the truth. Truth be told, I'm the chatty one among us - always have been. Yet now, words escape me as my moment approaches, nerves stealing my voice. 

Our package shifts, and I steady myself. My crumbs tremble as his fingers close around me. The porcelain plate presses cold and firm against my back. The knife rises above me. This is it - my moment has come. With every fiber of my crust, I cry out: "Pete, wait! It's me...your father."

In the shadow of towering glass medical centers and bustling urban clinics, a silent epidemic spreads through the arteries of modern civilization. The fluorescent-lit corridors of hospitals echo with the steady beeping of heart monitors, while outside, city streets pulse with the hurried footsteps of millions—yet paradoxically, it is our very stillness that threatens to stop our hearts.

**The Gathering Storm**

The cardiovascular disease epidemic looms like storm clouds over contemporary society, its dark tendrils intertwined with the creeping paralysis of sedentary living. In sterile conference rooms where public health officials gather around polished tables, the statistics paint a grim picture: despite global prevention guidelines that emphasize physical activity with the urgency of emergency broadcasts, the reality on the ground tells a different story. Changing entrenched habits proves more elusive than prescribing medication—like trying to redirect a river with bare hands.

Against this backdrop of mounting concern, the Ministry of Health launched their "Eat Dynamic Balance, to Health" campaign in 2010, designating November as "Walk Month." Yet the initiative seemed to vanish into the urban fog, reaching only scattered individuals while the masses remained unmoved, cocooned in their chairs and cars.

**A Ray of Hope Through the Medical Maze**

Deep within research laboratories where scientists pore over decades of data under the harsh glow of desk lamps, a simple truth emerged from the complexity: walking—humble, accessible walking—stood as the most elegant solution to our modern malaise. Unlike the intimidating chrome and mirrors of fitness centers, walking required no special arena, no costly equipment, no mastery of complex techniques. It could unfold on any sidewalk, in any weather, across any landscape where feet could find purchase.

The medical community, having witnessed countless patients struggle with more demanding exercise regimens, began to champion this ancient human movement. Research papers accumulated like autumn leaves, each one reinforcing the same conclusion: walking was democracy in motion, available to every age, every social stratum, every physical condition.

**The Wisdom of Experts**

In the hushed reverence of medical conferences, where the WHO's pronouncements carry the weight of global authority, walking earned its designation as the safest, most effective path to fitness and weight management. Professor Hu Dayi, speaking from years of clinical experience, described brisk walking as humanity's most accessible gateway to aerobic health—a key that could unlock cardiovascular vitality without the barriers that kept so many from starting.

The science painted a clear picture: true health-promoting exercise must be aerobic, creating that delicate balance where the body's oxygen hunger matches its supply. In this metabolic sweet spot, achieved through sustained, rhythmic movement, the heart, lungs, and circulatory system undergo their beneficial transformation.

**The Rhythm of Renewal**

Brisk walking—or "endurance walking" as it's known in clinical circles—emerges as a precise prescription rather than a casual stroll. Picture the dedicated walker, maintaining their purposeful pace 3-5 times weekly, each session lasting 20-30 minutes, covering 5-8 kilometers with determined strides. This isn't the leisurely window shopping pace of weekend wanderers, but the focused cadence of someone investing in their future health.

The body's metabolism tells its own story during these sessions: for the first 20 minutes, stored sugars fuel the journey, but beyond that threshold, fat begins its steady burn. The mathematics are elegant in their simplicity—with 300,000 excess calories accumulating daily in the average person, the remedy lies in 10,000 steps, equivalent to 45 minutes of tennis or 30 minutes of swimming.

**The Art of Movement**

To witness proper walking technique is to see the human form in its most efficient expression: head held high like a ship's figurehead cutting through the air, shoulders relaxed yet purposeful, core engaged, arms swinging in natural rhythm. Each step follows the ancient pattern—heel to forefoot to toe—that has carried humanity across continents and through millennia.

**Breaking the Chains of Inertia**

Yet knowledge alone cannot break the gravitational pull of modern convenience. In office buildings where elevators whisk people past perfectly good staircases, in neighborhoods where two-block journeys warrant taxi rides, the challenge becomes cultural transformation. Professor Hu Dayi's prescription cuts through the complexity: weave movement into the fabric of daily existence rather than treating it as a separate, burdensome task.

The solution lies not in finding time, but in reclaiming it—transforming the eight hours of daily routine into opportunities for gentle, persistent movement. In this vision, stairs become invitations rather than obstacles, and short distances become chances for renewal rather than inconveniences to avoid.

Through this lens, walking emerges not as another item on an overwhelming to-do list, but as a return to our essential nature—one step at a time, one breath at a time, one heartbeat at a time.

If you're still unsure about whether you *want* to buy a house, then this is the book to help you make up your mind. It covers the basics of mortgages, to finding a house, to deciding between types of houses, i.e. condos/new/resale/fixer-upper/etc. It's a quick and easy read, nicely defined words, pictures, but for if you decide to buy a house/condo, or have already decided, then you definitely need something else.

Roberto Mancini has agreed to a new five-year deal with Manchester City after leading the club to their first English championship in 44 years. The Italian masterminded an unforgettable end to the Premier League season when two stoppage-time goals in City's final match secured the title ahead of rivals Manchester United. The ex-Inter Milan coach has now pledged his future to the club until 2017. "I am thrilled to be able to dedicate all of my efforts to Manchester City for another five years," Mancini told the club's official website. Manchester City clinch Premier League title in dramatic fashion. "The potential to build on our recent achievements is tremendous. Manchester City is an outstanding football club, from the owner, chairman, board and executive team, to the players, staff and supporters. "I am eagerly anticipating the challenges and excitement to come." Mancini took charge in December 2009 following the dismissal of Mark Hughes, and has been given substantial resources to strengthen the squad by the club's wealthy owners. Players such as Yaya Toure, David Silva, Mario Balotelli and Sergio Aguero have all joined for significant fees and played key roles in securing the club’s first major silverware in 2011, when they lifted the English FA Cup. It was a goal from Argentine striker Aguero that sealed their sensational Premier League triumph in the final match of last season against Queens Park Rangers, now managed by Hughes. It signaled a shift in dominance in the Premier League as Mancini and his side ultimately dethroned Manchester United, who hold a record 20 English titles. City's interim chief executive officer, John MacBeath, commented on the agreement: "Roberto's managerial track record has been thoroughly demonstrated in Europe for many years. "Having guided a team to both the FA Cup and a memorable Premier League title in back-to-back seasons, his capability to manage successfully in English football is unquestionable. "This new contract enables Roberto to concentrate on the task of leading a team that can not only retain the Premier League title but also challenge for European success." City open the defense of their Premier League crown with a home fixture against newly-promoted Southampton on August 19.

This book is the type that is often assigned by educators, and it requires considerable determination to complete. The prose is overly lengthy and frequently confusing, lacking the vivid language that typically brings a setting to life and engages the reader. Rather than providing the enjoyment one might expect from reading, this book feels more like an obligation than a pleasure. I would only recommend this book to someone who struggles with insomnia, as its monotonous tone may help induce sleep. For others, I strongly advise against reading this book to avoid unnecessary frustration.

Wii U PS4 Xbox One PC Long ago, the four gaming platforms lived together in harmony... But then, everything changed when the console peasants attacked. Only the PC master race, master of all gaming, could stop them... But when the gaming community needed them most, they were off putting together a new kick-ass rig. A few hours later the PC master race finished their new rig... And although the rig runs GTA V max settings at 60fps, it has a lot of benchmarking to do before it's ready to give any console a run for it's money. But I believe the PC master race can save the world.
